You can put something on the wall. If it’s a real problem, get a teleprompter. Then you’ll be looking dead center. :)

Spaced-repetition by thanking after the interview and as you’re walking out, that’s never too much.

Mayanwolfe put a template: “Hi , I really enjoyed speaking with you and the rest of the team today about the role. Please let me know if you have any additional questions and I look forward to the next step in the process.” So basically the same messge, just slightly less formal

blackheadband: hack: run your voice through a voicechanger when playing back 😄

dvkr: I think it’s Amazon that has 1 - I will quit if you don’t hire this person 2 - I recommend you hire this person 3 - I recommend you don’t hire this person 4 - I will quit if you hire this person

Is only Js Ts not enough anymore? Your local area might not value these things… Finish a full course and from there you have a really solid foundation to go learn something else. JS and TS is enough. Some local areas… A lot of recruiters assume one is frontend one is backend, but that’s not true… It’s just the recruiter that you’re talking to.

Mayanwolfe: It’s not about the language, it’s about the skills and how you sell yourse;f

The 5 minute denial letters, there’s something they’re using as a knock-out question. Especially in the US, there’s a lot of stuff around employment law… and legitimately if you get past the simple knockout questions, you have to be seen by a human. What often happens is they quickly scan, and you don’t pass the 6-second sniff test from the recruiter… Use the resume template and the story builder for GPT so you can get past the AI.

ROxy RodBeck: i use AI to tweak my resume and cover letters for specific roles…i dont straight up copy and paste. i then tweak the AI’s response to be more like me/true.

There’s a signature in the text that you just copy and paste. I use WhisperFlow for everything… If you don’t go over things yourself, you want to redo it inyour own words, even retyping it. Your resume just sucks if you can’t get past those initial 6 seconds, and you can fix it! That’s a good thing.

Which option would best improve my chances of getting hired? It depends on what role you’re going after. Situation dependent. Research if it’s a research role. For most people an independent project would be best. Keep a wins list/dev blog of everything you’ve done so you can communicate it later on, if you’re working OR NOT WORKING.

<@153255600623452160> Any tips on how to “safely bypass” nda when it comes to delegated work from my mother company? Lets say I was woring for X but did work as support at Epic which is big name

You gotta talk to the team. You can always ask for concessions if you need them. For example, conferences… There are no shorcuts for NDA. You need approval.

How does one regain confidence after being let go? If you have the privilege, take a second. Let the past be the past. At least take a week, if you can… Depends on your emergency fund. Two weeks is better to take off, if you can. Let the stress go. Start making a game plan for what you WANT. What your goals are, where you want to be, what you really need things to be like. Treat that like a job. Do other stuff, read, explore, etc… but KEEP A STRUCTURE.

Confidence comes from proving to yourself that you are who you say you are. That you can do what you say you do.

Do CAR. Go to Banki brunch. Etc. PRACTICE.

How do I get back to banki after a couple of months? You can just wipe the slate clean, or up your review to 50-100 cards a day. Pick an amount of time that feels comfortable to you. Trust the algorithm. Start with a block of time you know you can commit to. Not specific time, but a time of day more or less. Lower the activation energy. Maybe a 5 minute session instead of a 30 minute session. Don’t let the total number of cards be overwhelming.

Walking pad, Anki, and Brilliant.

What do you use to record your interviews? Podcasts? Riverside. If in an interview? Typically OBS. There’s one people really like… but there are a bunch of AI voice recorders… Know what state you’re in. One-party consent area? Be wary of what you’re able to do.

When to follow-up if you haven’t heard back after a week? Try Sunday or Monday. Craziest was 8 MONTHS LATER.

Data structure and algorithms focus for frontend data role? It depends on what door you open. There’s only so many opportunities. Open up ALL the doors you can. What do you have time for. WHich are the most effective for you. FAANG it’s necessary.

You can get away with anything specialty0wise in the Bay. JS and Python is more than enough. You have to be smart with your hitlisting.

Don’t overthink a portfolio. Most people just won’t see it. Heros, projects, experience is just fine.

Add on accessory things, like React, once you’ve done your 100hours project.

Freelancing February to figure out how to reach out to potential clients? If you’re cold-emailing people, it’s probably not going to work. You have to do something that stands out. Keep it simple, or casual, and value. Especially cold.

How to approach a connection with a higher up? Theyre only a damn human. Don’t treat someone differently than a normal person. Same value as you.

Feelings on using OpenClaw to handle emails? There’s triage levels, yes/no… But there’s a lot of automation that can help. Gemini in line with Gmail has been absurdly helpful. Ex: last 5 projects I worked on with this person. Not having AI in the mix is the mistake. It’ll give you an edge.

Do a basic react project for react roles and over time just add to it.

Miguel (hand-raised): Video editor, changed to software engineer, then changed it back. Trying to get a job and it’s been 4 months, going to networking events, but when they see my linkedin… Putup on LinkedIn what you’re looking for roles in. Don’t give the impression that you arent who you say you are, and that you don’t have experience. You can incorporate your past into your present, but maybe you’re doing something that brings your background into your present search.

Daniel (hand-raised): asked about the backend language stuff. “I have experience with those! I’m interested in learning more. I’d love to talk about it.”

There’s an urge to not oversell yourself. This is the one time in life you MUST oversell yourself. No pre-apology.

Recruiters are trying to get as many crabs into the bucket as possible. Don’t kick yourself out of the running, if you know one of those options they’re talking about on the job posting.

A lot of bigger companies will let you interview in whatever you want to interview in.

Plethora.js

Lesliee Coder (hand-raised): read “how to work a room” if you have to lead a team of 5 engineers in an interview. FizzBuzz. Etc. Lean into what you’re being asked to do.

Try to find what they’re going to ask you.

PREP AND PRACTICE and you will be fine.

Lexi (hand-raised): How to get accepted into a higher role? Sometimes they’ll make a higher offer, but sometimes you just have to ask. Save it for the negotiating phase once you have the offer.

Matthias (hand-raised): getting a job bootcamp! of course you still belong here. if you don want to be a software engineer sometimes it’s the friends you make along the way.

Alex K: How valuable is restaurant management experience in the tech field? It’s more of a time thing. If you can do the management experience AND work to your goal, move the needle? Probably not… If you’re stable and able to have more time to learn, that’s probably the best, but do what you need.

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-03
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Tues 2/3/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[2:33 PM PT]

Likely the last day of Slido since this is the last day of Leon’s subscriptions. May be have a different platform on Friday.

“How should I show my projects in my resume? I don't want to deploy as I don't want to pay for it, and most of my projects are full-stack.”

Tricky question. There are platforms we can use depending on where we live. IP Address dating nowadays, but some allow us to post full-stack project for free

Render, Fly.io, etc.

All have tradeoffs, but there are hacks around everything

Leon uses Railway, Percell, Fly.io

Different things for different purposes

Lots of them will give you subdomains. WhateverYouChoose.fly.io

Don’t have to pay for domains. Lots give you subdomains for free. Use those subdomains for portfolio, and do it that way

Fly seems best for free account usage, but they IP-limit in terms of who can get free accounts

Leon asked them about this directly, and they were vague

If Leon was HitListing and had a great portfolio? He would get $5 to switch to a paid account, using the subdomains, just to make sure there are no cold starts. $5 should be able to get all projects up on the sub domains to help reduce friction during a serious HitListing period, and this should get us far enough to get hired

Lots of the providers change. It may be the best one month, but then the next month it’s trash. Leon doesn’t like giving blanket recommendations because of this

Location can definitely cause your best option to be different, too

Heroku used to be the choice, but it’s no longer free (Leon says this makes sense, as what they were doing was likely costly)

They used to be Leon’s default option

Heroku going paid crushed a lot of people :(

Lots of options

Many are being shared in the Huddle Stage chat

“How to handle interview invitations? Pause HitListing and prep for interview exclusively, then resume HitListing when the interview is over?”

Comes up a lot. Leon thinks this is really important for things to understand

Biggest mistake people make on the Hunt

Don’t over-invest in one opportunity, and then have the opportunity not pan out

This is an issue because we need to play the numbers game, and an emotional game

Most people not successful on the Hunt because the emotions become too much, and they stop

Don’t put all your eggs in the basket of an interview for a week, and then get ghosted after the first round

That’s hard to bounce back from emotionally

If all your eggs are in one basket for an interview and the interview doesn’t go the way we want it to go, it’s very hard to get started again

If we’re hitlisting that whole week and getting other interviews and other things on the horizon, that’s fantastic

If an interview goes poorly, we have another one coming up, and so we can bounce back emotionally much more easily

NEVER exclusively focus on one interview to avoid the emotional hit if it goes badly

Timebox your day when HitListing, and keep adding to the top of the funnel so that you get more interview opportunities continuously (adding more companies to the top of the funnel, allowing more interviews to filter out)

Easier said than done. See where you’re overextending, and adjust accordingly

Try not to focus too much on the individual interviews

Once we’re getting an interview at a company, that’s 1 out of 60, and we should get hired by 60 interviews

50-60 contacts per week is a good cadence, especially if we’re doing other things alongside HitListing

That should get us about 180 contacts at the 60 companies we’re targeting

Leon, with all his experience, can do about 10 good contacts (with research) per hour

Madeleine’s new Coffee Chat guide gives us tips about prospecting that can help with this

Start slow, and then ramp up over time, so that we can get our hourly contact rate up over time

Will start to get templates that work for us and generate responses, and we can start increasing the number more easily

“Trying to understand the process from Hitlist message to referral more than ‘just make a friend?’ For me that’s like saying just fly to the moon. I’ve read WFIP”

If you’re struggling with this personally, find someone who is good at it on the 100Devs Discord and talk to them

For some folks, the ambiguity in this process is very overwhelming, and the easiest way to get a little more comfortable with it is to see how someone else is doing it effectively

Helps us bring rules to the chaos

Some folks need to make rules to follow and see it. Just hearing how to do it isn’t as effective as watching someone else doing this

Leon has four classes in the 100Devs playlist that shows the process, and this is really Leon’s process and the one he recommends.

Roxy, who shares her process in the #Chosen-Follow-Along channel, is crushing the process, says Leon

Once some people get the Coffee Chat, it’s very easy to make the ask from the coffee chat person for a referral

“Hey Sarah, I really appreciated our chat a month ago. (Sarah works at Bob’s Burgers). Opened my eyes to the industry. I see that Bob’s Burgers now has an open role. Who is the best person for me to talk to about this role? Thanks so much. - Leon”

Most of the time you get ghosted. This is why you’re contacting three people at the company

Sometimes, they say “Joe is the best person to talk to”

“Wonderful. Can you please connect me to Joe?”

Sometimes you get ghosted, sometimes, Sarah puts you in touch with Joe. This is an implicit referral

You have now jumped the pile in terms of applications. This is awesome

Other times, the company has an internal referral system with incentives, and the person can potentially get a bonus if you get hired

They can then +explicitly+ refer you through this system, and also help you jump the pile

Remember: You’re doing this at scale. Don’t put all your eggs in one basket and risk the emotional hit that could derail you

Contacting 1 company at a time? “You’re fucked in they don’t respond.”

60 companies at a time, and 75% ghost you? No problem. You’ve still got 15 companies that are still potentials

One of the 100Devs former mods got crushed in an interview, but had another lined up the next day

Rocked the following interview, got a senior role straight out of 100Devs

This is why we keep a large sample size. We can be the exact same person, and just get much luckier with one opportunity over another. Biggest thing we can do to maximize our success? Increase our numbers

YouTuber that Leon finds helpful, which underscores Leon’s point that no one really cares if you screw up. You care the most, so just overcome the anxiety and give yourself the shot, and maximize your chances by not being too invested in each opportunity

Leon doesn’t think about any 100Devs individual all the time, but he thinks about the community

He’s not talking about (random 100Devs Huddle attendee) PowerRangers55 with his wife at dinner. Something like that has never happened. Ever.

Therefore, it’s important that you understand: “No one thinks about the things you’ve said or done outside of the 15 seconds since you’ve said/done them. There’s just too much going on.”

Over time some people might be more memorable, but even there, there’s very little risk to say something, make a post, or make an attempt

Risk fo failure or harm is +SO SMALL+, especially compared to the potential upside

Scared to ask a question during a Huddle? If you don’t ask, there’s very little chance you’re going to get the answer you’re hoping for. If you ask? Maybe you typo hard and someone laughs. No big deal, but you might still get the answer you’re hoping for, and how great is that?

How many LeBron James Instagram posts do we remember? None, and this is a super famous, influential athlete

Someone in the chat: “I don’t think I ever saw it”

Leon thinks this is beautiful. Incredible athlete, who is on TV every other night, and many of us have not seen even one of his Instagram posts. There are 7 Billion people alive right now. If you can’t remember what’s on Lebron’s socials, no one cares as much about what you’re doing as you. Try to make the best impression you can, but don’t worry too much about it. If you mess up, it’s okay, and it’s even more okay if you KEEP GOING. Don’t fall on your face and then stay on the ground. Get up and try again.

“Climb cringe mountain. Send the e-mail, send the tweet, because it doesn’t matter.”

“How can we sell ourselves and our achievements? What to say beyond writing code or creating an application. How to keep it factual without being underwhelming?”

100Devs GPT is a good place to start

Asks us about our wins and accomplishments and learn how to talk about them in a compelling way

Great use of AI. Helps with Bio blurb and get the ball rolling about our narrative

Mostly about not snitching on ourselves. It’s both, though.

People are afraid to speak highly of ourselves because we’ve been conditioned by capitalism to not outshine the master

People feel like they can’t brag about themselves because it might be perceived as lying

Don’t Ever Lie

You don’t have to. But saying every positive thing you can, and letting people fill in the blank around those positive things, is the strategy to use, because those are the types of people you’re competing with

Always worry about underselling and snitching on yourself

As long as we don’t lie, we can’t oversell

If we really struggle with this, which is common, we can use the GPT to learn how to speak positively about ourselves

“What about asking for referrals for open roles you see now or from someone you just met from your hitlist? instead of contacting people you met a while ago.”

Should be consistently networking while learning so we have a great network when we start Hitlisting

Leon puts search back 12 months to see about what companies have been hiring

If they have an open role? Can go straight for it. Great narrative, great portfolio? Reach out immediately to your contacts at that company and “go for the kill”

20 messages and 15 ghost you? No problem. The five you get is enough to help you get the success you want. That’s why we’re playing the numbers

“would it be better to go with website builders for things liike ecommerce and scheduling than to try to code out an entire solution. Which do you recommend?”

Absolutely. 1000%. E-Commerce? Use Shopify, or something else off the shelf, and then when we get product:market fit, we can optimize.

Shopify will get us a long way

Shelf product depends on what we’re doing

Shopify is probably the easiest right now

Narrative GPT Link:

https://chatgpt.com/g/g-68e95630e9b481918051aa54d9deec8a-bob-your-story-crafting-coach-from-100devs/c/69701321-51bc-832b-bb0c-309c421d5878

Companies that recently got funding are good for HitListing

“I don't know how to ask for more money from potential freelance clients. I believe people when they say they land large contracts. Mine penny pinch to the max.”

Depends on the companies you’re going after

Companies that have lots of money likely have more to give you

Family-owned business probably doesn’t have a bunch to offer you

E-Commerce established brand has more to give you to optimize the hell out of their website

Leon always asks clients what their budgets are, and then he can work from there based on what they have, what they want, and how much of his services they can afford

“Do you have access to a hiring rubric we could review? I’d like to know the key differentiators btw staff/lead/senior/junior that’s not a job description”

All made up. Every company is different. Between two companies, a senior can be a one year person vs. a ten year person

No universal rubric that would be helpful, as every company treats every term differently

This can be frustrating, but it’s the reality

Leon’s view is that entry-level is someone that needs help, and senior is someone who can reliable solve problems on their own. Leads can think larger scale about the impact across the org, and its their responsibility to think more strategically long term

Most companies don’t seem to think like this, in Leon’s experience, but it makes sense to him

When we’re looking at job descriptions, it’s a “WANT” list, not a “MUST” list

Many listings are made up by recruiters who don’t really know what they’re asking for, so don’t worry too much about the descriptions and meeting all the requirements exactly

“I just started building out the hitlist, I find jobs on HiringCafe and emails with Apollo. Should I just be looking for HR/Technical Recruiters? Or SWEs too? Ty”

Using the right tools

Recruiters are the easiest to get messages back from, because it’s their job

In certain cities, this may not be true. Recruiters might be overwhelmed

Leon starts with recruiters, and then will look for anyone that is technical on the team if the recruiters aren’t getting back to him

Chat talks about the value of using multiple tools, because not everyone is easily accessible on LinkedIn (recruiters often are, though.)

Leon needs to get referral codes for these tools so he can show them how much traffic they’re getting from 100Devs and should collaborate more with us (ideally with things like free accounts)

Leon has tried begging in the past, but they’re very picky

Leon also has a new .edu e-mail hack that he’s hoping to share soon

“are most interview online? I am curious attending in person interviews would be challenging for me because of my job”

In-person interviews frequent for companies that expect us in office

Many people use PTO time for interviewing. Sad, but that’s how this works

“Can you reshare the huntober doc with all the resources you mentioned/reviewed?”

Don’t have this done yet. It’s on Leon’s to-do list

Leon planning to record a 10 minute walk through for how to use the resources. Thinks they need a walkthrough to help people make the most of them. Hoping to do this this weekend.

“How long does it typically take from a hitlist message to a job? Also do you recommend splitting time 50/50 clicking( for open jobs)vs hit listings”

Leon doesn’t recommend applying. May be the thing for some cities, but Leon doesn’t find it successful

Typical path? CS program for 4 years, internships every summer, and then you get hired at the end

You’re ahead of the game if you can do it with just a 30 week program like 100Devs

Therefore, just keep at it, and realize it’s a process

“what about your coworker. What value do companies expect from junior/mid/senior? speaking about non big tech can someone teach himself to perform as a senior?”

Leon has seem some seniors that can’t do a fizzbuzz

…

Mids have some autonomy, but still need support. Seniors can be mostly independent, and might need some high-level guidance

Easier to grow under the tutelage of others, but can work on to senior doing open source or freelancing independently

From chat: Even someone who is hired as senior is still entry-level on a new codebase

Leon thinks AI can help people get more senior with a new codebase

“Do I connect with the recruiter for a company first or do I wait until ive made connections and Im ready for the job”

Don’t delay, start today. Outreach starts now

Know companies you want to work for? Start making connections even before you start 100Devs

“Looking for opportunities in 3-6 months. Can I reach out then?”

This commonly works. Build the network early

“How can I avoid going into a PIP and maintain my confidence despite my manager's strict performance expectations and my lack of technical knowledge?”

Fix lack of technical knowledge, for sure. PIPs are often layoff tools, and have nothing to do with workers. Most of the time, they have nothing to do with technical ability

PIPs can signal you’re about to get laid off at one company, but another company may use the PIP to try to bring you back in

Each company is different. Companies with supportive PIPs are good

Have conversation with manager about expectations, and then can get buy-in from other members of the team to see if these expectations are reasonable

Always good to be public and visible is IMPORTANT

Lots of the time, people don’t know what you’re doing, so you have to be public with your gratitude, your praise, and your achievements

Use public channels

Lots of people don’t do this, and they are surprised when other people don’t know all the good things they’ve done. Need to constantly communicate

Everyone around you should know that you’re doing what you’re supposed to be doing

Nights and weekends are where growth comes from, not on the job, so make sure you’re always making time to get the growth in

New RC Cohort

Every day a Slack thread opens with asynch update, and at night a thread there’s a thread about a win from the day

This helps them get comfortable tracking their wins and what they’re doing each day

Having this history helps you advocate for yourself for performance reviews, raises, and finding a new job

Plan to start something like this for the 100Devs community

Tool ties to Slack, with a usergroup for the Fellows. Ties to a dashboard that sees how many checkins we have done, and there are bots that review resume and LinkedIn

Can see history of checkins and wins

Every single student (“Fellows”) has a log of all the work they’ve accomplished and all the wins they’ve had through their days

As soon as someone is added to the user group, they’re automatically added to the dashboard and tracker

Should only take a few seconds for Leon to set this up for 100Devs, and the Chat seems to like it

[3:25 PM PT - Sorry. I clearly was forgetting to timestamp :( ]

Miguelisms asked for a resume Review

Leon could only do with a Google Doc, that Miguel had ready

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1uDBV1G\_zfYQ8BdXyIAn1R5ZPPOG2V7-bTqjDuBqN1H4/edit?usp=sharing

Leon thinks the resume needs to be finished using the 100Devs Bob Coder format

Blurb needs to be redone with ChatGPT

Baristas make coffee, so tell something new about your work (not that you make coffee if you’re a barista. Your job title already covers that)

Need to follow X, Y, Z format of bullets

First bullet point is most important

Use the 100Devs tools to optimize resume before a live review, but then Leon’s happy to review it live

[3:27 PM PT]

Daniel Hopkins live

How best to study after work. How do I focus my studying when I’m burnt out?

Leon thinks studying before work is best

DH thinks he might have some learning disability. Leon thinks he might just be tired. Most people can’t study after work, because they’re mentally depleted

DH thinks his situation is a little unique. Over ten years of experience in the industry, but he hasn’t reached a big place in it because he hasn’t done some after work growth. Just been doing the bare minimum

Leon says he needs to figure out his why, and then shoot for the stars. If you want more out of the journey, then you have to build your life to get more

Not working out to grow after work? Go to bed earlier, get up earlier, and try it in the morning. Need to keep doing experiments until you find something that works

You have to make sacrifices if you want the bigger wins. No cheat code or magic cure. It’s just about putting in the work

Common issue for people to struggle after work. Timeboxing is useful if you really want to try after work growth. Good to maybe exercise post-work, pre-growth, because it can help your brain refresh

Best advice is to just do it in the morning, though

Dan (HUNT)

Life changed through 100Devs

Hour long interview coming up Thursday

Senior developer and graphic designer

Wants Dan’s approach to troubleshooting and improving the CMS

Leon says Dan has been blessed by being told everything the interview that will cover

Want to show he can hit the ground running with any problem they have, and he can prep his answers to do this

Have proof from the things that you’ve done to show specific examples of how what you’ve done shows that you can solve their issues

Lots of work for the next day or two, but that’s the best approach

Dan is optimistic he can do this

[3:33 PM PT]

UncleNoob (100Hrs Project)

Connection was looking to review his resume for a job. Wondering if Leon can look at the Google Doc, even though we’re over time

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1LwB1m4s8LmSvLcLFl4hytVmOf7FOfmsoPFiP\_69JmPk/edit?usp=sharing

Leon remembers that Noob has put poop emojis on the follow-along materials posts

Proves Leon’s point that he does remember the routine interactions from people in our community, so we should always be on our best behavior :p

Issues with resume can be resolved with Bob the Coder Resume and Chat GPT narrative builder

Executive summary should help hook people and push people beyond the 6 seconds. If your summary can’t do that, then you should just omit it

Same advice that he gave Miguel. Don’t restate what a position does

Use X,Y,Z format

First bullet point should +always+ be X,Y,Z

Give projects real names so they seem more impactful, and not just random projects

First project is 100Hrs project

Should have real name, with impactful bullet points

If there’s not a real name, it’s not as legitimate

Review your resume, and make sure that everything you have on there is going to convince someone you are someone they should be +thrilled+ to move onto the next round

Lots of good comments in chat, Leon says

Also, only fire emojis going forward. No more poop

[3:39 PM PT]

GnBaker is Hitlisting

Using LinkedIn Connections. Free messages on LinkedIn

Connection doesn’t really matter. If they accept your connection, if lets you message them for free. Most important thing is to send the message at the end

Best strategy is to use Apollo to get company e-mails, which allows you to send a message

Free LinkedIn Messages are limited. Don’t waste connection messages on cold outreach. Usually just add people, and if they connect, then you can message them

Apollo may be more successful

Messaging is the most important thing, however you can get that message (either by making connections or by using Apollo)

Every person is a little different, and cities have different cultures

Most people on LinkedIn are probably looking for a job, so LinkedIn may not work as well to connect with people at companies who aren’t

Finding e-mail somewhere else, like Apollo, may be the better approach

Send that message, get the coffee chat, make the connections so that you can get the referrals

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-06
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/6/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:32 PM PT - Leon Hosting]

Still have a Slido

Leon excited to help the 100Devs community, and grateful for those here in chat helping people live, too

Hunt related questions to ask live: Negotiation, Preparing for Interviews

Leon’s not really following football. Wasn’t really sure who was playing. Leon lived in Boston for a while, but he’s into the Seahawks for this game. Likes that the Seahawks are incorporating some Rugby tackles into their game

Leon likes the commercials and Bad Bunny

[2:35 PM PT]

“I just made a LinkedIn and a twitter however, I’m not sure of the kind of content to post, do you have any recommendations?”

Whatever you put in time to learn, you’re ahead of the person that has not yet put in that time. Make their journey easier. That’s it. That’s the only sauce you need. Everything else is Fugazi. Don’t worry about it

Share accomplishments, and be helpful/useful to someone that’s a little farther behind that you are

If you do these two things, you’re crushing the game

These two things will find the right person

Once you’ve done this for a year, then you can start to optimize based on what has worked and what can be done sustainably

Sustainability is key. Lots of people burn out after a month because it’s not sustainable

Aim to show a path about what you’ve done and what has worked. Helps establish you as an authority that people will look to

This is a great way to get started

From chat: Don’t focus too much on gaming LinkedIn, as that loses sight of the goal

Leon: Just be helpful. Don’t try to game. Share your “wins” (anything successful, anything that works, anything you’ve built), build in public, don’t overthink it

Chat says anyone acting like a human on LinkedIn is great

Don’t overoptimize

Cringe Mountain

Most people are afraid of climbing cringe mountain and risking looking foolish. Just make the climb. It will be okay

“On class 32 and I'm trying to hitlist but having trouble finding hiring managers what should I be looking for?”

[2:38 PM PT]

Finding a Hiring Manager is hard because they’re not really labeling themselves as such

They’re the ones making the decision and that you’ll be reporting to

Better to find engineers on the team. Hiring Managers are often senior engineers

Best tool for this is Apollo. Free account lets you find contact information for people

Looking for job postings and things like that?

Look at posters on LinkedIn, because they’re often more likely to be a hiring manager or someone who cares about who gets hired

Apollo to find engineers, and use LinkedIn to see who posted about it, and try to talk to all of them. Remember, the more contacts the better

MayanWolfe in chat: Making silly stuff in public works. Don’t worry about it.

Leon: If you’re livestreaming and you’re building stuff? You’re already so far ahead, because everyone wants to be a DevRel person who can push their stuff live to an audience

Try to package fun in a way that can feature what you’re built or learned, but it’s fine to just focus on the fun in the beginning and you can optimize later (It seems to have worked for MayanWolfe <3)

[2:40 PM PT]

“How worried should I be of AI? spoke to a friend who is a software engineer and he says that they use cursor to do all the coding related tasks. AI writes code”

Leon: Then why is he still working there?

There is the issue.

They’re saying: “Look. I have this nice cushy job where I’m just letting AI do the coding…” But they don’t want you to do that?

That’s not a friend 😛

They should be trying to get everyone hired into that role

Try vibe coding an application and see how silly that is

Once you start building with these tools, you notice a lot of things. Even if it was perfect code every single time, which it is not, it actually makes more work for the engineers because you have to take the code and put it in front of a human. Once a human touches the application, all the weird stuff that you’ve never thought of starts to happen. Things break, things fall apart, and no AI can outdo the dirty cheeto-fingers that people bring to your app

What happens then? Now you have to build feature based off of human feedback. AI didn’t architect something that could know about this bug. Maybe SWE job is to architect systems that actually work! Now you’re back at square one

From chat: “It’s almost like you need a developer”

Exactly

AI will always overlook weird edge cases even if you ask it to account for this

Perfect code generation is still going to require engineering

Just accelerating the amount of work Leon gets done, but also causes him to have to spend more time with code.

Best startups right now. Ramp is having exponential growth. Shipping to prod 100 times a day because they’re AI-enabled, but needs tons of humans to be able to do this

Has always been something. Outsourced, offshored, nearshored, low-code (5 years ago with Bubble, etc.), AI is just the new Hype Cycle

Can’t listen to these excuses if we want to fundamentally change our lives and our income potential

If AI becomes really good? Businesses still need someone to implement these things for them. Best case scenario, there millions of low-tech people who need an AI-augmented engineer that can solve these problems

Worst case is the engineers will be the +last+ ones employed if AI takes away all the other jobs

Some people want an excuse not to learn how to code, but there has always been an excuse, and it has consistently not been a good excuse.

Those who have kept going despite the doomsayers have succeeded wildly. Let’s be one of those success stories, and inspire the next wave of 100Devers to succeed as well.

[2:47 PM PT]

“had an interview today & he said he would get back to me before 5pm and he hasn't... bad sign? it was an intro call”

Leon: Nope. Sometimes life gets in the way. Give them the weekend. Reach out on Monday, and be nice about reaching out

“How to Win Friends and Influence People” -> Always be kind and give people an out

If you don’t know how to be courteous like this, go find this book. YouTube readings, free pdfs online, no excuse

“Are coffee chats really the best use of time lately? I just don't think i have the energy ontop of everything else to do them.”

Leon: Stop everything else and do this. Networking your way in is the only thing that works. Real conversations with humans work at scale. Nothing else works as well

Find out how to rearrange life so that you can perform in a way that is optimal. Totally fine to take a break, but make sure you set a return date

Best use of your time has always been and will likely always be to talk to real humans and get referrals

It’s hard work, but it works. We need to put the work in.

[ Someone in chat got an offer, and Leon says he hopes to see it in the #celebrations channel at some point soon]

Coffee Chats also don’t have to be in person, especially in the modern era. Just need to interact with those humans

Some people in the chat have said they’ve never had coffee chats in person

Leon agrees. Post Pandemic, people don’t meet up in person as much anymore

Cohort 3 is still in the works

[2:50 PM PT]

“Learn, build, network, then interview comes around, get invested into prep. How to not get sidetracked by interviews?”

Leon: Once you’re taking actions, and you’re actively HitListing, the biggest trap is getting sidetracked.

Interview Friday, and it’s Monday? Don’t spend all week prepping, because most interviews will not result in a job, especially early round ones

Don’t put all your energy in an interview, and then have it not go well, and then your entire week can feel wasted, and now you have to restart the process from scratch. Creates stress and tension because you lose the momentum that you would have if you had also been continuing to HitList and generate more interviews

Having to restart momentum is emotionally draining

Timebox your day: Every day you have some time for HitListing, even on interview days

This keeps your momentum going

TimeBoxing, and having dedicated hours each day for high-impact activities related to the hunt

Keep this up even when you have interviews. It’s a skill that you need to develop, but Leon finds this to be the most successful strategy for people. Avoid the risk of getting overinvested

Some things are going to be harder for you than they might be for others. Just need to experiment with the strategies that work best for you at any given time, and use 100Devs’ wisdom to help inform you in terms of what might work best

[2:54 PM PT]

Leon points out that he and the Mods can routinely disagree, so there’s really no 100Devs dogma. Just need to be flexible, adaptive, and willing to grow together as a community

“What are some ways to heal from burnout? I can barely send a text these days, let alone start job hunting.”

For Leon, he needs a reset, and going someplace in nature where he has no access to the outside world. After camping for 3-4 days looking at the stars, he feels his brain finally relax

Ex. Looking at Washington Column in Yosemite, just staring at it from his chair, and he took a long sigh, felt his brain reset, and he was good for 3 months after that

“100Devs Summer Camp” in the chat resonated +strongly+ with Leon

Biggest thing for Leon is that some behaviors happen when we’re burning out that we perpetually spin into because we’re grasping for dopamine

When Leon is toasted, he consistently uses YouTube for dopamine hints. He knows when this happens, he’s very burnt out. Relying on YouTube or doomscrolling just for some type of reward

Feels almost like a compulsion, and it’s the one thing we can do, so we do it

Leon needs a full pattern disrupt to get out of this behavior, and it takes him 4-5 days for this “zombie mode” (from chat) to go away, and wilderness is what helps him

“What can I fill my time with productively that gets me away from the YouTube/Doomscrolling behavior?”

Books can help this. Reward himself with reading instead of watching. Something productive. Exercise in the morning. Healthy food. Fasting works for Leon. This self-care really helps Leon stay productive. Therapy can help

Most important thing is to figure out what the root cause of the burnout is

Most folks: Feeling like you’re not making progress in the things that matter

Need to break it down into digestible chunks that you have full control over

“Get a Job” is a bad goal

Low odds that you’ll get this on any given day

Better strategy: Focus on the little objectives you can do each day that will help you get that job

Hitlisting, anki, leetcode, etc. every day

At the end of each day, you can see that you’ve done these and made progress, and those daily activities will help you get the big goal

Lots of #celebrations posts about people getting contracts. Big shoutout to those people

Evidence that the 100Devs advice is still working. Just focus on the proven methods

From chat: “Failure is fuel” Leon really likes this. The struggle is what helps us grow

[3:00 PM PT]

“Can you do a "day in the life" e.g. of a web dev? Like how projects get assigned, timeframe given, how work is presented etc.”

Has to be some good ones out there. Leon’s probably not the best person to do this, because he’s an instructor most of the day. Not really a traditional webdev. Chat will share who they think is out there and accurate

“If I use the Harada method to become a Full Stack Software Engineer, what would be the 8 pillars in boxes to start with? My 8 boxes seem kinda basic?”

Need to do this yourself. Someone else’s Harada method will likely not work for you. Should take a week or a month to establish this, and then you’ll be constantly updating it

[3:02 PM PT]

Leon just answers the Slido question in the order that they’ve been upvoted

“If clients want to fix their WordPress and Shopify sites and you lack experience with either, what advice can you give?”

Spend some time and research it. Leon gets paid to solve problems, that’s it. He doesn’t care the tool or the framework. Client needs help with Shopify? He’s a competent engineer, and he can figure out what the issue is, what the client needs, and he takes a week to figure out some worthwhile solutions that work with the client’s budget. Here are my top 5 recommendations, what could work well, what the trouble points might be, etc.

Two kinds of decisions (may be from Bezos):

Cohort at Resilient Coders (RC) to build agency:

Decisions that are very easy to reverse

These are great. Go at it. No big deal if it’s wrong

Decisions that are very hard to reverse

Be more careful about these

Shopify should be pretty easy most the time. Very reversible decisions. Tricky stuff may just be going back to a previous commit, and that’s a very easy fix

Most of the clients Leon works with need help with someone Leon doesn’t have experience with. That’s fine. Spend more time on the harder decisions to reverse, and be willing to experiment with the easy decisions

Anytime you can do something with a client that opens up your knowledge, that’s great

More experience with Wordpress or Shopify, that’s an opportunity to expand your skill set so you can help other clients in these areas

[3:05 PM PT]

“Thoughts on using AI when completing 100 Devs? My current plan is to only use it in some projects after completing the full course so I have some experience”

Leon recommends not using AI at all. Huge mistake that cheats us out of learning

Any shortcuts we take while learning is preventing our brain from building the connection we need it to build

Most people do not have the restraint to keep AI from harming their long-term trajectory and learning

Advice for 100Devs? Do not use AI +at all+

Once you’re done with 100Devs, please feel free to use AI to help you learn how to build with AI on a project

Whenever Leon learns something new, he tries to avoid AI as much as possible, and only checks with AI when he feels like he has achieved mastery on a topic

Chat: Worst part of learning is you can’t skip the hard part. If you skip those parts, then you’re not really learning.

What AI does is it puts all the information right into your chat window. That’s a huge disaster for learning

Does not let you build the connections that you would build when looking up this information. Lets you see the differences and similarities, and the connections between tools. The journey is the learning process, and AI does all this for us.

[3:08 PM PT]

“I'm ready for the hunt and have more time for the next 2 months after being laid off. I find myself clicking more since the hitlist( 4month+)method seems slow?”

Clicking is going to leave you in the same spot without opportunities. Clicking is all about getting lucky. 300 bots are applying even though they’re not real people. It doesn’t work. Leon hopes you get lucky, but you need to HitList if you really want to succeed

Even doing both is not a good use of your time. Leon has data on thousands of people. Handful get lucky clicking apply. For the vast majority, it’s a complete waste of time

Chat: 4 months you can have a network looking for jobs for you, or nothing because you’ve been clicking. Great dopamine hit to click apply (40 applications today! WOO!). None of those got read by a human, and you’re not closer to your goal

“When is a good time to ask my contact for a referral if they're a new employee? They've been at this company for almost 3 months”

Just ask. Worst case is they say no, and then you can just move on. Shoot your shot. Can’t lose a job you don’t have. If you don’t ask, you’ll never know.

“How big of a role does job posting's date play? Is it okay to reach out to recruiters if it's been over 2 weeks since it's been posted?”

Our strategy does not require an open posting. Any company that hires within the past 3 months has funding to hire. Only 30% of jobs get posted. Clicking doesn’t work, as we’re only clicking on 30% of jobs

These are the stats

70% of jobs do not get posted, so if you’re not networking your way in, you’re missing out on this 70%

Doesn’t even need to be a role we qualify for. If they’re hiring, they have the budget to hire us, too

“What do you do when you are out in the woods?”

Sometimes he’s just solo camping and reads. Hugs trees. Smells stuff. Look at the sky. Go on hikes. Climb on stuff. Go down deep. Lay in the grass. Likes being there with family when he can, though. Spend time with the things that matter, and then come back refreshed

Chat wants a “Leon in the woods” tutorial 😛

Leon has a StarLink Mini with a battery pack, so he can still stream when he’s out in the woods, too

StarLink’s 5 hour battery has saved him when his cell dies and he has no GPS signal. Puts him back in business

When Leon’s out recharging, though, he stays away from StarLink. He just likes it so he can be mobile when he wants to be

[3:15 PM PT]

“how do you read fast and effectively? Im finding getting through docs is a skill in itself and eventually im sure i will need to write docs for my code. Tips?”

Reading is a skill, and needs to be developed. Leon is not the fastest reader. Likes to take notes and highlight, not for learning, but to explore material

Some things that work when reading to learn:

  1. Read the intro, conclusion, and chapter headings to give your brain a good schematic to fill in

  2. When reading, take questions, and then answer those questions at the end for active recall

  3. Figure out what your focus point is, and once you can no longer easily focus, then take a break, recharge (maybe 5 minutes, maybe 60 minutes). Once you’re having trouble retaining paragraphs or remember what you read, that’s break time. Pomodoro can help with this

Two things that hurt people:

  1. Beeline reader can help people transition from one line to another

  2. One word at a time reader can help people from missing stuff

This can get people to 300 words from 100 words in a given period, as they can really help with comprehension and the transition between words/sentences

Chat says dyslexic-friendly fonts can help, too

[3:18 PM PT]

“Can the chosen one come back on stage? I loved hearing from them and think them doing this series is awesome and inspiring!”

Leon hopes. They’ve been doing great in the 100Devs channel. Landing interviews very well, after dialing a bunch of stuff in. Leon (and Roxy, the current Chosen One) are looking forward to sharing the successes

“How should my first message to a recruiter on my hitlist look like if I'm not asking about the current job posting?”

Can go for the kill with recruiters and pitch them directly and tell them what you want

“What about for mid level and senior jobs? Is hit-listing a better use of your time if you want to get hired quickly?”

Hitlisting is the best. Networking is key. Mid/Senior usually only hire people they know. How else are they going to know you?

Networking gives you the cushion so if you ever get laid off, you have that network that can help you find the next opportunity

The more senior you get, the bigger your network should be and the more you should rely on it

“Posting on github increases my anxiety. To the point ive put it on private and want to build my projects on an IDE in isolation. How much do i need to share?”

No one cares about your projects on GitHub. Anyone complaining about your projects or some typo is probably not someone you have to worry about

Should be no anxiety about pushing to GitHub

Private projects are fine if you want to keep stuff secret

Leon has projects from 10+ years ago on his GitHub that are trash. No worries at all

Isolation piece is a problem. Developers don’t work in isolation. You have to be comfortable working with others, as that’s how most people work

There’s also a social anxiety community on 100Devs that is working together to manage this and get better <3

“I want to use the Bob AI story crafter but I genuinely have thought and have no legitimate job accomplishments, didn’t even graduate high school. What do? 🥲”

Use it! Just try! It costs you nothing! Just try it! (Leon made Michael laugh here. He +really+ wants you to try it.)

Don’t ever make that mistake in life again. ALWAYS TRY! You lose nothing by trying. Always try.

Leon understands where this come from, but remember our new 100Devs Motto: Failure is fuel

No one cares about our mistakes, especially if we’re learning from them

Don’t do the weird things to save ourselves minor pain, because this causes us major pain when we miss the opportunities we’re too scared to pursue

“My question is, are the learning depth explained or is it just to glimpse ahead and see?”

Having trouble with 100Devs

100Devs is about much more than code

Leon is trying to get people to show up and be part of community. This is the only thing that’s going to help you get a job

Get people used to reading, because Developers do a ton of reading. (That’s what Shay Howe is for. Help us develop a strategy to get through intimidating text or documentation)

Lots of 100Devs is a process Leon has honed over the years of teaching people to get people fighting fit to being a SWE

Some people skip lessons that they don’t feel very good about or think are important

This is a mistake. 100Devs was meticulously designed. We should follow the process exactly. If we skip around or jump, we miss a large part of what makes 100Devs alumni successful. Keep the pacing, do all that’s asked of us, and we’ll finish the process as prepared as we can be

There are places in 100Devs where people might realize they don’t really want this. That’s fine, but the tough parts aren’t designed to weed us out, they’re designed to help us build ourselves into capable SWEs

“I have my first ever live coding assessment interview soon. how to not be EXTREMELY nervous?? I am worried they are expecting more than I am able to do.”

Try to get confirmation from them about exactly what you’re running into, so you can be prepared

Do your homework, too. Online research about company. Record yourself, come meet with 100Devers and do interview practice as part of our community

It’s okay to be nervous. You’ll get more comfortable over time. First 20 interviews should just be you getting the comfortable interviewing. Even with this “practice”, you’re still likely to get an offer after 60 interviews

[3:28 PM PT]

Answering live

Miguelisms. CV review

Gave two versions in chat. Leon wanted to know which he thought was best. Went with this one:

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1q1zPzJO16b1omoenfigxykaqFNrMyuonh\_L-ecUOP\_U/edit?usp=sharing

“Version 2”

Looks fine, says Leon. Assuming that he’s switching from another industry. Miguel not mentioning that he’s a video editor

Leon would want an executive summary that explains that transition

Only get six seconds to start, and resume as written seems like something is missing

If a recruiter is unsure, they’re unlikely to move you to the next round

Executive summary helps explain transition. Companies are looking +a lot+ right now for DevRels with video editing experience. This would be a selling point. Don’t miss the opportunity to use it

Working professional experience will help set him apart

Fine to list previous professional experience, as long as you have a good reason for why you’re an engineer now

“10-90%” productivity gains seems too made up. Need to be very specific and be able to explain exactly how you did this

Goal is to create a resume that will cause the recruiter to be +excited+ to push you to the next round

Video Editing history should give the biggest wins Miguel can. Show he has a track history of success

Leon happy to review this again next week

[Huddle ended at 3:33 PM]

Work on Hitlisting over the weekend. Keep up the projects so that we have proof to show people. Have a great weekend <3

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-10
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Tues 2/10/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:32 PM PT]

Huddle announcement, with lots of wins for our community:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/738891289071714388/1470896779557077139

Leon loves to see all the wins in our community

Swinging through the wins

Discord Drama Discord is requiring ID verification for individuals that it doesn’t know if they’re >=18 Not rolling out for another month, but may be rolling out in stages before that Not sure how it’s going to affect 100Devs Discord seems to be getting pushback already. Could prevent people from participating in stages, which is the point of 100Devs’ move to Discord Leon hoping that Discord reverses some of these decisions, or at least makes it less stringent, based on negative feedback #general has some videos on how to get around it, which is probably the way forward for those affected … [Michael had an audio issue, so I missed a little bit] 100Devs may have some options, but we’ll see what happens Leon committed to finding workarounds for those that need them

Back to Announcement wins. Loves to see the community showing up to help folks. Helps underscore that some people have been 2-3 years on the job and are still coming back to support the community Not just a program for beginners MayanWolfe got a new job and a 60% salary increase with no technical interviews 2500% increase for someone who had been formerly incarcerated 63% more than what someone else is currently making Lots got clients, some firsts, some long-terms, and someone got their first client despite only being on class 4! Leon: “Freelance February, Ya’ll” Someone in the chat [2:38 PM PT] said they didn’t have a technical interview at the senior level, either

Answering questions from Slido or live Leon is almost done with his version of Slido, called “NotSlido” for now

“Company wants to reschedule my 4hr onsite, originally Fri, to EARLIER this week. I can’t! How to kindly let them know next week works? nervous to ask 😭” Leon: Depends on if you really can’t. If you really can’t, then you have to get through it and let them know. Big planning issues, especially if you have a job currently This may be a good sign, though. They may be trying to get you the job as quickly as possible Possible it’s negative. Maybe trying to fit in other candidates Tricky balance to play 4 hr on-site gives them all your time and energy, so they should be willing to give you flexibility Give them notice as quickly as possible regardless. “Pumped to participate. Unfortunately, I’m not able to schedule an on-site earlier this week due to prior commitments, but I can definitely do [whatever day works for you]. Can we make that happen?” Always be excited and enthusiastic, and +remember this moment+. Do they get grumpy? Are they not supportive? How they treat you in this moment is how they are likely to treat you on the job. Always lead with enthusiasm and consideration. “Hey, I have a life. Rescheduling an on-site seems reasonable because you’re asking for a lot. Can you please work with me to make this work?” Things do happen. (Leon just noticed Community Streamer Rufio is here. Got excited <3) Leon knows that it feels like every interview is do-or-die. Need to try to get to the point where it doesn’t feel that way, and then the Hunt becomes a lot more fruitful. You’re giving up 4 hours of your time for zero pay, so you should have no qualms about asking for some flexibility Make sure you set yourself up for success and pick the time that works best for you If a company is not going to give you the flexibility in the interview, they will NOT give you similar flexibility on the job, so maybe this isn’t the job for you anyway

[2:43 PM PT] “I’m using the hitlist google sheet. I know we don’t click apply but after messaging people at companies how long do we wait till we click apply for those jobs” It’s okay to click apply. It’s more the ethos of 100Devs about not JUST clicking apply If you just click apply, you’re a clicker If you click apply, but also reach out to three people? You’re no longer a clicker. That’s the point A few years ago, it was better to outreach first Clicking applying and doing outreach works today, and may actually be slightly better. However, you need to outreach Also remember even if there isn’t an opening to apply for, you still need to outreach for those 70% opportunities that don’t get posted, so don’t get lured into thinking you need an Apply button to pursue an opportunity

[2:45 PM PT] Sometimes luck does play a factor. Sometimes clicking is enough, but Leon doesn’t want luck to be your strategy Chat talked about how they saw video on why clicking apply is awful, and that video was gold

“Pt2. For my pitch, the huntober gpt is too long (my work history is vast). Do you have a recommendation on how to succinctly describe being a multihyphenate?” Don’t Pick the best path forward that describes why you’re a phenomenal software engineer. Look at what’s in demand in your area and what people are asking for Ex. Lots of video editors coming from 100Devs on the Hunt Restaurant Server → Video Editor → Software engineering? Just focus on the last two. DevRel is in demand, and so those last two skills will be gold right now When you’re thinking about your narrative, blurb the stuff that’s most in demand Can cherry pick your resume for highlights outside this, too Nurse → Server → Medical Company Tailor to whatever you’re pursuing Medically related? Nursing and Medical Company Restaurant Related? Mostly Server Should always have multiple versions of narrative and resume to meet local market demands, which can be varied

[2:48 PM PT] GPT was posted in the chat. Leon says he’ll try to tweak the GPT to handle this better

“What’s a good answer for why do you want to join this company? Its a startup(not strictly tech) and I would be the first software dev hire” I like food. When I work for places, they give me money. I can take that money, and take it to a place that sells food, and get food That’s the real answer When people ask that question, it’s a question to see how prepared you are, especially at a start-up At a start-up, they’re looking for someone who is willing to sip the Kool-Aid, so to speak Someone that believes in the mission and what you’re building. ESPECIALLY for first hires. They want to make sure you’re in it for the long haul Strong answer is based on reading landing pages, docs, mission statement, social media of CEO Thread in your past history with the goals and aims of the company to answer this question “why do you want to join” Leon wants from chat: What’s a past job? Start-up company sounding name and what they do? [2:51 PM PT] Job: Cat Herder looking at AI music company In my past life I was a cat herder. Lots of individuals each year lose their cats, and it was my job to find, herd, and deliver these cats to their owners. Highly rewarding work, but very long days, often deep in the woods with my thoughts. To get through the days, I would play music to give myself the energy I needed to continue herding. This became very special part of my day, but after hearing the same music over and over again, I needed something fresh. Turned to generating my own music while herding. Helped me ride out the very long demanding days and have music that really spoke to me, and get these cats back to their homes, unlocking some of the greatest memories I’ve ever had. Wanting to keep leaning into AI music generation that helped me through these long days. Honed software engineering skills, and now I’m back to help bring the thing that help me so much to the masses Know what AI music did for me, and I want to bring it to every other person. This seems like the best company that can get that done ** Chat goes wild ** Leon says this is just two random things, and you need to practice this so you can do it just as well Leon just improvised this. If you practice, you can make yours even tighter That’s what we’re looking for. What did you do in the past, how does it relate to what you want to do in the future, and how does it fit with the current company? If you have all three, you’re golden Chat said theater kids likely rule here, and Leon does have a theater background [2:55 PM PT] Chat says, and Leon agrees, if he can do it with nonsense, you can totally use it with real history. Can use 100Devs GPT to give you ideas, and then hone it. Record yourself to practice, and keep experimenting with good answers in interviews and see what works. You’ll get better over time Leon thinks a 100Devs Improv class would help a lot of people No job that you’ve ever worked that doesn’t have transferable skills to SWE (from chat) Leon agrees 1000% [2:56 PM PT] “ Not about the words, about how you made them feel” Chat talks about neurodivergence appealing to AI music Leon doesn’t like signalling any weaknesses. He plays the game hard until he gets the offer, and then once the offer is in hand, he can negotiate accommodations Some people are expecting certain answers (e.g. SMART method) Amazon, for example, has full guides on how to answer these questions, and expect you to have done your research (“Googled the Fuck out of It”), and come prepared May dismiss you if you don’t present how they publicly told people they should be presenting Rare, but it does happening, especially at Big Tech companies

“Company may bypass interview, due to direct hire from Director of recruitment. My connect needs me to apply to the job, and send them a pitch to forward (pt 1)” If you have Dir. of Recruitment that likes you, that’s fantastic This is why we do the networking bit. Want to find the person that has the hiring decision, and then get hired that way Harder to do this at bigger companies, but easier at smaller companies Use the director as a filter “This seems like the best pitch. Do you think this will land well with the reviewer? I would love to make changes” They clearly want to help you, so let them do so [2:59 PM PT] Chat asked about if I can start interviewing mid course? Chat also answered this Leon said you should always finish the course to open as many doors as possible Only halfway through? Fewer doors. Still possible for a job to come through them, but don’t make this your only strategy. Switch gears at the end and go 100% into the hunt Job titles don’t matter. Good to interview as early as possible to demystify the process, get the flywheel going, and get a better sense of how best to prepare for The Hunt

[3:02 PM PT] “What do you recommend doing to extend your lifeline for the hunt? I’m currently doing Uber Eats deliveries for rent. Part/full time work, freelance, etc?” Do what you can to bring stability to your life. That’s a great thing Lots of folks commonly look to use every resource US has things like EBT, Medi-Cal, religious communities can offer support, food banks. Extend your runway using all the resources available. If you’re a taxpayer in the US, you likely helped pay for these programs, so don’t be shy about using them when you need them Part time work, full time work. Do whatever you need Make sure to protect time to study and prepare for SWE Hunt. Make sure you’re hunting Freelancing is phenomenal Rank Order Get all public assistance possible. (Rent abatement, student loan abatement) Freelancing Give it at all for a week Every single RC (Resilient Coder) got a paid client within 6 weeks of learning over the past 10 years. All you need is to go hard for a week If you don’t live in an area with access to people, outside a large metro area, that might be an edge case Anything to stave the bleeding Gig work, part time work, etc. Don’t start things that keep you from pursuing your ultimate goal Easy to get stuck in one of these jobs and keep you at 60-80% less than what you could have made if you went all into 100Devs and SWE for 6 months

[3:05 PM PT] Best 100Devs graduates are Uber Drivers. Know what it takes to hustle, because that’s what it takes Leon has mentioned sales can be good, too, as it makes us stronger against rejection, which is common during The Hunt “I see in job offers efficient use of AI tools as requirement.Should we use Colab or Gemini during interview with them? Is that expected or dont use AI at interw” As always, you ask. This is what’s expected now Before: Can I use what language I want? Can I Google during interview? Now: Can I use AI? If can opportunity says AI is a requirement, you should absolutely ask, and the answer now is often yes. Should never-ever-ever go into an interview if you don’t know what you’re walking into Your job is to know as much as you possibly can about what’s going to happen in the interview, then prep accordingly. Always ask, then do the research on your own if no one tells you what you need

“returning from 2024 still barely starting JS. AI seems to be welcomed and used WAY more now. How does this affect our learning?” Yes, if you let it. Do not incorporate AI into your learning process, as it will rob you of the learning AI does the connection and searching for you, and this is the work that’s important for long-term retention If you can get on 1-on-1 tutoring, that’s fine. Just don’t let AI start giving you answers, because that’s going to ruin you. Leon thinks you shouldn’t incorporate it into your process until your learning process is established Don’t let AI generate code for you Don’t let it answer questions for you Max should be to help explain things for you, especially early in the learning process After completing 100Devs, or a similar program? Leon’s advice fully changes: You should be using AI all the time at that point DO NOT Use AI for 100Hours project Need something in your portfolio you can point to and say you built the entire thing Everyone has AI-built stuff in their portfolio now You need at least one thing to set you apart by saying you built the entire thing with zero AI involvement Lots of companies are using AI checkers to see how much AI was used in a codebase Every AI leaves signatures in our code, so don’t try to sneak by the checkers. “I need to learn X, give me a roadmap, timeline, and resources on how to do that” Good AI prompt for learning [3:11 PM PT] Chat joked that this is a very common Huddle question Leon never gets frustrated with repeated questions in Huddles. That’s how we learn, through repetition You internalize the material when you see it repeatedly The more times you hear the Huddle wisdom, the more easily it’s going to be able to come back to you in a high-stakes setting like an interview This is why coming back every week matters. 3rd round interview and you unlock generational wealth in one more answer? That 100th Huddle Wisdom is going to help you Just two hours a week for the Huddles. Exponential value in a high-stakes setting. Prioritize this time as much as possible “ what would be good portfolio project for full stack app in js and what other stack to use to link that in CV as expeience. Should we deploy it? if yes where pls” Something in past should explain what you’re doing now. Build based on this Built best POS system based on background as a server Something you hate and want to fix Something you need to see in the world These make it easier to motivate you to do 100Hours Railway, Render, all have trade-offs for free accounts, so play with them On the Hunt, should try to get $5 to pay for something so you can avoid cold starts, which can limit your opportunities Leon really likes Railway was a company right now. Good team, company cares about its workers. Leon plans to move a lot of his stuff over there to reward good behavior Leon loves Convex. (Best thing ever if you’re building with AI) Convex.dev Phenomenal MCP that makes it easy to build with. If he’s in Cursor and building with cursor, it’s amazing Best developer experience he’s had in the long time. Wants to make a video about it [3:16 PM PT] “Can you talk about the first 90 days after starting the job specifically the non-technical things we need to know to put our best foot forward?” Great book called “The First 90 Days”, along with “How to Win Friends and Influence People” Best books to read (“Never Eat Alone” is also great) General: Show up early and stay late, at least for the first 90 days. Then can coast on this reputation for the next 2-3 years. When things arise that are out of your control? They know it’s not you, it’s some weird thing that happened to you, because they know you go the extra mile Never eat alone. Spend as much time as possible to build relationships with team, with management, and then with other teams Hard to get laid off or miss promotions when you have really good relationships with people around you Day 1: Can I ask every simple question for the first 2 months, with 2 promises Never ask the same question twice Will document every answer, so people who come after will be more prepared Save you potentially hours on stuff someone else can answer for you in 30 seconds Early in, Late out; Never eat alone; Ask great questions Victory Log Track every good thing you’ve done Share wins publicly in company, with gratitude to others Increases your visibility, and establishes rapport with those around you Can share stuff publicly on LinkedIn, but this can happen later. Within company on company communication (like Slack) works Meet with Manager early, establish expectations for month, 3 months, 6 months, year Confirm in writing via e-mail Track progress, and then you can refer to this, and how you established expectations, when it’s time for performance reviews and promotions Someone in chat said this advice got their friend a promotion Leon says most people aren’t willing to do the small things. If you are, then those small things add up and let big things happen for you Boss told you what mattered? That’s why you had the conversation. If they give you more work? Then you refer to your receipts, and then you can either ask if they’re asking for extra thing if they want to alter the scope of what you’re doing. Publicly sharing and receipts helps keep those who are asking things of you accountable, and make sure that if they ask you do something new, what would they like you to deprioritize instead? Chat says this is all stuff from the books Leon says he reads a lot, so this help. Leon has lots of experiencing helping people. First years of this, it was hard, there was failure, but now he’s 8 years after and he’s very optimized. Tons of shots on goal because he helps people on a daily basis, and has been doing it for so long. Constant feedback from community about what works and doesn’t work This happens in chat during Huddles, too. Huddles matter Leon has an ExecDirector and a Board at Resilient Coders that he’s responsible to He does all the stuff he recommends, too, and it helps him get better results. Do what works, people <3

Huddles have been going on for about three years. There was similar stuff during the cohorts, though. [3:24 PM PT] “Leon there’s so much more to learn after completing 100devs. What do I focus on, react, wordpress(freelancing), figma, ts, python? Overwhelmed! (edited)” Can learn anything you want For a job? Not much more you need. You just need to do the things you need to get it (Hunt Stuff) Check local market for accessory stuff. (PHP one place, React another maybe) Avoid the trap of being the perpetual student Spent 6 months learning? Easier to learn than HitList Will learn a lot by continuing to learn, but you’ll never get a job To get a job, you have to go look for one Learn enough to be dangerous (and 100Devs is enough), then get out there and let the market tell you what you’re missing If you bomb 10 interviews because you don’t know enough Python? Then go learn Python. Before then, keep going for the interviews and jobs. Let the market dictate what you’re weak in, not your insecurities Favorite story: someone bombed a Thursday interview. Got a senior dev offer from a Friday interview. Got to keep Hunting Leon also loves when formerly incarcerated Folx go to SWE-level salary. That’s incredible, and he loves hearing about that

“should we have 1 github project described as “done with usage of Colab to ship faster” if yes how to write it in readme is NOT entirely AI done but using tools” Just be honest. Don’t have to put this in a ReadMe, but if you’re showing employers, always best to be transparent Don’t have to be super transparent, but Leon likes to err on the side of transparency, because honesty is typically very valued

[Answering Live] Miguelisms with a CV review: ​

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1q1zPzJO16b1omoenfigxykaqFNrMyuonh_L-ecUOP_U/edit?usp=sharing

[This is a Google Doc, so it may change from what the notes say if Miguel has changed it since] Leon reviewed the narrative Bangers were after the first line.
