Don’t bother with the first line, and then lead with all the great things you did. This was how it read: “I transitioned from creative production to software engineering after discovering how code could eliminate manual, error-prone workflows and dramatically improve delivery speed. My work has supported content shipped across major platforms, such as Playstation, Xbox, including a project featured during Nintendo Direct 2023. Today, I build production-grade systems that scale under real-world constraints and share my process publicly as a Twitch Affiliate. I specialize in turning complex, manual processes into reliable automation that increases velocity and reduces risk.” Bullet points in the resume don’t really support his narrative bangers. If he doesn’t name a project, it’s probably not real. “Leading the design and development of a full-stack trading platform” ←- That sounds fake Leon wants X,Y,Z format. In the first sentence, it should be so amazing that he can go tell someone how amazing the Resume person is This is like “Baristas make coffee” This line doesn’t seem exciting. Leon thought the narrative was exciting, and wants that excitement to feature all over the resume so the recruiter is excited “Delivered high-visibility game marketing content, as evidenced by features on PlayStation, IGN and Xbox YouTube channels, by creating trailers for titles including Golf With Your Friends DLCs, Blasphemous II and King of the Castle.” Good to name drop, but we really need the X,Y,Z format Freelancing would really make Miguel more impressive Try to separate these out, because the paid clients will be the most impactful Good to separate them client-by-client to show he has experience

[UncleNoob on stage] ​

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1WCsHFczmh5SkWwqCnEy8ZW5u0XcH1yAew-RU_Y377qM/edit?tab=t.0

^ CV Review Older format of the resume Should test if it works well in your area Summary: “Software engineer who builds systems people can rely on, shaped by firsthand experience where broken processes had real-world consequences.” Good theme, but tell me what happened. Don’t just say it. Show me. What broke and had real world consequences? How are you the hero? This quote is a good setup, but that’s the content? Be careful with Baristas make coffee here. Make yourself the hero “Improved day-to-day task execution by designing and shipping a structured workload system, resulting in 30% faster daily planning for users.” Leon doesn’t know what this is, and that means a recruiter will not. If we don’t know what it is, we can’t get excited about it. Would you really tell someone about this? No. People would just be confused. Needs to make sense for even non-technical people Need very Solid X, Y, Z format for everything Try to keep it to one page Noob doing screenwriting as a hobby, so he’s trying to incorporate that in

[3:37 PM PT] Noob notes the time, so he would like to come back next Huddle, and Leon is definitely up for this and helping community members continue to improve

author: Anny Levine
title: 100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT
html:
=== I used AI (Google Gemini) to try to improve the formatting below. The Raw version, with AI assistance, is below it. You should be able to search the document for “[Here’s the original document:]” to find it. =====

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities)

(Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord)

[Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Notepad while listening to the Huddle, multitasking, and using an ancient laptop. Sorry! I tried to get something, though <3

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

Huddle

[2:32 PM Started]

Topics:

ADHD and Engineering

Nothing inherent about SWE that prevents us from succeeding with personal limitations

If you have a limitation, it would likely impact you in any career. Use your resources to address those limitations, and then make sure to crush 100Devs and your amazing SWE career after

Lots of ADHD among the 100Devs community. Every person is different, though, so you need a plan that is tailored to you, and professionals can help with that

Don’t underestimate the power of lifestyle changes first, however. Medication does not need to be the first step, or even a necessary step.

==== Break, as Michael was multitasking too hard ====

I think what was covered here was “Can we be a digital nomad in the current SWE market”?

Leon said yes. He thinks remote work is here to stay

Hardest part about remote work is that we often have to be in the correct time zone

(From Michael): However, some companies probably want coverage at odd hours (like graveyard or overnight shifts). Thus, if you’re in a distant time zone, these might be normal daytime working hours for you. This could be extra great since, in many places, graveyard/overnight hours are +not+ popular, and sometimes they’re paid more highly as a result. (This is usually the case in healthcare, in my experience, for example.)

However, Leon has seen some companies (like Railway) go fully remote, global, and fully asynchronous

Employees just commit to working at a specific time period, for a standard full-time commitment, and it doesn’t matter when they’re working. They just need to show the company and their team that they’re dependable

Leon has also found StarLink to be very helpful to allow him to be more remote with his current work. He has done some 100Devs Huddles outdoors using StarLink to get connected, and he doesn’t think we have noticed.

===

Topic: AI is increasing the pace of development, but most of the work of an SWE is not coding

Leon does not see the human in the SWE loop going anywhere anytime soon

Need to know the fundamentals to make sure you know that the AI is doing the right stuff, and helping you build the product that your stakeholders need

Danny’s Masterclass on this is really good, though. Helps with AI prompting

====

How much notice do you need to give part-time jobs?

Rarely need to give notice at all, but part-time jobs especially. They’ll fire you without a thought (or just stop giving you hours)

Don’t hesitate to pursue better opportunities, but you can always feel free to give the current position an opportunity to keep you there

Two weeks is industry-standard for courtesy

Leon only has loyalty to people, like those on his team who he wants to keep in his network as good contacts. He has no loyalty to the company itself

===

[2:46 PM]

Back to what happens if AI makes perfect code?

Lots of opportunities for SWE, especially once everyone realizes that SWEs are necessary, since coding is not all that’s necessary

Leon is not worried at all. If companies can move faster, then they need more people

IBM just says they plan to increase new hires in 2026 by six times what they were doing before

AI can get new people productive day one, so now they need as many bodies as possible to take advantage of that. No need to onboard or onramp, or at least much less of a need

Lots of companies also don’t want to give up secrets to AI

===

2:48 PM PT

Remote in IT but want to be an SWE

Boss took a chance on me. Should I stay?

Leon thinks this is unlikely

If certs at job lead to raises? Then go ahead. “Taking a chance on me” isnt meaningful. If they want to pay you, great. If not, go somewhere that will give you better pay and career stability, and help you pursue what you really want to do (in this case, being an SWE)

====

2:49 PM PT

Topics I missed:

Apprehensive about being an SWE. Do you not want to do it? That’s fine. Are you just scared? Go fix that.

Remote work is here to stay (Michael did some post-session additions to this topic above. So, we covered that in this document, too) – It’s under “Can we be a digital nomad in the current SWE market””

====

[2:58 PM Pt]

Leon keeping up to date with software

Need fundamentals and theory. This helps you know what the AI is working off of, and what it’s doing

Be up to date

Can’t be a principal engineer on theory stuff. They’ve just been doing it too long

Need enough theory to not be behind, but need to be up to date (HackerNews, Twitter)

Leon can speak about any model or any trend in AI. His edge is that he can backfill the theory stuff, but he’s on the forefront of trying the new stuff

Understanding the skills, MCPs, and buzzwords. No principal engineer will ever consume more content than Leon

Backfilling theory, getting his edge by being current, and then building repeatable systems that have high-output when he’s using AI

More knowledge than Principal about how to build when AI is in the loop.

This is how Leon keeps his edge

Also refuses to let his skills atrophy (Last bastion)

People get too comfortable with AI, and forget how to write functions

Leon built a new Anki tool, and he uses this to review

Thinks the algorithm is better and the experience is more fun

Using it for a month now and likes it

Leon has no problem being confident, because he works hard to justify it

====

Maintaining attention span vs. looking for quick fixes?

Leon’s ADHD is his superpower. 10 projects at once

Learning at an accelerated speed when he’s taking good care of himself. Very good at context switching

===

[3:03 PM PT]

Don’t worry about what employer will decide

Just do the best you can, and then stay at your best so if you do get laid off, you’re great

3-6 month contract? No problem. Once it finishes, if they don’t hire you, you now have more experience to help you get the next job

===

[3:04 PM PT]

Best to follow-up early in the week, as stuff sent over the weekend or just before is less likely to be interacted with, as it just piles up with a bunch of other stuff

===

[3:05 PM PT]

Should only seriously start job-searching once you finish a program

Should be networking the entire time. At the end of the program, you should have at least dozens of people who can help you connect with an opportunity. That’s what 100Devs does

“Already had my heart broken once this weekend, so I would love to hear back from you.” (Since it’s Valentine’s Day)

This came from the chat, and Leon loves it so he’s going to use it when he tries to get people to connect with him

===

[3:06 PM PT]

New Dev Routine?

Same thing as always: Don’t make each day an accident

Every day: Learning, Review, Building, and Networking

Learning: 100Devs, Books, Videos, etc.

Review: Anki, something that helps actively recall

Building: Use the things you just learned

Networking:

If you keep doing these things consistently, you get an opportunity. That’s how it works. Focus on what works

===

[3:07 PM PT]

Leon does not price with freelancing

Finds out what client’s budget is, and then he finds out what he can do for that price point

Pricing in advance will cause a client to ask for everything but expect the price you set

Price point just makes customers unhappy. Does not help you at all

Understand customer first, then determine if their budget will allow you to help them based on how much it will cost to make it make sense to you

Leah apparently said in chat she’s having trouble with too much work using this method. (Leon says it’s not a problem for him either)

Using this strategy works for both of them

====

[3:09 PM PT]

Work does not come from your job, unless you’re +VERY+ lucky (Laravel seems awesome)

Growth therefore has to come from outside of work

Too tired at the end of day? Have to get up early and study before work.

Never eat lunch alone

Keep stability of the current role until you get a new one

This is how you transition to SWE and succeed at doing so

Leon’s normal schedule:

Teaching a little later (was 7AM before, now it’s 8AM)

9AM-11AM is coding for Leon (7-8 PM is also coding, if he can)

Before 9 AM is family time

Working remotely? How to eat lunch alone?

Virtual lunch works. Do a Zoom eating lunch together. Book that time

===

[3:11 PM PT]

How to find a job doing open source?

Very rare. You do open source because it’s fun to do and it helps you provide proof

Does not help you connect with a job. You have to go out there and Hunt

Some open source projects do hire, but it’s very rare and unlikely to be as successful as Hunting according to the 100Devs advice

===

[3:12 PM PT]

Scrummaster Cert?

If a company wants to pay for it? Great.

Leon has never seen a Scrummaster cert move the needle meaningfully for anyone

Many people get it because jobs often pay for it

Need more than certs to set yourself apart

===

[3:13 PM PT]

Being senior was never about coding proficiency or the ability to bang it out

“Senior” is all made up anyway

Leon is tempted to do a podcast on this.

Possible you may have equal code output to a senior with AI

Does not teach you teamwork, understanding stakeholder needs, etc.

Much more to being a senior than just code output

===

[3:14 PM PT]

System design course or tutorial?

“System Design Primer” on Github

Exponents YouTube channel (System Design interview walkthroughs)

Leon agreed with user “Stair-in” in the chat. That’s how it sounded anyway

===

Only front-end dev on backend team. What to do?

Growth does not come from job. Advice is to find the growth, mentorship, and help in other places so you feel like you’re growing

Also check with the team and management. “I love the work I do, I love my team. I don’t feel like I’m growing.”

Maybe they’ll pay for memberships and conferences

Any leader worth their salt wants their people to feel like their growing

This is a great conversation to have. Leon loves if people who report to him ask him for help to be happier, healthier, or feel more like they’re growing. As their manager, this is Leon’s job

Could be snitching on yourself if you don’t frame it right. “I love everything about what I’m doing, but I feel like I am missing opportunities to grow. (Leadership growth, etc.)”

Probably not a good conversation if you don’t have a good relationship with management, but you need to work on these relationships, too

Don’t want to let people think you’re looking to quit. Need to emphasize how happy you are otherwise

===

Leon’s Theory Reading

“Philosophy of Software Design”

Like “Clean Code” but all the crap is taken out, and this is all good stuff

“Deep Learning for Coders with FastAI and PyTorch”

Practical Deep Learning for Developers from FastAI is a great intro to AI for developers course

This book is the companion to it

“Designing Data Intensive Applications”

Leon really likes this book, but a new version is coming in March so he’s going to wait for that

“SCIP”

Full version in JavaScript now [this is the “big book” that everyone references. It’s like 4 words, everyone references it. DM Michael if you want him to find the full name]

[3:20 PM PT]

Leon loves that there’s a JavaScript version of SCIP now

“Eloquent Javascript”

Leon loves this book, especially if you’re very new to the field

Great book to help you start thinking bigger (Leon says this is a controversial take, though) [Michael: It’s SICP -> The Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs. Here’s the JS version, in an interactive format: ​

https://sourceacademy.org/sicpjs/index

]

===

“Brain feels flat after getting a job. Feels stuck in the middle and not progressing”

Growth doesn’t come from the job!

Progress, and shaping the system, comes from getting buy-in from people around you and building influence on the team

This is highly important as it helps you grow your career

“How to Win Friends and Influence People” helps you understand the game

Need to establish rapport first

“Never Eat Alone” is good, too

Once you get those relationships, you’ll likely feel better about shaping the system

“We need a course on what to do after we get a job” - From chat

Leon really likes this idea

[3:22 PM PT]

====

Leon put an open text form in the Slido

Would love to get content ideas from the community, as he has time to work on this at the moment

This feedback would be very helpful. If the Huddle regulars gave him ideas, it would help him provide more value at the Huddles, because he really appreciates us making the time

[3:24 PM PT]

===

Last Round of Interview. They loved me. Now they’re saying there’s a delay. Do I wait?

This happens. This is why you need to have multiple opportunities lined up

They are likely lying to you. Telling you and ten other candidates that they want to move on with you

May end up hiring someone internally, and the eleven of you are out of luck

This is why you have to always keep looking for opportunities. If you slow down whenever stuff starts to feel good, you’re now in big trouble with your lack of momentum if those pan out. (They often DON’T pan out.)

Don’t ride the rollercoaster, just keep getting those interviews lined up through HitListing, Banki, Leetcode, etc.

===

[3:26 PM PT]

If growth isn’t at job, why are years of experience important?

Years of experience has nothing to do with working

Years of doing engineering. That may be on the job or outside of the job

It’s also +ALL MADE UP+

No way to easily quantify how good of an engineer you are. “10 years of experience” makes this easy

Kids coming out of Waterloo can run circles around “senior” engineer in Midwest

Proxy that filters out most people, but it’s not anything worth paying attention to

See tips above to make sure you’re always growing

Front-End developer at day job? You’re atrophying elsewhere if you’re not continuing to work on them, especially outside of the job

Most people aren’t passionate about this stuff, so you have to actively work at it, otherwise you’re going to end up in a place career-wise that you don’t want to be

[3:28 PM PT]

Commission-based Software Engineering role at a Startup? Pay is based on profits?

This is awful. Do not go through with it.

This is a scam.

Whenever someone asks this question, someone in chat usually says something

90% of companies fail. How many meaningfully exit is way smaller

“Hey Leon. I got into Y Combinator. Getting guaranteed C funding. Can I give you equity beforehand? I can show you proof of all of this”

This works. But mostly, it’s just people with an idea trying to get developers to work for free. Don’t fall for it

===

[3:30 PM PT]

Leon really wants feedback from people so that he knows his content is helpful. That’s EXTREMELY rewarding for him

===

Bullets for 100Devs?

X,Y,Z format for stuff you’ve done

Client, 100Hours project, get real users and real feedback

Not here to make up things that are impressive. We do impressive things, and then we help you sell them effectively

No shortcut. Have to do the work

===

Coding is not the whole piece of being an SWE

Lots of other stuff. Working together, how to do testing, how to do review, how to deploy

Layers of learning

Once you’ve committed to learning, you now have a system you can use to apply to these other systems

Companies you work for likely have guides to follow that you can get good at

[3:32 PM PT]

Strain posted bullet point example in chat

Need users? Have a great reputation at 100Devs, and many people here will likely be there to help you, including by using your thing or helping you find opportunities

There’s a self-promo channel where you can share what you’re working on and ask for people to help you (as users, and to provide feedback)

[3:34 PM PT]

From Chat: Tonight, 7 PM PT, there’s something going on that Leon thought looked interesting (hopefully, it will be recorded later :p)

“The Social Network”

Not “The Facebook”. Sam’s calling his thing “The Daily Challenge”. Leon says, just call it The Challenge.

===

Do not use AI to do your 100Hours project

100Devs GPT is just helping you with ideas for good projects based on your background

===

“Don’t attribute to malice what may just be a simple error”

Always try to assume the best in people. this will cause them to be inclined to do the same with you in return

===

Content creation?

Don’t spend any money. Just start where you are and see if you like it.

===

“Any resources to learn how to do test-driven development”

No courses. Just try it. Use Jest and Playwrite for end-to-end. Read the docs and add it to your code base

Play with it for a while, and then figure out the best resources.

Get used to end-to-end testing and optimize later

Some things, Leon doesn’t like courses. Thinks we should play with stuff first, then seek out a course

TTD on Codewars all the time (says chat)

[3:38 PM PT]

===

How to use AI in the 100Devs program?

Don’t use AI, especially in the beginning

Part that is important is the resource gathering and making the connections

AI does all this work for you, and then you don’t build the connections

90% of people don’t use AI in a way that helps them. It’s just too tempting to use AI to do the hard work

Also very bad for active recall

AI seems to short-circuit the learning process right now, which is really harmful

“Effort is the algorithm” with learning [3:39 PM PT]

===

If you’re showing up to this community, Leon loves you very much, and knows the rest of the 100Devs community loves you, too

Happy Valentine’s Day and a great holiday weekend

Leon went over time and didn’t even notice. He was too focused on getting through the Slido

=====

[Here’s the original document:] ==== RAW Document Below. I had Google Gemini assist with formatting the above, but I don’t entirely trust it :p =====

===

- Every day: Learning, Review, Building, and Networking

-- Learning: 100Devs, Books, Videos, etc.

-- Review: Anki, something that helps actively recall

-- Building: Use the things you just learned

-- Networking:

- If you keep doing these things consistently, you get an opportunity. That's how it works. Focus on what works

===

[3:07 PM PT]

- Leon does not price with freelancing

-- Finds out what client's budget is, and then he finds out what he can do for that price point

- Pricing in advance will cause a client to ask for everything but expect the price you set

-- Price point just makes customer unhappy. Does not help you at all

- Understand customer first, then determine if their budget will allow you to help them based on how much it will cost to make it make sense to you

- Leah apparently said it chat she's having trouble with too much work using this method. (Leon says it's not a problem for him either)

-- Using this strategy works for both of them

====

[3:09 PM PT]

- Work does not come from your job, unless you're +VERY+ lucky (Laravel seems awesome)

- Growth therefore has to come from outside of work

-- Too tired at the end of day? Have to get up early and study before work.

- Never eat lunch alone

- Keep stability of the current role until you get a new one

- This is how you transition to SWE and succeed at doing so

- Leon's normal schedule:

-- Teaching a little later (was 7AM before, now it's 8AM)

-- 9AM-11AM is coding for Leon (7-8 PM is also coding, if he can)

- Before 9 AM is family time

- Working remotely? How to eat lunch alone?

- Virtual lunch works. Do a Zoom eating lunch together. Book that time

===

[3:11 PM PT]

How to find a job doing open source?

- Very rare. You do open source because it's fun to do and it helps you provide proof

- Does not help you connect with a job. You have to go out there and Hunt

- Some open source projects do hire, but it's very rare and unlikely to be as successful as Hunting according to the 100Devs advice

===

[3:12 PM PT]

Scrummaster Cert?

- If a company wants to pay for it? Great.

- Leon has never seen a Scrummaster cert move the needle meaningfully for anyone

-- Many people get it because jobs often pay for it

- Need more than certs to set yourself apart

===

[3:13 PM PT]

- Being senior was never about coding proficiency or the ability to bang it out

- "Senior" is all made up anyway

- Leon tempted to do a podcast on this.

- Possible you may have equal code output to a senior with AI

-- Does not teach you teamwork, understanding stakeholder needs, etc.

- Much more to being a senior than just code output

===

[3:14 PM PT]

- System design course or tutorial?

- "System Design Primer" on Github

- Expontents YouTube channel (System Design interview walkthroughs)

- Leon agreed with "Stair-in" in the chat. That's how it sounded anyway

===

Only front-end dev on backend team. What to do?

- Growth does not come from job. Advice is to find the growth, mentorship, and help in other places so you feel like you're growing

- Also check with team and management. "I love the work I do, I love my team. I don't feel like I'm growing."

-- Maybe they'll pay for memberships and conferences

-- Any leader worth their salt wants their people to feel like their growing

- This is a great conversation to have. Leon loves if people who report to him ask him for help to be happier, healthier, or feel more like they're growing. As their manager, this is Leon's job

-- Could be snitching on yourself if you don't frame it right. "I love everything about what I'm doing, but I feel like I am missing opportunities to grow. (Leadership growth, etc.)"

-- Probably not a good conversation if you don't have a good relationship with management, but you need to work on these relationships, too

- Don't want to let people think you're looking to quit. Need to emphasize how happy you are otherwise

===

Leon's Theory Reading

"Philosophy of Software Design"

- Like "Clean Code" but all the crap is taken out, and this is all good stuff

"Deep Learning for Coders with FastAI and PyTorch"

- Practical Deep Learning for Developers from FastAI is a great intro to AI for developers course

-- This book is the companion to it

"Designing Data Intensive Applications"

- Leon really likes this book, but a new version is coming in March so he's going to wait for that

"SCIP"

- Full version in JavaScript now [this is the "big book" that everyone references. It's like 4 words, everyone references it. DM Michael if you want him to find the full name]

[3:20 PM PT]

- Leon loves that there's a JavaScript version of SCIP now

"Eloquent Javascript"

- Leon loves this book, especially if you're very new to the field

- Great book to help you start thinking bigger (Leon says this is a controversial take, though)

===

"Brain feels flat after getting a job. Feels stuck in the middle and not progresssing"

- growth doesn't come from job!

- Progress, and shaping the system, comes from getting buy-in from people around you and building influence on the team

-- This is highly important as it helps you grow your career

- "How to Win Friends and Influence People" helps you undersatnd the game

- Need to establish rapport first

- "Never Eat Alone" is good, too

- Once you get those relationships, you'll likely feel better about shaping the system

"We need a course on what to do after we get a job" - From chat

- Leon really likes this idea

[3:22 PM PT]

====

Leon put an open text form in the Slido

- Would love to get content ideas from the community, as he has time to work on this at the moment

- This feedback would be very helpful. If the Huddle regulars gave him ideas, it would help him provide more value at the Huddles, because he really appreciates us making the time

[3:24 PM PT]

===

Last Round of Interview. They loved Me. Now they're saying there's a delay

- This happens. This is why you need to have multiple opportunities lined up

- They are likely lying to you. Telling you and ten other candidates that they want to move on with you

-- May end up hiring someone internally

- This is why you have to always keep looking for opportunities. If you slow down whenever stuff starts to feel good, you're now in big trouble with your lack of momentum if those pan out. (They often DON'T pan out.)

-- Don't ride the rollercoaster, just keep getting those interviews lined up through HitListing, Banki, Leetcode, etc.

===

[3:26 PM PT]

If growth isn't at job, why are years of experience important?

- Years of experience has nothng to do with working

-- Years of doing engineering

- It's also +ALL MADE UP+

-- No way to easily quantify how good of an engineer you are. "10 years of experience" makes this easy

- Kids coming out of Waterloo can run circles around "senior" engineer in Midwest

-- Proxy that filters out most people, but it's not anything worth paying attention to

- See tips above to make sure you're always growing

- Front-End developer at day job? You're atrophying elsewhere if you're not continuing to work on them, especially outside of the job

-- Most people aren't passionate about this stuff, so you have to actively work at it, otherwise you're going to end up in a place career-wise that you don't want to be

[3:28 PM PT]

Commission-based Software Engineering role at a Startup? Pay is based on profits?

- This is awful. Do not go through with it.

- This is a scam.

- Whenever someone asks this question, someone in chat usually says something

-- 90% of companies fail. How many meaningfully exit is way smaller

- "Hey Leon. I got into Y Combinator. Getting guaranteed C funding. Can I give you equity beforehand? I can show you proof of all of this"

-- This works. But mostly, it's just people with an idea trying to get developers to work for free. Don't fall for it

===

[3:30 PM PT]

- Leon really wants feedback from people so that he knows his content is helpful. That's EXTREMELY rewarding for him

===

Bullets for 100Devs?

- X,Y,Z format for stuff you've done

- Client, 100Hours project, get real users and real feedback

-- Not here to make up things that are impressive. We do impressive things, and then we help you sell them effectively

- No shortcut. Have to do the work

===

Coding is not the whole piece of being an SWE

- Lots of other stuff. Working together, how to do testing, how to do review, how to deploy

-- Layers of learning

- Once you've committed to learning, you now have a system you can use to apply to these other systems

- Companies you work for likely have guides to follow that you can get good at

[3:32 PM PT]

- Strain posted bullet point example in chat

- Need users? Have a great reputation at 100Devs, and many people here will likely be there to help you, including by using your thing or helping you find opportunities

-- There's a self-promo channel where you can share what you're working on and ask for people to help you (as users, and to provide feedback)

[3:34 PM PT]

- From Chat: Tonight, 7 PM PT, there's something going on that Leon thought looked interesting (hopefully, it will be recorded later :p)

- "The Social Network"

-- Not "The Facebook". Sam's calling his thing "The Daily Challenge". Leon says, just call it The Challenge.

===

Do not use AI to do your 100Hours project

- 100Devs GPT is just helping you with ideas for good projects based on your background

===

"Don't attribute to malice what may just be a simple error"

- Always try to assume the best in people. this will cause them to be inclined to do the same with you in return

===

Content creation?

- Don't spend any money. Just start where you are and see if you like it.

===

"Any resources to learn how to do test-driven development"

- No courses. Just try it. Use Jest and Playwrite for end-to-end. Read the docs and add it to your code base

- Play with it for a while, and then figure out the best resources.

- Get used to end-to-end testing and optimize later

- Some things, Leon doesn't like courses. Thinks we should play with stuff first, then seek out a course

- TTD on Codewars all the time (says chat)

[3:38 PM PT]

===

How to use AI in the 100Devs program?

- Don't use AI, especially in the beginning

-- Part that is important is the resource gathering and making the connections

--- AI does all this work for you, and then you don't build the connections

- 90% of people don't use AI in a way that helps them. It's just too tempting to use AI to do the hard work

-- Also very bad for active recall

- AI seems to short-circuit the learning process right now, which is really harmful

- "Effort is the algorithm" with learning [3:39 PM PT]

===

If you're showing up to this community, Leon loves you very much, and knows the rest of the 100Devs community loves you, too

- Happy Valentine's Day and a great holiday weekend

- Leon went overtime and didn't even notice. He was too focused on getting through the Slido

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-17
html:
by Anny Levine (

https://www.linkedin.com/in/annylevine

)

​

Happy birthday, Matthias!

​

Is it normal for 100Hours Project to take MORE than 100hours?

Yes, ABSOLUTELY. 100 Hours is the MINIMUM. Some people never stop working on theirs. Some people turn it into their business, or ... It becomes a lifelong thing.

It's just one component of what's going on to get a job.

Don't feel bad spending an hour on something small.

The point is that you're going to struggle SO MUCH that will help you LEARN.

Embrace the SUCK. Embrace the STRUGGLE. That's the POINT. You'll do fine.

​

Is it realistic to be a freelancer AND working a fulltime job?

Wait until you have at least 2 evergreen clients, and at least 3-6 months warchest, more if you have family/kids.

SO you have what you need to get through the stress.

Otherwise, going fulltime freelance is more stressful than it needs to be.

​

What to do when I get stuck on a task/project that I don't understand?

Make it fun to not understand something. It's a mindset thing. Some people are built different. If failure can be your fuel... If getting stuck on a coding problem doesn't mean danger to me, my loved ones, etc... everything else becomes a lot easier. Then you're doing puzzles or LEGO bricks. I have a deep-deated belief that given enough time and access to the internet, I can figure it out! Be bored. Once you make the binary decision, and let yourself be bored.. Eventually you can get your brain to view doing the thing you don't want to do as an actual reward.

​

Were you good at math in HS?

Yes, technically. But I got a B+ in class, and I freaked. I have a very good memory. I memorized, regurgitated, and then forgot. I don't feel like I was good at maths, but my academic record disagrees. I've taught myself maths.

​

jasminepvo (100devs alumna): To build that belief in yourself - build a proof list - i'm sure there are sooooo many things you've overcome in your life that have been wayyyy harder. Keep a proof list to look back at when you feel this way. That's what helped me believe I could be a software engineer is looking back my dev log all the time of everything I've learned and built

​

suicyco (Micah)

: unless you are doing math based code, like engineering or scientific computing, you really dont need to know advanced math to write code, they are unrelated except at the most basic logic level

​

vish213

:

Bob Coder 2-column: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Vuo7MQ5WKTMey-JLv8eZ17Zmo-l9or4WQVs3w1\_N2J0/edit?tab=t.0

Bob Coder 1-column: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1agRwOAW\_7fWdsLeUMyogCs0Goo7yHVdu7jzRxVVmB5U/edit?tab=t.0

​

Best way to present 100Devs experience. It's a full-service agency that offers a training program. It was unpaid but I did get clients. X, Y, and Z.

​

Angel_theDev

NVIM

: For folks at the tail end of the cohorts I was shown this website recently that would have paired well with the react docs : https://react.gg/visualized

​

Fighting the stuck: Keep a really good log of the things you HAVE figured out. A list of all your wins.

Also, adopt an identity: I am adopting an identity around not procrastinating. Not getting stuck.

​

I need a 100 Hours Roadmap. I can't finish something I can't define.

WHAT DO YOU WANT TO BUILD? WHAT DO YOU WANT TO SEE IN THIS WORLD?

It's done when it looks good. Doesn't look odd or like a scam. Looks like a real site people would actively start using once you finish it. Someone is willing to put their cc info into the website.

​

dvkr: We need a gippity that coaches someone through an idea

​

Angel_theDev

: There is also no "finished" its like art. You decide when to stop, or when its good enough. You can ALWAYS go deeper and make it better, then make that version better, then make that version better etc.. diminishing returns

​

Art is never finished, only abandoned. - Leonardo DaVinci

​

Email, LinkedIn, and Twitter are the go-tos for connection requests.

Clear them out and redo them if it's been a month or more.

​

JB

: I'm reminded of Aesop's Fable 'The Gnat and the Bull'. It's basically 'you're not as important to others as you think you are'

​

jasminepvo (100devs alumna): ITS THE FIRE HORSE YEAR - SHOOT YOUR SHOT

​

Relocation? A year isn't doable? Negotiate from the best-case scenario to the worst-case scenario, keep them believing you're the best person for the job. "Please help me make this work!"

​

Discord and IRC help Leon get unstuck. Tinkering. Alt accounts. Etc. I consume a lot of content. Podcasts, YouTube, blogs, academic papers.

​

Take the interview, or ask them to put it off 2-3 months. It depends on the company.

​

Cold messaging is fine with public information.

​

suicyco (Micah)

: I use weechat/hexchat for clients, and libera.chat is a great server for programming stuff, all the big open source projects host a channel there, plus languages etc... the top most experts are right there (but dont bug em directly lol)

​

Happy Lunar New Year! The year of the Fire Horse

​

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-20
html:
Some topics that I found helpful: I asked Leon what he thought about freelancing opportunities in DataEngineering, as they don't seem to be as plentiful as FS WebDev, (Code the Dream is in the process of opening a Data Engineering Pathway alongside their FS WebDev track, and I really like data-driven decision-making and its utility in helping scientific research, so I'm tempted :p) -- Leon agreed. More clients/companies seem to need websites and apps that help make their professional lives easier than those who need more specialized DataEng work. However, he's not sure if there's any clear data on this, and he thinks the opportunities in the high-end may favor DataEng instead of WebDev, so that's something to consider Leon also brought up why he DOES NOT share a syllabus for 100Devs, -- Too many enterprising people go, "Oh. The course is 30 weeks? I can do that in three," and then sabotage their learning in the process, either by not giving themselves enough time to really sit with the material by learning it at a slower pace, or by getting overwhelmed and just giving up. --- All the classes are available on CommunityTaught, so this doesn't seem to be helping people avoid this pitfall -- Another pitfall is that if people see all 60+ classes stacked together, it may seem insurmountable, and they won't even make the attempt. Leon prefers to have people stay consistent by paying attention to each step at a time. Then, once they're on top of the mountain, they can then look back and see how far those steps have taken them. (edited)Saturday, February 21, 2026 at 01:44

[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/20/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:32 PM PT]

“hi leon! i had an awkward debrief/semi-negotiation call with a recruiter yesterday; could we possibly talk it through on stage please?”

Yes! Raise your hand, let’s pull you up.

StevenMosesCodes

How do you handle negotiation? How should I be reading into this interaction?

Onsite on Tuesday. Grueling, but got through it. E-mail from recruiter for phone chat less than 48 hrs after ended

Phone: “Hey!” Not an offer yet. Asked how the on-site was. “Great experienced, learned a lot about product, super psyched”

“Feedback looks positive” Steven thinks he was vibing with everyone even if it was tough

Recruiter has some questions

Confirmed title: “Full Stack Engineer”

Path to senior when joining team

Confirmed desired pay range, which was higher than first conversation with Recruiter

Confirmed years of experience. 4, based on freelancing

Recruiter confirmed this was not counting internships (Steven has never had an internship)

Steven said he can confirm start date 3-4 after signing. Recruiter countered 2-3 weeks after signing

Team just needs to debrief and that she’ll get back to him next week

Steven very bad at playing it cool. So just checking with Leon

Leon: Seems like interview went well, and recruiter is trying to advance Steven to offer

At that stage, they have to weigh a bunch of things. Team needs someone soon and at current price point

Very good sign. Would have probably rejected him already if not

May be looking at multiple candidates

Leon likes not going up and down on rollercoaster. Steven played cards very well so far. Now just wait for offer, and keep going on the hunt

Navigated lots of big traps: Title, Years of Experience

Timing seems important to them, so Leon would have said something like “flexible on start date” to be vague so as not to create friction for them

Once we have formal offer, we can negotiate harder

At this point, probably not worth Steven reaching back out to the recruiter and say, “I can start whenever you want!”

(Especially for enough money)

Leon says don’t worry about this. Just wanted to bring it up for the Huddle

Recommends reaching back out to them if they don’t get back to him by Monday end of day. Ask if they need anything else on his end. (Shows continued interest)

Thinks Steven did very well

Looks forward to having Steven come back up with an offer so we can help him get more money

Only way to learn this stuff is by going through the process yourself and failing a bunch of time, or learning from others

Steven: (in the chat)

Recruiter: “Salary Expectation?”

S: “What’s the range?”

R: “We have a range… What’s your expectation?”

S: “Depends on the range.”

Leon says this is how we do it.

[2:41 PM PT]

100Devs version of Slido will not have character limit

“Have a written offer I accepted. Got another verbal offer somewhere else. Want to rescind original written but haven’t got new offer signed. How long do I wait”

Until the verbal becomes a written offer. Make sure new offer is what you want to pursue

Hopefully, you’re not in a country with slavery

Whoever wants to give you more money for labor gets your labor

Company will lay you off without a single thought

Don’t worry about going for more money/opportunity

[2:44 PM PT]

Be kind, respectful, and appreciative of all the people involved, but you have to pick the better offer

The company will always go with their better offer, too. It’s just business. Don’t take it personally, or be personally unkind when you make the same decision

MayanWolfe in the chat said someone at new job got laid off after 20 years without warning. “Go for the money. That’s what the company does”

Just be very kind and open about why you’re going with one offer over another, and then keep moving

“This other offer is just going to make more sense for my family, and I have to take it. Thank you so much for your time.”

No apology necessary

Good line with range and expectation overlap

“Are there fewer freelance opportunities in Data Engineering (Python, AI/Cloud) vs. FS WebDev? Any tips, Leon+Chat? Just a much less flexible career path? Thx!<3”

FS Web Dev has more freelance. Not sure any data supports this, just Leon’s take

Just easier. Everyone needs a website. Maybe everyone doesn’t need DataEng services

Every store/company needs a web presence. Most are building applications

More clients in this area

High end may actually be in DataEng. FS Web dev just seems easier to break into

Lots of opportunities in the field. Freelance would likely be harder. Taking on more serious projects, which probably needs more client service or extreme expertise

[2:47 PM PT]

Leon tries not to apologize as much. Would rather not do so unnecessarily

Argument: “We don’t need a website, we use Facebook”

Has it helped you get consistent leads?

“Are you at capacity? You wouldn’t want more clients?”

If you’re able to get consistent leads, you may be priced ineffectively. I helped client with better website, and they were able to get clients at higher price point. Now they can make more money by doing less work. Is this something you would be interested in?

Always an angle

[2:50 PM PT]

“Can cohort 3 hit advanced topics? 1-2 cover the fundamentals and I’d like to see how you unit test, load balance, back of envelope calculations and etc.”

Lot of people who are coming back in #celebrations at Year 2 or 3 of job

Not a lot of content for these people. Programming Podcast can help

Leon would love to have more formalized training. Thinks this is something that needs to happen in 100Devs, and hopes he can rely on community to help surface some of this stuff

[2:52 PM PT]

“How would you network your way in for a position posted on linkedin by an engineering manager, with a job application link?”

Talk to them

“Saw this opportunity. Very interested. My experience seems to fit for this role. Can we make a time to chat?”

Contact 2-3 other people at same company, too

(From chat) Leon thinks about Roman Empire quite a bit. (Identified strongly with a meme about this)

Book by Mary Beard mentioned in chat that Leon really likes

[2:52 PM PT]

Normal advice for question above. Just shoot your shot. Fortune favors the bold. No reason to hesitate

“Are you still finding AI to be useful? I loved it 2 years ago but now the output has been disappointing. Seems like it got worse over time or something.”

Yes. Leon actually feels the complete opposite

2 years ago it was trash and did not solve any of his problems

Now, he can produce more work at a speed he would not be able to without AI

Really still needs to be involved

Burnt out pretty hard this week. Very ill. Dizzy. Attributed to coding with AI too much. He has been going +very+ hard. Rarely doesn’t have an agent running, especially with long-running agents. Felt his brain got dopamine’d out

Thinks he went too hard. Has a backlog of ideas he keeps in a document. Has access to AI for free. Can trial agents for zero cost

He went 12-15 hr days with these for 2 weeks. Not a second where he wasn’t ripping. Thinks this was too much

Can see how fast and more he can go. Helps him realize what he wants to spend time on. Find balance of focusing in on 2-3 meaningful projects instead of 12. Code does not really matter anymore. It’s the engineering and product dev that is slowing him down now. Unburdens him in code production, but re-burdens him in the other areas. New bottlenecks now that coding is no longer one of them

Models change very quickly and have different strengths

AI-enabled engineering is now a new skill set to learn

Ex. Project structuring, using tools to help AI understand. Need to employ all this effectively to harness it well

Month or two to learn these skills, and then you can really unlock the true functionality of AI

Most people underestimate how long this ramp is

Always someone new to learn, and AI is just it right now

Leon thinks there’s always going to be areas of incremental improvement that we need to be open to pursuing if we want to stay at the top of our game

Don’t stagnate, and we couldn’t even before AI if we wanted to be successful

Leon doesn’t think we’ll all be product managers. Thinks we need engineering more now. AI is just predicting next steps. Need to combine AI’s ability to grok a codebase and laser in on fixes with our real engineering ability. That’s the real weapon. No harness is really great at full project understanding. Great to save your time in moving to specific files and moving stuff around

Leon wants to do a course on this

Finding this helps SWE feel fun again. Dark arts that take away a lot of the pain points for him

[3:00 PM PT]

“Why don’t you share a syllabus in the cohort? Why is it bad to work ahead?”

2 reasons

When people reach a chapter end, they often quit (based on research)

Creates barrier to get over for next chapter

Syllabus gives exact times to stop learning

Even if you’re not quitting, people often burn themselves out

30 week course minimum? I can see everything on CommunityTaught. I’ll do it all in 2 weeks

Not learning anything, because brain hasn’t had enough time to absorb anything

Might make you feel productive, but is counterproductive to you achieving your goals

This type of individual would otherwise be phenomenally successful, but the syllabus causes them to get in their own way. Leon doesn’t want this to happen to them

Syllabus can also look like a big ladder from the bottom. Becomes too burdensome, and causes us to doubt our ability to climb the mountain. Leon would rather take it step by step

[3:03 PM PT]

“may I put pomodoro website in my CV experience or projects section or its to easy for junior role and I need to create more complex projects?”

Good to remove simpler projects as we get better ones

Pomodoro project may be great

No shame in having simpler projects in the beginning, but as we get better projects, we just let the simpler ones fall of our resume and what we’re using to sell ourselves

“with rising expectation of AI use in job descriptions should we intentionally create projects with Claude files to show we have ecperienxce using them?”

Should definitely understand how to use AI and what the limitations are

Roxy (Chosen One) has been asked to showcase ability to use AI

This is likely going to become increasingly common in interviews

Leah from chat: Also important to be able to talk about HOW you used AI. Need to be able to speak more about things like PRDs and workflows, how plan mode works, how to-dos get broken into agents and subagents. Commands for certain agents. Reptile, bugbot, etc. Vercell agent to check that my hosting is fine. Need a good sense of how to map what your AI workflow is

Leon’s familiarity comes from being online too much and playing with the AI tools

Thinks we should try to do both, in a sustainable fashion ;)

Leon and chat realizes this is an entire class

People often glaze over when Leon starts talking about AI in depth like this

Leon is prioritizing this AI class

[3:06 PM PT]

“Plan Mode” in AI is this product requirement stuff

“Do current interviews include AI use to test if we know how to use it efficiently? What AI should we learn Claude, Gemini or something else? Or stick to no AI?”

Yes. There are more commonly in interviews an expectation from companies that we should be proficient with AI tools

Coming up enough where we should at least have some answers for it. Like opening a door

100Devs goal is to open as many doors for ourselves as possible

Understanding AI tooling is a part of this now

Depends on types of roles. Some are more AI heavy than not

Cursor is Leon’s current favorite AI tool

He has received lots of credits from them, so he has spent lots of time here

Thinks they have the best harness for most of the model

Uses Gemini with Cursor harness to get the most out of Gemini

Thinks he would still use Cursor for this if he had to pay for this

Cursor allows us to switch between different models very easily. Leon now knows which tool he would use for which task. This seems like the secret sauce

Deep understanding of models, which has been helped by Cursor allowing him to very easily swap

New projects? Claude

Dashboards, one-off standalone apps? Gemini

Deep code? CodeX

Start with the free accounts on everything. Play with it. Google subscription gives you pretty good Gemini

Do similar projects across tools. Don’t pay for anything. Shouldn’t be necessary.

Antigravity has good perspective on agent window, but now CodeX has it

CodeX inside of CodeX is faster

Lots of people already have openAI subscription, so that helps with this

Likes CL utilities, but uses these less. Cursor has been a dream

[3:11 PM PT]

“I’m good at coding, but I haven’t had a salaried role yet. Is a junior dev role worth it? I work as a cable technician now and would like a different role soon”

Yes. Personally don’t like “junior”. Entry level roles are great places to start

Engineering is not just the coding. Learn a lot in early professional roles, so helpful to get your foot in the door wherever you can

[3:12 PM PT]

“Do I tie my story to my 100Hours project? They seem a bit different than each other”

Good 100Hours project is typically something that ties to your story, but might also be based on a passion

Use 100Devs GPT to help craft story, and see if your project can tie into it

From chat: AI skills are very transferable

“Can you recommend something to see how to write tests, how test should look like and what to test please? I would like to learn it as some job offers list this”

Every company does testing +wildly+ different. Leon hasn’t traditionally taught it because of this

Just take any of the big testing libraries, read their documentation from beginning to end, and start adding coverage to your application. Best ways to learn

“Test Driven Development” book or course can then help

CodeWars incorporates this, so you may be doing more test-driven stuff already than you realize

[3:14 PM PT]

“I listen to your podcast and got curious. Can you pls do one huddle when you can show how you work with Ai agents, assign them different tasks as team etc pls?”

Sure. We can do this

Don’t want people to get the idea that Leon knows what he’s doing. Doesn’t think anyone does if they’re honest. Leon may be a couple of days ahead because he has been doing it too much. Changes every three minutes, though. Anyone pretending to know if likely lying and trying to sell you something

Can share current setup, but Leon is just playing around and finding things that seem to work for him at a given time

Feels like people are getting taken advantage of online

“I got four Mac Studios and now I’m making $1mil/month and you can, too!”

Leon wants to be a counterbalance to this. Don’t get taken advantage of by the snake oil salesmen that are now using AI to swindle us

[3:16 PM PT]

“I keep hearing AI is taking over Software Engineer jobs, even on CNN. It's making me a bit nervous, because they're making it seem as if anyone can use it.”

It’s their job to make you scared. That’s how news makes a living. They need to keep your attention

Look at hiring data and major company. Hiring is still increasing. IBM is going to be hiring 3x the entry level talent going forward

AI is a multiplier of force, as opposed to a replacement of force

One or two articles shouldn’t dictate your future

Companies are shipping more than ever before. Need way more people to help with this. Leon is very optimistic

[3:19 PM PT]

“I saw that Spotify developers haven’t written code since December and are just checking the AI written code. How do we learn/practice this?”

Try it. They have no idea what they’re doing. They’re just trying it more than you are. Try stuff, adjust, and try it again

Doing it at scale. Non-deterministic systems. It’s a black box

No idea what’s happening beneath deep learning. All made up and we’re just experimenting

“Power law of scaling”

If you keep investing more into LLM compute, you keep getting better outcomes

More electricity and compute gives you better outcomes. Just need to put resources into it to get better outcomes. Not actually sure why this is happening fundamentally. It’s all just experimentation at this point

[3:20 PM PT]

“Leon can you share a doc with these AI models and what they’re good for. This is a lot of information. I get to play with it, but help us start”

Programming Podcast covers this well whenever a new tool drops

Cursor puts out a good price chart. This should be the most important factor, says Leon

Lots of people talk to have either different opinions or very similar opinions as him

Lots of it likely comes down to personal preference

[3:22 PM PT]

“Is this weeks episode of The Programming Podcast going to be released?”

Lots of life stuff right now. Danny has a new opportunity. Might be a little delayed

[3:22 PM PT]

“what are the best and worst months for job hunt. I will be ready in 2-3months to apply and bit worry that it would be worst time as cs grads may join job market”

Best times are typically January, May, October. Those are the three best months to be in the market. Other softer ones. March, September a little less. Summer is typically kind of bad because people aren’t in office as much.

100Devs tries to schedule around these

Great time when CS graduates are coming in, because lots of jobs open up, and few CS grads have a lot of stuff that they’ve done. They’re usually just kids that studied, got decent grades, and typically don’t have any projects. 100Devs graduates have way more career-related experience, so CS grads should not be much competition

[3:24 PM PT]

“Are we going to make it?”

Definitely! Great hiring period right now. Easiest to learn at the moment, easiest way to get connected with people now. 10 years ago? Way harder to get in touch with people. Now, everything is online. Much easier to network and connect.

HuntOber is key, but that’s more preparing you for January. HuntOber helps you get all your stuff together so that you’re +on it+ in January. Lots of hiring at the beginning of October, too, though.

Feb can slow down a bit, and then March can come back hot depending on what area you’re in

[3:25 PM PT]

“Can you name resources to learn ai engineering please”

FastAI course. Practical Deep Learning for Coders (Jeremy Howard)

Best place to start.

Companion book from library “Deep Learning FastAI…”

Chip’s books on FastAI

Better suited for experienced engineers

Howard’s books are a little bit easier to approach

Danny also has an AI book coming out, but it’s not released yet

Check stage chat for more resources [3:27 PM PT]

This should be more than enough to get started

Thoughts on crafting interpreters?

No. He’s very practical. Doesn’t do as much learning to learn stuff. Wants very practical engineering stuff

He’s not as much into CS theory

“should we put udemy coursera and freecodecamp course cetrificates on linkedin or only certificates from masters like AWS or CS50 pls?”

None of these should be on LinkedIn

These just make you look like a beginner

AWS is fine if it’s the actual professional, industry standard certifications

“I just learned this thing” cert just makes you smell like a newbie, and we don’t want that. We want to reek that we’re a professional who is capable of solving our clients’ problems immediately

This is Leon’s personal opinion, but he thinks they hurt us more than help us

Leah in chat says we should use these skills to build a project, and then feature the project all over the place, not the cert. Leon agrees 1000%

[3:30 PM PT]

“We have Jobuaray and HunTober, are we going to have 'May-ployment' or 'May-nifest'?”

No. May/June is Leon’s vacation time. Lots of time in the woods

“When do you recommend starting to read ai related topics if you’re still early in 100devs? Like when to introduce it?”

Supplemental reading at any point is fine. Don’t use AI in learning process until you’re done with a course (like 100Devs)

Need to learn fundamentals without AI. Otherwise, our learning process is getting interrupted

Can read about and play with AI, but focus on coursework first. The coursework is what is going to get us the job, the AI tool is just extra. AI is a speed unlock, it should not be used at all when we’re learning how to do something

Talk about a 100Devs summer camp in the chat

[3:31 PM PT]

Leon makes a lot of tofu and rice dishes when camping. Tofu, rice, veggie dishes. Shelf-stable for a very long time. Lots of bean chilis. Has a really nice cooler that can help keep them out there for two weeks at a time

Bring musical instruments to play. Harmonica practice recently. (Laughed that ocarina was pretty close. [Zelda reference, Michael thinks <3] )

Back on Tuesday.

“One of the best times to be alive, to be a developer, and if you’re here with 100Devs, you’re so far ahead. You could have been picking your nose and watching Netflix, but you’re here putting in your vision of being a badass SWE into motion. Let’s keep up the momentum, and we’ll meet again to re-energize together next Tuesday <3”

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-24
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Tues 2/24/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:33 PM PT]

Talking about the Blizzard on the east coast. Shout out to the people digging out there.

stevenMosesCodes

“hi leon! i have a new development in my not-offer-yet saga 😅😅 may i talk it through with you? Thanks!”

Discussing live

Followed up with the people. Got a response. How does he respond to a specific e-mail, and what does it mean?

S: “Hey, just checking in about any updates. Still very excited and looking forward to next steps”

Recruiter: “I will get shortly back to you. There may be another link I would like you to apply to, and I will send that out by tomorrow”

[Michael’s audio from Leon is cutting out]

Leon thinks there’s a fit thing here

Recruiter might be finding a role that’s a better fit for Steven. Not a negative flag. Recruiter seems to be in Steven’s quarter, and now he just needs to work with them

Recruiter confirmed with Steven that title and pay band were okay. Steven somewhat frustrated. Thinks role might be too high for him, so maybe that’s the problem

Startupy team at a giant company

Leon said smaller subsidiaries can have wild head count requirements

Doesn’t seem odd that this is happening. Good that they didn’t ghost Steven

Don’t put all eggs in one basket. Keep hitlisting going, but don’t be too worried

Steven e-mailed, and they responded 6 minutes later, so Leon sees that they’re being responsive, which is a great sign

June in chat (2:39 PM PT)

Sometimes subsidiaries have more budget with parent, so they need you to apply to the parent. Internally, this helps with budgeting

Steven was afraid that with another link he’ll have to do the entire process again

Leon isn’t worried about this. They’re very responsive, they’re keeping Steven in the loop. This happens. Just moving stuff across different buckets. Leon doesn’t think we should be worried, but don’t get too invested in this opportunity. Keep exploring other opportunities, even if there don’t seem to be any red flags here

Leon agrees that Steven should follow up with confirmation. Give them a couple days, and then follow up again

[2:41 PM PT]

“In 100Devs, we learned about creating Classes when learning OOP but I've never seen anyone use Class in a project yet. How do we use/practice them in projects?”

Just have to practice

Can take OOP to code bases or any project we build

Can use principles of OOP in many ways. Especially in any of the node frameworks

Encapsulation, OOP best practices, etc.

May not be what we’re envisioning like if we were making a game

Different projects are organized in different ways. Open Source tools can have larger codebases that put some of these principles in play

Some teams you join may use fully functional programming and not use OOP every day, so don’t sweat it too much

Steven built a Discord bot using OOP principles

Leon likes building a project, especially using AI, and then have the AI give him advice on how he could have done it more optimally, and maybe how he could have done it using OOP

Build without AI, and then use the LLM to give you feedback. No longer need to find a human to do this

Just keep building

[2:44 PM PT]

Chat doesn’t think anyone is doing full-on academic OOP anymore in the real world

Leon not concerned about AI taking over and stealing his job. He has more work now than he has ever had before AI

Leon often gets stuck in ruts and will build random stuff to make sure his unused skills stay sharp

OOP is often used in many ways we’re not really paying attention to (see chat for examples)

[2:45 PM]

Chat thinks the tech that AI is creating will keep SWE’s employed for a long time. Leon kind of agrees

Slido clone Leon is building is using lots of AI tools

Leon has twelve projects right now just because he’s having lots of fun building. Impacting his sleep schedule 😛

Building a project to help address one of our pain points is one of the best ways to improve

Something we’re passionate about is great fuel

[2:46 PM PT]

Chat discussing that we shouldn’t feel like we’re late to the game. We’re here, and we’re ready for AI, so we’re exactly where we’re supposed to be

MayanWolfe in chat saying no one in her family is talking about AI at all. It’s just that we’re in a tech echo chamber that it’s a big topic for us

“Each dot is 3.2 billion people” AI usage image from Twitter that Leon screenshared

Very few people are actually using AI. Even fewer are paying for it

We’re worried, but only 2-5 Million people on the planet are seriously using AI at all

If we’re in this group, we’re so far ahead of the curve that it’s absurd

People are really overestimating how far ahead everyone else is in using AI, and at the same time underestimating how well we’re doing in terms of using AI in our growth

We’re just in a bubble. If we’re actively using it, or even if we’re not but at least understand something about AI, we’re doing wonderfully

[2:50 PM PT]

“What do I prep and expect for an online js and language agnostic technical? There will be 5 Qs to do in 60-90 mins within 3 days? Im out of practice 😕”

Always ASK first

“Super broad. Five questions about what? What should I be prepared for? I just want to make sure we’re both using our time effectively. What am I walking into?”

Most of the time, the people who want to interview will tell you

Then, turn over every stone. Ask LLM, go on Glassdoor, gitHub, anyone who may have been asked these questions by this company before, and then prepare from that

Try +really+ hard to go into an interview knowing exactly what’s going to happen

Language agnostic, so pick your favorite language

60-90 min is normal technical interview

Doesn’t seem like much to worry about

Just make sure our coding challenge game is good in terms of technical prep (LeetCode/Codewars)

Have a project recently built in the language you plan to use, just in case there’s a practical component

Best step is to ask to make sure that you know as much as possible about what to expect

Steven in chat [2:53 PM PT]

Typical pattern to these challenges. Make sure you know DS&A basics

“I have a final interview with the Managing Director, who seems to be the office's "boss." What should I expect?”

[2:54 PM PT]

Again, always ask what you’re about to walk into

Need to have at least a rough idea of what’s about to happen

Do your research

Managing Director (MD) can do one of two ways

Last technical check to make sure everyone else was right about you

Higher level interview is more common

Do you know company culture, strategy, etc?

Not only do you have raw engineering skills, but the ability to transfer those skills to provide value for the team

Did you do the due diligence to show that you’re a good person to work with who can help the team?

Leon has had interviews with CEOs who have asked him to reverse a string, so it can go either way

Companies often have published things about what they want you to bring up in interviews

Amazon has a guide for interviewees, for example, and if you don’t follow this guide, you’re not getting the opportunities, because they know you didn’t do your homework

“how to answer what are your weakness on interview to make it acceptable and show we learn the lesson. Also "why Google?" question as ppl say same boring stuff”

[2:56 PM PT]

Weaknesses is something you prepare

100Devs BANK is +very+ important

After an interview, you need to actively recall what was asked, so you have better answers the next go-around

Interview is more about preparing you for all the other job opportunities you have going forward than about the actual interview

This allows every interview to be a success, especially since most interviews will not lead to an offer

Focus on improving interview-to-interview, and that’s what’s going to get you the opportunities

If Leon doesn’t have a “greatest weakness” answer that rocks, he’ll be going home after the interview, getting with community, and making sure he has that great answer

If you have to think about answers to behavioral questions during an interview, you fucked up. Leon said this twice

Behavioral answers should be second nature +before+ the interview

All about doing your Banki. Are you using the CAR method for every question on Banki? Do you have a good CAR answer and a way to develop a solid answer afterwards to a novel question?

Specifically:

Tie weakness to something that’s real. Show you are reflective and can grow. Not something like “I’m too much of an overachiever”

“When working with customers in the past, I was too quick to acquiesce too quickly to their demands, which created too much work for team at the last minute. Learned to validate customer demands, put together a reasonable time frame for them to get what they want, and also make sure my team’s time was protected. Constantly working on this to improve so that I’m doing my best to help all stakeholders, internal and external”

Leon likes this because it show growth

“Why Google?”

This is an easy prep from doing research. Need to know about the company and give a reason for why you’re applying to that company

[3 PM PT]

Someone in the chat was new to the Huddle and feeling like it’s an entirely new language with all the terms we’re using

Leon says that’s how everyone starts. Just stick with us and it will become your language, too

Strain made a good comment in the chat about Weakness questions

MayanWolfe as well. Shout out to Banki

Weaknesses tied to actual event (lot of gold in the chat that Leon is highlighting)

HIGHLY IMPORTANT: Do not blame other people

Take accountability for what happened, demonstrate capacity for growth. Blaming others is not something someone is going to be impressed by

“Not a question just need some encouragement right about now”

Did you see that graph about most people not using AI to code?

You’re ahead of 8 billion people

You’re more informed, and with more skills, than 8 Billion other people on this planet

If this doesn’t convince you that you’re doing well, Leon can’t really do much more

Showing up to the Huddle? You’re fantastic. Just keep up the great work

No matter what the future of AI holds, you’re already ahead of 8 Billion people. You can seize the opportunities before they can, adapt before they can, and you’ll be alright

Keep showing up, keep working, and help blaze the path for others to follow in your successful footsteps

[3:03 PM PT]

“If we have time, can we quickly go over my story? I used the GPT '100 Devs Craft Coach, but feel I might be focusing on the wrong things in my life story” - UncleNoob

Yup. Leon has other questions in the Slido, but he seems to like prioritizing the live answers, so don’t be afraid to raise your hand and ask live

Don’t need camera for live answers. Audio is fine. Noob is putting story in the chat

Story:

“(Career of solving problems → code as another tool)

"I spent years organizing other people's chaos - coordinating authors, editors, and designers at Bint Publishing, helping small businesses at TELUS find systems that actually fit how they worked, maintaining operations at a medical clinic where things breaking had real consequences.

I got good at spotting what was broken and figuring out what would fix it. But I kept running into the same wall as an individual: I couldn't find a task management tool that worked for how I actually think. Every app I tried showed me everything at once - my entire backlog, every priority, every project - and instead of helping me start my day, it paralyzed me.

I tried to hire developers to build something better it was not practical at the time, or they didn't understand the problem well enough to solve it.

So I learned to code. Not to become a developer - to build the thing I actually needed. That became BloomSpace: a workload system built around individual execution, not project tracking. The idea is simple - separate what you need to capture from what you need to do today, so you can start your day without staring down everything you're not doing."

[Link from chat: https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475991903324606709 ]

Leon thinks this is a solid first step. Hopes chat will share their thoughts, too

Couple pieces. If he’s just talking to another Dev, this is a good story

May be some things you can do to be better

Try to keep it a singular paragraph. Shorter is better

Speak more grand. “Big publishing” becomes “One of the largest publishing firms on the east coast”

Want audience’s mind to wander about what your experience could have been, and have them overestimate

Be vague in ways that help make you look better

Ex. Helping medical clinics. Don’t be too specific. Talk on grander scale. Be specific about what you fixed. “Helped hundreds of medical clinics fix X”

“Running into the same problem”

Second half of this paragraph seems negative. Project management but got paralyzed? Maybe Noob can’t handle workload. Too risky as it can cause people to go negative

“As I was working for the largest publishers and on medical breakthroughs, task management became a challenge, and so I build this tool to help me and teams of folks I worked with, and now I want to bring these same skills to your organization and help solve these problems together”

Name-dropping BloomSpace seems like more of a pitch for BloomSpace than a pitch for UncleNoob

Frame everything as a hook that puts us in the best possible light

[3:10 PM PT]

Gotta be the main hero of your story:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475993162563457247

Leon strongly agrees

Leon tries to put himself in the shoes of the grumpiest hiring manager

Just checking boxes and keeping stuff moving. Doesn’t want to read more than what they have to. Don’t give them the easy “No’s” for your candidacy. Bangers only

100Devs wisdom from chat: “When you walk into the interview, you are the savior.”

Leon remembers a quote “All your gods are dead. I’m here now” Loves it.

“How to prepare for cultural fit interview. Its easy to find online possible questions but answering them is another story. Any website with good examples pls?”

[3:12 PM PT]

Can find answers to these. Still have to practice and prep

RECORD YOURSELF saying your answers. Leon can’t understand why people don’t do this more

Helps you see what your answers look like. Once you watch the recording, you can see much more about how you’re conveying yourself. Much easier to do this vs. trying to do it live when you’re actually saying it

Banki Brunch can help others help you surface these issues

Can also pass your responses to an LLM, ask it to be as critical as possible, and help you improve them
