Other thing: They are looking for a specific answer. Your goal, and it’s a difficult skill for many of us, is to look at stated culture on their website and how your background upholds the values they are looking for

This is a lot of work, but we need to put the work in before an interview for it to be natural, and we need to be willing to do this for every interview if we really want to do our best

MayanWolfe from the chat: Can’t just watch guitar videos. Need to practice it yourself if you want to get good

[3:14 PM PT]

WhisperFlow and ChatGPT for feedback

Single party consent state? Record your interviews, and feed them to GPT to see what you can improve

Gemini seems really good for this. Leon will record his audio for everything. Sales calls, interviews, and then he asks Gemini what he could have done differently

Chat: Rufio is streaming tonight, and Leon wants your support for the 100Devs stream team, as always

[3:15 PM PT]

“how come i dont get any phone calls after applying for 100s of roles (java backend) on linkedin? is there anything better than linkedin? YEO: 4 years”

Yes. applying for jobs DOES NOT WORK

Watch the 100Devs videos on how to get a job

If you’re just clicking apply, this does not work. You might get lucky once in a blue moon, but it’s not nearly as good as 100Dev’s approach of networking your way in and having a standardized process

Something may be wrong with your online presence, narrative, resume, etc. 100Devs has tools for this, including a Narrative GPT, that can help

Check with the 100Devs community about this

LinkedIn is also very flooded. If you did something other than LinkedIn, and did nothing else, that might help (100Devs uses HiringCafe)

Use the 100Devs methods. It still works

If you’re using AI in any part of the application process, it’s an auto-reject now, so don’t use AI. Everything needs to be in our own words. People want authenticity

Leon offered to help this questioner live

Every major AI tool leaves a signature in our text. As soon as they paste your text into an ATS system, it will flag it as AI generated. Really need to type it out and edit it in your voice

Leon has talked to countless hiring managers that will automatically reject anything that has AI in it. They +HATE+ dealing with AI-generated applications that waste their time.

No copy-pasting AI-generated content, as it can keep the AI signature

[3:19 PM PT]

Even if they don’t use an ATS to check, all the AI submissions are going to look similar enough for them to tell

“Brain Freezing Up When Encountering New Tools and Unexpected Bugs. Resources to Recommend?”

Brain Freezing up is likely a mindset shift

Biggest mindset shift for Leon, especially for non-deterministic stuff

Used to ask when he hit a bug: “What went wrong?”

Now thinks this is a mistake

Now: “I wrote the code in a particular way. Why was this the outcome?”

Slight difference, but now the bug is the by-product of what he has done. It’s not something that randomly happened to him. It’s something he did that led to a result

Did he do something differently? Is the bug repeatable? Does he keep getting the same error?

Realizing that he generated the bug helps empower him to know that he can also find a way to fix it and, ideally, not generate this type of bug again

Getting Good at Debugging as a Skill

Good to have process you go through. Pre-flight checklist, like if you were flying a plane

Should have something similar when debugging. Checklist of steps you walk through each time

Get used to using debugging tools. Use a debugger, logger, print statements, etc. to your advantage to get more clarity around what is happening

Get better at reading through the flow of information and all the touchpoints of your code to see how the error is being produced

Mindset that it’s fixable, checklist that you work through, and use of the appropriate tools

You now have a system to work through that can make the process of debugging much easier.

Typically 90% of what is going to happen to you is one of ten or so different things

Async thing? State thing? Only so many things that can go wrong.

If you’re able to repeat it, you can learn how to narrow down which thing is likely happening

GPT can give you a road map about debugging methods and what to look for. It’s definitely a learnable skill, and so we can find debugging techniques that have worked for people that are likely to work for us, too

Much of debugging is about practice and experience. The more we do it and stick to an effective checklist, the more the issues surface in a familiar way, we get more practice at getting familiar with a process that works, so each time we debug it can be a little easier and we can be more confident in our capacity

Straine in the chat: “The mindset Leon is talking about is engineering condensed”

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475996294949638206

Leon agrees 1000%

[3:22 PM PT]

“Five years of experience. Talked to a recruiter who said I would be good for an associate position. How do I get rid of the smell that’s keeping me from getting a full stack job?”

With five years of experience, you need to be leaning into your expertise that allows you to demonstrate that you are obviously the best fit for a role.

This takes some effort to do

Have to look at all the things you put forth in your applications or online presence that may be contrary to you being a good senior fit for a full stack role

Old portfolio, lack of distilling experience into a meaningful narrative that highlights meaningful things that would enable you to enact positive change at the company from day one

Good narrative, good story, good examples of meaty problems you’ve worked on and the solutions that you’ve crafted

Be able to articulate this and share it with others

Maybe make content around this, so you can point to things and say clearly, “Here’s what I did”

If you haven’t yet, take stock of those five years and turn them into actionable insights that you can share during your interview and online publicly

Make sure that you did get rid of the smell. Get other people to review your online presence and materials. The 100Devs community is here for you, and hopefully you’re here for them, too.

Anything that doesn’t sit right? You’ve got to get rid of it to make sure you’re obviously the best candidate for the roles you’re pursuing

Chat: “Pro tip: Keep a "wins" diary as you do your work.”

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475997050364756131

[3:25 PM PT]

Absolutely. Current cohort of RC (Resilient Coders) has to do a daily wins journal and share their wins every day

Sometimes it’s silly stuff: “I found the hot sauce in the back of my pocket. Wonderful.”

Important to get in the habit of writing stuff down. Lots of time you’ve done something cool, and having it marked down is very helpful when you’re looking for opportunities, in line for promotions, showing up to performance reviews, etc

Without this log of wins, you’re not going to remember something that happened months or years ago, and we have to be our biggest cheerleaders and keep track of this stuff to effectively sell ourselves and provide proof for why we clearly deserve the things we’re striving for

“Contract looking to negotiate says pay range is around 70K for a QA role. Should I keep the negotiation written in email?”

You do not begin negotiating until you have a signed offer letter (Leon’s typical advice)

Once you get a signed offer letter, you can play hardball

Until then?

“Sounds great! Your range seems like it might be within my range…”

Once you get the signed offer letter, then it’s time to negotiate

For negotiation:

Find out what the real range is for the job

Check GlassDoor about what people are getting paid

Salary bands for a specific area

QA compensation for those in NYC is going to be very different from compensation in a small Midwestern city.

Local demands on salary is very important

Use all of this information when you go to negotiation. Important to be as informed as possible, just like during the interview

Classic 100Devs line: “It seems like we’re close. I’m very excited to join the team. This seems like a great opportunity, is there any wiggle room in the starting salary?” … and then we play it from there

Leon vastly prefers negotiation on video, or at least via phone.

When he throws out a number, facial expressions tell him a +ton+

From these expressions, he can see if he’s reaching too far with his ask, or maybe he’s not reaching far enough

Writing it down is not optimal for him, because he loses all of this information

Some people may prefer written negotiation, but Leon finds this very suboptimal for him, and he identifies as someone with social anxiety, so if it’s worth the effort for him, it’s likely to be worth the effort for us to do this stuff on video

“Name of Networking App that Leon uses?”

Leon uses Apollo.io to find people’s professional contact

Free account gives you enough information to find people and their contact information

“Hunter” is a good one, too

“AI Tools that you recommend checking out?”

Anything that has a free plan, you should check it out

You’ve got to play with everything

Leon a big fan of Cursor

Raffle should happen on Friday

Most of his stuff is done is Cursor these days

Has Claude, Gemini, and Whisperflow. With Cursor, these four are his main stack

Does some local stuff with oLlama

Leon feels pretty happy with his tooling at the moment.

[3:30 PM PT]

Michael had to leave to donate blood, so he missed the end of the Huddle after this point. Sorry :(

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-02-28
html:
by Anny Levine (

https://www.linkedin.com/in/annylevine

)

These notes were taken in a hurry, since things move fast during the Huddles. So some things are paraphrased for efficiency's sake. Also, had a few computer hiccups. Sorry!

Happy Pokemon Day!

Legend: "Should be hearing back from the hackathon I got headhunted for!"

Leon: Great! Post it in the celebrations channel!

Go post questions either here in the chat, or on sli.do at

https://app.sli.do/event/bv4LFC3jQWBpLUU37aL2NQ/live/questions

HeyItsSaraDev: Not well, but gonna be here to listen 😊

Leon: Feel better!

BeastApple (Anna) class 29: "Finished my last day at my 9-5 today, the hunt is now my job and I have lots of freelance work calls lined up through the next 10 days! Terrified and excited at once"

Sli.Do: "starting my new role next monday (yippee) but i am nervous. I was sent onboarding stuff yesterday and do i need to do this asap before wknd or on monday?"

Leon: It depends. Hopefully they've shared some context around the onboarding materials, sometimes they give a guide or what the expectations are. try to read everything, though, unless it's something they tell you you're going to be doing on the first day(s). lean into the new person vibe where you're learning expectations. first 90 days, get in early, stay a little late, never eat alone, ask questions and let others know what you're doing so others dont have to ask those questions later. write down everything you work on, set a meet with your manager about expectations for the first 3, 6, 9 months or the year. (more to say here but hmm)

jasonetorres: Nerves go away they know you’re new so it’s cool"

"How do I frame a layoff due to restructuring or short work terms on resume in a way that feels confident not defensive"

Leon: you don't have to worry about layoffs these days, because over the last few years the stigma has gone away, most people have been through it. everyone understands the game for 10+ years. it's part of the tech hiring cycle to overhire, then let people go. you don't have to say too much. show something like freelancing, being active, building with others. don't worry about the short period. you can put something on there.

one school of thought. never make the recruiter think. (4000 people layoff) so it's not tied to your personal performance, but the recruiter sees what happened. everyone understands.

"Does working two jobs carry a stigma?" you better have a good logic behind why.

[chatter about different ways to make cottage cheese tasty including from Leon]

BeastApple (Anna) class 29: "Normal school food in Finland = cottage cheese in shredded carrot. Sometimes with raisins" i've had that and it's tasty.

[Leon's shirt keeps green-screening. t-shirt in pic in follow-along materials channel. it's all cute bulbasaurs.]

"did you rethink/update your AI agent permissions/auth after that last programming podcast? What did you change? What have you learned?"

Leon: so this is based on our last programming podcast with a vp from Okta. a lot of companies are using okta. make sure your agents have the right permissions, are authenticated in the right way, etc. i haven't figured it out as part of my regular dev workflow. i haven't found something i'm happy with yet. i'll probably update you when i figure it out, but i'm still in figure it out mode.

"can u have a local version of openclaw?"

Leon: yes, a lot of people get a mac mini. they want to imessage it.

jasonetorres: "I have it running local on a Linux pc"

"James Jahner (class 52)

100D

: @Leon you'll like this guy.

https://youtu.be/QWzLPn164w0?si=rI_oil3-yIfg9S5T

he's deep in AI corperate."" hey, thanks! (AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones: Prompt Engineering is Dead. Context Engineering is Dying. What Comes Next?)

"is it ok to list tech stack used at 100devs on github/Linkedin/CV without context and project separately or we need to back up every single stack with projects?"

there's no international committee for int'l standardization of Cvs. you should have something to back up what you're saying on there. should have a skill in or at least dabbled in, and be able to talk about how you used it. have strong proof of flimflam throughout your resume if your local economy is all about flimflam.

"I fail to consistently plan out my days; I do it for a week or two and then stop altogether. Do you have any advice?" see if you can figure out why, like WHY you stop doing it. executive functioning, adhd, etc... make time for it. schedule it on your calendar right now. put it on their daily, if you have to. get external reminders to actually do it. have a dedicated block of time that will almost always be open. make systems be in place that have it easy to make things happen. even make sure it works on your worst days. build out a system that works even on your worst day. figure out your why. figure out why you wanna do it if it was impactful for you. make it happen every week. get accountability. setup reminders, workflows... remove friction. Obsidian. Apple Note. Google whatever their thing is. my todo list is just one file where i add things to the top off and just either delete them or do them.

jordan: "you can put a list of things you want to do daily into gemini, and it can plan your day out for you on google calendar"

"### chrizzle (100hrs project/hunt): "I use Joplin since it syncs across devices for free and can organize through notebooks like Evernote"

jasonetorres: "Where @Dthompsondev Mayanwolfe can DM right now with his “know your why” video"

DthompsonDev:

https://youtu.be/1ytFB8TrkTo?t=68&si=V6rI4nciW1iDSATH

https://myskylight.com/calendar/

or physical family calendar?

pillarofautumn_: "Apple reminders you can set up different lists within a list"

Cmyers92: "I like the rpgtodo list app"

"Michael Govaerts

: "What was the Why, Leon? Why are you doing this? Or why are you struggling to plan the day?"

Molly (lurk) (class58): "I use finch for my recurring tasks... its fun cause its like a tamagotchi"

"What would udo if LinkedIn forced u to upload ID? There are many articles this week with Discord and LinkedIn and all the Persona data leaks. Can I avoid LI?"

Leon: Not having a linkedin is closing a very very big door. it's a big part of a lot of peoples professional lives. they only want you to have one account, and they want to make sure it's real people on there. especially if you come from a non-US area... it depends on your area, but it's a very important door to have open. it's unfortunate, but it's coming. a lot of platforms are doing this, rolling it back, and trying to make it more palatable.

"I was told all I need to get job in my area is Python and I don't need front end. Do you agree? Can I build just backend? What projects can be build that way?"

Leon: i don't know. i need to know your area. i'm a big fan of learning fullstack web development. learn to build fullstack and then figure out what works in your area and what interests you. generalize at first, then break into other areas once you're in. a lot of the frontend stuff isn't wild to pick up but can lead to more opportunities.

"When looking at roles on company sites, is it generally worth doing the "Join Our Talent Community" thing? Do hirers actually look at that?"

Leon: Ehhhhhhhhh, I dn't think it hurts. It shouldn't be your strategy though. Don't lean on it being your only way into a company.

"I had my first big tech interview recently. I think I did well on one leetcode question but bombed the 2nd one. I think being too nervous got to me. Any advice?"

Leon: is it true? did you go back and review your answer(s) to make sure it was correct? the rest is just time in saddle, butt in seat time. the more exposure you have the easier it gets. you cant do the job you don't have. don't get excited going into an interview so you don't get devastated if/when it doesn't work out. practice practice practice (list of all sorts of options here)

"I am working through Cohort 2 and verbally committed to never send a Linked-In connect without a message. Now they limit those unless we pay. Any advice?"

Leon: be selective with the messages that you send. if you can't send a message with the connection, find a different place to send the message. (more here but cat intervened)

Roxy RodBeck: even if you cant get the email on apollo getting the name helps you search the webs

KeepPushingKetchupKrew: Comment on their posts if they have one or repost it

ghosted (lurk): I encountered a few blockers when their work emails wouldn't accept my out of company email

"in the past huddle I heard freelancing can be done as agency instead of self-employed. Can you please tell us more about it how to advertise ourselves as agency"

Leon: there's no actual terminology around it, its pretty made up. even if you work for a fullblown agency, its still the same as being 1099 or self-employed.

"jasonetorres: One thing I’ve noticed a lot of is “framing” so many people are doing the right things. They just can’t articulate or explain well (thinking of “agency”)"

people have a lot of trouble speaking about themselves or in grander terms. i blame capitalism.

anjomi: practice! (plugging amandas PREP school, and sams fcc challenges)

stevenMosesCodes📚🌈: if you need practice come to PREP school with @ichtragebrille (Amanda) !

intelagense: If you want some friendly exposure people you can post in "Share Your LinkedIn"

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/933076191961837638

blackheadband (HUNT / Class 28): come to Amanda's PREP school fridays @23:30 (3:30pm CST-US) and Sam's freeCodeCamp weeknights @05:00

"People keep saying that at tech interviews if I use methods to solve a challenge they will tell me to do it without methods. Is this true? Shouldn't I use them?"

Leon: have you tried it? a lot depends on how well you're communicating and collaborating throughout the interview. explain the method, if it seems like someone's struggling, to make sure they understand the problem. a lot has to do with how you move throughout the interview. the interview question was to get you out of your comfort zone to see if you have the base level of education around data structures, arrays, etc... a lot of times the builtin methods are the bet way to do something.

people keep saying "you should never listen to random people on the internet" myself included. you should be learning something from each interview and bringing it to your next.

blackheadband: up to the discretion of the interviewer but always be prepared to switch gears if they ask

you can always ask if it's okay to use stuff

Quancom: In interviews do they really ask you to share your screen?

ichtragebrille (Amanda): yes?

intelagense: depends

tehuberjohn: I feel you should know how to do it without methods anyway, learning fundamentals will help you in any language. You really don't need methods to do anything, it can just be more efficient

stevenMosesCodes📚🌈 : i've done both screen share as well as interviewing platforms like hackerrank and codesignal

stevenMosesCodes📚🌈 : my in person screen share was just that he stood behind me and watched LOL

Zarth: I've had one on a team call and used a VS Code extension for same doc collaboration

intelagense: thats better than write it on this yellow paper :cry:

"Is Bob the mannequin Uncle Bob?" no author of "Clean Code"?

Leon: yeah, no. Bob's lit though. Robert Cecil Martin, aka "Uncle Bob"

"How do you feel about posting your projects on LinkedIn or twitter?"

Leon: you should be doing it. (on repeat like 100 times) there's a million other people. dont write content for the turbonerd. write for the people that didn't take the weekend to figure it out. don't care about the people who'll never see your content anyway. post for other people that want your content, and that's a lot that know less than you probably do.

(Leon telling a story about a newer friend, cheesecake factory, telling the story about why we say NERD to each other... driving in the middle of Harvard Square because they weren't there for the story of what it meant. OOPS)

"Am no experience career changer. How many projects should I list in CV as I have nothing else and not sure if I can list doing freelancing - no paid project yet"

Leon: you have to think of everything in terms of steps. if you have one project, you put one project. two to two. better projects then go up there. put what you have then be quick to change it as new things come up with. try getting a paid client. it really does help open up a lot of doors that wouldn't normally be open. if you're following the 100devs way, you'll have projects that start from a little and go to a lot and you'll replace a lot of what you did in the beginning. start with what you got, though. really.

"What is better do virtual hackathon with team of total 4 strangers or go solo to make sure project gets done and no one breaks code it just before deadline?"

Leon: uh, it depends. if i'm going to a hackathon it's to make friends. have fun. meet people. i'll always pick working with strangers. network. meet people. have fun. if it works out well, stick with those people who slowly start add more competent people to your team.

suicyco (Micah): strangers is great, get to know people and learn how to work with others

stevenMosesCodes: Team, for the behavioral fodder lol

jasonetorres: get that communication and networking

Sabrina_Class52: also can meet great teammates for next one

BeastApple (Anna) class 29: Learn how to communicate and demonstrate that as a team

"Any advice before scoping and pricing a client portal for a real estate company? I'm handling the frontend and meeting their backend engineer on Monday"

Leon: figure out what their budget is. WHAT IS YOUR BUDGET TO GET THIS DONE. WHAT DO YOU WANT TO GET DONE AT THIS PRICE POINT. THEN SCOPE IT.

Sabrina_Class52: I tried to get budget then the client was like “we don’t disclose” then sent me another one with “btw many ppl want this job”😂

"I got gifted my first raspberry pie and don't know how to start with it, and what to do with it? Do you have any idea or sugestions or resources please?"

Leon: youtube, reddit, openclaw, alternative internets like zero, a lot of cool projects that include it. (i was spacing out since it's 1am here, sorry!)

https://itsfoss.com/raspberry-pi-projects/

this will change your life:

https://pi-hole.net/

Did anyone see the dog vibecoder?

https://youtu.be/8BbPlPou3Bg?si=u2zjgMh1OPdcEQeP

{ My computer died for a few minutes so missing some things that happened }

https://cheerful-composer-2995.kit.com/83157cd5d0

to sign up for leon's newsletter.

Is Twitter still as good for networking as it was a few years ago? I keep hearing not but it's from non-tech folk. There may be some political influence in that

m.Campos: "A lot of the times consultants do opportunity hires even when they don't have open roles.

Learn the job you are in. Figure out if any software is used in your role or the project managers role or the engineers role. Ask about it. Build a project that you can talk to ppl about in the org and the consultants. Show interest and that you can do the job"

Legend: When you build a portfolio, does it ever give off a bootcamp smell?

intelagense: its an iterative process and you can always improve it. get feedback from others here for sure.

"what's the post you liked seeing the most this week?" my feed is full of pokemon. i love it. also (missed some things here)

"what sites or sources can we use to find Start ups companies? (LinkedIn doesn't seem to be cutting it)" thewatchlistAI, you can see by people who've raised fundraising, growjo, hitlistmagic inside of google search will surface all kinds of companies, some people like wellfound. it's not as good as it used to be though.

author: Anny Levine
title: annylevine/2026-03-03
html: No huddle! Big news coming probably on Friday

author: Ashton (class 10)
title: ashtonclass10/2024-05-11
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

took some notes during the huddle, had to leave about half way through but some notes are better than none

a good amount of jobs/interviews/people paid recently!, A lot of people on social media seem to be doom posting when it comes to the job market,

I have my very very very first interview, they said it's a quick chat with the hiring manager and a senior dev I am freaking out HELP,

have to remove emotion from the hunt, even excitement, and act normal! this can be hard to do for many though,

the people interviewing you are your peers, they'll have an easier job if you get hired so don't freak out!,

one interview is just one interview, you'll have others, do your banki, talk about your 100 projects, know the stack that they want and know the company, ask about what kind of interview it is,

how many rounds of interviews is too much?, depends on the company level and compensation, entry-level should be one, FAANG will have more,

3.Hi Leon, I came across this defeated linkedin post from one of our own. How can we avoid becoming utterly crushed like this? [link removed for privacy] things will weigh on you (it's called the trough of sorrow for a reason) it's not easy! there's a reason Leon shows the journey first thing.,

this is not an easy path, but if you make it through, it's worth it. compare it to a traditional college degree, 4-5 years of work.

be realistic, things will fail and you'll have to keep going. take care of yourself, manage your frustration, and be consistent. it's the journey, not the end goal. you'll drain yourself otherwise., the goal is to be a better developer than you were yesterday., Atomic Habits is a really good book to read,

Is May a hot month for job searching for the hunt?, yes, but it's not optimized for non-traditional learners., internship/entry-level jobs pop up for college students and companies have a better grasp on the hiring budget for the year,

What is the best way to contribute or get involved with open-source projects? I am confused about how even to get started., get to a point where you understand code basics to be helpful,, look for first-timer-only projects that'll teach you how to contribute, get on projects you care about! look for low-level issues and get involved with the community. don't contribute just to contribute., 100devs will have community projects soon!,

Any news regarding Cohort3?, it'll happen when it happens,

How do you separate learning for gaining knowledge versus exploration? I often get overwhelmed when reading the coursework (Shay Howe, for example) trying to capture all the important things for my Anki, and this holds me back from progressing in the coursework., always ask yourself what the point of the material is., a lot of engineering is not knowing precisely but knowing where to get information. get the GIST and the fingers on the keyboard practice., What will this do for you? will it help you accomplish what you want? make a list of things you want to check out later.,

I got to the final interview of a job 6 months ago, rejected. they've been posting the same job every month for the past year but no new hires. Thoughts?, the clown show came to town, don't be surprised they have clowns., it's free to post on most job boards and nobody doing quality control., some companies want to seem like they're hiring. keep moving or hit them back up.,

How can we volunteer for cohort 3?, fill out the maintainer's form,

I'm getting to the 2nd/3rd interview, usually behavioral, & getting declined. I'm autistic & struggle with interviews. I'm doing banki. Anything else I can do?, practice interviews with people who will tell you the truth, be more aware/get BRUTAL feedback and be analytical about it, and come up with solutions., you will find a fit.,

Hey Leon, I am 6 interviews deep. Half a year into the hunt, got to a few final rounds but all rejections. Have any advice?, same thing. as the previous question., it's 1 out of 60 interviews that you get a win.,

What are your thoughts of accepting a role in tech support? I currently drive for Uber so it would be a pay increase and I’d get to work at a tech company., yeah. get paid., take any role that will make your life more comfortable for the hunt but don't forget the hunt.,

Hey Leon should I only fill out the talent base form if I have finished huntober. Also thank you for reminding me the other day to be kind to myself., nah, fill it out wherever you're at., Leon needs info to adjust things., make sure to give view permission for all the links you submit, he hasn't been able to try and match people because he can't see their stuff,

bombed the role, reached back out, and got a second chance. is the expectation higher now, no, it's usual that companies extend the chance if the role isn't filled., companies understand people have bad interviews sometimes. don't expect more unless they verbalize it., a lot of companies have a default reapply after 6 months and know that the hiring process is broken.,

author: Ashton (class 10)
title: ashtonclass10/2024-05-14
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

is cohort 3 going to take place in May or are there plans to postpone it for some other date? don't delay, start today. don't put off becoming a software engineer if you want to be a software engineer. when a start date is made for cohort 3, it will be abundantly clear it's happening. there's no way you'd miss it.

i don't have a portfolio since i'm in class 8. how can i join the talent base? don't worry about the talent base right now, it doesn't have any practical value for you since you haven't finished the cohort. having people fill it out was to try and figure out what people need right now it's more for people who are finished/close to finished.

When’s the deadline for 100devs talent base? there's no deadline because it's not a fully fledged thing yet even though some people have gotten interviews from it.

My 1st interview & 2 qs that caught me off guard 3 words ur cowrkrs would use to describe u & 3 words ur friends &family wuld use to describe u how do I answer? those are silly interview questions but make sure to be doing your banki questions because keywords you could use come from Banki. don't snitch on yourself/say something negative about yourself when answering questions like this, interviews are a game and there are ways to play it well, not a time for blunt honesty. there's no humbleness in the HUNT! you want the company to know you're awesome.

As a guy, how can I make sure when I invite someone to a coffee chat, it's an extra safe space? I want to only make friends and absolutely respect boundaries. if this is a worry you have, you're probably going to do okay. keep all professional commuication; keep on Linked In/Twitter, don't ask for personal phone numbers to call or text. if you don't know how to be professional, don't do coffee chats with strangers. do a practice coffee chat with someone you know(coworker/friend) and get feedback. don't comment on physical attributes. you don't need to talk about someone's clothes, hairstyle, or body in any way. if you do mess up, apologize. it's pretty easy to tell when you've messed up and you should recognize it, apologize, and make sure to not do it again moving forward.

I work in a small startup and my boss is pressuring me about task deliveries even though I'm on time with them I even do design besides coding because no UI dev. startups have a habit of overworking people for little compensation a lot of start-up bosses are folx that have never been bosses. they might not know how to manage or treat employees. keep track of your own tasks and show them the body of work, show them you're doing more work and exceeding expectations share your wins with your team/your boss, they might not notice otherwise. know when to set and hold your boundaries. a lot of startup founders want to find people they can trick into doing a bunch of work for little pay. PLAN YOUR DAY AND HAVE WELL DEFINED GOALS. Ask in an email what they want to be accomplished and when, so if they try to move the goalpost later, you can point that out. be aware of how "fragile" your boss is when it comes to their management style.

How to answer the question what is some critical feedback you've gotten recently? This got me tripped up during an interview there's a reason Leon has us do a freelancing project. It lets you get feedback that you can use to answer. if you can't work for whatever reason, you can use volunteer work to get feedback. when answering a question like this, explain what the issue was that you got feedback on, what action(s) you took to resolve it, and the result.

My local area hires a lot of php and laravel should learn these? yes. you've learned how to learn through the course, start learning and make a project.

I was data analyst in Africa (1.5 YOE), since moving to Canada couldn't get any job for a year. Do you think I can have more chances in SWE ? Also, any advice? Leon doesn't know the specifics of getting a job w/ Canada and a visa issues if any. build a list of global remote hiring companies see what you can improve on before switching and think of what's best for your long-term

Haven't got an interview all month after getting many last month. Feeling down and letting doubts getting into my head. Thoughts? hiring is cyclical, it'll ebb and flow. don't get caught up in the pattern, be consistent with what you're doing to get hired REMOVE THE EMOTION, IT'S ONE OF 60 INTERVIEWS don't get excited about getting an interview so often, the first one is super exciting because it's the first but getting so hyped up on every single one will make the doubt feel worse don't stop doing the things that got you interviews. some people start off trying to get interviews strong but after a while stop going at it so consistantly what time of year do companies hire the most? January, March, May, November, and October are usually pretty booming but companies hire all year I interviewed and got an offer but I need to stay working with them for 2 years, normal? Recruiter said not to worry about that it's normal practice no. there's a rise in these companies that convince you that you're less than others and that you "need to be trained before becoming full-time". And if you leave before the two years, you'll have to pay them for the "training" they provided. might be illegal or legal depending on where you live.

The company I'm interviewing for told me there is no hierarchal structure and they aren't big on role progression is that a red flag? it depends on the company and depends on the size. some places are great but some can be lord of the flies-esque sometimes it is sort of a stinky smell so make sure to check the company out. check Glassdoor for reviews, check Reddit, and any other place you can think of. if it's a good offer take it, rather get paid for 3 months and quit than not get paid for 3 months remember what they say during the interview but evaluate it later

How long is too long to spend on 100Hours project? it depends, some 100Hours projects turn into businesses but be careful not to only build on the 100hrs project, work on getting employed

salary not keeping up with the cost of living. like what I do, who I do it with but it's becoming less affordable. been getting back into the hunt. staffing agency pipelined to a company that I like. But once I said didn't want a temp job, the recruiter's tone changed immediately. Positions are only 3 months long according to the recruiter. am i wrong for wanting more? how to proceed in the hunt in the age of AI? it's the recruiter's job to make the most money for them and the company, not you staffing agencies don't always have your best interests in mind and the recruiter has an economic interest in you getting a lower salary for them to get a larger cut. in general, don't take the word of ONE person as fact, people are incentivized to make you feel a certain way Use AI as a tool to: help you tweak cover letters, resumes, and messages, and find companies the practicality of AI is up to you always do the last pass yourself, AI can generate some WILD stuff. watch the classes on speedrunning you can connect GPT to Google Sheets and do prompts in the Google Sheets and Leon's been meaning to do a video on that now that GPTs are more accessible a kind of side conversation that was had was about how great libraires are. in a lot of places, you can get cards for free and it lets you get access to books, magazines, music, movies, etc. some services like Libby and Hoopla let you get access to things remotely even if you don't live an area where you have a library near you

author: Ashton (class 10)
title: ashtonclass10/2024-05-18
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

I’ve made it to the final stage. It’s a cultural INT with the VP of tech and head of product. Already had a behavioural INT,what’s the difference between the 2? depends on the size of the company, especially if it's a small one the VP might want to sign off on the new people coming on always ask what you're walking into is there anything about the interview that you can find online? check sites like glassdoor know about the company, values of the company, missions, etc, so you can talk about how you uphold them the hardest interview of leon's career was a cultural interview with the CEO that went completely technical How do you handle employment gaps on your resume and discuss them in interviews (Returning to school, young children, etc.)? freelancing! you were freelancing the freelancing project you are asked to do will back that up and give you clients that they can talk to had a coffee chat, but didn’t quite hit it off with the other person. i feel awkward asking for anything else, let alone a referral. how to move forward? there is no HUMBLENESS IN THE HUNT can't lose a job you don't have, always make the ask what is pride? shoot your shot (for the job) sometimes the interaction is only awkward for you, assume the best you're your own worst critic sometimes, your brain is good at being mean to you for people in catchup crew - if let's say you start cohort 3 in couple months, and catchup ain't done, should they redo the full live cohort ? or is it okay? make that decision when it happens if you're deep in cohort 2, you might pick up a few more things but the content will largely be similar is it true that we are lying by saying that 100devs was an employer? ive seen this posted around discord it depends on what you say you did training program tied to an agency is the truth the threshold people have for resumes is made up show the freelancing you did because of it, that's undeniable work that you did you can't please everybody; some interview folx might not agree but one of 60, keep going What’s the best way to increase dms to a coffee chat? I customize each message but seem to have such a low response rate even just for a coffee chat. Thanks! what's a low response rate? think in the hundreds depends on the type of message and type of area(Twitter, LinkedIn, Instagram, email) keep reaching out, and think about what type of message would YOU open and respond to. always snowball, "Who should I talk to next?" Im the person who got the opportunity to teach esl in korea & I was wondering how to network in a country where my visa only allows me to teach? is it worth it? you have to be super careful, this is Korean labor law, talk to someone who knows reach out to fellow expats working in tech don't listen to people on the internet talking out of their butt about legal issues Friends co is hiring 4 DevOp/Cloud Engineer position. Asked me to apply & send resume so he can forward 2 HR. Do I ask to lmk if a SWE opens or apply to this? depends have honest convo if they're an actual friend if not, go the route they say(apply and send in your resume) and add on that you'd like to know about SWE positions too if they pop up How do you never eat alone in a remote position? the same way you do in person, eat awkwardly in front of your coworkers ask to hangout over lunch, or let people know you're available to talk during lunch be the person to set up events! gatheround was a site used early on in the cohort I have a company that wants proof that I worked at 100devs through pay stubs what do I do. I have volunteer experience, do I just tell them about it if you completed Huntober, ask via ModMail for the mods to verify your time how to i reach out to busy people who said they'd get back to me about coffee chats but haven't yet, what kind of message can I send them be persistent! you don't know what people's lives are like, they could of had something crazy going on, just follow up Do we need a job to play csgo? no Hey Leon, Is it okay to use the same cv bob template when applying to the next job or is it for first job only ? the CV Bob template is more geared toward first job as SWE while the template itself is fine, some people switch to the Harvard CV after How and what do we ask about an interview's content - for example, who is interviewing me, is it technical, and if so what kind of technical questions? that's exactly the way you ask just make it more formal and interesting sounding! How many months of work experience does it count as? If I started 100devs 2 years ago and only made money a few months ago? Is that a few months or 2 years? up to you, that's the beauty of self-paced learning some people only count the time they were most recently actively working on the cohort Whats going on with the CS:Go server? I saw mention of it on your twitter; can we make this a thing and get to work (at the office 😉)? Leon has it running but hasn't launched it there's a planned week of events when maintainers launch and it might be fun to hangout in the evening there 17.Im at 6kyu in codewars, and still my code is mostly brute forced. Am I lacking in my basics? no 6kyu is the time to switch to LeetCode if you're brute-forcing be analytical of your solutions and reinforce your patterns use the 20 min rule before analyzing the solution and adding the pattern to Anki feeling competent and confident helps a LOT and that's what code challenges should do know your strengths and weaknesses but don't take it to heart! it's a game! it should be fun side conversation: this is a pretty great video to watch if you have a Mac, it brought a lot of settings Leon didn't know about to his attention, especially a break timer called Time Out that has you take small 10 second breaks by greying out your screen. Perfect time to take a break from typing. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GK7zLYAXdDs take longer breaks are important too ! stretch! move a bit! take a quick walk! YouTube Syntax Set up a Mac in 2024 for Power Users and Developers Image

author: Ashton (class 10)
title: ashtonclass10/2024-05-21
html:
Huddle Notes May 21, 2024 by HEMAD (Class 24)

​

I just began the #100Devs course, not sure if this is addressed later in the course, but how do I know what is worthy of being added to Anki?

Do as much as you can, you’re gonna write trash cards

Needs to be your deck, part of the learning is making your deck

You'll gradually weed out ones that don’t work

What are some important questions to ask when you receive a job offer?

Base salary

What does bonus structure

Yearly review

What's the team like

Ask A LOT of questions, anything is fair game

Whatever impacts you the most

*a lot are in the bank to go over

Alexandria asks: good news first client, her mother. She wants a landing page and email list. She wants to know when negotiating with family

Family and friends pay double. Its usually a lot more work and treat it like ANY other client/job.

Ballpark budget

Proposal

EVERYTHING written down and agreed upon up front, if they don't agree don't take the job

Always make sure things are clear.

Little_data asks: what is Leon’s perspective? What were some more difficult engineering problems?

People. Its always people. [he pivoted to make sure this huddle is more about ‘the hunt’ questions, offered answering other questions like this via modmail]

​

Legend (ming) 100 hour project is working but the backend is really messy. What should he be focusing on right now?

making it less messy, also doing things to increase chances for multiple, many jobs/opportunities. Polish is for recruiters that don't know any better. You could have garbage code, but as long as you could talk about why it's garbage, that would be okay.

​

A recruiter reached out for a job but have been changing the date of the interview many times. Seems like they dont have organization. Should I interview there?

Yes always interview

​

how easy is it to find a fully remote role (UK/EU based) as a jr dev?Should I give up on it?It's the only way I can manage to work right now due to health stuff

its difficult, its possible but hard. Other 100devs folks have done it, but its A LOT of work

​

Is it important to not quit your current job until you find a new one as a software dev. Or should you take a risk and go all in ?

never quit if you can avoid it until you secure a swe job. When you are secure and stable and have money coming in and not worried about food on the table, guess what studyings a bit easier, going into interviews is…never quit your job until your new one

​

I have worked in the restaurant business for the past 20 years. I was just wondering what a typical day is like for a software engineer? Do you ever work late?

depends on who you are. Some colleagues RUN at 5 o’clock. Some never work late/weekend while others don’t seem to mind. Millions of swe’s, culture is different at different jobs. Find what you want. Is it better or worse hours? Typically way better, you tend to have more control over hours and time worked.

​

Is it productive to have a project before looking for coffee chat? I find that without a thing or project busy people refuse because it is poor use of time.

Dont delay start today. Often engineers like being supportive to those up and coming.

​

Can you remind us how we should respond to recruiters who reach out to us first but we don't have exp with the language they're hiring for ?

Depends on where you're at. Cant lose a job you don't have. If they're reaching out, who am i to not interview. Interview practice is interview practice, at least get that behavioral practice in. This is different if you already have a job, happily employed. At that point, may have a canned response involving absurd requests.

​

Should i have a camera for virtual coffee chat?

prefer camera on, even with first one. Leans toward respectful but wouldn’t think bad if not.

​

Is it advisable to ask in a coffee chat for them to suggest 3-5 or more people for me to network with?

just ask who else can I connect with. Every once and awhile had a coffee chat with a suit with manufactured questions. Don't mind that but not gonna be friends. Point is to make friends. If they come in treating it like an interview, that's when it should end. Avoid that ‘3-5 friends…’ sounds too transactional.

​

when you say get a client are we building websites for these clients? sorry I just started 100devs today 😅

YES. while completing 100devs, be tasked with getting a paid client. Helps when applying for opportunities.

Hey Leon, Are You doing any Resume review anytime soon ?

Not really, might do it again. ask on discord

​

I was talking to someone about 100devs & she was able to rec some1 to get in touch with &have a cofffee chat with and she is going to help me get an internship

Great that's why we network! Letsgo!

​

Should we give up on remote positions to be more competitive? Like focusing on hybrid and on-site positions?

Depending on the privilege of time dictates what job to go after. Prob go after the local job market first, then remote. Being local is competitive advantage

​

Started to prep the technical questions in the bank. The answers are easy to find but fully understanding the concepts is a tough one. Can you provide any tips?

Do the work. Put in time to learn the answers. Don't memorize as facts, they are guiding points for knowledge to do well in an interview. REal work is understanding the answers. Get WAY more at answering them on your own. Point is to understand them, ask on discord. Attend banki brunch.

​

should we cold call small companies for jobs?

Way more impactful things to do with your time. You can if you want.

​

Have you dealt with hard of hearing students/co-workers/clients? Any tips for people with this disability working at a SWE company? And maybe also for the hunt?

when you need accommodation, ask late in interview. Its about working with the company to get what you need to do the job. Specifically hard of hearing, try reddit communities or here on discord

​

I thought you recommended not working with friends as clients (due to scope creep).. were you saying you'd recommend it as a 1st client to allow leniency?

Yeah. Don't think those are contradictory. Friends and family, charge double and have a VERY clear scope. Charging double could be time (not just money), too. Take longer to do the project, if needed.

​

should we let them know about our ADHD if it helps us get extra time etc?

Ask for it at the latest point in interview process. Asking before even a phone screen is bad idea.

​

How do you suggest pitching ongoing maintenance to a freelance client for a website I've been working on for way too long (they keep requesting edits)?

Stop doing work for free! Create a boundary.

​

Is using age an asset in networking like college graduate?

i think so, senior folks like helping younger. Although, age doesn't matter too much in networking

​

What do you think of all the bootcamps that are shutting down?

Good? Haha you can learn this stuff for free. A lot of these boot camps were vc funded large corporations

​

A company that interviews for react developer then when sign up they expect you to use next. Js, twalind. Css, Redux and Typescript in 3 days, is it worth it?

Yeah, if you have that skillset, right? Ramp of 3 days is wild, maybe misreading, but thats a pretty common skillset

​

I did most of an electrical engineering degree, stopping when Covid started. How do I frame that as a positive thing? Low level, but conveys my tech background

yeah, there you go, put it on resume

​

Ats score matter? [note taker here…he deleted the question just as I tried to copy it down]

Yeah, should be 100.

​

We spend a lot of time getting rid of the bootcamp smell but if an employer googles 100devs the first thing they see on the website is "bootcamp". Why?

The first thing doesn't matter. you networked your way in, talking to real human. The second [Google] result mentions our hiring, thats a good thing.

​

I'm going to start a bank based tech internship but I would also like to start 100dev course. Is this advisable since I'll be learning different things?

No. please focus on internship first, deep not wide once stable, maybe ome 100devs. Don't do both and def not off the jump

​

author: Ashton (class 10)
title: ashtonclass10/2025-10-16
html:
from Ashton (Class 10)

Leon is making a Bob the resume builder AI based on what is most effective. No word on a release date. People in the hiring process: sourcer then recruiter, then inbound sourcer and/or recurring coordinator. Hiring manager is a part of the last step, like say at a large company when they have it narrowed down to 5 individuals. Hiring manager has the most pull but sources and recruiters are who are most visible. **AT NO POINT IS AI OR A ROBOT KNOCKING YOU OUT** Say a company receives 250 resumes submitted. All 250 go into an applicant tracking system queue. After they’re screened that number dwindles down to 200, and they receive 5-20 seconds from recruiters to determine whether to invest more time and read more. Then the number lowers to 30, who are the ones given more time. This leaves 20 who go through a technical screening test, which can be a technical phone screen or take home coding challenge. Then the number narrows down to 5: these are given several onsite interviews like coding, systems design, meeting the hiring manager. All of this ultimately leads to a single offer for one person. Leon says resumes should fall in an F pattern because that is how people read. Left to right, downwards. Make sure the F quickly and easily shows your name, your current role, and your highest level of education. Another formatting tip: XYZ. Accomplished X as measured by Y by doing Z. Explaining how is SO important. How to stand out without experience: “Have good communication skills. Have a past you’ve woven into your narrative. Be able to communicate technical concepts in a digestible vernacular. Show excitement for the position. Follow up. Be able to show a history of consistency.” List software engineering role on resume first. Have a mailing address where they are hiring if they’re not in your state. Avoid “learning and aspiring”. Technical, leadership, and impact are the key values. Language matters when a human reads it. Help / supported VS Led / Launched/ Which sounds better? Which supports leadership and impact? OK: Won second place in hackathon. Better: Won second place out of 50 teams in hackathon. Best: Won second place out of 50 teams in hackathon at NJ tech by working with two colleagues to develop an app that syncs mobile calendars. Never going to have call work and ask if you truly led versus helped. But you need to have the proof that you’ve done all of this. Here during the huddle Leon showed a two column resume which I believe he posted the link to in the follow-along-materials thread or the newsletter. Here is my screenshot: He said he thinks it’s worthwhile to use a summary for switching careers and you don’t have a ton of evidence to show why you’re there. He also said that this resume and the other he shows aren’t meant to be perfect, objective ways to do things. Different things will work for different people and not every recruiter will like the same thing either. FANG and big companies wouldn’t appreciate the two column format as shown above, but a medium sized company is more likely to. Also? More bullet points does NOT mean more impact. 3-4 is okay! Didn’t mention any opposition to 2. Projects section may be too much in first 6 seconds, may have a smell. But it’s not an absolute no-no. Jose Silva mentioned he thought projects are the same as student learning. Don’t call it one if you can help it, instead refer to it as a tool that does X. Try to make it seem like work. IG Hunt different suggested “stealth start up”. Lead and directed are Leon approved words but strategy is not, because it does not give the how, and is especially lacking if it doesn’t relate to software engineering. Volunteering is always good to list, make sure you still stick with strong wording. Putting the skills first can he helpful, helps the right keyword(s) be found and can help you move to the next stage, figure out what’s most in demand for your market or area to add to your list of skills. Leon recommends going on hiring cafe to see what’s being asked for in your area. On a resume someone listed their 100 hours project under their experience, which is simply titled Experience, excluding an explicit mention of jobs. It mentions it being open source, over 100 hours put in, with a link to it via github. This is Leon approved, because it IS an experience and would help with the six second pass without calling it a project, which Leon said smells bootcampy. Just have a title for it that aligns with keywords, lean into keyword recognition. Random but PDF is the recommended format for resumes, not docx. The recommended way to name your resume is to have it be firstName-lastName-resume2025 to make it easier for the recruiter to find. NEVER misrepresent that you have a degree, but you don’t have to say what kind. Max players enroll in a local community college for a few hundred bucks to show they have a graduation date. “The team can be of one too, only to tell them it was that way if they asked. -tehuberjohn “You always to position yourself as THE dev.” -Brynn “So many people I personally know have gotten great SWE roles in the past two months. Do you know what they all had in common? They had a coherent story, they practiced their behavioral questions, and when it came to tech? They used PREP or something like it.” -Sam aka Leader of the Pizza Gang

author: Ashton (class 10)
title: ashtonclass10/2025-10-17
html:
from Ashton (Class 10)

As a kid Marcia was really into Excel, had dial up internet but could only play with stuff in Microsoft Office due to the poor quality of her internet. She was motivated by the burn out of being a farmer, specifically a plant scientist. She had to work from 5am-11pm! She used to listen to Leon while she was on the tractor doing farm work. While she did finished 100devs in 4 and a half months she did burn out and get sick, she paid the price and couldn’t get out of bed for a week. “If you don’t rest your creativity is going to take a restraining order out on you.” She couldn’t even open VScode until like class 10, couldn’t get it to work. She used a 15 year old Macbook, even Leon worked on a Chromebook for 2 years. She initially used pen and paper, would try to predict what Leon would do next using active recall. Re: clients She stayed local! She lived in Amish country and built a website for a dairy farmer, which is an underserved market. She gorged Python for an interview, had no experience in it and had two days to learn as much as she could, as it was a requirement for the interview. The guy who interviewed her basically gave her the answer for the tech question, really liked her communication style, how she was keen to fix issues when given feedback. She reached out to managers and hiring managers directly using a script she tailored that was consistently successful. She ended up being remote forever at her job despite everyone else getting called back to the office, getting a 50% raise and doubling her bonus ALL because she was looking at another position and her company really wanted to keep her. She didn’t even ask for all of that! The second company couldn’t compete with the benefits of the one she was currently with and asked if she worked freelance and now she works on projects for them that way. Re: networking She goes to auctions and country clubs, sometimes pretending she doesn’t know how to golf and scouts out for people with expensive trucks and horses to connect with. “Helps having a farmer background when dealing with the Amish. So essentially it’s finding your niche.” -Matthias (She told us she’s a third generation farmer) “Hi I’m Bob Vance, Vance Engineering.” How she introduced herself. “I’m Marcia, I make apps and websites.” Always keeps business cards on her. She attributes her boldness and bravery especially in social situations to working sales in college. Leon and her agree that sales is something everyone should do at least once in their life alongside customer service. Her mindset in sales and networking which changed the game for her inside and out: “just make a friend.” It helps to get to know their first name, find out about things that matter to them, and are big parts of their life. Can be used for networking in person or say interacting with people in LinkedIn. Also? Her confidence is fake, “it’s so fake, it’s all fake”. If Jake and Brad, random dudes online can be 100% wrong and say things with complete confidence, she can too. (And so can you!) While she knows it won’t work for everyone especially international folks she used Nextdoor, Facebook, and LinkedIn for freelancing. FB especially worked for her because it’s one of the only things Amish and farm people are familiar with using. When she negotiates pay she pretends she isn’t herself but Mark, Marcia’s agent whose only goal is to get more money for Marcia. Pretending she’s a man and someone else helps her be gutsier. She also completely encourages us to copy her negotiation script and use it freely. One of her particularly popular apps is Bunindex, an app for rabbit breeders. She got pre-paid signed up users, was well received by Amish people who secretly keep phones. Was skeptical about it being a good idea versus her 100hours project, which was for horse riding warm ups. She called it “crappy”. General advice from her: “Don’t be afraid to try stuff.” Also don’t freeze up if you don’t know something, it helps no one. Also? She manages to do all the things she does by keeping a strict schedule: she freelances, has her dev job, AND teaches at a community college (somehow). LINKS: Marcia’s youtube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aKeJcMf5SBA What Marcia used to learn Python over the weekend for an interview, and was the first step to Python being her dominant coding language today: https://www.py4e.com/ Marcia’s templates/scripts for networking, negotiating, and salary negotiation: https://medium.com/@marciacripps/steal-my-job-search-scripts-proven-templates-for-networkin g-resignation-and-salary-negotiation-8e98aa7c21e0 Note: This has successfully worked for her with hiring managers and on LinkedIn.

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2024-06-05
html:
The following notes come from HEMAD, thank you again for stepping up.

I'm having coffee chats with people that the company is hiring but by the time I meet with them the company is no longer taking applications. Any tips?

It's still ok to build those relationships and they'll have you in mind next time hiring. If it's significant, the local environment may just move quickly. No shame in applying while setting up coffee chats.

Is The Bank still a good resource to use for developers with one year of experience who are looking to switch jobs?

Probably even better for you, honestly. You may be expected to know a lot of those questions AND you'll have better answers for behavioral questions. 3-5 years revisit questions as you'll have that experience.

Finally getting in touch & replies with engineering managers/leads on LinkedIn . What's a good resource to generate good questions to ask them and build rapport

-On the Bank, six questions you should be asking during interviews, that's a good place to start.

-If beginning, build rapport, make friends, and get them to talk about themselves, their lives, and good things about their company.

-Read how to win friends and influence people

Don't come with a preset list of questions just reading through. Be natural.,

How do I get better at git/github? I am struggling

Quantify what you're struggling with, and ask for help here on Discord. The cmd line can be overwhelming, give yourself time to figure it out. Initially use the same six commands over and over then just google it.

High school graduate is doing 100devs and WGU, studies just a couple of hours, and spends the rest of day wasting time and on the phone, advice pls

Oh boy.

-is it a problem? Are you able to do the amount of study you want to do? If you're able to do both, that's a lot of time. very few people can study 12 hours a day. Probably not a good thing for most people. Focused, reinforced learning time is more impactful. The rest of the day, do what you want to do!

-seek help if you can't get the things done that you want to get done.

-look into setting the phone to grayscale, you’ll not want to use it

CAN I / SHOULD I have a selfie/headshot on my personal website? Are employers allowed to see what I look like in that capacity before hiring me?

People are going to look you up, like on Linked In. What does your local market dictate? do that. Do what you are comfortable with, some don't have the safety to put headshots online.

How do you know when you know enough to be employed at a regular 9-5 swe role? It's hard to know when you know enough. Is it API and backend? Is it frameworks?

If you've done 100devs you've done enough. It comes down to showing you can do it. Your portfolio of work, paid work, etc.

Just finished my portfolio, updating my Linkedin. How do I list 100 devs on my Linkedin & resume? As Work or Education?

It's up to you. This is a training program, some put it as education or as work. Did you get a paid client? If you got paid because of 100devs, Leon considers that work. It should be under work.

best 4 projects to get me hired? im fullstack dev

No such thing as ‘best 4 projects’. Mentions 100hours project that solves a need, that's all you need. Go into interviews with a real problem that you solved, the time you put into it, challenges you overcame.

Do I need to get paid to add to my resume or is it okay if I do free things for my friends (can't get paid outside of my normal work due to visa stuff)

Everyone's situation is different, even if free, should have a contract in place as you open yourself up to liabilities. Demand the BEST reference because work is free. Unsure if that works for a visa, not an employment lawyer.

-you want to get to a point where someone vouches for you as a swe

I'm driving 90 minutes each time for meetups and want to try to find more opportunities through virtual. Any advice for that that's been updated since cohort 02?

Use meetup.com, twitter, a lot are still virtual. Communities are big, too

It’s hard trying to set up a daily schedule m? Any help?

-Don't go into your days like an accident. At a minimum, ask what ONE THING are you going to do tomorrow. Stop avoiding it, ask the question. Answer it.

-once you do the one thing, for a week or two, you can believe you can do two, then three, etc.

-if you have real issues with your daily schedule, you're not gonna magically study 12 hours a day. Start with one thing and do it.

I want to start a local 100Devs "chapter" - like getting local POC together in my segregated city to go through material. Any advice on how to start/what to do?

It's not something we advocate. Don't need to be local, just go here on Discord and get people together.

Can we get a reference from you?

If you do Huntober, you get a letter from Leon

Reading a 100Devs members (scars) guide to networking. Step 1 is to remove linkedin connections that do not serve you. should i just remove those that never reply?

I don't know any data behind that. I don't know if that harms/helps. Need to see data on it but don't think it matters, should focus on other things LIKE NETWORKING.

How do i know when I know enough to not smell?

If you've completed 100devs, crafted a story, and gone through interviews. Have to understand there are people getting jobs with half the skill and competence but have the vibes and confidence.

-show them you have value

Some students get jobs in 4-5 months or finish the cohort less than 9 months. What are they doing that I am not?

-Sacrificing mental/physical health, may have the privilege of more time. This is something that you will do for the rest of life.

-Normal pathway is HS, then four years grad, then internship. Normal process is FIVE years. So, you're worried about taking 5 months..stop. If it takes you less than five years, you have WON. no debt, built more (freelancing), stop trying to shave off three months

-comparison is the thief of joy

I know age doesn’t matter but honestly does it really not? How old is too old to be entry level engineer

I have no evidence of older folks doing worse. The folks that are older typically do better, they usually have other experiences that add to it. Yes, you may run into bad people that treat you poorly, but that's rare. Optimize your life for the thousands that bring positivity and fortune your way.

-stop being mean to yourself, bring positivity to yourself.

Work full time with exhausting toxic surroundings. After work is always tired. Any recommendations on how to put coding agenda into the work day?

Do your learning before work.

how can i stop stressing while coding?,everytime i code or theres an error i shake my leg and sweating and mad and so burnout

Its worth it to talk to a professional if you have a barrier that gets in the way of something you want to do with your life. Coding should be fun, its problem solving. Work with the community, lean on people. Use and connect on the discord!

how do we do huntober?

Just a list of req as you do the program

Is 2hrs a day enough to learn 100Devs?

Some, yes..some no. less time a day only limits the job

A company wants me to take over their site, a few things they want changed. Should I push to make a brand new website or work with what they have?

That's up to you and their budget. Leon is a fan of the new site where they can control it all, but they may not have that budget. Are you comfortable with doing all that?

How would you talk about experience gaps when you've not got a client yet but contributed to open source?

Get a client, the best way to explain gaps. Talk about open-source contributions then.

Met 2 people at my first meetup and used the followup/sheet, but noticed that neither of them are active on Twitter anymore. Is that still a valuable resource?

Yes, just maybe not for those two people

How do you respond to a website client that’s dodging the “what’s your budget” question, and throws out fiver and friend discount buzzwords?

I say no to way more clients than yes, use compassion, and point toward Fiver

At what class number do you feel its best to start freelancing during the cohort?

Class 15 or so

If you can work anywhere in the US, what city would you recommend we network in to maximize opportunity? SFO? I'm in Canada (dual citizen) but pay here is low.

It's all different. Anything that has a large tech economy that's not the valley.

My only real job has been as a housekeeper and I have been taking classes at community college. How do I craft a story based on this with zero tech experience?

You ran a small company that did housekeeping, and kept hitting tech problems so decided to expand your engineering skillset, talk about a project related to that

Freelancing has been rough, the local location everything handles things via WhatsApp and nobody seems interested, is it possible to freelance remote?

Yes, it is possible but 100% harder.

why is freelancing remote 100% harder in your opinion

Hard to build trust. Local is a real person, can meet in person.

Suggestion on using work time to study?

Depending on your job, don't do it without approval.

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2024-06-07
html:
notes by HEMAD

What is the best way to remain positive when you have been on the hunt for almost 2 years and still getting no offers? Just recover from burn out, any tips 4 it

If done in less than 5 years, absolutely crushing it, BUT if you are actively hinting, doing everything 100devs ask..gotta be analytical have people look at your behavior answers, technical, etc..there could be a ‘smell’ somewhere.-

-do banki brunch

-mock interviews

-do all of the above anyone months into the hunt process

​

I am really scared that I'll never become a software developer in this current market. Any tips on how to stay positive? Recently just recovered from burn out.

-Get off reddit, twitter. Life will improve.

- Misery loves company, as soon as people say “in this market” but we just heard of FIFTEEN ppl getting jobs.

-Don't listen to the folks not being successful, who hang with same people. And when probed, find out they’re not doing anything.

-There’s less “i sneezed and got a job opportunity”. Its hard now, HAVE to do good everything: narrative, 100hrs project, github, networking, etc to put yourself ahead.

-the things that we put in our brain are going to have the most lasting effects on us

​

what kind of story can one craft if they have never had a job and only did a hobby project for 8-yrs but no work experience.

-You my friend were freelancing

-possibly it was an unsuccessful business and you're transitioning.

-tie your narrative and your story into your 100hours project

​

around what time of the day is the prime time to reach out to recruiters?

Its not really worthwhile to worry about this, as long as its business hours you should be good (Mon-Thu) don't send on a Friday.

​

If a senior dev makes a mistake, is it better for the safety of my job to leave it alone and mind my business or is there a way to respectfully bring it up?

-be careful, take notes from how to win friends and influence people, admonish privately not publicly. Reach out directly to them first. Gotta know youre team, some may be sensitive.

​

How do we ask members of the community for coffee chats? 😊

Just ask, but network outside of community more, for hunt specifically. Look in local community. Starting off look here. Virtual coffee too, but eventually shift gears to focus on local tech economy.

​

When I send Hit List messages to Engineering Managers asking for a coffee chat, many respond by telling me just to click apply. How best to respond?

-you can tell them that you did but would still love to chat

-messages may come off too”i need a job”

​

Is there anything wrong with confirming that you're a self taught developer at the interview when they ask? If I do say yes do you think thats a bootcamp smell?

It's definitely a bootcamp smell, up to you what to say. Typically we say “community-taught”, training programs. Some co have biases, so be careful

​

I've been referred to a company, and I want to apply, but I'm currently on lesson 33, so I haven't gotten to node.js or the 100hr project yet. What should I do?

-Let them know you are not open to opportunities now but looking in six months or so

-this is very common esp sharing stuff/setting up online presence

-offer to circle back when more ready

​

Currently junior level roles are really competitive and keep landing mid-senior level interviews. Ideally junior is better, how do I know which one is best 4 me

Why would junior be better if you can land higher and you can handle it? You can always go down, harder to go up.

​

I will be attending renderATL next week. This will be my first conference and I am not sure what to expect/do. Do you have any advice? Will you be attending?

Leon will be speaking, attend it at 9am! Render sends out a guide, def read those.

-Going to make friends, listen to great talks. Don’t be afraid to talk to new people, have lunch with them(don't eat by yourself).

-Reach out online if you see people going. Go to afterparties!

-Might be a small 100devs meet-up.

-look into volunteering at cons

-take time to recharge!

​

On Tuesday's huddle you mentioned possibly having news on Cohort 3, is there any information you can provide?

-Fifteen jobs last month! Don't make an excuse, don't delay start today! In the amount of time you waited, you'd be one of the fifteen!

​

How do you overcome the fear of moving to another job and dealing with the new work environment, and meeting expectations?

-Make sure expectations are very clear. Have a meeting with mgr right away. Talk about 3, 6, 9 month plan written down, shared with mgr. Then GO GET it done

-as for environment, go slow..read How to Win Friends and Influence People

-never eat alone, join in after work culture

-take good notes, keep a dev journal. Note down wins! Keep a record.

-dont wait until end-of-year to go over accomplishments, go over often

​

Many companies apply unit testing for their projects. When they look at my projects without it, is that already a red flag? My 100 hours project doesn't have it

-No. be surprised how many co don't use unit testing.

-codewars, etc you've done basic testing.

​

Upcoming Amazon interview on Monday. I have been working on prep questions and not able to code answers correctly. I feel completely unprepared recommendations?

-Who cares cant lose a job you don't have, go into it rocking it!

-relax over the weekend.

-if it goes horribly wrong, reapply in six months.

-specifically, KNOW amazon’s processes..they are documented and easy to find.

​

If you’re coming to 100devs from a professional background (ie accountant, lawyer), how do you recommend including (or not including) that kind of experience?

-Do include it, bridge engineer to your past via 100 hours project.

​

– Laura came up to the stage –

-Currently has job opportunity came up. Has a call with CTO on monday. Looking for help on how to phrase work done in current job to look good to CTO. Its php/wordpress. Not very challenging, very simple front-end. She's been filling in a design role, but not what she wants to do. Says her sister got her the recommendation.

-Leon: don't overthink it. Its a vibe check. Think of good talking points of your job, try to “come across as normal”. Dont appear to be “sweating” the interview, be confident.

-this type of interview seems to be just a vibe check, they trust her sister to talk to Laura, so this is probably just that. Have a simple project to talk about your strengths.

-also talk to your sister again to get info on what to talk about, what to be prepared for. Take advantage of that privilege.

​

Do you have any recommendations for keeping structure in a gig based highly on call work environment?

-gig based: assuming, on demand work? That work is rough. Maybe depends on clients, on call schedule would be helpful. Find someone else that does similar work to pass work to them when get work outside of your schedule.

-keep 12 hour windows and referrals.

Mayanwolfe (Claire) added:

Not having set work hours and 24/7 on call is rough... been there in the past, never doing it again. It literally broke me, mentally

​

I am having a hard time coming with a 100 hours project idea even for struggles. Any tips?

-Pick something you hate or love. Or find a problem others have that you can fix. Ideally a problem you have to solve to add to your story.

-need to find that something to help bridge story and project.

​
