I am on a government support for unemployment however they just want me to get any job to survive. Should I just get any job whilst still looking for a Dev role?

Yes. always have money coming in! Survive and live to study another day!

​

I am a huge kpop fan would doing a 100devs project based on that be too childish?

-No, not childish but would it be professional? If worried, maybe it is not professional. BUT seen people build what they love to build, so build it…so if you have a interview with another fan..BAM you’re in.

-example: weed delivery program, maybe doesn't work for interview, switched it to a coffee app

​

is 2 full stack projects is enough? to apply?

One is enough! Ideally more, but really just one good project is enough. Talk about what you built and learned.

​

How do you stand out when you're against CS grads that have internship experience? They say internship has more significance over anything else than projects.

You have the same thing: freelancing experience. Also, most CS grads don't have that experience. Don't listen to online chatter, don't compare to min/max people that are rare. EVEN IF you are, you’re PAID freelance is comparable.

​

Does having a CS degree really make you stand out? My friend with one keeps saying I need some certs to derisk myself. Hence why I keep failing the technicals.

No, it does open some jobs to specific CS-req jobs. Has nothing to do with you failing technicals. Question that friend, careful that type of advice.

​

What are the essential parts when talking about your past experience and skill set? All I can talk about is my 100 hours projects and freelancing. That enough?

Thats a big part of it but what makes you unique? Why would they hire you over the ~100 candidates? Have something that sets you apart

​

What do you show when you have past work but you can only show your work and not the entire project? (WP Project)

-You talk about it, show as screenshot, don't need to link the entire project.

​

When talking pay, a recruiter said they're new to the role and are not sure what devs at this level make and they haven't told them the budget. What do I say?

Please talk to them and get back to me, thats it

​

Do we have any tea spills scheduled soon? Can we have some of the recent ppl who got jobs come back and share their good vibes & tips with us?

Hopefully soon

​

if I say I was freelancing for many years, wouldn't it be expected of me to apply for a senior position while I want an entry level job..how do I ask for entry?

Im interested in entry-level roles

​

My non-coding job that asked me to make a tool using Google Sheets for error reports, wrote some JS to automate parts of it. Can I leverage this for resume?

Yes, that should be on your resume, you did that

​

how to get loads of green boxes on git all my projects are set on private cause they are work related

You can include private on your heatmap. Turn on that setting

​

I'm in a data QC position, how do I convince my supervisor to let me have a set number of hours spent coding a way to automate my tasks?

Let them know you have those abilities. Ask to automate it, show your worth. Worthwhile to ask.

​

been doing 100devs for 1yr and 4 months. i am only on class 53 and i feel useless

Build something, that's all. You’re USEFUL. You’re putting in the work, asking questions, you’re doing more than the ones that dont get past class 1. You have skills and experience that others would love to have.

​

Why are there specific companies that only want candidate with a CS degree? Surely CS grads mainly study the theory and a build a few projects on their course.

Don't know how to hire, just a filter.

​

My full-time job pays peanuts, but it’s something. It seems like waste of time, and not relevant to my goals. Suggestions for networking into a better role?

Yes network into a better role

​

Any tips for someone who is a returning citizen, as far as reaching out for chats, explaining it on a resume, ect?

Dont. you just don't have to. It probably wont come up. Don't snitch on yourself. Reach out to folks here.

​

What are the people getting placements projects like? i feel like my own everything is rubbish and i might be too shit to do this

Gotta focus on the things you accomplished in the short amount of time you’ve been doing this!

​

Why we choose Codewars over Leetcode?

Leet kinda silly until you get better. Brute Forcing is better

​

Any tips for having a consistent study schedule?

Start super small. One victory a day, work up to more.

​

hobby project = blog but did have sponsorship a few times.

Thats a biz, not a hobby if got paid

​

two parter - so that job posting i have an interview with on monday. but it seems like it is 3-4 jobs in one. what are you thoughts on this and I am countering!

Doesn't matter, just get the offer!

​

Do you have to submit all HW to complete the cohort 2?

Yes, need it to be eligible for huntober..IF you want it

​

I feel like i remember either seeing/hearing a remark from Leon a while back about looking at alternatives to X for class sign ins- is this still a possibility?

Not yet. When cohort 3, may be alt then.

​

I feel like i remember either seeing/hearing a remark from Leon a while back about looking at alternatives to X for class sign ins- is this still a possibility?

Dont pay money to learn

​

is it okay to build stuff out side of what 100devs teaches like how 100devs teaches js can you make something in another language like php for the 100hrs project

Don't feel constrained to stick only with 100devs. Just complete a project then go to the next one.

​

Hi Leon almost all the jobs in my area require a CS degree If I'm unable to network this job do I hit apply anyway and what if recruiter asks me if I have one?

Apply anyway. If a real hurtle, enroll in community college. And say you're on the way to cs deg

​

got dc, wanted to know how do I ask for entry level job if I say I have many years of freelancing, when they would be expecting me to apply for senior position

Not abnormal to want to go to an entry level role.

​

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2024-06-12
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

Extra Info: Leon is speaking at Render ATL this Friday at 9am. If you're attending, check out the thread here ?unknown !

Next week will have Huddles like normal but also smaller community sessions where the focus will be getting things nailed down for 100devs. There will be a teaspill lead by Mayanwolfe on the 21st.

[02:03]sheep:

At 54 years old and switching careers, do I realistically have a good chance of getting a coding job due to my age and lack of experience?

• yes, of course. will be harder, and age bias will be a thing. but you'll find a bunch of companies don't care.

• use your age to your advantage! So many skills can be transferred to tech. so many people have led the way already and 400+ people want to see you win!

• craft your story/narrative! show your value. don't let your age be an excuse, you're awesome, tell the world. use your hitlist and network!

[02:05]sheep:

2.After what happened to Twitter/X since cohort 2 , would you still recommend it as strongly for networking? Or do you have an alternate preferred platform?

• hustle everywhere you can! some people don't have the privilege to change platforms and Twitter/X can still be a strong place for networking. you can't be too picky when it comes to what will help you put food on the table

• if you don't want to use it, don't. LinkedIn, Instagram, etc are all options, just find out where folx in your local area are connecting.

[02:07]sheep:

I am worried that 10 years down the line all of this will be for nothing because of AI. And I have existential worry's about my future as well, Thoughts?

• there's a nonzero chance but SWE is a great job right now. If you listen to the doomers, you'll never know what's possible if you don't try.

• tech has big cycles/phases like any industry and many people saying that AI will take over aren't even SWEs. AI is pretty bad right now, you still have to go through verification on the code it does. And the worst case scenario, why not get paid well now and then deal with the "in 10 years" scenario in 10 years?

• not learning because the potential AI takeover is a really good excuse and a lot of people use it

[02:08]sheep:

How do you keep a coffee chat connection alive after the first chat without making it transactional?

• by remembering something about them that you can bring up later. people love to talk about themselves and have people bring up the things they like. don't be afraid to take notes in a Google Doc or set up Google alerts.

• don't be afraid to talk to people, sometimes talking with someone is transactional. and if you go too far, you can always apologize. don't overthink it.

[02:08]sheep:

5.Hey Leon! Can we still join the #100Devs linkedIn group if we don't have a job/are a student? (If that's not okay and we don't have a job, what should we do?)

• yeah, the only requirement is a photo and a title! And Leon plans to do more stuff on LinkedIn in the future.

[02:08]sheep:

6.How do folks manage in the catchup crew? I get that taking care of myself is important, but it feels like every minute could be better used by studying.

• work hard or recharge hard. The death of learning is when people exist in the in-between. kinda taking care of yourself and kinda learning doesn't work. focus or relax. Have a schedule, don't go into your day like an accident.

• people can rarely properly study for 12 hrs, most people do 2 hours a day. you're probably going to burn out quickly if you try to do more at first. after a while, try using body doubling, a silent study channel, or things like focusmate to increase your time. remember the focus and diffused mode.

[02:09]sheep:

7.If watching the classes aren’t enough to grasp a concept, is it ok to watchYouTube videos about it or read more documentations? For example javascript.info

• absolutely! use all your resources. sometimes when we introduce something in class, it's just the first pass. Watch the next class and see if you still don't understand and then check out more resources.

• sometimes, things are cognitively difficult (ie having to put in the actual work) vs you not understanding.

• CHECK OUT MAYANWOLFE! She has great YouTube videos going over much of the material

[02:09]sheep:

8.Will your talk at Render be streamed online for those that aren't attending and want to watch?

• some talks have been recorded before leon doesn't know if his talk will. leon will put up the slides and maybe record himself. it's not a learning talk, but more about about being a part of your community and giving back to your community.

[02:09]sheep:

9.I got hired, how do I make the most out of my first year on the job to learn and grow the most?

• make sure you talk to your manager about clear expecations about 3/6/9/12 month goals, have them written down, and leave a paper trail. keep regular communication about them and make a paper trail about any changes. so many people don't do this and worry about where they stand when it comes to performance reviews. This way you should know where you stand pretty easily.

• never eat alone. get to know your coworkers, team, managers then other teams, everyone should know who you are. hype people up and celebrate their wins! Check out the books: Never Eat Alone and How To Win Friends And Influence People.

• set the tone in the first 3 months for the next two years. be present, lean into asking questions, and document answers.

• document everything. wins, anything you've done, document it all. onboarding, setting up your dev environment, etc, and sharing this with new people or anyone else who asks.

[02:09]sheep:

10.Anything different you recommend we do during hunt compared to what you suggested during the 2nd cohort? AKA any new tactics?

• a lot of the advice is slow to change because it's more fundemental. the only things that change are how you do it, you might use more AI to help develop messages or your hitlist now.

[02:09]sheep:

11.Where do I find the video on coffee chats and networking?

• search through the yt channel for the networking vids

• planning on having more teaspills soon. The following Friday(the 21st) will be a teaspill Mayanwolfe leading. keep push notifications on!

[02:10]sheep:

12.You said if you can’t show your code from prev roles, talk about the project in a technical way. I worked on a big project how deep show I go? (8 sec rule?)

• as much as they'll let you. just ask them how deep they want you to go!

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2024-06-18
html:
notes by HEMAD

1.how was the conference? Any professional beans to spill with the 100devs community? ◦ so many of us there! photo in ?📝follow-along-materials ◦ people from 100devs two years ago showed up! ◦ cool to see how deep our professional network really is!

2.Advice on finding more clients? I have been strictly cold calling. Any out of the box ideas? ◦ cold calling works in certain markets ◦ start locally, show up. go to any small business in your area that looks like need help online presence. face to face works, you're more trustworthy. ◦ make sure you have solid website/online presence and examples, also a testimonial or reference ◦ maybe lawn signs? like political campaigns? so many ways to grow freelancing biz once you have everything in order ◦ do you have the ability to close? if not, work on that. ◦ do things that dont scale, use revenue from that to do the things that do scale 3.Advice to someone on the hunt for 8 months? I feel like everything except job hunting is a waste of time (ex: coding, freelancing, meetups, etc) Thoughts? ◦ once past three months range,time to be analytical of yourself, what can you improve, your gaps and work on fixing them. ◦ If you cant identity, work with someone that can help point these things out ◦ most common: not talking highly enough about self ◦ talk about yourself as a real professional, build yourself up, carry that energy into the hunt ◦ still need to code challenge, contribute to projects to keep skills fresh ◦ one example of alumni: conferences worked better than linkedin for them. Switch things up if you’re finding things you are doing aren't working ◦ REMEMBER: if you do it less than five years, you’re way up. You’re still doing a shortcut! from Mayanwolfe (Claire): It can be helpful to get a second option on your job hunting approach, because if you've been at it for a while you'll be more likely to beat yourself up rather than looking at things from an analytical perspective. From chat: I'm asking this as an introvert! How can I get connected to people? ◦ read THE book How To Win Friends and Influence People ◦ just start. We all suck at the beginning. ◦ bring a friend at first, join spaces that work for you to build to other spaces ◦ anxiety responses that get in the way seek professional help! ◦ networking doesn't have to be local meetup in person, twitter works, discord. Find them that works for you! -noone cares about your social missteps more than yourself!! - Mayanwolfe

4.Tips on making a website for a family member? (freelance) probably not going to charge them. ◦ wrong path! Family pays DOUBLE! Always. Family is always gonna cost you the most energy, time, etc. if they care they’ll want to support you ◦ if they don't, why should they get it free if they're not supporting you! ◦ worst client you ever have most likely will be someone close to you. ◦ if you don't charge that’s really fine, its up to you. BUT: USE A CONTRACT: it stipulates what happens when something goes wrong. Easy to point at and review when conflict happens. ◦ if you ignore everything else, at least USE A CONTRACT ◦ tips on making the site: quick on revisions. Don’t ignore your normal process and treat them just like any other client. Stick to contract to make thing smoother

5.Someone has a potential client that want me to work on the project for them. We have a meeting set up to discuss it, what to bring up doing our meeting? ◦ great, its still the same process. ‘What's your ballpark budget’ give them a proposal at that budget. Demand the same deposit, time paid, etc. review how to freelance class. Side note: don't be afraid to barter for goods! Trade services for your work 6.Sorry, this probably gets asked so much but tips to deal with burn-out on the hunt? I cant get over the feeling that i don't want to hitlist anymore. ◦ its okay to take a break with a defined start/end date. Get loved ones on board with your break and have them check in ◦ burn happens because those in your life may not be bought in on your doing this. They don't realize the seriousness, that you're trying to better yours and their lives. Get them bought in, make sure they know how hard you're working, that way they support, encourage, which helps with burnout ◦ another symptom of burnout: thinking of long term long goals that are out of your control. If your goal is to get the job at the end of this journey you WILL burn out. Do the small things first. Become a better developer each day. ◦ when you meet and finish these small daily goals, celebrate them and with those around you! ◦ focus on the process, you will reach the goal**

From chat: I also like to do reflections, what worked and didn't work. Leon: yes! Journal your journey! Celebrate the work you did do. 7.Thoughts about the overemployed movement? Should I try having 2 dev jobs at the same time? ◦ yeah why not? If you can handle it. As long as you're bringing value to the company and not doing anything unethical. 8.Are there folks that have landed a job remotely in a first world country being from Central/Latin America? Could you do a tea spill with them? ◦ yeah of course. A lot of first jobs from 100devs were there. Did some teal spills in the past Leon says tea spill on Friday will be led by Marciac95! Marcia came up to stage: ◦ was a farmer, found 100devs in four and a half months couldnt open vscode to getting hired as python back end dev! ◦ on friday 6pm est she’ll give more details on the process 9.Starting my own local meetup called Techtastrophe in a small city in Virginia. Any tips to make it big and provide value to attendees so they come back monthly? ◦ don't worry about the size, big can be death of a community, focus on the connections, build rapport with those that show ◦ ask those that show why they came. What they wanted to get out of the meetup. Provide it! ◦ as you scale, over time worry about the size

10.What should we do if the salary for software engineer is bad compared to first world countries? They want one person that does everything an IT department does. ◦ sometimes the local economy does not support the job that others may elsewhere. ◦ may go after global hiring, but know that it is ten times harder to get! ◦ if leon was in this situation, hed make it his life goal to know every single person in every global hiring firm. Its a lot of hard. Work.

11.My client is ghosting me on payment. I didn't get a deposit or contract because they're a family friend. Should I still use them as a reference? ◦ NO. They wont say anything positive about you if they did that! ◦ you want a client that is so happy that they used you, they’ll talk so highly of you. A person ghosting you wont talk highly of you! ◦ CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT. YOU MUST DO THIS NEXT TIME ◦ if payment is involved, ALWAYS get a deposit. Minimum 25%, then milestones. As you get that next payment, finish that portion of the work.

12.catchup crew here! Since there isn't a live cohort, how do we show that we've been actually going through the program? ◦ submit everything like normal. Check in on twitter, etc. use https://communitytaught.org/

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2025-10-16
html:
from choir241 (Richard)

How to build the best resume ● Communicates your experiences and advocates for you ● Some things Leon shares will make you feel uncomfortable, and you don’t have to it if you don’t wanna ● There are different resumes for people who can finesse, and people who can’t ○ People who can’t communicate and articulate certain points that aren’t necessarily a lie, but for things that aren’t explicitly stated ● People in the hiring process ○ Hiring manager that determines whether you get the job or not. They have the final say, they have the head count, and budget for who they can hire. ○ Recruiter (bigger companies a sourcer) - essentially the filter or mid person before the hiree’s information even meets the hiring manager. ○ Sourcer is the person DM’ng people usually, their job is usually to find talent to hire for what they’re looking for ■ Recruiter and sourcer will only spend so much time looking at your resume. Recruiter and sourcer will be the most visible, but the hiring manager has the final say. What does the process look like: ● 250 Resumes Submitted -> ○ 250 applicant tracking system Queue (ATS) -> ■ your application arrives in the ATS. It will progress through stages, until either a rejection or offer. ■ For ATS system, you will never have your resume rejected by AI. It’s legally not possible due to knockout questions, where if you answer a certain way, you will automatically be rejected, and due to the nature of the US laws regarding these questions, a human must view these applications and not AI. Examples of knockout questions are do you require visa or do you have a CS degree (depends on the company) ○ 200 resume screen -> ■ recruiters spend 5-20 seconds to determine whether to invest time in reading more. ■ You have to prove that you are worth spending time going further in the process and that you are the person they’re looking for this role and you’ll be able to perform at this role ○ 30 recruiter screen -> ■ - a phone call with the recruiter, confirming experience, motivation, touching on soft skills ○ 20 technical screen - > ■ a technical phone screen and/or a takehome challenge, focused on coding ○ 5 onsite -> ■ several onsite interviews like coding, hiring manager and systems design ○ 1 offer -> ■ if you get here, congrats! It’s been quite the journey. ● Hiring manager is looking for people to stay at the company long term, recruiter is looking for people that are qualified and safe (think about how the recruiter is qualified on how they do their job) ● Everything they see (see screenshot below) can influence the bias of what employers determine to pass/fail for hiring due to how many applicants they receive ● Current role should be a software engineer ● Location should be at a location where they’re hiring ● If your name could potentially cause bias, consider using acronyms ● In those 6 seconds, they’re looking for if you’re technical, if you have impact, and if you have leadership (think about the way you word things and how that positions you as an hiree) ● You can be hyper explicit, or you can let them dream a bit during a resume. For example, if you had to take a gap year for family reasons, you can place that on the resume explicitly to explain the gap year. Some recruiters will see that, understand the gap, and moves forward. Some recruiters just don’t care and just want to get their work done. For resumes that don’t explicitly share things, some recruiters will see the gap in a positive light, and others will see it in a negative light. ● Thinking about the difference between helped and supported vs led and launched ● Resume should follow the XYZ: accomplished X as measured by Y by doing Z ○ X: What you did/what was the impact you had ○ Y: How did you measure that impact ○ Z: How did you make that come true ● Examples: ○ Ok: Won second place in hackathon ○ Better: Won second place out of 50 teams in hackathon ○ Best: Won second place out of 50 teams in hackathon at NJ Tech by working with two colleagues to develop an app that synchronizes mobile calendars. (or leading a team of three) ● More examples of XYZ bullet points ● 2 bullet points are okay IF and ONLY if they are high impact ● 2 column resumes work really well for career changers, people new to software engineers and it really worked well for a long time ● Career summaries are typically only for career changers or seniors ● Recently career changers are not seen valued as highly if you’re not ready to get started to work right away ● Bigger companies (potentially mid sizes) two columns may not be the best option ● What does a Projects section make you think of? - potentially a student or a newer dev. Not that we should remove the section right away, but just a retrospective of what we should consider to keep and/or remove for us personally ● The X, Y, Z format is super important for experiences that are not technical ● The bottom should be saved for activities/volunteering they you may have done that you want to highlight, but should never smell “bootcampy” ● Hiringcafe website to find local companies and the technologies they’re looking for ● Make sure your job title experience matches the keyword for the job you’re applying for ie. if you’re applying for a software engineer job, your job title should say software engineer

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2025-10-20
html:
from choir241 (Richard)

● The goal is to get recommended so you have an easier time through the process ● Don’t make the take-home test your whole personality - you don’t want to overinvest, you wanna be in and out ● There’s always a potential reason to get rejected during a technical interview, and it’s just not worth the energy investment to only get rejected by the most niche reasons ● Whiteboard interviews aren’t as common, but recommend to practice a couple times just to be familiar with it ● Every time you practice interview or have an interview, always use the appropriate system to make sure you don’t walk in like an accident ● Try to look for the company's interview process when it’s a mid-size or higher company (smaller might be harder to find). You can find it through research or by simply asking. ● If you get blown off after asking what the interview will be like, ie. it’ll just be a technical, it’ll just be a behavior, make sure to ask for specifics and details with follow-up questions. Ie. cool, am I going to be expected to use x language? Should I be expected to walk through my code? Should I be expecting Leetcode hard/medium/easy questions? ● CAR - Cause Action Result ○ For all your behavioral questions and should be committed into memory and should have a lot of time invested into them. 3 sentences max for the beginning, but as you become more comfortable, you can add more if needed ○ There is no need to add in qualifiers, 100% all positive. There is no need to say anything negative to come off as more relatable. Think “why are you a genius” ○ Leon demonstrates asking a Banki question using CAR ○ You will talk more than you will memorize. The memorization is to give you a sense of comfort and make sure you cover all the important points. ● EUE - Explanation Use Example ○ Depending on the company you’re applying for, you might see yourself using this more ● Banki Questions ○ Will you be asked every question on this form? No. Will you be asked some questions that match exactly on the Banki form? Yes. The point is that if/when you are asked the same questions/questions in the same ballpark, you’ll be able to be more prepared to answer effectively ● PREP - parameters, returns, examples, pseudo code ● Leon uses PREP for Fizzbuzz and talks it out

author: choir241 (Richard)
title: choir241richard/2025-10-21
html:
by choir241 (Richard)

10-21-2025 ● Funnel where we’re eventually going to put opportunities into this funnel ○ The most important part of the funnel is the top - there’s no point optimizing the end parts like data structures and algorithms if you can’t even get the interview ● Workshopping Banki questions and answers live Volunteer 1: Banki 1. Give me an example of the project or initiative that you started on your own. It can be a non-business one. What prompted you to get started? ● A bit long, got a bit lost. Needs to focus on using a strict CAR response ● Leon Q: How much time do you think that saved? ● Leon Q: How many people are using the solution you made? ● Leon Q: How many people were doing this before you? ● We’re here to talk about ourselves in the best light possible - peel off all the layers and present the gold ● Record yourself and hear yourself as you practice banki questions ● In one sentence, what was the problem? ● In one sentence, what was the solution? ● Read more technical content to become more familiar and comfortable with terminology Volunteer 2: Banki 4. Tell me about when you had to deal with conflict within your team. How was the conflict solved? How did you handle that? How would you deal with it now? ● Missing result from CAR formula ● Painting yourself being a little too harsh during the conflict (in the way you paint yourself) may cause the interviewer to focus on that for the rest of the interview ● Don’t use any technical jargon words or acronyms - there’s a really good chance your interviewer doesn’t know them ● It’s best to think things through before you get to the actual interview so that you’re not caught off guard ● Impact needs to feel more impactful ● These kinds of questions are trick questions to have you snitch on yourself Volunteer 3 9. Tell me about a situation when you were responsible for project planning. Did everything go according to your plan? If not, then why and what kind of counteractions did you have to take? ● Waited until the very end for the actual banger ● What you consider a small win might actually be a huge win ● Don’t initially give all the details regarding background information - give initial tight CAR answer first, and then IF they ask for more details, that’s where you give them that info ○ This way you have more of a back and forth conversation instead of rambling on for too long and keeping it focused on what you want to cover ● It’s important to know what the correct labels are and organizing the story so it’s easy and digestible to understand Q&A Q: Should I spin my non-tech background in a way that sounds like it’s more of a technical background? A: Yes, they’re hiring for a software engineer, so your experience should sound like you’re a software engineer and have technical background as much as possible Q: How should I list my 100Hrs project in my resume? A: Leon lists it under experience and labels the project as an open source project. Especially since this is a project that had 100+ hrs put into it. Q: I have a one year gap (or longer) on my resume/experience, how do I list it on my resume? A: You were freelancing (or really freelancing). You don’t want to make them guess and it makes sense.

author: Hamza
title: hamza/2024-07-12
html:
How to Network Deep Dive with Jason E. Torres

by Hamza

Key Takeaways:

Know Your Strengths Understand and leverage your unique skills., Share your journey and projects on social media., Be responsive to messages., Aim to give more than you take.,

Engage in Conversations Schedule intentional coffee chats., Value your and others’ time.,

For Introverts: Focus on your “why” to help you step out of your comfort zone., Shared struggles can build connections., Join community spaces like Discord, Twitter Spaces, and tech forums.,

Building Connections: Networking is a vital life skill., Engage genuinely with people’s projects and interests., Aim to build friendships and relationships rather than just job opportunities.,

Jason’s Insights: Networking can significantly increase your chances of getting a job., Consistency and genuineness are crucial., Offer help and show up regularly without expecting immediate returns., Build connections organically rather than focusing on numbers.,

Quality Over Quantity: Focus on meaningful connections rather than the number of connections., Regularly engage with a small group that supports and shows up for you.,

Tracking Network: Jason doesn’t formally track his network to keep interactions genuine.,

General Advice: Networking doesn’t have to be limited to tech people., Being nice and approachable is more important than being important., Take your time and be helpful to others.,

Quotes and Community Feedback:

“Give, give, give, ask.” Helping others can indirectly help you solve your problems.,

Uncle Iroh: “Sometimes the best way to solve your own problems is to help someone else.” (edited)Friday, July 12, 2024 at 00:33

Practical Tips:

Post or show up consistently on social media and share your journey. {can just be replies, joining spaces}, Engage with familiar faces in community spaces., Use Twitter to share thoughts and connect with others., Be proactive in helping others, which will eventually pay off., Focus on building genuine relationships, not just increasing your follower count.,

Networking Strategy:

Start Small: Identify a few key people who consistently show up for you., Invest time and effort in these relationships.,

Be Consistent: Regularly engage with your network., Show genuine interest in their projects and successes.,

Stay Genuine: Keep interactions sincere and not just for personal gain., Avoid rushing the process; let relationships grow organically.,

By following these guidelines, you’ll build a strong, genuine network that supports your growth and helps you succeed in your career. (edited)Friday, July 12, 2024 at 00:33
