I'm struggling with feeling like I'm such a weird and awkward person that nobody will ever recommend me for a job. Help

image.gif failed to upload

not true. If focused on the job portion, you're doing it wrong. Coffee chats are to make friends. Just like any other time, sometimes that doesn’t work. Move on network and see who you meet. Try a different meetup, something else you’re interested in to start.

never eat alone, read HTWFAIP, talk here on discord

Just started a new dev job this week. How do I say "I don't even know what I don't know" when asked if I have questions without looking incompetent?

image.gif failed to upload

Very common question, ask your team and managers if its okay to ask lots of questions! Most will always say yes.

Make sure to be proactive when you get answers, make a spreadsheet of questions and answers, team will notice. Ask a specific person if they can be a point-person/on-boarding buddy to ask all silly questions during your beginning there.

make sure to bring them a coffee, or something else as an appreciation.

Any updates on the Colossal or other Agency jobs? The Agency Projects channel has been very quiet. Is there more information on another channel?

Nope, working on direct talent placement that introduced a lot of good and also new hiccups to deal with

Hey leon, I'm on the hunt, i have sent msgs to 60 companies, setting up chats via linkedin and email, and have had no luck, should I switch to events & x? Thts?

But how many questions total? Need to send more. Use different platforms

I cold called 600 people and no clients yet, tips?

maybe thats not working for you, a lot of ppl arent responsive to cold calling.

showing up in person goes a much longer way to the company

How do you respond to an interview offer after you were rejected for the job, that is, they contacted you to put you back into the running for the job?

This happens all the time. Not a bad sign, just got to the slightly better candidate

no feelings at all when they do reach back out to you, just accept the interview. Once get an offer, then can question the process

Suggestions for onboarding as a new dev? I know, "never eat alone, and document the process." Is there anything else I need 2 know 2 get up 2 speed/shine? Thx!

make sure talk with manager for expectations 3 6 9 etc months and how youre crushing those goals

How do we market as an overall engineer and not just web development? Say the position is not web development?

Youre a SWE, web is just something you know how to work in

A company that I interviewed with went with someone else. Is there a good format for a thank-you letter I can send back? Anything key that I should include?

Just thank for time, looking forward to a good fit

Code challenge given for interview. I have 30 minutes to complete. I know that is not enough time with my learning disability. Do I submit challenge incomplete?

Nope, ask for accommodation if you need it

How to study better? I'm very interested in being more effective. Thanks leon! Learning how to learn, put those in practice! As an experienced developer, can I contribute to Agency jobs? I don't mind doing it for free as long as it helps the community

image.gif failed to upload

Don't like free labor! Look into maintainers

Is DSA (Data Structures & Algo) required for Full stack engineer interviews?

For some, not all

How do you prepare for a second interview with the team but this time the CIO is included? Nothing much changes My work encourages me approve PRs, but I'm afraid I'm making their code base worse if I miss something. Advice? You don't have to do singular PRs, ask for a second set of eyes, ask team manager I have seen videos advising junior devs to focus on different stacks not the MERN, as most companies work with c# .net java, thoughts?

It doesn't matter. Learn basics, then learn what you need to for the job

Seems practical, but doesnt speak to the education. People don't often learn the code, yes you may need to learn that eventually,but start with the basics.

Stack doesn't matter, its can you learn, can you pick up the skills you need resume has 100devs & freelance employed mid 2022- pres. got a non-SWE job that is related enough to put on res. bad for resume to say curr. employed at 3 co’s?

That ends when you're employed elsewhere

what are good resources or what kind of work to grow as an engineer outside of your job to get better?

Building stuff

What is the best way to get feedback from a client regarding the entire process (consultation, contract, building, end product, etc)? I want to improve!

image.gif failed to upload

Just ask don't overthink it

author: Michael Govaerts
title: 2025-09-12: 100Devs Huddle Notes
html:
2025-09-12: 100Devs Huddle Notes

Written: By

Jess St-Pierre

You need to quickly start looking for jobs. Dont over-analyze!! You need to practice interviewing and stuff!-> Is my resume good?-> Am I getting callbacks? Start as soon as you can to get a feedback loop going!! Most people will be ready after completing 100hrs project if you’re doing 100Devs. Need to know what interviews look like to study for them!!

If someone criticizes my work not-constructively than I would communicate with them politely. Just explain how you want the person to constructively criticize, in private. Only if that person is a random colleague who wasn’t assigned to criticize you!

Interviewers probably wont look at your portfolio. Might look at resume and LinkedIn so dont waste too much time on the portfolio! Need to know what folks actually care about.

There are still plenty of remote opportunities! Depends where you’re located. Some are back in office and some are hybrid some are remote!

You kind of wanna have all your ducks in a row before you start taking all your time job searching, so you should have done your 100hrs project.

If you had an internship, it depends on your official title for it! You should be talking about the things you’ve done and the things you accomplished. Nothing smelly about a work experience! You always wanna let them dig if they wanna dig, there is no need to state every-single thing but never lie! Want them to formulate most grand opinion of me as possible. Dont snitch on yourself.

If diagnosed with ADHD = take Ritalin or Adderall, get that good stuff. Find a like-minded community. What works for me might not work for you! Work with a professional directly for strategies gonna be different for each person! Therapy / exercice / eating right! Or something like that.

Clients that want more but cant pay more. You respond with “hey this is a great change of scope, we would need a new contract / proposal for the work with a new payment.” (Not sure if Leon included the payment part so take with a grain of salt from me)

Very soon we have Huntober starting! The month for hiring until January. This year we will do it biiiig!

Can I use projects from class if I havent done 100hrs yet? Yes! But make it your own, dont just take the homework directly. Not for portfolio sake, so you have something to talk about = 100hr project!

At what point do you lower your quote if u dont get a reply from your client! = Never!! If anything you up it “hey I know we’ve been talking for a while but I’m starting to get booked this month. I could do it next month but for a higher price”

100hrs is to talk about during interviews, not simply to showcase on your portfolio!!

Reach out to 3 managers in 1 company, keep reaching out. Stop messaging them only when they tell you to stop. initial than 1 week than 1 month at least. And keep them in the loop. Click apply shouldn’t be the only thing you do! Reaching out is for every other role they also post afterwards not just this one.

Its been 2 years since I graduated. How do I stay motivated? Have to audit your process! What has to change/ improve? Could be anything! Take that in a systematic way! Be analytical not critical of yourself and your process. Bigger hitlist! More networking! More people in funnel so that the holes in it are obvious! Need feedback loop. Either not enough in funnel or big cracks in it!

How much wiggle room do companies have from initial offer? Depends on a lot of things! On average 10k to play with. Its such a hard question to answer! You have to figure out what the range is for each opportunity!

If it takes 2-3 yrs to finish studying web dev: network the whole time!!! Need to have so many people in your corner that would be willing to refer you!

For case study, can I redo client projects because the old are cringey?! Yes you can show the improvement you had over time!

Tips for ppl going to CYC alone! = You’re not going alone, 100Devs will be going! A lot of people are saying in the CYC channel that they are going and want to be your friend! Use that channel to meet with folks! See who’s going, follow the speakers on socials, make Twitter lists of people going! And then get a conference buddy, can say you have social anxiety and go with someone! The programming podcast also has a good ep on how to conference!

Networking and a little bit of freelancing! Kind of gotta do it all. Do freelancing, just for things to talk about during interviews. Networking is going to be the most helpful post-grad.

How add 100hr project to resume? = What value did you provide? What have you done that no one else did? What can a recruiter take and then brag to engineering manager about?

How to learn/ take notes? = As Im watching and learning I ask questions, note what I cant answer + goes into my Anki for review. No need to take notes on stuff you know already!

People can smell if you write your resume with AI, so dont do it!

By apply I mean network your way in! Talk to them build a relationship, the jobs will come!

author: Michael Govaerts
title: 19-09-2025: 100Devs Huddle Notes
html:
19-09-2025: 100Devs Huddle Notes

Written By:

Jess St-Pierre

Coarsera course went paid but there will be a work-around

When adding AI on my projects can I use googles cloud or Microsoft’s cloud without putting my billing info? = Azure used to not require a credit card, but I will check… In the past we had a way to do that, azure not having to have a credit card put in

I applied online and I said yes to having experience in AWS - you wanna have those keywords on your resume so that there is a match with what they care about, modify each resume and skip AI when using to do this. They can smell it. Network your way in. If you’re clicking and applying that’s different (without networking).

Any advice on how to get started with AWS? = they provide good docs and free certification program,

roadmap.sh

is a way to get started as well. Play with these tools, take an old project and spin it around one of these tools. Use limits to not make mistakes when using these cloud providers! Free limits! I tend not to use them that dont have limits!

To prepare my schedule with Huntober, do you have a start date? = Yes starts on one of my normal days, that Friday or Monday. Usually lasts around a month. 3rd or 6th that we start! We meet every day. Try to do data-structures and algorithms, practical advice around interviewing. How to get hired, tea-spills, and mix a lot of stuff into it to make it fun. Biggest tradition we do in 100Devs. Typically around normal time 5:30pm EDT and go half and hour usually. I’ll share more after CYC.

How can I be kinder to myself due to my slow learning progress? = Slow learning pace is totally okay!!! Its like comparing a F1 driver to a hiker. The hiker will observe more, will maintain more info. Even if the F1 driver will get there first. Difficulty grasping even simple concepts = they dont know how to learn, that’s okay because you can learn how to learn! Dont just throw yourself into a book and you dont read! If you throw yourself into a lecture and havent been in a lecture in 10 years you’re gonna struggle. You need the tools!! You need things like chunking and space-repetition, etc… you have to learn the tools first and than the learning can come after. Every single person who learned how to learn has become successful at learning! Everything changed! 8 hours a day of studying = LeBron levels of studying. There’s always going to be outliers, where people dont get tired and dont loose focus. Dont compare yourself to them!! It is a muscle you need to build it up over time but also dont compare yourself to LeBron! Got to give yourself grace when learning! Build up the muscle to keep at it for the long haul. Measure the years not the hours!

When freelancing, when do you add privacy policy and terms and conditions to websites? = Day 1, with chatGPT and stuff. Cause of the google web crawling.

Received my first interview on class 39… how do I prepare to not snitch on myself? = You’re gonna have lots and lots of interviews, some will go well and some will go horrible! Not entirely in your control, if you’ve done all the prep we talk about. Then its just a matter of being a fit or not! You wanna be getting out of the interview knowing what other interviews will look like: be analytical for this first one. What went well and what didn’t go well. You’re gonna get so much experience by going through the process. Nothing bad can come out of it, as long as you focus on improving. Look at the Bank, for interview questions example. You wanna have a really good narrative, why does it make sense to be the best candidate for this role and talk about certain projects. Look at the “how to interview” class! My biggest pet peeves in life if someone sending the interviewer an email saying “I know it didn’t go well”, when that’s not necessarily true, you just lost it by saying that. The other person could’ve been grumpy and hungry. Dont sell yourself sort. Talk your talk. No humbleness.

Is a startup without financial stuff a good job? = Its not a job, its a trap. Please do not work a job for no money. Some internships with big companies are worth it but not often. Its not a startup that’s trying to get free labor.

What is the 100Devs wisdom on those that can only work 15-20 hrs per week? = Freelancing is great for people without structured hours like parents and stuff. I highly advocate for freelancing. There are a small amount of part-time remote software engineering jobs, you really have to network your way in. Easiest = freelancing. But there are companies who hire for part-time.

How do you handle competitive at work? How to handle bullies? = It comes down to there’s levels to it and so its hard to give 1 size fits all. Read how to win friends and influence people, works with a lot of people. Can pull someone to the side and tell them you dont appreciate their behavior. If you’ve done all the stuff then talk to HR and people. Is competition part of the org or individual person? I would talk to the person if its the latter. Time to find another opportunity if its a company culture thing!

I completed 100Devs last year but didn’t submit any homework? = You dont need to submit anything! Doesn’t do too much for you than certify that you did the course. This is a jobs program so wont help you too much in getting a job.

Im from Canada and not willing to change countries for a while, should I network with fellow Canadians? = Yes!! Mostly you want to network with Canadians. I would make a list of companies and add to your list to network your way in and than find countries outside your country and make it a secondary hit-list. First you do the Canadian networking hitlist and than the American or NA one! Some USA companies hire remotely and anyone from NA so should still take that into consideration!

Thoughts on walking into offices with my Resume? Not from those cities ? = It could probs hurt more than it could help. I dont think that’s gonna be a good look, especially some have security now. Dont really work in tech offices. You wont get anywhere with it. What I would do however: Find every living being that is in the company and city and say : Hey I’ll be in NYC wanna do a coffee chat? Should also do meetups and networking while you’re there!

My resume has both 100Devs and professional sabbatical? = You need to have a good narrative for professional sabbatical. Ive seen people put it on there and seen people having trouble putting it in their narrative.

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2025-10-16
html:
from Ashton (Class 10)

Leon is making a Bob the resume builder AI based on what is most effective. No word on a release date. People in the hiring process: sourcer then recruiter, then inbound sourcer and/or recurring coordinator. Hiring manager is a part of the last step, like say at a large company when they have it narrowed down to 5 individuals. Hiring manager has the most pull but sources and recruiters are who are most visible. **AT NO POINT IS AI OR A ROBOT KNOCKING YOU OUT** Say a company receives 250 resumes submitted. All 250 go into an applicant tracking system queue. After they’re screened that number dwindles down to 200, and they receive 5-20 seconds from recruiters to determine whether to invest more time and read more. Then the number lowers to 30, who are the ones given more time. This leaves 20 who go through a technical screening test, which can be a technical phone screen or take home coding challenge. Then the number narrows down to 5: these are given several onsite interviews like coding, systems design, meeting the hiring manager. All of this ultimately leads to a single offer for one person. Leon says resumes should fall in an F pattern because that is how people read. Left to right, downwards. Make sure the F quickly and easily shows your name, your current role, and your highest level of education. Another formatting tip: XYZ. Accomplished X as measured by Y by doing Z. Explaining how is SO important. How to stand out without experience: “Have good communication skills. Have a past you’ve woven into your narrative. Be able to communicate technical concepts in a digestible vernacular. Show excitement for the position. Follow up. Be able to show a history of consistency.” List software engineering role on resume first. Have a mailing address where they are hiring if they’re not in your state. Avoid “learning and aspiring”. Technical, leadership, and impact are the key values. Language matters when a human reads it. Help / supported VS Led / Launched/ Which sounds better? Which supports leadership and impact? OK: Won second place in hackathon. Better: Won second place out of 50 teams in hackathon. Best: Won second place out of 50 teams in hackathon at NJ tech by working with two colleagues to develop an app that syncs mobile calendars. Never going to have call work and ask if you truly led versus helped. But you need to have the proof that you’ve done all of this. Here during the huddle Leon showed a two column resume which I believe he posted the link to in the follow-along-materials thread or the newsletter. Here is my screenshot: He said he thinks it’s worthwhile to use a summary for switching careers and you don’t have a ton of evidence to show why you’re there. He also said that this resume and the other he shows aren’t meant to be perfect, objective ways to do things. Different things will work for different people and not every recruiter will like the same thing either. FANG and big companies wouldn’t appreciate the two column format as shown above, but a medium sized company is more likely to. Also? More bullet points does NOT mean more impact. 3-4 is okay! Didn’t mention any opposition to 2. Projects section may be too much in first 6 seconds, may have a smell. But it’s not an absolute no-no. Jose Silva mentioned he thought projects are the same as student learning. Don’t call it one if you can help it, instead refer to it as a tool that does X. Try to make it seem like work. IG Hunt different suggested “stealth start up”. Lead and directed are Leon approved words but strategy is not, because it does not give the how, and is especially lacking if it doesn’t relate to software engineering. Volunteering is always good to list, make sure you still stick with strong wording. Putting the skills first can he helpful, helps the right keyword(s) be found and can help you move to the next stage, figure out what’s most in demand for your market or area to add to your list of skills. Leon recommends going on hiring cafe to see what’s being asked for in your area. On a resume someone listed their 100 hours project under their experience, which is simply titled Experience, excluding an explicit mention of jobs. It mentions it being open source, over 100 hours put in, with a link to it via github. This is Leon approved, because it IS an experience and would help with the six second pass without calling it a project, which Leon said smells bootcampy. Just have a title for it that aligns with keywords, lean into keyword recognition. Random but PDF is the recommended format for resumes, not docx. The recommended way to name your resume is to have it be firstName-lastName-resume2025 to make it easier for the recruiter to find. NEVER misrepresent that you have a degree, but you don’t have to say what kind. Max players enroll in a local community college for a few hundred bucks to show they have a graduation date. “The team can be of one too, only to tell them it was that way if they asked. -tehuberjohn “You always to position yourself as THE dev.” -Brynn “So many people I personally know have gotten great SWE roles in the past two months. Do you know what they all had in common? They had a coherent story, they practiced their behavioral questions, and when it came to tech? They used PREP or something like it.” -Sam aka Leader of the Pizza Gang

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2025-10-17
html:
from Ashton (Class 10)

As a kid Marcia was really into Excel, had dial up internet but could only play with stuff in Microsoft Office due to the poor quality of her internet. She was motivated by the burn out of being a farmer, specifically a plant scientist. She had to work from 5am-11pm! She used to listen to Leon while she was on the tractor doing farm work. While she did finished 100devs in 4 and a half months she did burn out and get sick, she paid the price and couldn’t get out of bed for a week. “If you don’t rest your creativity is going to take a restraining order out on you.” She couldn’t even open VScode until like class 10, couldn’t get it to work. She used a 15 year old Macbook, even Leon worked on a Chromebook for 2 years. She initially used pen and paper, would try to predict what Leon would do next using active recall. Re: clients She stayed local! She lived in Amish country and built a website for a dairy farmer, which is an underserved market. She gorged Python for an interview, had no experience in it and had two days to learn as much as she could, as it was a requirement for the interview. The guy who interviewed her basically gave her the answer for the tech question, really liked her communication style, how she was keen to fix issues when given feedback. She reached out to managers and hiring managers directly using a script she tailored that was consistently successful. She ended up being remote forever at her job despite everyone else getting called back to the office, getting a 50% raise and doubling her bonus ALL because she was looking at another position and her company really wanted to keep her. She didn’t even ask for all of that! The second company couldn’t compete with the benefits of the one she was currently with and asked if she worked freelance and now she works on projects for them that way. Re: networking She goes to auctions and country clubs, sometimes pretending she doesn’t know how to golf and scouts out for people with expensive trucks and horses to connect with. “Helps having a farmer background when dealing with the Amish. So essentially it’s finding your niche.” -Matthias (She told us she’s a third generation farmer) “Hi I’m Bob Vance, Vance Engineering.” How she introduced herself. “I’m Marcia, I make apps and websites.” Always keeps business cards on her. She attributes her boldness and bravery especially in social situations to working sales in college. Leon and her agree that sales is something everyone should do at least once in their life alongside customer service. Her mindset in sales and networking which changed the game for her inside and out: “just make a friend.” It helps to get to know their first name, find out about things that matter to them, and are big parts of their life. Can be used for networking in person or say interacting with people in LinkedIn. Also? Her confidence is fake, “it’s so fake, it’s all fake”. If Jake and Brad, random dudes online can be 100% wrong and say things with complete confidence, she can too. (And so can you!) While she knows it won’t work for everyone especially international folks she used Nextdoor, Facebook, and LinkedIn for freelancing. FB especially worked for her because it’s one of the only things Amish and farm people are familiar with using. When she negotiates pay she pretends she isn’t herself but Mark, Marcia’s agent whose only goal is to get more money for Marcia. Pretending she’s a man and someone else helps her be gutsier. She also completely encourages us to copy her negotiation script and use it freely. One of her particularly popular apps is Bunindex, an app for rabbit breeders. She got pre-paid signed up users, was well received by Amish people who secretly keep phones. Was skeptical about it being a good idea versus her 100hours project, which was for horse riding warm ups. She called it “crappy”. General advice from her: “Don’t be afraid to try stuff.” Also don’t freeze up if you don’t know something, it helps no one. Also? She manages to do all the things she does by keeping a strict schedule: she freelances, has her dev job, AND teaches at a community college (somehow). LINKS: Marcia’s youtube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aKeJcMf5SBA What Marcia used to learn Python over the weekend for an interview, and was the first step to Python being her dominant coding language today: https://www.py4e.com/ Marcia’s templates/scripts for networking, negotiating, and salary negotiation: https://medium.com/@marciacripps/steal-my-job-search-scripts-proven-templates-for-networkin g-resignation-and-salary-negotiation-8e98aa7c21e0 Note: This has successfully worked for her with hiring managers and on LinkedIn.

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2025-10-20
html:
from choir241 (Richard)

● The goal is to get recommended so you have an easier time through the process ● Don’t make the take-home test your whole personality - you don’t want to overinvest, you wanna be in and out ● There’s always a potential reason to get rejected during a technical interview, and it’s just not worth the energy investment to only get rejected by the most niche reasons ● Whiteboard interviews aren’t as common, but recommend to practice a couple times just to be familiar with it ● Every time you practice interview or have an interview, always use the appropriate system to make sure you don’t walk in like an accident ● Try to look for the company's interview process when it’s a mid-size or higher company (smaller might be harder to find). You can find it through research or by simply asking. ● If you get blown off after asking what the interview will be like, ie. it’ll just be a technical, it’ll just be a behavior, make sure to ask for specifics and details with follow-up questions. Ie. cool, am I going to be expected to use x language? Should I be expected to walk through my code? Should I be expecting Leetcode hard/medium/easy questions? ● CAR - Cause Action Result ○ For all your behavioral questions and should be committed into memory and should have a lot of time invested into them. 3 sentences max for the beginning, but as you become more comfortable, you can add more if needed ○ There is no need to add in qualifiers, 100% all positive. There is no need to say anything negative to come off as more relatable. Think “why are you a genius” ○ Leon demonstrates asking a Banki question using CAR ○ You will talk more than you will memorize. The memorization is to give you a sense of comfort and make sure you cover all the important points. ● EUE - Explanation Use Example ○ Depending on the company you’re applying for, you might see yourself using this more ● Banki Questions ○ Will you be asked every question on this form? No. Will you be asked some questions that match exactly on the Banki form? Yes. The point is that if/when you are asked the same questions/questions in the same ballpark, you’ll be able to be more prepared to answer effectively ● PREP - parameters, returns, examples, pseudo code ● Leon uses PREP for Fizzbuzz and talks it out

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2025-10-21
html:
by choir241 (Richard)

10-21-2025 ● Funnel where we’re eventually going to put opportunities into this funnel ○ The most important part of the funnel is the top - there’s no point optimizing the end parts like data structures and algorithms if you can’t even get the interview ● Workshopping Banki questions and answers live Volunteer 1: Banki 1. Give me an example of the project or initiative that you started on your own. It can be a non-business one. What prompted you to get started? ● A bit long, got a bit lost. Needs to focus on using a strict CAR response ● Leon Q: How much time do you think that saved? ● Leon Q: How many people are using the solution you made? ● Leon Q: How many people were doing this before you? ● We’re here to talk about ourselves in the best light possible - peel off all the layers and present the gold ● Record yourself and hear yourself as you practice banki questions ● In one sentence, what was the problem? ● In one sentence, what was the solution? ● Read more technical content to become more familiar and comfortable with terminology Volunteer 2: Banki 4. Tell me about when you had to deal with conflict within your team. How was the conflict solved? How did you handle that? How would you deal with it now? ● Missing result from CAR formula ● Painting yourself being a little too harsh during the conflict (in the way you paint yourself) may cause the interviewer to focus on that for the rest of the interview ● Don’t use any technical jargon words or acronyms - there’s a really good chance your interviewer doesn’t know them ● It’s best to think things through before you get to the actual interview so that you’re not caught off guard ● Impact needs to feel more impactful ● These kinds of questions are trick questions to have you snitch on yourself Volunteer 3 9. Tell me about a situation when you were responsible for project planning. Did everything go according to your plan? If not, then why and what kind of counteractions did you have to take? ● Waited until the very end for the actual banger ● What you consider a small win might actually be a huge win ● Don’t initially give all the details regarding background information - give initial tight CAR answer first, and then IF they ask for more details, that’s where you give them that info ○ This way you have more of a back and forth conversation instead of rambling on for too long and keeping it focused on what you want to cover ● It’s important to know what the correct labels are and organizing the story so it’s easy and digestible to understand Q&A Q: Should I spin my non-tech background in a way that sounds like it’s more of a technical background? A: Yes, they’re hiring for a software engineer, so your experience should sound like you’re a software engineer and have technical background as much as possible Q: How should I list my 100Hrs project in my resume? A: Leon lists it under experience and labels the project as an open source project. Especially since this is a project that had 100+ hrs put into it. Q: I have a one year gap (or longer) on my resume/experience, how do I list it on my resume? A: You were freelancing (or really freelancing). You don’t want to make them guess and it makes sense.

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2025-12-05
html:
by Anny Levine (

https://www.linkedin.com/in/annylevine

)

fizzbuzz? the most growth comes from doing the hard thing. some folks are natural yappers, but then their projects atrophy and their coding ability atrophies.

The Phoenix Project - novel about an IT org, and you're following this manager who gets pulled up to being a VP...

Job fairs and networking: Advice is you're there to get to know the person, not to apply for a job. I'm going there to meet a point of contact at that company and trying to turn that person into an acquaintance if not a friend long-term. I try not to go in with the intention of 'please let me into the job'. Maybe I can get to know someone, get a coffee chat, and build a relationship over the long term. There are different job fairs that are more specialized... It depends on the niche... Try to make a friend at each booth... Anything to provide value and make a connection.

Can you explain how to get an implicit referral from someone? If you've had a conversation with someone in the past, you send them a message. "Hey, Bob! I saw there's an open role XYZ at your company. Who would be the best person to talk to about it?" Skip the line, and build a connection.

The more personalized your messages are, the better it is, and the further it goes. Favorite time of year to connect is January because you have the greatest message you can send to literally anyone... "Happy New Year! I thought about our conversation last year. How are you doing?" Birthday ones are good, too! January, a lot of jobs open when people have budget again!

Recruiter says relocation needed. It's one day in office per week... Wait until you have an offer in hand to talk about ANYTHING relocation. A lot of things are negotiable when you have an offer in hand. Everything is negotiable. Start with base salary and things like that, THEN talk relocation and things like *that*. Relocation is becoming more of a real thing, but so many people in 100Devs have had 1 day a week in office, but it NEVER actually happened. Don't reject something before you have the offer in hand...

You can negotiate a salary that says non-negotiable salary in the post. You're negotiating so many things you SHOULD be negotiating that aren't just salary, but they will also usually negotiate salary as well.

"Hey, is it best to wait for a job to open or is it best to get into the referral pool and try to get in that way." Ask for a connection in recruiting, or whomever is hiring.

Never Split the Difference - book recommendation

For a 20k mobile app project, anything recommended to add to the project proposal beyond what is in the template... At that level of money, you want to have some level of deposit, or milestone payments... Some logic around when the next payment comes IN THE CONTRACT. Make sure you have that spelled out. A Break-Up Clause is ALSO important. You want that in there. Indemnification Clause is in there. Universal License once you're paid in full... Then Client Service. I expect a level of polish and communication at that level. Timeline, expectations, stuff like that that might change. They might hold you hostage, otherwise. How they get preview builds. How often...

We're ALL building on someone else's codebase. You can always have stuff that are deviations from other codebases... but be UPFRONT about it. Don't clone and then build off of the clone. The whole thing is about showing them what YOU can do. You can put everything in the readme. What happened, where it's coming from, what is yours.. You should be able to explain everything that is in that codebase. Be able to talk through the process of how you did everything. What's the logic behind it.

Do we still contact our hitlist in December? Around the holidays? Two ways of viewing this. Either have less time or more time... It's worth it to shoot your shot, but always always always give people an out! Usually wait until the 5th or 6th of January that way people have time to dig themselves out of the holiday buildup.

Job markets go in waves, and every local job market is different. You gotta be shooting your shot, always.

Let the market tell you what to focus on next, whether it's learning a new language, or networking.

Whatever you start with, finish it to the end. Anything that's free, do it to the end, then fill in the gaps per your local market. Always always always with the networking.

Grit, resilience, ability to yap... Every week is a do or do not type of moment... It filters out quite a bit.

How to talk about 100Devs during networking, etc... Training program.

How is it a coherent story, and how can you get to work and they know that tomorrow you will start solving their problems TOMORROW. Get your story solid, and screaming how you can solve their problems.

Give yourself grace. Take the month. Build the project. Then come back in January... Build the proof, then look at the proof of what you can do.

Anything that doesn't come to you through the tools you're using, that's a red flag. If they're asking you to fill out stuff before the process... If they're trying to send you stuff... Scams...

Thanks CrispyNugget for making the stream music.

There will be a COHORT 3!!!

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2025-12-09
html:
01:37]Michael Govaerts

:

I wasn't seated during today's Huddle and couldn't take notes. Does anyone want to please help memory dump what cool things you heard in this thread for the people who might have missed it? Thanks in advance

[01:47]Michael Govaerts

:

What I recall, in reverse chronological: don't sweat the interview process too much, even if getting to the interview stage and hopefully doing well based on good preparation is definitely something worth celebrating. You might think you do really well, or really poorly, and it may work out vastly differently than what you expected. No worries! Determine what you want to take forward and apply to future interviews, and what was weird or went poorly that you're going to leave behind with that potentially unsuccessful experience. (Maybe you opted not to advocate for yourself in the face of negative feedback because you felt shy, and next time you can leave that shyness behind and make sure not only are you getting meaningful feedback but also that they actually are telling you about +your+ interview. Leon said in +many+ cases RC (Resilient Coders) students will get very odd interview feedback and it will turn out there was a mix up and the RC student is being evaluated based on the interview of an entirely different candidate in the pool. Don't just walk away without asking for clarification or further feedback potentially solely for this reason. Maybe there has been a mix up!)

Regardless, Leon finds that the getting too caught up with the interview outcomes during the job hunt is one of the biggest factors that cause people to burn out and quit. (Getting frustrated learning CSS or JavaScript, in that order, is probably the other big hurdle.) Don't ride that rollercoaster. It's just going to make you nauseous. Focus on what's in your control, not on what's not. We have no control over how interviews happen or how they turn out. We have control over going through the 100Devs process of getting interviews and preparing for interviews, and as long as we're being analytical to make sure we're doing the right things well, the paid opportunities will materialize eventually, but we cannot control when they do. We can control our behaviors, though.

[01:53]Michael Govaerts

:

Other topics that I'll expand upon if I can:

Use ChatGPT to help you determine what to expect in an interview. Got a code review interview where they give you code to review, but know what the code snippet is likely to cover? Ask ChatGPT about what that type of code is likely to cover and where the "gotcha's" might be in terms of code that's not working.

Cohort 3 is likely going to happen in the near future, but Leon is still working out the details. Three aspects had made him hesitant to do a third cohort that he has now determined are no longer issues:

  1. He hates missing cohort classes, but that means he can't really travel and needs to be home to stream during a cohort. That's no fun, especially not for people in his life that are also impacted, so he has a new system with a StarLink and a little portable bag that will allow him to stream from anywhere if he needs to. He has been testing this and has been able to stream from the middle of nowhere ("In the woods"), and since he always uses a green screen, he doesn't think we noticed that he changed locations. He loves this, because he wants to try to keep the experience of him streaming very uniform for his audience, and he also wants to flexibility to be able to travel while routinely streaming.

  2. He doesn't want to push students into "a slaughter." Recently, with AI and tech layoffs, he didn't think pushing a bunch of students through a cohort and out into a challenging hiring landscape would be fair to those students. [Michael's note: Please remember, though, RC was still going, and 100Devs was still going asynchronous, so people +were still getting jobs+, even when it was tough.]

[01:54]Michael Govaerts

:

However, this has been the best hiring period he has seen in the last few years, so he's very excited about giving another cohort the opportunity to "graduate" into a good hiring environment. RC students are getting jobs all the time, and he has been seeing people get jobs almost every other day, so he's much more optimistic than usual.

[01:55]Michael Govaerts

:

  1. He wants to make sure he can give people an edge, and while he thinks the Cohort 2 material still holds up incredibly well, and likely is enough to get people hired, he's going to try to add some things to Cohort 3 such as learning theory, emotional regulation, incorporating AI into the learning experience, and bringing friends of the community who have more expertise than Leon in certain areas into the cohort in order to help enhance the experience and what people get out of it.

3+) Cohorts are also a lot of work for Leon and the mods, and ask for a significant commitment from cohort students, so Leon wants to make sure it's worth it for everyone involved.

[01:58]Michael Govaerts

:

New topic: Social Media:

It's all very likely fake or carefully controlled posturing, so please keep that in mind if you start getting sucked into it. Leon timeboxes his social media time and his news time, and doesn't put in at the beginning of the day so that he has time to get ready to experience it without starting his day with negativity. The algorithm is designed to keep you engaged so it can either get more data from you to sell to other people, or directly sell you stuff. Please keep that in mind as you're getting very emotionally charged content.

Also, remember that internet strangers likely stop thinking about you almost immediately, so if some stranger online gives you negative feedback, just let it wash off you like water. (Leon used the analogy "Dust in the Wind".) We don't know the full names of our eight great grandparents in most cases, let alone very many details about them, so some internet stranger, or even someone much less connected to us, is likely not thinking about us at all shortly after we interacted with them, so don't stress out about what they might be thinking of you or give them any psychological real estate that you could otherwise use more productively, either.

[01:59]Michael Govaerts

:

Also remember that people are using social media for a variety of reasons. Some might just be sharing, especially with people they care about, and other people are selling themselves or trying to impress people with the best possible version of their lives they can muster, which may or may not be true.

[02:00]Michael Govaerts

:

Thus, you don't need to ride that rollercoaster if you don't have to, just like with the job hunt. Take the stuff you want to take, and then move onto more productive things that are more likely to enhance your life and the lives of the people you care about

[02:02]Michael Govaerts

:

Other things to cover:

Beginner's mind/attitude (GREAT for coffee chats and meetups, since people love talking to those who are very interested in what they want to talk about and are often at meetups because they want to help the next generation (or inspire that generation to continue to be interested in, support, and maintain the things and technologies they like), but much less great for the job hunt, where you want to portray yourself as someone who is extremely competent, can succeed right from the start, and is a good risk (in terms of investments by clients or employers))

Also, NETWORKING IS HUGE! Huddle started with people, including Leon, talking about how networking often gets you freelancing, and freelancing is one of the best ways to consistently earn money when you need it, especially to put food on the table, because you have much more control over how much or how little freelancing you do, and when you do it, vs. a more stable, long-term job (Michael also remembers a freeCodeCamp podcast where the guest talk about how she +prefers+ freelancing solely for this increased flexibility and control over her schedule and level of commitment.)

-- (Here's that podcast, where the guest is also a career-changer:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5k4asZXrzHI

)

YouTube

freeCodeCamp.org

How to become a developer in your 30s with Anjana Vakil [Podcast #162]

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1448096219074396262

Michael Govaerts

changed the channel name: Tues 12/9/25 2:30pm-3:30pm PT Huddle Memory Dump — 12/10/25, 02:37

[22:32]Michael Govaerts

:

On letting go, especially of things we can't control:

https://rickhanson.com/let_it_go/

"... Let yourself be surprised – both by what might replace what you’ve released and by the power of letting go in general. As the great Thai Buddhist teacher, Ajahn Chah once said:

If you let go a little, you will have a little happiness.

If you let go a lot, you will have a lot of happiness.

If you let go completely, you will be completely happy."

Rick Hanson, PhD

Rick Hanson

Let It Go - Rick Hanson, PhD

Are you holding onto at least one thing that’s way past its expiration date? Self-critical thoughts, obsessions, defensive about your issues, or drinking too much. These things are relatively straightforward to deal with, even though it could be difficult.

https://rickhanson.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Rick-Default.jpg

[23:47]Anny (class 11)

:

This is amazing. Thank you, Michael!

[23:47]Anny (class 11)

:

Also, Rick Hanson is great. I love the Being Well podcast he does with his son.

[00:24]Michael Govaerts

:

Thanks for the positive feedback and the endorsement, @Anny (class 11) .

I'm always happy to help however I can, and I really appreciate all that you're doing for our community, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too

Thanks, as always, for choosing to be awesome.

[10:44]Michael Govaerts

:

On the health front, I share some more resources I've found particularly helpful here :

?(Mental) Health Resources that…

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-01-06
html:
(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan: https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts )

100Devs Huddle - Tuesday, Jan 06, 2026, at 2:30 PM PT

100Devs: https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

- Sessions are focusing on helping people with "The Hunt" (for paid opportunities)

(Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord)

[Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Or, they can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it's about the job hunt.]

Leon says people have been getting jobs every day. Lots of good news in the #celebrations channel on our Discord, which Leon encourages people to share in to help encourage others that it is possible to succeed through this program.

Recent highlights:

- Rich getting new developer role

- Scout getting full-time technical support

- Blendy for starting a new role.

(He's just recapping this post with more details here: https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/738891289071714388/1458211085759811714 )

Chat: "Getting a job with a tool/skill we learn with 100Devs is such a flex."

Leon: "Yeah, it is!" [Let's keep doing it!]

Leon encourages people who have succeeded to share their success and help motivate others. "Tea Spills" during the Huddles are also very popular, where people discuss their success live and how it happened. One of our recent successes said they're up for it:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1458227767152742655

RE: Hitlist Magic

Don't make constraints too rigid. Common problems: Not going back far enough, being too restrictive on technologies, focusing on only entry/junior roles

- Make sure that you're using the most updated hitlist magic guidelines

- Smaller metro areas can be harder to find leads

-- Expand the search parameters. Go back an entire year. Find the companies that have been hiring recently so that you can focus on those companies (since they likely have funding to hire you) with your hitlisting

RE: Banki

- Everyone can get answers to the Banki questions, regardless of background. Just need to be creative about forming answers

-- ChatGPT tool from HuntOber that should be in the Discord to help us frame our narrative and form good answers

- Come to Banki Brunch or ask on Discord to help craft better answers, especially if we're having problems. Don't ever hesitate to ask for support, especially if we've already put the work in to try to help ourselves. 100Devs is about growing together.

- 100Devs uses "CAR" instead of "STAR". Leon thinks STAR has one too many letters :p

RE: Personal vs. Professional Opinions on AI.

Leon: ""Job asking us to do certain things AI-related?" Not understanding the question."

- Some people have an apprehension to using AI. Leon sees AI as a StackOverflow. Not up in arms about StackOverflow, no personal qualms about AI or using it in his professional day. Leon is pretty AI-positive at this point

- AI is a tool. He's not going to get mad about a hammer.

- If we're an artist or someone who does design work, AI definitely impacts this more.

- If Leon ever gets asked on a job to do something that he feels ethically opposed to, then it's definitely time to find a new job

-- What a time to find one, too, since at least 5 people got jobs during our holiday break!

RE: How to get reference for first tech job?

- Reference can be anyone. Doesn't need to be technical. Anyone that has experience working with us in any capacity

-- Former coworkers. Doesn't even have to be a boss. Just someone who worked with us in a professional capacity. Previous opportunity. Worked on a client project with someone at 100Devs. All of these are fair game for a reference. Up for employer to determine if they don't like the reference. Most of the time, it just needs to be someone who we've worked with in a professional capacity. Commmunity projects or hackathons can work. Just need someone who knows we work well with others and are professionally capable

RE: Navigate full-time job offer when we're doing lots of volunteer work? (Like what Leon does but on a smaller scale)

- Tell job up-front that the volunteer stuff is important and it's beneficial for your personal success, but only if it's during work hours. If it's after work hours, then your time is your time. If it's during work hours, just make sure the employer recognizes the importance, and make sure you make it contingent on your job

- Collaborating with others is very helpful for personal and professional growth, so these opportunities are typically worth keeping (especially, as Leon mentions later, for keeping up or professional networking and skill set if we ever need to transition to a different job (or get laid off and then need to be able to quickly find a new job.))

RE: Support job. How not to get stuck and move to dev work?

- Make goals visible. Have a visual reminder in your space every day of what you actually want. What do you want in your life, for your family, in your future? Vision boards can work. Can be a printout of a word. Leon had a family member print out the amount of money they want to make in bold letters and put it on a wall to look at every day. Make the goal visible so it can't be shuffled away as you get more comfortable. Keep the goal top of mind. Adopt an identity that is different from your job. If you're in a full-time support job, and someone asks you what you do, you say you're a software engineer and you're doing a support job in between. Tie your identity to what you want to ultimately do, which will keep you focused on that goal. Identity setting very important to setting good habits and moving in the right direction. Get loved ones involved to help you stay focused on getting the goal you're working towards.

Madeleine: "Or you can make your 100 hours project a vision board and you kill two birds with one stone 🧠" [Leon liked this.]

- https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1458230562157367338

- Jasmine shared her vision board template in the chat, which Leon appreciated:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1458232103098515516

Banki is a list of questions that people take and put into Anki to help people do well with interviewing

- Something that we will encounter as part of the 100Devs program

- Key to crushing interviews. 100Devs is a jobs program, not so much a coding program.

- 60+ videos on YouTube that help cover many of the topics covered in the Huddle in depth (https://communitytaught.org/class/all)

-- Lingo in the beginning can be hard, but it helps create our 100Devs community. As you spend time with us, it will all start to make more sense.

"I struggle with terminology in interviews; I know JS well, but I mix up simple terms and phrases, making me seem unknowledgeable."

- Practice and reading. That's it, and it will get you where you want to be

- Meetings can be helpful to talk through terminology. Many people doing this remotely don't talk about stuff technically with others enough. Need this practice

- Coming to Banki Brunch on Discord can help with this. Or record yourself talking out loud

- Programming Podcast can help you hear other folks talk about the terminology. (Or other podcasts, but Programming Podcast is the one Leon hosts)

- When Leon was first learning FrontEnd development, he listened to a popular FrontEnd podcast religiously to get familiar with the terminology and what was being discussed

100Devs Cohort 3 Core Curriculum will be fundamentals that will stand for at least 3-5 years after the content is created

- Agentic engineering is too new right now. FlimFlam (made-up word) may be hot right now, but may not be in the near future

-- Leon plans to cover AI engineering fundamentals, though

How to get started in 100Devs? It seems like a lot.

- Start from the beginning, do all the homework, check out the #catchupcrew channel on the Discord. CodeHelp channel whenever we're stuck. Figuring stuff out in the beginning is part of the plan. Every day as an engineer is a new problem, so we need to learn how to get ourselves out of this, and 100Devs teaches us. Work our way out of the problem, but ask for help when we need it, too.

- "Where to Start" from the Discord: https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1087405263767736463

"I think i went through a final round a couple hours ago and it went very very well but i didn't ask about next steps cause shy. What should I do? "

- Follow-up! "I had a great time at the interview, and I would love to talk about next steps."

-- Don't hesitate to do the right thing, even if it's a little later than you would like. "We miss 100% of the shots we don't take."

- Always follow up, and it is always appropriate to clarify if necessary

-- "Thought this went really well, curious about next steps"

--- Hopefully, they get back to you and let you know, but make sure you always do your part regardless

Leon doesn't like being called "Sir." Too formal. Live conversation with those in the chat about this.

- "Senor Leon" in the chat was received well, since Leon is learning Spanish.

- "Unc" ("Uncle Leon") was something he used when he was younger with older men who weren't always related. Some respect, some humor. It worked, though, and he likes it. Gotta drop some knowledge to be Unc, and you've been around a little longer. Leon thinks he may still be too young for this, but he's open to it.

Next cohort?

- Soon.

"What have you been doing lately, Leon? Have you explored any new technologies or projects during the holidays?"

- "I've been Unc'ing" from the chat. Leon said, Exactly

- Leon thinks this question is hilarious. He has been asking himself this sometimes lately, too :p

- No. Leon was super sick this last week. Superflu. Wasn't COVID, his family tested. A little cough now, but he still sounds fine. He was out for a while, though

-- Has a whole month now to learn, as it seems like his day job with Resilient Coders (RC) is light right now without teaching obligations. Lots of reading to get through, lots of AI/agentic stuff. Trying out other people's patterns and workflows. Some wonderful people are helping him. AI-engineering fellowship for Resilient Coders (RC) in February. Solidifying curriculum for that, and he'll bring it to 100Devs. Not teaching every day, and therefore spending 8 hours a day studying/reading/trying. He has a lot of free credits for AI-related services. Unlocked limits for Cursor(sp) that let him do a lot of learning (company gave him the benefit)

--- Hopes to bring all his best lessons to our community

- When he's not working, he tries to spend the 8 hours he would spend working on learning and growing

-- Even his nights now involve lots of reading, articles, and going through all the major foundational papers in AI. Tries to keep it pretty tight when he's not teaching. If he's not diligent with his time when he's not teaching, he just ends up spending time playing Pokémon, which he's much less satisfied with after

The new 100Devs Platform being developed is what Leon is using for much of his learning.

"DevOps/Security important for Full-Stack jobs, or is 100Devs enough?"

- DevOps and security could be worthwhile to explore if you're interested, but probably won't be the difference-maker in getting you a job

-- Try not to get distracted by this stuff if you really want a job, especially in software engineering. DevOps or security would get you a software engineering job, a better SWE job, or an SWE job faster. It is typically just a distraction

-- Focus on the proven stuff from 100Devs that works if you want to get the SWE job ASAP.

"How should I go about writing log of wins at the job?"

- Bell chart meme. Apple notes to more complexity with Obsidian, etc., and then ultimately realize the simple thing you started with was the thing you're most likely to use. Just keep it simple. Apple Note or equivalently simple that you can put in the doc, either weekly or immediately. Best way to remember and keep track so that you can use them in your performance reviews, and not try to remember every 6 months or a year when you've forgotten most of the wins, or at the least have forgotten most of the details of those wins. Over time, if you do it every week, that's 52 chances as opposed to the one that people often do at the end of the year. Leon recommends putting it on your calendar weekly. Lots more shots on goal, and you're much more likely to get the goals that can really make a difference when you can bring them up at your performance review.

Might be Jobuary activity for January (the chat was +very+ interested)

"What are your thoughts on using a framer or webflow template for a portfolio? Going heavy into the hunt and would like a nice out of the box look"

Leon prefers a template we can do on our own. WebFlow is also expensive. Save money, and have something we can show off better. Astro or Tailwind template. Same amount of speed, but it ends up being code that we've written and tends to be slightly better. Long-term better to have these self-built options vs. something fast like framer or webflow. Very easy with Tailwind now to get something that looks nice out of the box, and we can talk about Tailwind during interviews.

Supporting role vs. SWE role? Supporting is 3-4x income.

- Leon: Take it, but try not to let it be a big opportunity cost. Don't get too comfortable if being an SWE is your ultimate goal. If something is on the table for 3-4x your income, take it regardless, and then use that added financial stability to pursue SWE more seriously

"I think i went through a final round a couple hours ago.... Do I follow up with the recruiter? or one of the 3 people I did the interview with? "

- VERY IMPORTANT: Everyone gets a follow-up and a thank you. **Every person you talk to**: "Had a wonderful time, [thank them for their time and for the connection.]"

-- Costs nothing to be polite, and it really does make a difference. Interviews are another networking opportunity

- You can bomb an interview and then get people to like you, and you're much more likely to get an interview offer months later and ultimately get the job. Always follow up. Thank you messages get jobs

[People had their hands up, so he's answering questions live]

Alec: New job about three weeks ago. Not a development job. Cable technician job. Helping install cable at houses

-- 4x his income, but he wants to get out of it as quickly as possible to get back to SWE. The environment he is in is not software-centric. Much more focused on the cable technician/field role, even if it encourages growth and networking in that field. Alec doesn't want to risk seeming too fancy for his job

- Leon: Growth should not come from your job. If you want to grow in SWE, it's probably going to happen outside of 9-5. You hit the jackpot if you can get this on job, but for most, it's happening outside of your workday. Internally, get to know all other teams that are doing SWE-related stuff. Can have virtual meetings with people in the company that you reach out to internally via company communication (eg, Slack). Always easier to transfer with the blessing of our manager. 3+ months on job, reach out to your manager about how you're crushing goals. Ask if your SWE skill set can help out the team in any way. Get them sold that your SWE skill set can help the manager look good, and then use that proven performance to help them help you make the transition you want to make. Reaching out within the company to SWE contacts doesn't need manager's blessing, and it's typically a different thing. Can let manager know you're interested in SWE work and would love to connect with more at the company who work in the field, so that they're not surprised, but Leon finds this to be two separate things, just like if you were running into people at an in-person company. Leon thinks that now that people are remote, it's pretty easy to connect with people virtually within the company, so Alec should be sure to reach out and connect with other people in the company in the line of work that he's hoping to transition into. "I'm Alec, and we're all remote, so I'm trying to connect with more people at our company. I would love to talk to you about the work you're doing. Is there a good time we can do that?" Many people may not say yes, but we won't find the ones who will if we're not out there asking.

Anonymuz: Recent interview. Told people at Banki Brunch about it. Interview today. 2nd round interview. Pretty well. Senior position. They're asking him for a virtual interview this Thursday. All technologies that he is unfamiliar with. Very little experience in all of them, and feeling fearful since it's a senior role. How to prepare to the point where he can accurately give his best performance? Past interview was supposed to be technical, and Anony kept asking interviewer a bunch of questions and skirted past the technical stuff. Not confident in his ability to be technically-skilled enough

Leon: Go into an interview as positively as possible. Every person on the interview is your best friend and wants to see you win. Often, they will respond accordingly. Can ask if there are any other languages that we can interview in, even if they don't mention it. Ask for clarification on this in advance. They might have just thrown out technologies, and didn't mean it to be restricted to this.

(Michael: NPR's Ted Radio Hour just featured a Stanford professor who researchers this. If we presume the best in people, they will rise to the occasion, and will reciprocate our generous view of them. If we do the opposite, they will respond negatively, accordingly, too:

https://www.npr.org/2025/01/10/g-s1177-41448/cynicism-is-a-trap-heres-why-hopeful-skepticism-is-better )

- Anony said they're going to go through resume and talk to him about things to see what he thinks. Not really coding challenges, but may do code review. Main thing seems to be going through his resume

Leon: Maybe you don't need to worry about languages as much, then. A tutorial on languages can be helpful, and translate what you've done into those languages. Can try to push the interview to Monday for more time, but if not, go in with the best mindset possible, and remember we're just hoping to get one offer out of 60 networked-in attempts. (This is the 100Devs wisdom. It usually doesn't take more than 60 networked-in attempts using the 100Devs recommendations to get an offer, so just focus on making those attempts to the best of your ability and the offer will come automatically. Try not to get too caught up in each attempt and how they went, as we can only control our own performance and actions, not whether or not an offer will materialize. Just make the attempt, learn from it if you can (and assess if what you're doing is working, including by checking in with the 100Devs community if you see areas of concern), and keep making more attempts.) Just do your best on this, and then focus on the 59 other attempts you're going to be making. Be confident that you can do your best. Not "I hope this is going to work," but, "I'm going to do my best with this and leave it all on the field." Attitude makes a difference. Leon tells Anony that they've got this!

Daniel Hopkins: New to 100Devs. Applying and got a job, but how does he go through 100Devs now that he has a full-time job? Unemployed for an entire year. Fearful he will be let go, even if he thinks that might be irrational. Nothing wrong with the job so far, but he wants to keep his options open.

Leon: The Biggest mistake is getting a job and then stopping everything in terms of growth and development. Keep doing the stuff you were doing, but probably in smaller amounts. One meetup a week, 2-3 coffee chats per week. That way, when it's time for us to need a network, it's there, and has compounded over time. Smaller pieces will add up, and we'll have that safety net if we need it. Every 6 months, people should be ready to interview. An important part of being an SWE is always being ready and not getting too comfortable (and deconditioning our skills). Every 6 months, we should interview, regardless. Helps us see what opportunities are out there and see where our weak points are so that we know what we need to develop. Too many people wait 2-3 years to get that feedback, and then they have so much to work on that it can be overwhelming, especially if we've just been laid off or something and are desperate to find a new role. Small bits every week can keep you ready, so you have less work to do when you need to do it.

Daniel says this really resonates because he stagnated in his job before his last unemployment period, and it hurt to recover from. Leon says don't do this again, and use that as a learning opportunity ;)

LinkedIn states that he has been out of work for a year and has been in his new job for less than six months. Post-Covid, Leon says no one cares about this anymore. Layoffs are rampant. COVID complications are rampant. Get a client, and then you were freelancing during the gaps, and that's more than enough. Even without that, though, the gaps aren't as big a deal as they used to be, especially in knowledge-based job.

- Leon says Daniel may be new, but he's official now, even if he doesn't feel part of the 100Devs community yet. "You're knighted now. It's official. You're stuck with us."

-- [Then he reacted, potentially to the chat, that we should just ban Daniel, now? :p Totally kidding, of course. 100Devs doesn't ban kind people.]

"Got offered a 2nd phone interview for S.Engineer (small Healthcare startup). First interview was 15min. 2nd will be 15 min long but indepth. What to prep for?"

- Leon: Have to ask! The more you know going into the interview, the better. If you don't know what's going to happen in an interview, you messed up. Ask and try to dive deep on GlassDoor and other places so you have a good idea of what may happen if they don't get back to you, but asking is always best. No one knows more about what's going to happen than the people who are structuring the interview.

"What's your advice on starting your own saas before getting a full time position"

- Bad idea, unless you're independently wealthy. Get a 9-5 full-time role, and then you can work on it in your free time if you want, but realize the chance that something like this is going to succeed and financially support you is +very+ low.

"Is a 5% response rate for coffee chats normal?"

- Spraying and praying with no personalization? This is a common response. More personalized message according to the 100Devs recommendations should be better than this. Check the 100Devs courses and ask for help on the Discord if you're not getting the results you want!

Leon's very excited about the new year and all the great energy he's seeing in the 100Devs community. See you in Friday's Huddle!

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-01-16
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Or, they can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

I took notes on mobile (with my thumbs :p ), so this was less polished than normal. I hope it helps someone, though!

100Devs Huddle - Friday 1/16/26 - 3 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

Amanda onstage asking a question – Salary negotiations after final interview

Email to Amanda that Amanda’s not sure about: “revisit salary expectations. Share range she had in mind, and any flexibility.” Will tell her by Tuesday if she’s hired, after pushing off telling her

Leon doesn’t like going hard on negotiations until an offer is in hand. Stay flexible, get the offer, and then negotiate for more specifics.

top of their range is the bottom of hers, so leon says stay flexible, get the offer and then negotiate from there – try not to spook them. May be other candidates in the mix and they are trying to get the cheapest. Stay vague until you get the offer. Once we have the offer, the power shifts. Don’t do anything that could jeopardize the offer. Once you get the offer you can go harder because they have picked you at that point, and then you can work with them to make the position possible for you +++ Screener on Tuesday. No experience with tech (.Net)

do a tutorial over the weekend and then port a project you have to use that technology stack instead. Build a project related to what they do as well. Try to interview with this tech stack more times, so that you’re not wasting this effort. +++ Hitlist only after you finish a program (like 100Devs)

then adapt to popular technologies appearing often in your hit list, especially when it’s common in the local market +++ Networking events are all about making friends. If you make one friend, it’s a win. Don’t go in with too high expectations about what you’re going to achieve or what to ask. Try to do more listening than talking; ask relevant questions to event, since people are all there for the same thing; read “how to win friends and influence people” if you’re extremely anxious about social interactions (100Devs standard); leon goes for food/drinks first, and then starts talking to the first person he can to get the ball rolling. Hardest part is always getting started. Just go and see what happens +++ (3:17pm pt) 4 days not usually enough to build a polished project. 100 hours project is 100 hours to try to help it get polished

with 4 days, have a single core feature, make it look decent. Leon would stay away from Android and focus on web dev unless it’s very job specific. Projects based on past experiences are good, or related to what companies you’re focusing on are doing ++ Question about interview prep (Michael missed this one, sorry.) ++ Let the market decide what you’re ready for. If you apply to 30-40 senior roles and you’re not getting interviews, maybe you’re not ready for senior. Experience levels are made up, tho, and many 100devs alums go right to senior ++ 3:21pm Success is a numbers thing. Can freelance after 5 weeks, but need to go until you get a client. Don’t just call five people and then give up. Start small and then build. How to Freelance 100Devs class should get us there. Online presence should be locked in professionally, too. No “aspiring”. You want people to be confident that you’re competent. Just keep putting the work in ++ 3:24pm Key use case for AI is to explain a code base and identify low-hanging fruit to help with. Great for interns. Also never eat alone and ask others about what you might want to focus on to make a positive impact

bring lunch or buy your own. Shouldn’t be buying lunch for others ++ 3:25pm “After 2+ years of networking/events & growing LinkedIn to 200+, only landed “free” job (Career advisor recommend to stay for exp.) (Bay area btw.) Thoughts?” 200 LinkedIn contacts is like a week of work. Just need to keep at it However, Hitlisting and Networking, are two different things Here’s how Leon would do it:

Hiring Cafe to start identifying companies that are hiring. Starting local is usually the highest yield –840+ companies hiring in the bay area — 33% are good fit, using familiar tech. Should talk to 3 people at each company. Minimum of 900 contacts

500 other companies should have contact from you to recruiter about your availability and interest in roles

Hitlisting is targeting companies that have recently hired. Not just networking

use apollo to find emails. Customize messages for good fit. Should do about 10 customized per hour. Should see something after 900 contacts. If not, something is off with your online presence or resume, but this process helps you determine what to focus on

Apollo should get most contacts with bay area companies, as they’re likely fairly well-established – 4 different 100devs classes on how to do hitlisting. Start there +++ 3:31pm pt “Chatting w/ connection from non-engineering Microsoft Tues, planning to just get general advice after applying for two roles. Anything specific I should ask them?”

just reach out and ask about how to pursue open roles and make sure you’re doing the right stuff and know who to contact about software dev roles. Nothing really specific. See what happens from the contact

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-01-20
html:
[Shout outs to @an.jomi for sharing the 100Devs ChatGPT story crafting tool, and to @leahtcodes and our other 100Devs community members for being so helpful in the chat during these sessions. Please don’t just rely on these notes to get Huddle recaps, Everybody. The chat is always gold, too, especially since there are always 100Devs community heroes looking to help support our community and Leon during the Huddles. Please be sure to be one of them whenever you can, too, both during the Huddles and outside of them. 100Devs is the community that we make it to be. Let’s do Leon proud and help him to make it wonderful <3 ]

[Rushed notes, and I’m behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.] [20260120 - 4:26 PM PT Edit: Alright, I did a first pass spellcheck and edit, but if something is still unclear, please let me know.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Notepad++ during the Huddle. As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

100Devs Huddle - Tues 1/20/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[2:32 PM PT] Huddle Started (Leon hosting, as usual.)

“LEON, I GOT DA JOB AT O’REILLYYY, now in the salary negotiation phase but know I nailed that 2nd behavioral b/c of your CAR method & Anki studying!! Thank you!!” - Amanda Leon: That’s how we start off, folks! That’s how we do it!

If you want to talk about the negotiation piece, that’s our specialty. We can do it live on stage, or you can send us a message.

Most importantly, if you get the discount codes for the job, you can just let Leon know <3 (“It’s the book place, ya’ll.” That’s a code Leon wants ;p He sounds like he’s joking, for reference <3 )

100Devs methods work. We just have to put the work in to internalize the 100Devs methods and wisdom

“What do I talk to people who I coffee chat with? I’ve read Madeleine’s guide but natural convos are not my skill.” - Richard Whittington Leon recently incorporated some things that helped him. Podcast: “What does it take to be charismatic?”

Comes down to asking one more question than you would normally ask.

When you get into an elevator, what do you do?

Normal person: Stare into the void – Say just a little more than usual: “Hi.”

Just work to up your default by one step.
