Leon says hello and smiles to everyone, so, for him, he’ll now ask how they’re doing as the next step for improved charisma. – You may not get anything back, but sometimes you will, and with practice, it will become easier to talk to people, and you’ll become more charismatic – Leon has gotten great feedback from this. Can do this anywhere. Maybe in line at the store.

Sometimes the conversation might end after you exchange the initial pleasantries, but it doesn’t have to. Add that extra sentence. Cashier at the register: “Oh, you switched from plastic to paper. How has that been going?”

Michael: "Research shows even introverts like small talk, even if they think they won’t, so please put this stuff into practice people <3 " – People tend to treat us the way we treat them, so be friendly and trusting, and they’ll be much more likely to do the same to us <3

Leon thinks this adds up over time, and then you’ll be in a great place to feel much more confident during a coffee chat, where you can put these skills into practice in a way that can directly help you professionally

Coffee Chats can just be connections and getting to know people. It’s nice if it’s about tech, but it doesn’t have to be. Look to ask follow-up questions on topics. Those strong connections are what are going to – From Leah in the chat: Best coffee chats involved talking about “Hello Kitty” or random things [

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1463301809996300371

]

The key is practicing being good at open-ended conversations, and you can practice this everywhere in your life

First 10-20 Coffee Chats may not be the best, but we’re learning and growing, so each Coffee Chat will hopefully be a little better than the next, just like anything else you attempt that you’ve never done before. We just have to keep making the attempts and look to improve at each subsequent attempt. [2:41 PM PT]

Another big key is to follow up, and Leon likes warning people that he’ll be following up: “This was a great conversation. I’m planning to follow up in a month and check in.” Then, you follow up in a month. Most people don’t follow up, so if you’re following up, you’re ahead of the game

Leon Recommends Madeleine’s guide: ​

https://abrupt-acoustic-c3f.notion.site/How-to-Coffee-Chat-12d4b7b6398f8075baf4f7a4e550bc31

– Madeleine says in the chat she’s coming up with a 2.0 version.

Leah says the best way to go about Coffee Chats is to just aim to make friends.

“I see 100devers who started later and finished before me with many tools on their belt, Im still doing 100hrs project, I feel behind how to build confidence ?”

Comparison is the thief of joy. Just compare yourself to yourself. If you’re a little better day by day, you’re doing great. Everyone starts off at different places, with different backgrounds and skill sets, and with different obligations in life. It’s never going to be a fair comparison to compare two different people. Comparing your past self with your current self, and keeping your future self in mind, is the best way to judge how you’re doing and if you’re moving in the direction you want to go. [2:44 PM PT]

The difference between a successful SWE and someone who didn’t succeed at SWEngineering is that one person didn’t give up on the way there. As long as you don’t give up, you’ll get there eventually. Just don’t give up – If you’re doing a 100-Hour project, you’re already ahead of the game. Funny that people get that far and still think they’re struggling. You’re already so far ahead of thousands who did one class and gave up. Tens of thousands of people said they wanted to get where you are, but they gave up.

Cohort 2: First class was like 7K people. Second class was 5K. Last class of the cohort was like 2-3K. Big attrition, and we have no idea what people had going on in their lives. Hopefully, they’ll come back <3, or maybe they were just taking the cohort a little more slowly. (4 years since Cohort 2, for reference: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1463303441945202740

… Leon noticed people were pushing for Cohort 3 in the chat, which he is still planning to do :p )

“I feel like I’m taking too long to build projects because I keep looking for best practices. Please help? 😒”

Leon thinks learning best practices is great. Take that time so that every project is better, unless your goal is to get a viable project so you can apply to a job. If so, just get the viable project done, and you can polish it later. No such thing as too long if you’re meeting the goals that you have for yourself. – Lots of people focus too much on building projects, but that’s not sufficient to get you a job. Don’t use project-building as an excuse not to job hunt. Finding best practices with personal projects is one of the best things to use projects for. Just don’t use project work as an excuse to hide from the “harder” job search stuff, especially the stuff that involves talking to other people.

“What types of projects should I include in my portfolio to get started, being relatively new to web development?” - Londa

Best projects are ones that explain why you’re showing up.

Previously a restaurant server? Build a POS system that solves one of the significant issues that you had as a server. Medication tracker, if that was an issue when you’re a nurse. These projects can also help explain your transition to software development, and can build up to a 100-hour project that is a much bigger thing. That helps your resume make sense

Projects that you find fun are also great.

There’s no magic project that’s going to get you a job, though.

“What platform can you recommend for full stack deployment + maybe domain please, ideally free of charge for portfolio projects as well as potential freelancing”

Railway and Render are the big ones right now, depending on what you’re building. Vercel can work, too.

Leon likes

Fly.io

as well.

Appwrite and Vercel is what Leon is using right now. 100Devs community may have some free credits floating around on the Discord.

Railway, Render,

Fly.io

, maybe Vercel, and potentially something else. All have trade-offs, though. There’s no Heroku anymore, unfortunately. “Completely free, no weird stuff.” doesn’t exist anymore

Once you’re in application season, save up $5 so you can have no cold starts with applications. Cold starts can make it harder for a recruiter to see something because they may not stick around for a Railway instance to boot up. $5 to get through the application season seems worth it, even if Leon doesn’t like telling people to spend money.

Fly is good, but it’s not easy to get a free account for everyone. (Can’t get it on a VPN or in certain parts of the country, for instance.) [2:52 PM PT] – Spam detection with Fly might be tough. Github Pages and Netlify are good for front-end, too

“I applied for a remote role that might not be eligible for residency in my state.(CA) Can you explain again if you can set up an address out of state?”

Don’t try to get around residency requirements, in most cases. Typically, if people know they’re going to move to Florida, for example, they can open up a UPS-store address (address looks like a normal address, not a PO Box), and that helps give them a local address that can look more serious to companies. Companies typically see PO Boxes and know that they’re potentially less legitimate. [2:54 PM PT]

Leon’s not a lawyer, so be careful with residency requirements. The address thing can help with a transition to a new local market, though, especially if you haven’t made the move yet, but would be very interested in making the move if you could find a job there.

“After you get a coffee chat from a hitlist message, when would you ask for an explicit/implicit referral? Do you wait for the company to post job publicly first”

Yeah. Next time you see a job that pops up, reach out to your connection and ask that connection who the best person would be for you to talk to about this role? That’s what 100Devs means by an implicit referral.

The longer you can obviously have space between the meeting and the ask, the better. If you’re in turbo mode, you don’t have time for that.

“Can you explain what it means not to sign exclusivity again?”

Certain recruiters will ask you to sign an exclusivity agreement with them, which means that only jobs that you can get are the ones that they send to you.

#1 Advice from Leon: Never sign exclusivity with a specific recruiter. Lots of shady recruiters do this to lock you into them. Any good recruiter will not do this. Any job that you get while under an exclusivity agreement, even if you get the job without the recruiter’s involvement at all, will get something like a 20-30% reduction in first-year salary based on the exclusivity agreement’s cut with the recruiter. From chat: “Nothing to lose when asking for a referral. If you don’t ask, it’s a no.”

People getting afraid of asking for a referral have main character syndrome. No one cares that much. Worst thing if you ask? They bring it up negatively with their partner at dinner as a conversation piece exactly one time, and that’s the last time they ever think about you. (Michael: and, if they reacted profoundly negatively to you, especially for no reason, you probably want to focus on more supportive people anyway :p Otherwise, maybe you just had a bad day, and you can “Wow” them the next time you might interact with them. Just think how impressive it’s going to be for you to hit a home run in an interview if they vaguely remember you falling on your face in the last interview you might have had with them months ago.)

Don’t limit yourself in this way, when a potentially 6-figure opportunity might be on the other end of it. That’s $3 million over a 30-year career. That’s a really good thing that could happen, which is not uncommon in SWE. If they say no, there is Nothing to lose, especially when we compare the potential risks to the potential benefits. Why would you be worried about asking for a referral? Just ask the question! – Many companies have referral incentives, so if you’re a successful hire, your referrer will make money! So, just ask. It is very unlikely that anyone is going to hold you asking against you, and definitely not for long.

“Why shouldn’t you use the word ‘connect.’ Like: “happy to connect” or “Let’s connect.” Is it weird to use the word ‘connect?’” - bob on Slido

“Connect” is just what spammers use. Word that folks use when they don’t know you. Seems like a bias against it. – Feels bot-like

Leon wants to connect with people, but in the context of a “let’s connect”, it’s often a soft ask… Leon’s not sure how to explain it

“Leon, I have no idea who you are, but I would love to connect.” “Why? If I have no idea who you are, why would I want to connect and make people in my network think you’re something worth connecting with?” – Seems like someone is trying to up their LinkedIn numbers

Authentic asks to connect are great, but that’s what you should be doing and asking for. Mention something in a profile, talk about something you know about them that you like

Example: Someone showed Leon how to get a discount on Legos to get the new Pokémon set. That’s now Leon’s new best friend :p Saving Leon money and providing immediate value? Instant connection with Leon. Knew Leon’s special interest. Helped him in a way that cost them Nothing. GREAT strategy

100Devs doesn’t gatekeep, especially in education, but Leon says Pokémon is the exception. Anything that could cost Leon money with Pokémon? Kindness has limits. He needs to keep those discounts :p (If this is someone’s first Huddle, Leon is a +big+ Pokémon fan.)

“How to deal with rejection sensitivity dysphoria (RSD)?” - bob

Fear of rejection? Exposure practice.

Leon isn’t a therapist, but at least one month out of the year, Leon will ask for a discount on every single thing for his exposure practice. This annual month has been the biggest unlock for him to get over the fear of rejection.

On the Hunt, you’ll get lots of rejection. Coffee Chats, Interviews, lots of places to get rejection. It’s just part of the process. Need to slog through the rejection, and it’s often the most overwhelming part of the process because you’ve sunk tons of time and effort into this process, and now you’re having a hard time getting the thing you’ve been going for. This, and the very beginning, is the time that 100Devs loses the most people.

Discount request on everything? Great practice. Any time Leon buys something during his rejection exposure month, he asks someone for a discount. Starbucks free stuff happens all the time. At Target, a cashier used their own phone to price-match with other stores and then gave Leon the discounts. Nothing bad happens when Leon asks for discounts. Sometimes they will chuckle or say no, and that’s very little downside. Upside is he gets +FREE STUFF+ or discounts. Upside is asymmetrically larger. Keep this in mind whenever you’re afraid of rejection. Rejection is Nothing, but the potential upside when you do succeed is incredible. Get comfortable with the rejections so that you can keep moving towards the successes. “Success is going from failure to failure with no loss of enthusiasm.” - Anonymous – From chat: “You’ll be surprised what retail workers will do for you if you’re just nice to them.” [3:08 PM PT]

“saw that a friend works at a company i’m interviewing for; they’re nontechnical though. best segue to ask if they know anyone I can speak to? thanks 💜”

Just ask. Some people might be uncomfortable and ghost you. Others might not know who to refer you to, and tell you that. No worries. Just ask. You might even help them determine that their company has a referral bonus program they can benefit from. Just ask

“Kindness costs you Nothing”, so remember this as well when you’re in a position to help other people

Real friends? You’ll give them everything they need to succeed at their interview with your company, as you know they’ll do the same for you. That’s real friendship in Leon’s book. Hopefully, that’s how your friendships work, too.

[3:10 PM PT] “Well I gotta head out I girl bossed too close to the sun and my 100 hours project is now an app w a grant and now I got an office building all thanks to an old connection back when I was learning HTML 3 years ago thanks to 100Devs! So please keep track of ur connections you never know!” - MarciaC95 in the chat: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1463309277300002917

“Got a job offer and negotiating salary bc its lower than current. Thinking of focusing on tech skill and networking for year but unsure if job in edu can help”

Ask them to help you. “Please help me say yes. I really want to be here, and I see the long-term growth potential, but I have these issues. Less money than currently making…” – You’ll be amazed at how they might be able to help you, but they can’t unless you share with them that you need help.

Leon wouldn’t disclose the exact current salary, unless he knew the people were looking out for Leon specifically, but he would just say the salary was lower than his current one, and that’s a major problem for him.

“what is your suggestion for someone who graduated in CS but didnt know what they were doing? currently working through 100Dev on week 7 atm.”

Many 100Devs alums went through a CS program but needed 100Devs skills to get a job. Great interview skills, great projects. Focus on how to get a job and freelancing portions. This is an excellent place for you. Make sure you can translate the skills in your brain into tangible things that can demonstrate those skills. The degree is not enough to show you can hit the ground running on day one and solve someone’s problems.

“Im documenting my learning journey on youtube @christencodes. How can I leverage my channel when looking for a job? Do employers care about github or youtube?”

Leah would be the one to answer this, and she’s answering it in the chat.

Make sure 100Devs project … (Michael missed this. He thinks it could either have been “Make this part of your 100 Hours project”, or “Use your 100 Hours Project as content”

Leah answered in the chat later, at 3:18 PM PT.

Here it is: "Youtube, twitter, and twitch can definitely help when applying for jobs! I would say to post short clips or updates from what you worked on during your streams or videos to post on twitter. Also possibly writing blog posts about these same things as well. All of these are great ways to build in public and help showcase your skills to employers. Through streaming and creating content, I’ve gotten multiple job offers and referrals. As well as skipping some parts of an interview process since they already knew from my content I’d be a good fit

Doing these things also helps open you up for more roles such as devrel, developer advocate, tech evangelist, etc. But even for dev roles it makes you more competitive because it shows you can talk through technical problems etc" ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1463311385571102902

“How do I begin crafting my story? I have a ton of experience doing all sorts of different things, and the adhd was strong with me. how do i connect the pieces?”

There’s a 100 Devs ChatGPT tool for this that was recently shared in chat for this purpose. Start there. [Michael found it: "

https://chatgpt.com/g/g-68e95630e9b481918051aa54d9deec8a-bob-your-story-crafting-coach-from-100devs

" ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1463310345878900948

] [3:15 PM PT]

“will have chat with founder at startup for QA/Jr. SWE role. Aim to make them laugh twice and remember me due to I failed first round 3 time. Any other tips?”

If you failed the first round three times, don’t worry about making them laugh; figure out why you failed and fix that piece. Understand market, space, and understand +deeply+ about the company. That should go over well with the founder. However, the key is to figure out why you’ve been failing. That’s more important.

“What are generally likable traits that a cto or founder would like vs fellow engineer?”

Product experience. Ability to deliver on your own. Technical expertise that the team will be missing. Nothing too wild. They likely just want good SWEs.

“Can we have Jobuary or Fabjobary plese? Would be greatful for more of this patterns what it is/ why used/ how things works and why technical vocabulary prep”

Freelancing February was mentioned in the chat, which Leon likes, as he’s a big fan of freelancing and how paid clients look much more impressive on a resume than unpaid projects. However, Leon’s family all has the flu, and Leon doesn’t have it yet (but may miss Friday if he gets it). He doesn’t really have the time to commit to every day for a month, which something like a Jobuary would require, and he doesn’t want to commit to starting something he isn’t sure he can finish.

[3:18 PM] Leah put her response to the thing before in chat. [Michael included this in the relevant section above.]

“what is beter take low salary apprenticeship and have job but low salary for 2 years contract or spend more time learning to apply and survive on entry level”

Leon hates the 2-year contract companies. Pay you half to a third of what you should be making, label it as an apprenticeship, and then you have to pay if you end early. No. That’s garbage. Some people go through them and are happy, but Leon hates these types of agreements and the companies that use them and thinks we should avoid both like the plague.

[3:20 PM PT] “How do I get started with content”

Just start. The first version of 100Devs was in a garage, and his audience could hear the AC blowing on him the entire time. Just start, and it snowballs. You figure out what works and what you like, and you improve over time, just like with anything else. The hardest part is always starting. Just start. Don’t wait for the magic to come. Show up consistently, and the magic happens.

==== Start Sidebar === Michael just heard other people say this exact thing:

"

https://www.hiddenbrain.org/podcast/you-2-0-how-to-break-out-of-a-rut/

"…Shankar Vedantam:

So a motto that recurs in your book, Adam, is action above all. What does that mean?

Adam Alter:

Yeah. It means that… A lot of the book is about the emotional experience of being stuck and then about the mental strategies we can use to move forward. But all of that ultimately is in the service of action. Being stuck is about being physically in a particular place or mentally in a particular place and wanting to be somewhere else. The only way you can change that is not changing how you emotionally appraise the situation, not with mental strategies, but ultimately the thing you have to do more than anything else is to act, is to move in a particular direction. … “”

https://www.npr.org/2025/12/18/nx-s1-5647765/kumail-nanjiani-standup-night-thoughts-the-big-sick

" … And even now, the biggest, I think, asset I have is working really hard. Like, I prepare a lot. I - when I’m writing, I - and it’s something I’ve taken from Emily as well because I used to think of writing as this sort of thing where, like, you have to wait for God to speak to you. And she’s like, no, you got to go to church every day, and sometimes God shows up and sometimes God doesn’t show up.

MARTIN: Oh, that’s good (laughter).

NANJIANI: Yeah. It’s really, really good. Like…

MARTIN: Yeah.

NANJIANI: And she has a real ritual. She lights a candle every day. She sits down, and she will write from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. every day. And I’ve learned - I’ve picked that up from her, too, and from my dad. So I think working hard. And I think from my mom, I’ve just - there’s, like, a really appealing softness to her where she’s, like, just a very, very compassionate person. And my dad is compassionate, too. I don’t mean to say that these qualities are mutually exclusive. But I think I’ve learned that from her, is just to be, like - just to be soft with the people around me. Sometimes I’m not soft to myself. But I think - and I also think I don’t want to get too much into specifics because it’s not my story to tell. …"

==== End Sidebar ===

“How many points of contact should I pursue at each company on my hitlist.”

Current thinking from Leon: Go to Hiring Cafe and find 600 companies that have open positions in your area. This is a common thing for Leon when he’s helping people in a big metro – Half of those will be close to what fits your skill set and experience (Other half may not be good fits. Maybe you’re a JavaScript dev and the other half don’t use JavaScript) – 300 companies: Find someone at that company, and do the normal, find something about them and send a non-generic message. Typically, 10 per hour, 3 hours per day, is a good target for Leon, and he’ll do this until he gets through the entire list.

One person with targeted messaging for the first 300 contacts.

Then, look at the other half of 300 companies that weren’t a good fit, and make that message a little more generic when you find the person to contact, because these weren’t good fits

Then go back and find another person at the 300 good fits, and send a targeted message

Repeat this alternating process three times, and you get three contacts at each company. This alternating process also likely means you’ll be getting responses throughout the process, which you can follow up on. At the very end, you’ll have made 900 contacts, and should hopefully get a paid opportunity out of one of them, and might not have to go through all 900 anyway.

3 messages to three different people at a company is ideal, and the above process is a system. It’s the trough of sorrow, though, so just keep trudging through it. You’ll get there, as long as you keep going. “When you’re going through hell. Keep going.” (Don’t stop and get stuck in Hell :p) For most people, not having a system is what gets them stuck in Hell, because they give up.

“how do i answer the salary question in an early interview and no band is posted and they said they don’t have bands”

“What have you budgeted for the role”

If they don’t tell you, you say you’re looking for the fair market rate for a developer with your skill set and experience. You’ll need to look it up as you learn more about the role.

“Posted salary range is in my range”, but typical 100Devs negotiation is you’re always looking to be the upper edge of a range. Company wants to give you the least they can get away with, and you want to get the most you can get because you’re the one looking out most for you.

Give them a range if they press you: “80k-170K is what I’ve been looking for.”

Most companies will eventually cave and give you a number. Just need to practice negotiation, and 100Devs can help with that.

Leon jokes that 80K is his one-day-a-week-of-work, fully remote price.

Just like everything else, you have to practice negotiating salary and say it out loud. Practice at Banki Brunch, practice with ChatGPT in voice mode. AI Prompt: “Pretend you’re a recruiter that wants to give me the least salary you can, and I want the most. Help me roleplay”. You can do these things. Just need to go out there and practice using all of these tools, and do do that serious practice consistently.

“Any resources you suggest to learn SQL quickly? I know MayanWolfe made a series on YT 😄 Any others?”

Leon recommends starting with MayanWolfe’s content, then actually building something with it

“Should I go for FOUNDING roles as entry level?”

Can’t lose a job you don’t have, but founding-level engineering roles are harder jobs, typically.

“Don’t let your dreams be dreams”, even if these aren’t the jobs Leon would be looking for, especially for a recent 100Devs graduate.

“In your opinion what are the best type of 100Hours projects”

Projects that help explain why you would be a good fit as an SWE with the company

Try to tie past experience with current experience

“what are good projects ideas for python and flask one week. or 2 solo commitment for portfolio to learn skills what are good skills to learn at entry lvl”

YouTube may try to sell you videos about the perfect project, but Leon says it doesn’t exist. A good project does at least one of three things: bridges your experience from your past to your present as a SWE, is in line with the company you’re applying for, or demonstrates your skill. Outside of that, it doesn’t really matter. Just build stuff

Start applying early, and the interviews will help you determine what types of projects you need to build to be more successful in those interviews.

[3:31 PM PT] Chat discussing 100Devs Blog, and Leah may be writing for this

“what is beter spend more time to learn Js or apply to get interview practice. Did not started 100h project, failing interview will burn few bridges on the way”

Don’t take that many interviews until you’ve completed the full 100Devs. Everything at 100Devs is designed to help you succeed at the interviews. Big O and 100Hours are key for interview success. Don’t skip stuff. If you finish 100Devs, you’ll maximize your success. Leon always recommends finishing a job-focused course before you start seriously looking for a job. You should be hitlisting, coffee chatting, and networking the entire time, as it’s built into the program itself, but the focus should be finishing 100Devs until you’re done with 100Devs if you want to maximize your success on the job hunt

“Hey Leon, when will we be able to get early access to the jobs platform?”

Leon’s alpha testing this. He’ll be done soon, but he has been working on it. Stay tuned. Leon wants to get it to a point where he’s happy enough with it that other people can start contributing to it. He’s not there yet, though.

“where can we find start-ups and co-founder roles? (asking for a friend)”

Where start-up founders hang out. Y Combinator has a “find a cofounder” service.

Cofounders should really know each other, says Leon, who has founded companies with multiple people. Founders should know each other intimately. It’s worse than getting married, as you’ll be spending more time and money with this person, and much more quickly, than you would with a significant other. Anything that could go wrong in a marriage could likely go wrong with a startup, and would go wrong much more quickly

Good to do experimental foundings, with a 3-month window, just to see if you can work well together and figure out where the pain points are to work on

For the record: Leon says marriage is amazing, and he loves marriage.

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-01-23
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Notepad++ during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 1/23/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle Started at 2:32 PM PT, Leon was Hosting]

Amanda just took the job at O’Reilly! (please see those successes in the #follow-along-materials Huddle announcement): ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/738891289071714388/1464371483584495737

Good healthcare to @danvgar after succeeding as a software engineer. Yet another benefit to taking the program seriously

@brazilianCoder : Had to be 100% remote, but they kept at the grind and pulled it off

“Client that wants a Wordpress site. Is this resume worthy?”

Yup. Job as a Freelancer is to solve a client’s problems. Doesn’t matter how you solve it. It’s all different tools to help solve problems. Different tools are needed for different jobs, so just focus on what you did to help the client – Playing the game optimally? Applying for a backend Java role, and the only experience in Wordpress? That would be odd to a recruiter. Not a lesser experience, but we have to craft a story that makes sense when someone is only spending six seconds looking at our resume. Highlight something about the Wordpress project that is as relevant as possible to your pursuit (Not PHP if you’re looking at a Java-related role, for example)

[Going to the Slido. 2:38 PM PT]

Old laptop doesn’t matter for 100Devs. Just need access to a web browser, and you should be fine. Leon used a chromebook for the first 2-3 years on his software engineering career. Most things are in the cloud, but it is good to have the option to develop locally on your machine – RAM and SSDs in Laptops are very expensive right now. Usual recommendations are to get an old Thinkpad (X220, T210+). However, stuff inside them is getting expensive, so they are getting hard to find — Older model stuff with the RAM soldered on is probably what we’re looking for [From Michael: Organizations like Human-I-T help connect people with affordable tech stuff.

https://www.human-i-t.org/

]

“Graduated recently in computer science, struggling to find a job at the moment based in new zealand. Found a 100 devs not sure were to get started”

Need a large project you can talk about, great resume and LinkedIn, and fantastic narrative. Once you have all that, then you move into HitListing, which is how we reach out to people to form connections and get referrals. At least 4 classes on the 100Devs playlist on getting jobs. Look at those, and go through the process, then come back to 100Devs Discord and ask more tailored questions based on what’s happening. Need to get that Baseline, and work on things like Banki, and then you just can start putting the work in to connect with the opportunities

(100Devs Class Playlist: ​

https://communitytaught.org/class/all

)

[2:41 PM PT]

Mac Minis are also a really good option right now. Fairly expensive, but great if you have the extra money. Decommissioned Chromebooks and Optimus desktops when big corporations have to rotate (every 2-3 years), so try to look for those. Used laptop should be gettable for like $200

Best Buy Open Box can be great for good deals

Library will often rent tech to use for like a week at a time, so always check local resources, too

Leon uses Linux on his Chromebooks as a dual or singular boot, and that gives him more options than the default ChromeOS, which is very limited

Pixelbook with Linux is great

[2:44 PM PT] “Is there a time frame that you can tell when you will be adding people to the group on LinkedIn? And how important do you think LinkedIn is for gaining work?”

Once a month. Need a photo and a title to get added

“How can I find the motivation to practice LeetCode until I can solve medium-level problems consistently? How long does it usually take to reach that level?”

Super important, as people often look at this first to find out about us. Have a presence that makes sense and has a good story. Danny Thompson (100Devs community hero) has a bunch of content on optimizing our LinkedIn

[2:46 PM PT] “How can I find the motivation to practice LeetCode until I can solve medium-level problems consistently? How long does it usually take to reach that level?”

Depends on why. Someone in celebrations got a job with no technical interview, and that’s common with half the people who get jobs at 100Devs. – Going for a certain caliber of company? Need to be at that level for FAANG-level. Commit to a system that can get you there. NeetCode has great videos and explanations in a structured way. Aim for a week on each set to reinforce specific problems. NeetCode 150 or whatever gets you a great foundation, and then you need to put in the practice. 3 months is usually what it takes to get up to speed if you’re already a software engineer looking to interview. 6 months if this is your first time through these types of problem. Your own speed may vary

Motivation is an entirely other story. Build systems that allow you to get the things you want in life. Structure your environment so that even on your worst day, you can still get the things you need to do done to get you where you want to be in the future

[2:48 PM PT]

Leon has not yet sent a letter in 2026 yet. Doesn’t send them out unless he has something new to say. Doesn’t want to spam people. Respond to one of Leon’s e-mails to allow your e-mail server to recognize Leon’s e-mails at not simply promotional

Stick to JavaScript when teaching, is Leon’s advice. Get language to work off of

FastAI is best for working on AI fundamentals, says Leon

“Whats the best way to get coffee chats and how do you make the most out of them”

Get a referral from a friend. “Bob, we had a wonderful chat last week. Anyone else I should talk to?” Snowball method. Best way – 2nd best way, go meet people in person or ask people online. Be very broad in the beginning while still learning

Post-100Devs completion, be very surgical and HitList seriously, as you’re going for a job

If you need a guide on how to Coffee Chat, community hero Madeleine has one for us ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1464392270840074489

[Direct link: ​

https://abrupt-acoustic-c3f.notion.site/How-to-Coffee-Chat-12d4b7b6398f8075baf4f7a4e550bc31

]

“Whats the best way to build your linkedin, like how often should you be posting and what should you be posting”

LinkedIn cares about use. Popping LinkedIn will not lead to job. Don’t hyperfocus on followers for followers sake. It’s what we do with the audience that matters – Time on platform is what matters. Before you post, scroll for 15-20 minutes. This is part of their algorithm, for some reason. Warm up your account, then post – Care about replies to other people. Replies are a great day to get LinkedIn going (great for Networking, too)

Post your wins. Anything that you have learned, and may be a step ahead of what anyone else is doing. – If you put work into learning something, you should share it. At the very least, to get the word out about the cool resources you used. (Michael: Like 100Devs!)

Don’t skip lessons. Do all of the thing you’re practicing. (Michael thinks this is a question from the chat) [2:55 PM PT]

“How to divide your time in the hunt? Banki/ hunt and what else beside that do you recommend doing? I have got 3/4 hours of focus I can put in per day”

On the Hunt, messaging people on your HitList is the biggest bang for buck.

0.5 hours prospecting for HitList people

2.5 hours messaging HitList

1 hour of Banki and Leetcode-style questions

Lots of hours and messages. Need to time manage and stay consistent. Start worrying after you send 1000 messages using the 100Devs method and aren’t finding success. Many people send 10 messages and give up due to getting discouraged. Trust the system, and put the work in to succeed with it. – Most people who go hard get to about 1000. Easy to find 600 companies on Hiring Cafe who are locally hiring (HiringCafe:

https://hiring.cafe/

)

Talked about this last Huddle in detail. 300 good fits, 300 less-good – Better messages to Good fits, more generic for less-good. Find three people at each company, and rotate between good and less-good, prioritizing the good ones first (300 to good, 300 to less-good, 300 to a different person at good, repeat.)

“I haven’t done any networking, and I’m almost in the hunt, can I just start with the hit listing?”

Yup. Just start. While learning, you should be networking so that when you go on the Hunt you have 100+ people you can reach out to and get the ball rolling, because when you’re learning, you don’t need anything from anyone, and you’re just a curious, passionate learner looking for cool, friendly people to hang out with. – Once you’re on the hunt, you more obviously want something and people can get suspicious because it seems more transactional

Go to Meetups, talk to people, and build that network of folks you can go back to and let them know you’re looking for opportunities now

Do whatever you need to where you are, but be sure you’re targetted on the hunt.

Always easier to do it now. Just start

[2:59 PM PT]

“Were in the discord is the best place to get feedback on cv, linkedin, github, etc”

Specific help channels on the Discord

#career-help channel for this stuff

Leon asks 100Dev community to take some time to help as much as possible in the help channels and help keep our community flourishing

“Have you noticed a decrease in the number of people interested in learning how to code in the last year?”

Last year maybe, but the beginning of this year has been pretty wild

Leon’s e-mails is flooded, but lots of people are realizing that AI is not the magical thing that takes everyone’s job. Realize they let AI scare them out of being a software engineer two years ago, and so now they’re trying to catch up. Veil of Haterade and YouTube Doom has dropped, and Leon has now been getting tons of interest. – 2 years ago, there wasn’t this much interest, likely due to AI

So many people have also been sending Leon thank yous based on successes, and he loves this. Kids have things they could never give them before, which is proof that people are still succeeding – “My kids have food” is fantastic. Leon loves hearing this. That’s why we’re here at 100Devs

“How to get a job with no projects and no experience on real companies? Is the market really that saturated or am I doing it wrong? CV review pls Leon, love ya!” - Diego

Company thing has always been his gray area. He doesn’t think “real” projects/experience matter that much, but he tells people to Freelance, so that they can show they have real experience and can show they had someone paying them. 100 Hours project is enough and counts as real experience, as does open source, and a portfolio you’ve developed at 100Devs. Don’t need paid experience to succeed, but it doesn’t hurt. Plus, it’s money :p

Best market in the last 3 months that he has seen in a very long time. More interviews, more offers. Definitely not saturated. Just need to play the game

From chat “95% is figuring out what clients’ need”, and only 5% coding for software dev – AI can’t do this, so we have nothing to worry about as software devs, at least not yet :p

Can also ask in Huddles to get stuff reviewed. Just make sure you have a shareable Google Doc

“When looking for a job do you hit apply and reach out or do you just reach out”

Area-dependent. Some areas have jobs that close the same day they are posted. Leon plays a longer strategy game. Looks for companies that have been hiring, and build rapport with people at those companies. Leon is not looking for an open role. He’s just building connections with companies that are hiring. Only 30% of jobs actually ever get posted. Missing out on 70% of the opportunities with companies that are actually hiring, but aren’t posting publicly

Closing fast? Click apply, then reach out is a good strategy. Make sure you’re immediately reaching out to a human, though. Better long term success is by going person first (implicit referrals go a long way, and are gained by asking people for the best person to connect with at a company to get noticed for a role.)

“how to convert hit list coffee chats into job referrals, having trouble doing that?”

“Hey, it was really great chatting with you last week. XYZ Corp (where you work, or where you have a strong connection with) looks like they’re hiring. Do you know who I can talk to?” – “Sure. Let me put you in connection with Bob, who would be the best person to talk to.” — Implicit referral – “Sure! We actually have a referral program at XYZ, and I work there, so let me get you into that pipeline” — Direct referral

Leon didn’t like how Mr. Robot protrayed people with Bipolar disorder. Took too much away from it for him. Liked the first season until all that weird hallucination stuff started.

[3:09 PM PT] “How exactly do the Cohorts and Houses work? Will there be a new Cohort in February, or are new students expected to catch up with the last Cohort?”

Catch-up-crew channel. New thread each month to connect people who are joining the program asynchronously, which is how 100Devs happens now

Just start as soon as you can, though. No plans for another synchronous cohort, but Leon does hope to have one.

“How do you know what companies are hiring when they don’t post the jobs they’re hiring?”

Last three months is a good sign. 3-6 months is great, too. Any companies that might be a good fit, go on his HitList

During Jobuary, he shared how he looks for companies who are getting funding in his areas. Now that they have money, they’re probably going to start hiring. Look 3-6 months out, who’s raising money, and target companies you think are cool. That gives you a good mix of companies to target, and always focus local first, because it’s easier to form connections with people and get jobs locally.

Funding info: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1464397240037544164

(WatchList) ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1464397296564306139

(DevLeads) (WatchList and DevLeads, the latter of which has a built-in scraper)

Networking is KEY! That’s how you see the jobs that never get posted. Public posting is the +last+ thing that companies do to find people to hire.

“Any suggestions to getting ADHD under control when it is preventing the completion of tasks and making it difficult to focus?”

Good sleep, good hydration, regular schedule, evidence-based medical treatment (including medication, in Leon’s opinion) – Self-care allows Leon to manage most of his worst ADHD symptoms, and medication takes the last edge off. Self-care is key, too

Leon uses Valerian root (in tea), magnesium, and potassium, but he uses supplements less now and just focused on good self-care routines. Great sleep schedule helps. Just need to get past the first two weeks where you adjust to it.

Adopt identities that help make things easier. “I am a person who is healthy, goes to sleep on time, and takes my studying seriously”. This helps you find motivation to do the right things by default, and build the systems to help get you there

For sleep: Keep your phone outside the bedroom helps Leon. Go get a normal alarm clock, for like $5, and avoid the phone temptation

Leon had 109 alarms, and QR codes around his house that he had to go scan to turn off the alarms. Someone cross-stitched him QR codes that looked pretty for him to hang around the house. Lived it so that he could keep his schedule. All of this just made his sleep hygiene horrible. One small alarm clock that ideally is not illuminated unless he presses a button? Phenomenal. One chance to wake up, and if you miss your commitment, your brain doesn’t like it, and you’re less likely to do this because of all the negative repercussions of the choices that you’ve made – Now all Leon does is a single alarm clock, and that’s all he needs. Top 5 biggest changes in his life. Risk seems high, but that risk helps you wake up when you need to wake up.

[3:20 PM PT] “How long should you spend tailoring your cv and cover letter for a job or should most of the heavy lifting come from the reaching out part”

Most is the reaching out part. Have a couple good resumes that fall into different niches based on what you’re applying for – Fullstack Javascript Resume – Python resume if your local market has lots of Python

Hitlisting and reaching out is the most important thing. Interviews are what are key to help you determine where your areas of improvement are. Having those resume templates for the local market means that you need to only make little tweaks to pursue those other opportunities

Leon noticed that fancy alarms just made him more manic, and his online search says he’s not the only one

“Not related to the hunt but could you please recommend me a tech book I could read? Trying to inmerse myself more into the field and a good book can help thanks” - Bob the builder

Leon read “The Phoenix Project” for the second time. Great origin story for DevOps. Crushed it on two plane rides

“What technical Q&A to prep for Devops apprenticeship, what I should know and what resources to use to learn, is there even such thing like portfolio for Devops?”

Not his zebra, not his zoo. No idea how to do this

Look what the apprenticeship is asking for, and tailor your approach to that. Need to check other resources, though, as Leon does not know this field

“I’m going to a huge tech conference on my own in the summer. I know it’s still a while but is there any way I could prepare in advance? Already freaking out 😄”

Nothing you need to prepare. Maybe find some people who are going and connect with them, but just show up and aim to meet some people. Need to take a break and cry? No big deal. Just go to meet people, make sure you sign up for things you’re interested in in advance, and just show up without any big expectations. Try to make the most of it :)

“should we list on github/CV all software we are familiar with or only the one we know well. I put everything and was told its a lie and listed stack= know well”

No international community for the standardization of resume. THERE ARE NO REAL RULES – No rule you need to know something super well to list it. Don’t lie, but if you’ve built something with a technology, you should be able to put it on your resume.

No council that determined what the minimum expertise threshold is to list a skill

If you’ve done something meaningful, you can list it.

Sam: “That’s the interviewers and reviewers job to evaluate.” Don’t limit yourself in advance, because that limits the information they can use

“Is Python the most common language in Vancouver, Canada? Should I learn it based on my Indeed search of different languages?”

If the local market says this is the dominant language, then be sure to add it to your skill set. Don’t stop learning the path you’re on, though. Finish your learning path (like 100Devs, FreeCodeCamp, Odin Project, etc.) and then add your accessory skills, like the local popular language

Leon likes HiringCafe far more than Indeed

“What does it mean to build in public and how to do it, how does it benefit me?”

Build in public just means showing people what you’re building. Some people get customers, recruiter interest, and interest in the open source stuff you’re doing. Also can get encouragement from your peers who are also doing it. Still need to HitList and Network, though :p

“how to get that smell off while you are hoping for a job but trying to network to just be cool and “not need anything” because networks are good to build!”

Be interested in the other person, and share the cool stuff that you’re working on. That’s it

“Life keeps getting in the way of the dev studies and homework. Any advice for keeping going in spite of (sometimes constant) roadblocks? (edited)”

No idea about your life and situation, but as someone who has gone through a bunch of hard stuff in the last couple months, he just adapts as necessary and makes it work. Need to be flexible and build systems that on your worst day you still want to get done done. If it’s not working, that you need to work on a better system to help you succeed in the ways that you want to. Maybe get up a little earlier? Stay up a little late? Don’t discount small amounts of time, as that will add up over a longer period. 30 minutes/day will put you so far ahead of not putting that time in. Then keep finding more time, maybe by rearranging things or declining opportunities that are not related to your goals. Ask for support. Illnesses happen, and you may need a break, which is fine. It’s a marathon, even if you need time to recharge. Just keep at it, and keep working to get the results that you want. (And don’t be afraid to ask for help, too, especially if you can tell people you’ve been putting the work in <3)

“What type of interviews should I expect for mid-level roles? System design, Leetcode? just want to know how to prepare for them.”

Every company is different. Company dependent, not role dependent. Research company, see what interview process is like, and ask ahead of time for how you should prepare. Good to practice these topics, however, because they do come up in mid-level roles. But the best thing to do is research and ask. Goal is not to be surprised by anything, because you’ve done the research in advance

100Devs has been dealing with lots of spam lately, so please remember that: “Using this community to point people to other communities is against the ❗rules (this includes Discords, Slacks, other online spaces)” -dvkr (100Devs Discord Mod) ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1464403522505277524

“How should I go about using linkedin when I work in a client-facing role and my managers actively check it to ensure I’m on the company brand?”

There are settings to become more private, but it’s kind of tough. Less active posting and more messaging might help, but LinkedIn may not be the best situation for you. Getting your manager on board with your engineering journey is very helpful – Tell them that you would like to improve your technical expertise to help in current role, and then your manager won’t feel odd to see you posting about getting more technically skilled

LinkedIn doesn’t like secondary accounts, so probably don’t do that. Leon has seen people get banned.

“At a contract job through a staffing co. Found another contract job that turns FT at end of 4 mos. Do both? Don’t go forward w/2nd? Advice 🙏🏼”

Depends on what contract says. Leon always values full-time jobs over everything else. Can potentially double-up, but full-time is always better

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-01-27
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Tues 1/27/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle Started at 2:31 PM PT, Leon was Hosting]

Lots of people talking about being cold, and getting new jobs in the chat Leon asks that people share their successes in the #celebrations channel, as it really helps encourage the rest of the community to continue on their journey

New Coffee Chat Guide from Madeleine [100Devs Community Hero] that +also+ helps us do the prospecting for people to chat with:

https://madeleinepbelanger.substack.com/p/how-to-coffee-chat

Huddle Announcement: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/738891289071714388/1465819914425340227

Mentions a Programming Podcast episode where Logan and Danny interviewed Logan Kilpatrick, a Google Gemini lead, about what Google’s doing with AI and how Gemini is seeking to serve an AI utility function – Analytics of Spotify and YouTube seem to really reward content creators who have viewers that watch all the way through, so try to run the entire episode however you can

[2:38 PM PT] From chat: Advice for just starting 100Devs? Huddles twice a week, ask for help on Discord (available 24/7) Don’t go through your days like an accident. Make a plan, and stick to it Do the Homework! Everything is intentional in 100Devs. None of the assignments are fluff, and the repetition is important for solidifying mastery and building a strong foundation that can last for a 30 year career.

“Whats the best way to find freelance work, is cold calling the way?” Depends on the person. Best way is to let +everyone+ we know know that we’re freelancing Old friends, family members, groups we belong to (church, community groups) “I am a software engineer that builds websites and web applications.” Have a landing page or a business card that you can share with folks and help showcase what you do This first layer, of letting everyone in our radius know, is often the highest yield for helping people find opportunities, especially in freelancing The difference between finding a client and not finding a client, is that some people are willing to message everyone in their phone book, and some people don’t. The people willing to make the effort are the same people that get the rewards. “Fortune favors the bold.” Person you met on vacation 10 years ago? Reach out. Any contact you’ve ever made is a potential opportunity 2nd base: Being local is your superpower Restaurants and small businesses are getting up weekly for building a better website Leon’s boxing trainer gets hit up twice a week for a new website Lots of people are mass dialing to sell stuff. Being local and having a relationship with someone is a huge plus Local means you can walk into a building and say you’re real, here, and available Don’t need to actually show up, but let people know that you’re local and can show up whenever they want Sets you apart from the remote people trying to sell them Other strategies: Platforms aren’t the best unless we niche down, as it’s often a race to the bottom Some 100Devers have found some success Most people should be able to get first client by using existing contacts and going local Don’t usually need to use other strategies to find clients if we do this. [2:43 PM PT] Tell people in your casual circles. This is what Leon is talking about. Just mention what you do, and someone always needs someone, and no one is going to trust a random like they will trust a warm connection [Based on a comment from the chat about something getting work from a comment in a Pilates class] Not just selling website or app. You’re fixing a problem they have, or solving some business need. This helps unlock a new level of freelancing. If you think you’re just a website builder, you may peg yourself to small amounts of money. Helping with lead generation? That’s a huge revenue generator and should give you a large share of that revenue Leon solves problems for people and then finds a price that works with their budget Many businesses don’t want to spend the time and effort do figure out their tech needs. They want to focus on other things, so they’re willing to pay you to do this Struggling businesses probably aren’t up for this, but you just need to find the right customers Leon has also said don’t sell stuff to people if they don’t need it. Establishing trust and rapport will pay dividends when they do actually have something you need and will come to you because they know you didn’t take advantage of them before [2:46 PM PT] Slido: “Got 1st tech job due to SWE freelance client work & 100Devs. TY!! Title is Solutions Associate, can I negotiate it to Software Engineer? Contract not yet signed” Should discuss this live in Huddle. Everything is negotiable, though, and this is definitely something to negotiate Titles are great to negotiate, especially if salary, compensation, benefits, etc. are at a dead end Titles cost nothing to a company and are therefore not difficult to negotiate Way Leon would do it: “I’m super excited about the offer, can’t wait to join, looking for long term growth. Please help me say yes. Couldn’t get wiggle room on pay or benefits, and I need to make a decision that’s good for my family. Can you get me somewhere that the more effort and energy I put into this role will allow me to get that effort out of it. Engineering is going to be a large part of what I’m working on. Can we please get Engineering in the title? That will be what I need to have in order to sign with you today.” From chat: Someone got $15K extra at job because they ask for it. Always ask.

[2:49 PM PT] “Should I finish 100devs first, or focus on learning what’s in demand now based on the 2026 coding roadmap episode?” Always finish the course first. It covers the fundamentals, and then you can add the extras on top for the present market If you keep chasing the hot things, you don’t have the fundamentals that are going to help you get through the interviews that you need to get through Leon still stands by the 100Devs classes. They still work, and the 100Devs community helps a lot, too From MayanWolfe in chat: Companies are likely going to be 10 years behind on their tech stack anyway. Unlikely we’ll need the new hot things ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1465841451316543732

Do the foundational stuff, then look to see what’s in demand in our local market and within the opportunities/companies we are looking for Some companies are still potentially using floppy disks “Phoenix Project” book that Leon read covers this. Trying to progress from legacy systems to newer stuff

[2:53 PM PT] “At what age do interviewers stop considering you seriously as a 1st time SWE? I’m a career switcher and MUCH older than most candidates. Suggestions?” Leon doesn’t think people care. Can structure resume and bio so this is less of a concern. Might find some people that are prejudiced in this regard, but it’s much more rare than people realize Just need to show that you’re competent and can solve problems immediately People often look for excuses for why they shouldn’t pursue opportunities. This should not be one of them. Focus on the 100Devs fundamentals for establishing your professional image, and you’ll be set Can remove years from degree on resume/LinkedIn, but this is likely a very small part of it. Focus on the 100Devs fundamentals to present yourself as a competent, capable software engineer, and you should be fine From chat, 40+ SWE with no issues:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1465842467323642100

Can also focus on companies that don’t have these hangups. Remember, at 100Devs, we don’t optimise for the outliers, we focus on what works for the majority MayanWolfe says they’re 35 and are often the youngest member of their team

[2:56 PM PT] “Should you include your graduation date on your cv if you have recently graduated in cs?” Yes. Some roles are specific to new grads. In general, can A/B test this. Pursue with the date on, pursue with the date off, and see which we seem to do better with Doing experiments like this with the stuff we’re concerned about is a great way to go about it, and share your experiences in the 100Devs community so we can all experiment and grow together!

[2:57 PM PT] “What is the best way to learn technical jargon?” Listen to technical podcasts. Leon highly recommends the Programming Podcast (personal plug), that they release weekly. For Leon: He reads HackerNews every day, and if there’s a word he doesn’t recognize on the first page, he looks it up. 30-60 minutes in the beginning, and now it’s very quick. Read comments to see what people are arguing about This allows Leon to hang in any technical conversation, even if he hasn’t used the tech, because he remembers relevant discussions and can speak with that knowledge The Verge and ArsTechnica were also mentioned in the chat Podcasts: ShopTalk show, CSS [CripsBane?] <- Web Dev Podcasts Leon walks a lot, 50% of the time he’s listening to a podcast Try out a bunch. Some may fit better, and your preferences can change

[3:00 PM PT] “Underqualified for a position, but confident I’ll excel with some guidance and a little bit of time on the job. How to address this in an interview?” YOU DON’T! You either portray that you can solve their problems day one, or you don’t say anything at all Don’t try to sell them on hiring you. You need to demonstrate that you’re capable of solving their problem Ex. Cat Herding New specialty gate that herds cats and has cat-chasing robots? Talk about how great you are with cats, the robotics experience you have, your experience with building great gates Not: “Never have experience with cats, robots, or gates. But if you give me enough time…” No. Job during an interview is to translate your experience into proof that can make them confident you can solve their problems on day one If you can’t do this, then you need to go get that proof and learn how to sell yourself by practicing (Banki Brunch) and continuing to interview From chat: When your toilet is overflowing, you don’t want an aspiring plumber Leon: Never have to lie, but your job is to figure out how to mold your experience to sell yourself on the job. You have to be your biggest advocate, as no one else is going to do this in your interview, or at least not as capably. No one knows you and your background as well as you do. [3:04 PM PT] “I am learning Js with 100devs however my area is mostly Python jobs. How to approach this? Learn am Js and pm Python or do Js and Py next to avoid mixing syntax” Do not trying learning both at the same time. VERY easy to mix this up in interviews Backend and Fullstack JavaScript make it very easy to add Python on top. Best to add mastery to one and see it all the way through, and then add the accessories for the local market after MayanWolfe has a great playlist on learning Python Similar to Leon’s rule about finishing one Learning Journey before starting another

[3:06 PM PT] “How do i get over the feeling of imposter syndrome, always hearing about a bad job market and even people with years of experience struggling to find a job” Impostor Syndrome and Job market are two different things Impostor Syndrome is very specific. Probably just experiencing Impostor thoughts Feelings of inadequacy, etc. Syndrome is persistent over time Address Imposter Thoughts by developing proof that you’re capable of doing what you want to do Polished 100Hours project, great portfolio, history of freelancing Proof that you are who you say you are, regardless of what anyone else thinks Second: When you are focused on a faraway goal, your day-to-day starts to have negative effects on you if you’re not achieving anything that gets you closer to the goal. Lofty career goals are hard on people because they don’t feel like they’re making daily progress Normal SWE process is 5 years (undergrad CS, internships, etc.) Goal is typically five years out, and 100Devers often get frustrated a month in Have to keep the perspective in mind. 100Devs is 30 weeks. You’re making +massive+ progress compared to people who take five years. Nontraditional pathway. Need to realize that you have to focus on the day-to-day to avoid burnout Focus on daily tasks. Did I do my Codewars, Anki, Lecture, Hitlisting etc. today? That’s in your control, and you can accomplish this each day, and the imposter feelings will dissipate because you’re making progress every day Bad Job Market? Normal Look at what the actual data says in terms of unemployment. 4% Electricians, the hot blue collar job right now, is 7% unemployment. That’s almost double. Don’t get distracted by the doomloops. Social media algorithms serve you what will keep you hooked. This is often scary stuff. Don’t let the noise scare you. Focus on the facts Leon has said in the past that this is the best hiring market he has seen in years for 100Devs and Resilient Coders (RC) graduates Chat endorsement for Anki to help people learn. Someone said it has made them a memorization machine. Leon agrees

[3:11 PM PT] “what are your thoughts on framing your freelance work as an agency/company? stating you run a web development agency” One person agency counts as an agency. No rules on this. One person dev shop is still a dev shop. Bringing designers in can become an agency, but one person can also be an agency Leon likes framing work as an agency. Better business, better for resume, etc. Leon has a one person agency for a long time, and he worked with other contractors who were often doing the same. Still an agency

[3:12 PM PT] “If someone recently graduated with a cs degree were would you recommend them to start 100devs, feel like i relied too much on ai during my degree” Lots of folks who have come out of CS programs and most CS programs have a single web development class if they’re lucky For a long time, there were none, and many CS graduates don’t have the fundamentals down to get a job Don’t feel like you can skip content just because you have a degree. Need to build fundamentals just like everyone else, so be sure to do the entire program, start to finish

[3:14 PM PT] “coffee chat turned awesome connect stating he’ll help me secure a role with anyone in their network (VPs/founders). How do I choose btw them? All big companies” Pick the ones that match your abilities or that have a role that you think might be an easier onramp, and start there

[3:15 PM PT] “Should I learn Typescript before jobhunt or stick to Js. Was told Typescript is more efficient and used by many companies. Please share what you think” Look at local market and see what they are using. Probably not TypeScript Good to know TypeScript these days, but Leon is skeptical it’s dominating the market right now

[3:16 PM PT] “Should I restart 100devs after forgetting information during a long break, going from Class41 back to Class26?” Yeah. If you feel like you don’t have mastery over a topic, there is no shame or mistake in going back and rebuilding that mastery. You’re investing in an entire career. No problem setting that strong foundation. That’s part of the beauty of doing 100Devs on your own pace. Lots of Super Reviews in the class list that can help bring you up to speed, too

[3:17 PM PT] “When a job has x years experience requirements how do you go about that, feel like every role i see needs atleast 1-2 years experience.” Remember: Job postings are a want list, not a must list. Only you engaging in the process will reveal what is a must and what is a want Leon ignores this, and just pursues the opportunities. Demonstrate that you have the experience that they want Everyone’s “years” are different. One person’s year may be someone else’s two years All about having the skills necessary to solve the specific problems they have, and potentially better than someone else with more years of experience

[3:18 PM PT] “How can I manage my fleeting motivation during my 100-hour project and the trough of sorrows when I doubt my ability to succeed?” Pick a 100Hours project that you actual want to do it. You should geek out to do it every day. First thing: Pick a project that you’re excited to work on Fleeting motivation is another issue. Have systems that help you do what you want to do. Timeblocked days and environments that let you be productive whether or not you want to do something Journalling, meditating, good self care area also very important Try to figure out why your motivation is fleeting Are you not getting enough help? Are you doing work you don’t enjoy? Try to identify the root issues and address them systematically Don’t go into your days by accident. Just develop systems that allow you to systematically make the progress you want to be making, regardless of how you feel Remember to take breaks, too!

[3:20 PM PT] “How much time do we owe new jobs? Does it matter if we negotiated super hard? Like 6 months a year? 2?” No one owns you. They’re giving you money for labor. If someone else gives you more money for that labor, you should go with them. Company will lay you off in an instant if they can’t afford you anymore, so you need to always look out for you. Leon likes to keep loyalty from people he works with, so if he’s the middle of a big project, he cares more about being there for his team and not abandoning them than pursuing more money. Once the project is done, though, he’ll probably bounce Companies like seeing 1-2 years at an organization on a resume, but this is not good enough to keep you in a worse opportunity

[3:22 PM PT] “I recently added an achievements section to my resume. Does including a high school senior superlative (most reliable) come across with that junior smell?” Yes. Probably want to take that off. Kind of funny to mention as a tangent during an interview, but people typically don’t put high school stuff on professional resume

“How do I gain more group working experience-I only had a hackathon experience with other devs. Got ask a lot during interview” Good options: DannyThompson does Commit Your Code Cohorts Open Source Projects Lots of 100Devs Community Projects Pick something that you use every day that’s an open source project, and find ways to contribute to it

“Any possibility of a Huddle devoted to the Hirata Method?” There was interest in the chat on this, so Leon thinks he can do this

[3:25 PM PT] “can you please do one huddle with intentional gitgub merge conflict so we can learn how to merge and fix conflicts and what all this magical options do pls” Leon likes this, too. Good idea

“What system should we follow for our 100 hours project? Any specific requirements or system design components to consider?” Binary Upload Boom is what most people follow Base for the 100Hours project, and that’s usually enough to get us a good project Anything else should come up during the project as we identify things that we want to add

“How can I go from 100 hours project completed to software engineer at NVIDIA?” Work hard, learn more, and make connections Lots of 100Devers go right to FAANG companies Like going from high school baseball to playing MLB Yankee baseball. Same answer. Just keep putting the work in to get you where you want to be

“Next Cohort” Soon (Trademarked. This is what Leon always says <3)

Alec asking live how to craft story while doing on-site cable work for an ISP LinkedIn is outdated and story is not crafted Does he need to present himself as a cable/field technician, or can he portray it as a more engineer-oriented way Leon: Depends where. Use the titles that you have, because it is something that can be verified When you’re talking to others, you can say you work in tech, and you’re a software engineering currently focused on the more physical/hardware side Leon is an educator now, but still refers to himself as a software engineer Software Engineer who is currently working in education. No rules about how to talk about yourself publicly, just remember that titles can be verified by potential employers, so don’t be fuzzy about this Alec isn’t sure how to navigate the difference between his personal growth and his work Leon: Growth usually don’t come from work. 100Devers are doing this outside their daily jobs. Craft narrative/story about what we’re doing outside of 9-5. Use potentially irrelevant job experience to add additional skills to the SWE talents and capacity we’re developing

Yasmin: Negotiating Title (live) USD$40K capped salary If she tells them she wants a better title, it may concern them that she wants to leave Leon: Tricky when you’re going through a third party. Yasmin is contracting through a third party or placement provider Is it something that’s worthwhile with you for the long haul? Inherent risk when going through a third party, but they’re also interested in getting the money from having placed you Company wants you, and you’re ready to work? They’re probably not going to throw this away just on something like title. If they would throw it away, this is probably a bad opportunity and you saved yourself from it by advocating for yourself and seeing that they don’t care about you Best way is just have a conversation: “Super excited about the role, passionate about joining. I just have a few questions…” Always emphasize how excited you are and how much you want to make it work If they show reticence to help support you, this shows that you didn’t want to work with them anyway Our job is to convince them that if they help us, we’re thrilled to help them, too These are just routine questions we’re asking to try to make this opportunity as successful as possible for us so that we can thrive in it and do our best Not just “I want a title change NOW, OR ELSE!”. You’re just working together to solve a problem as part of a team who wants to succeed together

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-01-30
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 1/30/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

From the Huddle announcement on the Discord:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/738891289071714388/1466908559504838697

“Hey @everyone your #100Devs Huddle starts in 60 mins (<t:1769812200:t> your local time)!

<:anothaone:740581548717506712>**Give Congrats in <#735936014832369687> to **

<@426417024638517249> For their new Senior Software Engineering Role!!

"This new role and my first came from recruiters reaching out to me on LinkedIn, and my second one came from someone I met at a virtual tech conference. Having my LinkedIn profile up to date with all of my skills, speaking about myself in a positive way that really shows my value (bangers only!), and networking!!! really make such a difference. I wasn't the strongest at either of those things before 100devs, but I really put in the work to make it happen."

<@624016178309890058> On their first paycheck!! as a freelance technical consultant!

"Back in June, I started volunteering for a childhood cancer nonprofit, and what began as a scripting role quickly grew into data engineering and analysis work. By fall, I was asked to join as a freelance technical consultant. And last night, I received my first paycheck!"

<@318870135240654848> for finishing their 1000th Codewars kata!

--

<a:heartrainbow:687043845636096054> Some links worth your time this weekend:

☕ "How To Coffee Chat" Guide: https://madeleinepbelanger.substack.com/p/how-to-coffee-chat

📺 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=45YTtESYyY8

📻 https://open.spotify.com/episode/3PZQiZ0EMOoo0EgaFcx8ST?si=7dfc12b149db48e3

--

Then come ask all your **#THEHUNT** related questions live!!
