Start-up company sounding name and what they do? [2:51 PM PT] Job: Cat Herder looking at AI music company In my past life I was a cat herder. Lots of individuals each year lose their cats, and it was my job to find, herd, and deliver these cats to their owners. Highly rewarding work, but very long days, often deep in the woods with my thoughts. To get through the days, I would play music to give myself the energy I needed to continue herding. This became very special part of my day, but after hearing the same music over and over again, I needed something fresh. Turned to generating my own music while herding. Helped me ride out the very long demanding days and have music that really spoke to me, and get these cats back to their homes, unlocking some of the greatest memories I’ve ever had. Wanting to keep leaning into AI music generation that helped me through these long days. Honed software engineering skills, and now I’m back to help bring the thing that help me so much to the masses Know what AI music did for me, and I want to bring it to every other person. This seems like the best company that can get that done ** Chat goes wild ** Leon says this is just two random things, and you need to practice this so you can do it just as well Leon just improvised this. If you practice, you can make yours even tighter That’s what we’re looking for. What did you do in the past, how does it relate to what you want to do in the future, and how does it fit with the current company? If you have all three, you’re golden Chat said theater kids likely rule here, and Leon does have a theater background [2:55 PM PT] Chat says, and Leon agrees, if he can do it with nonsense, you can totally use it with real history. Can use 100Devs GPT to give you ideas, and then hone it. Record yourself to practice, and keep experimenting with good answers in interviews and see what works. You’ll get better over time Leon thinks a 100Devs Improv class would help a lot of people No job that you’ve ever worked that doesn’t have transferable skills to SWE (from chat) Leon agrees 1000% [2:56 PM PT] “ Not about the words, about how you made them feel” Chat talks about neurodivergence appealing to AI music Leon doesn’t like signalling any weaknesses. He plays the game hard until he gets the offer, and then once the offer is in hand, he can negotiate accommodations Some people are expecting certain answers (e.g. SMART method) Amazon, for example, has full guides on how to answer these questions, and expect you to have done your research (“Googled the Fuck out of It”), and come prepared May dismiss you if you don’t present how they publicly told people they should be presenting Rare, but it does happening, especially at Big Tech companies

“Company may bypass interview, due to direct hire from Director of recruitment. My connect needs me to apply to the job, and send them a pitch to forward (pt 1)” If you have Dir. of Recruitment that likes you, that’s fantastic This is why we do the networking bit. Want to find the person that has the hiring decision, and then get hired that way Harder to do this at bigger companies, but easier at smaller companies Use the director as a filter “This seems like the best pitch. Do you think this will land well with the reviewer? I would love to make changes” They clearly want to help you, so let them do so [2:59 PM PT] Chat asked about if I can start interviewing mid course? Chat also answered this Leon said you should always finish the course to open as many doors as possible Only halfway through? Fewer doors. Still possible for a job to come through them, but don’t make this your only strategy. Switch gears at the end and go 100% into the hunt Job titles don’t matter. Good to interview as early as possible to demystify the process, get the flywheel going, and get a better sense of how best to prepare for The Hunt

[3:02 PM PT] “What do you recommend doing to extend your lifeline for the hunt? I’m currently doing Uber Eats deliveries for rent. Part/full time work, freelance, etc?” Do what you can to bring stability to your life. That’s a great thing Lots of folks commonly look to use every resource US has things like EBT, Medi-Cal, religious communities can offer support, food banks. Extend your runway using all the resources available. If you’re a taxpayer in the US, you likely helped pay for these programs, so don’t be shy about using them when you need them Part time work, full time work. Do whatever you need Make sure to protect time to study and prepare for SWE Hunt. Make sure you’re hunting Freelancing is phenomenal Rank Order Get all public assistance possible. (Rent abatement, student loan abatement) Freelancing Give it at all for a week Every single RC (Resilient Coder) got a paid client within 6 weeks of learning over the past 10 years. All you need is to go hard for a week If you don’t live in an area with access to people, outside a large metro area, that might be an edge case Anything to stave the bleeding Gig work, part time work, etc. Don’t start things that keep you from pursuing your ultimate goal Easy to get stuck in one of these jobs and keep you at 60-80% less than what you could have made if you went all into 100Devs and SWE for 6 months

[3:05 PM PT] Best 100Devs graduates are Uber Drivers. Know what it takes to hustle, because that’s what it takes Leon has mentioned sales can be good, too, as it makes us stronger against rejection, which is common during The Hunt “I see in job offers efficient use of AI tools as requirement.Should we use Colab or Gemini during interview with them? Is that expected or dont use AI at interw” As always, you ask. This is what’s expected now Before: Can I use what language I want? Can I Google during interview? Now: Can I use AI? If can opportunity says AI is a requirement, you should absolutely ask, and the answer now is often yes. Should never-ever-ever go into an interview if you don’t know what you’re walking into Your job is to know as much as you possibly can about what’s going to happen in the interview, then prep accordingly. Always ask, then do the research on your own if no one tells you what you need

“returning from 2024 still barely starting JS. AI seems to be welcomed and used WAY more now. How does this affect our learning?” Yes, if you let it. Do not incorporate AI into your learning process, as it will rob you of the learning AI does the connection and searching for you, and this is the work that’s important for long-term retention If you can get on 1-on-1 tutoring, that’s fine. Just don’t let AI start giving you answers, because that’s going to ruin you. Leon thinks you shouldn’t incorporate it into your process until your learning process is established Don’t let AI generate code for you Don’t let it answer questions for you Max should be to help explain things for you, especially early in the learning process After completing 100Devs, or a similar program? Leon’s advice fully changes: You should be using AI all the time at that point DO NOT Use AI for 100Hours project Need something in your portfolio you can point to and say you built the entire thing Everyone has AI-built stuff in their portfolio now You need at least one thing to set you apart by saying you built the entire thing with zero AI involvement Lots of companies are using AI checkers to see how much AI was used in a codebase Every AI leaves signatures in our code, so don’t try to sneak by the checkers. “I need to learn X, give me a roadmap, timeline, and resources on how to do that” Good AI prompt for learning [3:11 PM PT] Chat joked that this is a very common Huddle question Leon never gets frustrated with repeated questions in Huddles. That’s how we learn, through repetition You internalize the material when you see it repeatedly The more times you hear the Huddle wisdom, the more easily it’s going to be able to come back to you in a high-stakes setting like an interview This is why coming back every week matters. 3rd round interview and you unlock generational wealth in one more answer? That 100th Huddle Wisdom is going to help you Just two hours a week for the Huddles. Exponential value in a high-stakes setting. Prioritize this time as much as possible “ what would be good portfolio project for full stack app in js and what other stack to use to link that in CV as expeience. Should we deploy it? if yes where pls” Something in past should explain what you’re doing now. Build based on this Built best POS system based on background as a server Something you hate and want to fix Something you need to see in the world These make it easier to motivate you to do 100Hours Railway, Render, all have trade-offs for free accounts, so play with them On the Hunt, should try to get $5 to pay for something so you can avoid cold starts, which can limit your opportunities Leon really likes Railway was a company right now. Good team, company cares about its workers. Leon plans to move a lot of his stuff over there to reward good behavior Leon loves Convex. (Best thing ever if you’re building with AI) Convex.dev Phenomenal MCP that makes it easy to build with. If he’s in Cursor and building with cursor, it’s amazing Best developer experience he’s had in the long time. Wants to make a video about it [3:16 PM PT] “Can you talk about the first 90 days after starting the job specifically the non-technical things we need to know to put our best foot forward?” Great book called “The First 90 Days”, along with “How to Win Friends and Influence People” Best books to read (“Never Eat Alone” is also great) General: Show up early and stay late, at least for the first 90 days. Then can coast on this reputation for the next 2-3 years. When things arise that are out of your control? They know it’s not you, it’s some weird thing that happened to you, because they know you go the extra mile Never eat alone. Spend as much time as possible to build relationships with team, with management, and then with other teams Hard to get laid off or miss promotions when you have really good relationships with people around you Day 1: Can I ask every simple question for the first 2 months, with 2 promises Never ask the same question twice Will document every answer, so people who come after will be more prepared Save you potentially hours on stuff someone else can answer for you in 30 seconds Early in, Late out; Never eat alone; Ask great questions Victory Log Track every good thing you’ve done Share wins publicly in company, with gratitude to others Increases your visibility, and establishes rapport with those around you Can share stuff publicly on LinkedIn, but this can happen later. Within company on company communication (like Slack) works Meet with Manager early, establish expectations for month, 3 months, 6 months, year Confirm in writing via e-mail Track progress, and then you can refer to this, and how you established expectations, when it’s time for performance reviews and promotions Someone in chat said this advice got their friend a promotion Leon says most people aren’t willing to do the small things. If you are, then those small things add up and let big things happen for you Boss told you what mattered? That’s why you had the conversation. If they give you more work? Then you refer to your receipts, and then you can either ask if they’re asking for extra thing if they want to alter the scope of what you’re doing. Publicly sharing and receipts helps keep those who are asking things of you accountable, and make sure that if they ask you do something new, what would they like you to deprioritize instead? Chat says this is all stuff from the books Leon says he reads a lot, so this help. Leon has lots of experiencing helping people. First years of this, it was hard, there was failure, but now he’s 8 years after and he’s very optimized. Tons of shots on goal because he helps people on a daily basis, and has been doing it for so long. Constant feedback from community about what works and doesn’t work This happens in chat during Huddles, too. Huddles matter Leon has an ExecDirector and a Board at Resilient Coders that he’s responsible to He does all the stuff he recommends, too, and it helps him get better results. Do what works, people <3

Huddles have been going on for about three years. There was similar stuff during the cohorts, though. [3:24 PM PT] “Leon there’s so much more to learn after completing 100devs. What do I focus on, react, wordpress(freelancing), figma, ts, python? Overwhelmed! (edited)” Can learn anything you want For a job? Not much more you need. You just need to do the things you need to get it (Hunt Stuff) Check local market for accessory stuff. (PHP one place, React another maybe) Avoid the trap of being the perpetual student Spent 6 months learning? Easier to learn than HitList Will learn a lot by continuing to learn, but you’ll never get a job To get a job, you have to go look for one Learn enough to be dangerous (and 100Devs is enough), then get out there and let the market tell you what you’re missing If you bomb 10 interviews because you don’t know enough Python? Then go learn Python. Before then, keep going for the interviews and jobs. Let the market dictate what you’re weak in, not your insecurities Favorite story: someone bombed a Thursday interview. Got a senior dev offer from a Friday interview. Got to keep Hunting Leon also loves when formerly incarcerated Folx go to SWE-level salary. That’s incredible, and he loves hearing about that

“should we have 1 github project described as “done with usage of Colab to ship faster” if yes how to write it in readme is NOT entirely AI done but using tools” Just be honest. Don’t have to put this in a ReadMe, but if you’re showing employers, always best to be transparent Don’t have to be super transparent, but Leon likes to err on the side of transparency, because honesty is typically very valued

[Answering Live] Miguelisms with a CV review: ​

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1q1zPzJO16b1omoenfigxykaqFNrMyuonh_L-ecUOP_U/edit?usp=sharing

[This is a Google Doc, so it may change from what the notes say if Miguel has changed it since] Leon reviewed the narrative Bangers were after the first line. Don’t bother with the first line, and then lead with all the great things you did. This was how it read: “I transitioned from creative production to software engineering after discovering how code could eliminate manual, error-prone workflows and dramatically improve delivery speed. My work has supported content shipped across major platforms, such as Playstation, Xbox, including a project featured during Nintendo Direct 2023. Today, I build production-grade systems that scale under real-world constraints and share my process publicly as a Twitch Affiliate. I specialize in turning complex, manual processes into reliable automation that increases velocity and reduces risk.” Bullet points in the resume don’t really support his narrative bangers. If he doesn’t name a project, it’s probably not real. “Leading the design and development of a full-stack trading platform” ←- That sounds fake Leon wants X,Y,Z format. In the first sentence, it should be so amazing that he can go tell someone how amazing the Resume person is This is like “Baristas make coffee” This line doesn’t seem exciting. Leon thought the narrative was exciting, and wants that excitement to feature all over the resume so the recruiter is excited “Delivered high-visibility game marketing content, as evidenced by features on PlayStation, IGN and Xbox YouTube channels, by creating trailers for titles including Golf With Your Friends DLCs, Blasphemous II and King of the Castle.” Good to name drop, but we really need the X,Y,Z format Freelancing would really make Miguel more impressive Try to separate these out, because the paid clients will be the most impactful Good to separate them client-by-client to show he has experience

[UncleNoob on stage] ​

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1WCsHFczmh5SkWwqCnEy8ZW5u0XcH1yAew-RU_Y377qM/edit?tab=t.0

^ CV Review Older format of the resume Should test if it works well in your area Summary: “Software engineer who builds systems people can rely on, shaped by firsthand experience where broken processes had real-world consequences.” Good theme, but tell me what happened. Don’t just say it. Show me. What broke and had real world consequences? How are you the hero? This quote is a good setup, but that’s the content? Be careful with Baristas make coffee here. Make yourself the hero “Improved day-to-day task execution by designing and shipping a structured workload system, resulting in 30% faster daily planning for users.” Leon doesn’t know what this is, and that means a recruiter will not. If we don’t know what it is, we can’t get excited about it. Would you really tell someone about this? No. People would just be confused. Needs to make sense for even non-technical people Need very Solid X, Y, Z format for everything Try to keep it to one page Noob doing screenwriting as a hobby, so he’s trying to incorporate that in

[3:37 PM PT] Noob notes the time, so he would like to come back next Huddle, and Leon is definitely up for this and helping community members continue to improve

author: Michael Govaerts
title: 100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT
html:
=== I used AI (Google Gemini) to try to improve the formatting below. The Raw version, with AI assistance, is below it. You should be able to search the document for “[Here’s the original document:]” to find it. =====

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities)

(Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord)

[Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Notepad while listening to the Huddle, multitasking, and using an ancient laptop. Sorry! I tried to get something, though <3

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

Huddle

[2:32 PM Started]

Topics:

ADHD and Engineering

Nothing inherent about SWE that prevents us from succeeding with personal limitations

If you have a limitation, it would likely impact you in any career. Use your resources to address those limitations, and then make sure to crush 100Devs and your amazing SWE career after

Lots of ADHD among the 100Devs community. Every person is different, though, so you need a plan that is tailored to you, and professionals can help with that

Don’t underestimate the power of lifestyle changes first, however. Medication does not need to be the first step, or even a necessary step.

==== Break, as Michael was multitasking too hard ====

I think what was covered here was “Can we be a digital nomad in the current SWE market”?

Leon said yes. He thinks remote work is here to stay

Hardest part about remote work is that we often have to be in the correct time zone

(From Michael): However, some companies probably want coverage at odd hours (like graveyard or overnight shifts). Thus, if you’re in a distant time zone, these might be normal daytime working hours for you. This could be extra great since, in many places, graveyard/overnight hours are +not+ popular, and sometimes they’re paid more highly as a result. (This is usually the case in healthcare, in my experience, for example.)

However, Leon has seen some companies (like Railway) go fully remote, global, and fully asynchronous

Employees just commit to working at a specific time period, for a standard full-time commitment, and it doesn’t matter when they’re working. They just need to show the company and their team that they’re dependable

Leon has also found StarLink to be very helpful to allow him to be more remote with his current work. He has done some 100Devs Huddles outdoors using StarLink to get connected, and he doesn’t think we have noticed.

===

Topic: AI is increasing the pace of development, but most of the work of an SWE is not coding

Leon does not see the human in the SWE loop going anywhere anytime soon

Need to know the fundamentals to make sure you know that the AI is doing the right stuff, and helping you build the product that your stakeholders need

Danny’s Masterclass on this is really good, though. Helps with AI prompting

====

How much notice do you need to give part-time jobs?

Rarely need to give notice at all, but part-time jobs especially. They’ll fire you without a thought (or just stop giving you hours)

Don’t hesitate to pursue better opportunities, but you can always feel free to give the current position an opportunity to keep you there

Two weeks is industry-standard for courtesy

Leon only has loyalty to people, like those on his team who he wants to keep in his network as good contacts. He has no loyalty to the company itself

===

[2:46 PM]

Back to what happens if AI makes perfect code?

Lots of opportunities for SWE, especially once everyone realizes that SWEs are necessary, since coding is not all that’s necessary

Leon is not worried at all. If companies can move faster, then they need more people

IBM just says they plan to increase new hires in 2026 by six times what they were doing before

AI can get new people productive day one, so now they need as many bodies as possible to take advantage of that. No need to onboard or onramp, or at least much less of a need

Lots of companies also don’t want to give up secrets to AI

===

2:48 PM PT

Remote in IT but want to be an SWE

Boss took a chance on me. Should I stay?

Leon thinks this is unlikely

If certs at job lead to raises? Then go ahead. “Taking a chance on me” isnt meaningful. If they want to pay you, great. If not, go somewhere that will give you better pay and career stability, and help you pursue what you really want to do (in this case, being an SWE)

====

2:49 PM PT

Topics I missed:

Apprehensive about being an SWE. Do you not want to do it? That’s fine. Are you just scared? Go fix that.

Remote work is here to stay (Michael did some post-session additions to this topic above. So, we covered that in this document, too) – It’s under “Can we be a digital nomad in the current SWE market””

====

[2:58 PM Pt]

Leon keeping up to date with software

Need fundamentals and theory. This helps you know what the AI is working off of, and what it’s doing

Be up to date

Can’t be a principal engineer on theory stuff. They’ve just been doing it too long

Need enough theory to not be behind, but need to be up to date (HackerNews, Twitter)

Leon can speak about any model or any trend in AI. His edge is that he can backfill the theory stuff, but he’s on the forefront of trying the new stuff

Understanding the skills, MCPs, and buzzwords. No principal engineer will ever consume more content than Leon

Backfilling theory, getting his edge by being current, and then building repeatable systems that have high-output when he’s using AI

More knowledge than Principal about how to build when AI is in the loop.

This is how Leon keeps his edge

Also refuses to let his skills atrophy (Last bastion)

People get too comfortable with AI, and forget how to write functions

Leon built a new Anki tool, and he uses this to review

Thinks the algorithm is better and the experience is more fun

Using it for a month now and likes it

Leon has no problem being confident, because he works hard to justify it

====

Maintaining attention span vs. looking for quick fixes?

Leon’s ADHD is his superpower. 10 projects at once

Learning at an accelerated speed when he’s taking good care of himself. Very good at context switching

===

[3:03 PM PT]

Don’t worry about what employer will decide

Just do the best you can, and then stay at your best so if you do get laid off, you’re great

3-6 month contract? No problem. Once it finishes, if they don’t hire you, you now have more experience to help you get the next job

===

[3:04 PM PT]

Best to follow-up early in the week, as stuff sent over the weekend or just before is less likely to be interacted with, as it just piles up with a bunch of other stuff

===

[3:05 PM PT]

Should only seriously start job-searching once you finish a program

Should be networking the entire time. At the end of the program, you should have at least dozens of people who can help you connect with an opportunity. That’s what 100Devs does

“Already had my heart broken once this weekend, so I would love to hear back from you.” (Since it’s Valentine’s Day)

This came from the chat, and Leon loves it so he’s going to use it when he tries to get people to connect with him

===

[3:06 PM PT]

New Dev Routine?

Same thing as always: Don’t make each day an accident

Every day: Learning, Review, Building, and Networking

Learning: 100Devs, Books, Videos, etc.

Review: Anki, something that helps actively recall

Building: Use the things you just learned

Networking:

If you keep doing these things consistently, you get an opportunity. That’s how it works. Focus on what works

===

[3:07 PM PT]

Leon does not price with freelancing

Finds out what client’s budget is, and then he finds out what he can do for that price point

Pricing in advance will cause a client to ask for everything but expect the price you set

Price point just makes customers unhappy. Does not help you at all

Understand customer first, then determine if their budget will allow you to help them based on how much it will cost to make it make sense to you

Leah apparently said in chat she’s having trouble with too much work using this method. (Leon says it’s not a problem for him either)

Using this strategy works for both of them

====

[3:09 PM PT]

Work does not come from your job, unless you’re +VERY+ lucky (Laravel seems awesome)

Growth therefore has to come from outside of work

Too tired at the end of day? Have to get up early and study before work.

Never eat lunch alone

Keep stability of the current role until you get a new one

This is how you transition to SWE and succeed at doing so

Leon’s normal schedule:

Teaching a little later (was 7AM before, now it’s 8AM)

9AM-11AM is coding for Leon (7-8 PM is also coding, if he can)

Before 9 AM is family time

Working remotely? How to eat lunch alone?

Virtual lunch works. Do a Zoom eating lunch together. Book that time

===

[3:11 PM PT]

How to find a job doing open source?

Very rare. You do open source because it’s fun to do and it helps you provide proof

Does not help you connect with a job. You have to go out there and Hunt

Some open source projects do hire, but it’s very rare and unlikely to be as successful as Hunting according to the 100Devs advice

===

[3:12 PM PT]

Scrummaster Cert?

If a company wants to pay for it? Great.

Leon has never seen a Scrummaster cert move the needle meaningfully for anyone

Many people get it because jobs often pay for it

Need more than certs to set yourself apart

===

[3:13 PM PT]

Being senior was never about coding proficiency or the ability to bang it out

“Senior” is all made up anyway

Leon is tempted to do a podcast on this.

Possible you may have equal code output to a senior with AI

Does not teach you teamwork, understanding stakeholder needs, etc.

Much more to being a senior than just code output

===

[3:14 PM PT]

System design course or tutorial?

“System Design Primer” on Github

Exponents YouTube channel (System Design interview walkthroughs)

Leon agreed with user “Stair-in” in the chat. That’s how it sounded anyway

===

Only front-end dev on backend team. What to do?

Growth does not come from job. Advice is to find the growth, mentorship, and help in other places so you feel like you’re growing

Also check with the team and management. “I love the work I do, I love my team. I don’t feel like I’m growing.”

Maybe they’ll pay for memberships and conferences

Any leader worth their salt wants their people to feel like their growing

This is a great conversation to have. Leon loves if people who report to him ask him for help to be happier, healthier, or feel more like they’re growing. As their manager, this is Leon’s job

Could be snitching on yourself if you don’t frame it right. “I love everything about what I’m doing, but I feel like I am missing opportunities to grow. (Leadership growth, etc.)”

Probably not a good conversation if you don’t have a good relationship with management, but you need to work on these relationships, too

Don’t want to let people think you’re looking to quit. Need to emphasize how happy you are otherwise

===

Leon’s Theory Reading

“Philosophy of Software Design”

Like “Clean Code” but all the crap is taken out, and this is all good stuff

“Deep Learning for Coders with FastAI and PyTorch”

Practical Deep Learning for Developers from FastAI is a great intro to AI for developers course

This book is the companion to it

“Designing Data Intensive Applications”

Leon really likes this book, but a new version is coming in March so he’s going to wait for that

“SCIP”

Full version in JavaScript now [this is the “big book” that everyone references. It’s like 4 words, everyone references it. DM Michael if you want him to find the full name]

[3:20 PM PT]

Leon loves that there’s a JavaScript version of SCIP now

“Eloquent Javascript”

Leon loves this book, especially if you’re very new to the field

Great book to help you start thinking bigger (Leon says this is a controversial take, though) [Michael: It’s SICP -> The Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs. Here’s the JS version, in an interactive format: ​

https://sourceacademy.org/sicpjs/index

]

===

“Brain feels flat after getting a job. Feels stuck in the middle and not progressing”

Growth doesn’t come from the job!

Progress, and shaping the system, comes from getting buy-in from people around you and building influence on the team

This is highly important as it helps you grow your career

“How to Win Friends and Influence People” helps you understand the game

Need to establish rapport first

“Never Eat Alone” is good, too

Once you get those relationships, you’ll likely feel better about shaping the system

“We need a course on what to do after we get a job” - From chat

Leon really likes this idea

[3:22 PM PT]

====

Leon put an open text form in the Slido

Would love to get content ideas from the community, as he has time to work on this at the moment

This feedback would be very helpful. If the Huddle regulars gave him ideas, it would help him provide more value at the Huddles, because he really appreciates us making the time

[3:24 PM PT]

===

Last Round of Interview. They loved me. Now they’re saying there’s a delay. Do I wait?

This happens. This is why you need to have multiple opportunities lined up

They are likely lying to you. Telling you and ten other candidates that they want to move on with you

May end up hiring someone internally, and the eleven of you are out of luck

This is why you have to always keep looking for opportunities. If you slow down whenever stuff starts to feel good, you’re now in big trouble with your lack of momentum if those pan out. (They often DON’T pan out.)

Don’t ride the rollercoaster, just keep getting those interviews lined up through HitListing, Banki, Leetcode, etc.

===

[3:26 PM PT]

If growth isn’t at job, why are years of experience important?

Years of experience has nothing to do with working

Years of doing engineering. That may be on the job or outside of the job

It’s also +ALL MADE UP+

No way to easily quantify how good of an engineer you are. “10 years of experience” makes this easy

Kids coming out of Waterloo can run circles around “senior” engineer in Midwest

Proxy that filters out most people, but it’s not anything worth paying attention to

See tips above to make sure you’re always growing

Front-End developer at day job? You’re atrophying elsewhere if you’re not continuing to work on them, especially outside of the job

Most people aren’t passionate about this stuff, so you have to actively work at it, otherwise you’re going to end up in a place career-wise that you don’t want to be

[3:28 PM PT]

Commission-based Software Engineering role at a Startup? Pay is based on profits?

This is awful. Do not go through with it.

This is a scam.

Whenever someone asks this question, someone in chat usually says something

90% of companies fail. How many meaningfully exit is way smaller

“Hey Leon. I got into Y Combinator. Getting guaranteed C funding. Can I give you equity beforehand? I can show you proof of all of this”

This works. But mostly, it’s just people with an idea trying to get developers to work for free. Don’t fall for it

===

[3:30 PM PT]

Leon really wants feedback from people so that he knows his content is helpful. That’s EXTREMELY rewarding for him

===

Bullets for 100Devs?

X,Y,Z format for stuff you’ve done

Client, 100Hours project, get real users and real feedback

Not here to make up things that are impressive. We do impressive things, and then we help you sell them effectively

No shortcut. Have to do the work

===

Coding is not the whole piece of being an SWE

Lots of other stuff. Working together, how to do testing, how to do review, how to deploy

Layers of learning

Once you’ve committed to learning, you now have a system you can use to apply to these other systems

Companies you work for likely have guides to follow that you can get good at

[3:32 PM PT]

Strain posted bullet point example in chat

Need users? Have a great reputation at 100Devs, and many people here will likely be there to help you, including by using your thing or helping you find opportunities

There’s a self-promo channel where you can share what you’re working on and ask for people to help you (as users, and to provide feedback)

[3:34 PM PT]

From Chat: Tonight, 7 PM PT, there’s something going on that Leon thought looked interesting (hopefully, it will be recorded later :p)

“The Social Network”

Not “The Facebook”. Sam’s calling his thing “The Daily Challenge”. Leon says, just call it The Challenge.

===

Do not use AI to do your 100Hours project

100Devs GPT is just helping you with ideas for good projects based on your background

===

“Don’t attribute to malice what may just be a simple error”

Always try to assume the best in people. this will cause them to be inclined to do the same with you in return

===

Content creation?

Don’t spend any money. Just start where you are and see if you like it.

===

“Any resources to learn how to do test-driven development”

No courses. Just try it. Use Jest and Playwrite for end-to-end. Read the docs and add it to your code base

Play with it for a while, and then figure out the best resources.

Get used to end-to-end testing and optimize later

Some things, Leon doesn’t like courses. Thinks we should play with stuff first, then seek out a course

TTD on Codewars all the time (says chat)

[3:38 PM PT]

===

How to use AI in the 100Devs program?

Don’t use AI, especially in the beginning

Part that is important is the resource gathering and making the connections

AI does all this work for you, and then you don’t build the connections

90% of people don’t use AI in a way that helps them. It’s just too tempting to use AI to do the hard work

Also very bad for active recall

AI seems to short-circuit the learning process right now, which is really harmful

“Effort is the algorithm” with learning [3:39 PM PT]

===

If you’re showing up to this community, Leon loves you very much, and knows the rest of the 100Devs community loves you, too

Happy Valentine’s Day and a great holiday weekend

Leon went over time and didn’t even notice. He was too focused on getting through the Slido

=====

[Here’s the original document:] ==== RAW Document Below. I had Google Gemini assist with formatting the above, but I don’t entirely trust it :p =====

[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Notepad while listening to the Huddle, multitasking, and using an ancient laptop. Sorry! I tried to get something, though <3

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/13/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

Huddle

[2:32 PM Started]

Topics:

- ADHD and Engineering

-- Nothing inherent about SWE that prevents us from succeeding with personal limitations

--- If you have a limitation, it would likely impact you in any career. Use your resources to address those limitations, and then make sure to crush 100Devs and your amazing SWE career after

-- Lots of ADHD among the 100Devs community. Every person is different, though, so you need a plan that is tailored to you, and professionals can help with that

--- Don't underestimate the power of lifestyle changes first, however. Medication does not need to be the first step, or even a necessary step.

==== Break, as Michael was multitasking too hard ====

I think what was covered here was “Can we be a digital nomad in the current SWE market”?

Leon said yes. He thinks remote work is here to stay

Hardest part about remote work is that we often have to be in the correct time zone

(From Michael): However, some companies probably want coverage at odd hours (like graveyard or overnight shifts). Thus, if you’re in a distant time zone, these might be normal daytime working hours for you. This could be extra great since, in many places, graveyard/overnight hours are +not+ popular, and sometimes they’re paid more highly as a result. (This is usually the case in healthcare, in my experience, for example.)

However, Leon has seen some companies (like Railway) go fully remote, global, and fully asynchronous

Employees just commit to working at a specific time period, for a standard full-time commitment, and it doesn’t matter when they’re working. They just need to show the company and their team that they’re dependable

Leon has also found StarLink to be very helpful to allow him to be more remote with his current work. He has done some 100Devs Huddles outdoors using StarLink to get connected, and he doesn’t think we have noticed.

===

Topic: AI is increasing the pace of development, but most of the work of an SWE is not coding

- Leon does not see the human in the SWE loop going anywhere anytime soon

-- Need to know the fundamentals to make sure you know that the AI is doing the right stuff, and helping you build the product that your stakeholders need

- Danny's Masterclass on this is really good, though. Helps with AI prompting

====

How much notice do you need to give part-time jobs?

- Rarely need to give notice at all, but part-time jobs especially. They'll fire you without a thought (or just stop giving you hours)

-- Don't hesitate to pursue better opportunities, but you can always feel free to give the current position an opportunity to keep you there

- Two weeks is industry-standard for courtesy

- Leon only has loyalty to people, like those on his team who he wants to keep in his network as good contacts. He has no loyalty to the company itself

===

[2:46 PM]

Back to what happens if AI makes perfect code?

- Lots of opportunities for SWE, especially once everyone realizes that SWEs are necessary, since coding is not all that's necessary

- Leon is not worried at all. If companies can move faster, then they need more people

- IBM just says they plan to increase new hires in 2026 by six times what they were doing before

-- AI can get new people productive day one, so now they need as many bodies as possible to take advantage of that. No need to onboard or onramp, or at least much less of a need

- Lots of companies also don't want to give up secrets to AI

===

2:48 PM PT

- Remote in IT but want to be an SWE

-- Boss took a chance on me. Should I stay?

- Leon thinks this is unlikely

- If certs at job lead to raises? Then go ahead. "Taking a chance on me" isnt meaningful. If they want to pay you, great. If not, go somewhere that will give you better pay and career stability, and help you pursue what you really want to do (in this case, being an SWE)

====

2:49 PM PT

Topics I missed:

- Apprehensive about being an SWE. Do you not want to do it? That's fine. Are you just scared? Go fix that.

- Remote work is here to stay

====

[2:58 PM Pt]

- Leon keeping up to date with software

-- Need fundamentals and theory. This helps you know what the AI is working off of, and what it's doing

- Be up to date

-- Can't be a principal engineer on theory stuff. They've just been doing it too long

- Need enough theory to not be behind, but need to be up to date (HackerNews, Twitter)

-- Leon can speak about any model or any trend in AI. His edge is that he can backfill the theory stuff, but he's on the forefront of trying the new stuff

--- Understanding the skills, MCPs, and buzzwords. No principal engineer will ever consume more content than Leon

- Backfilling theory, getting his edge by being current, and then building repeatable systems that have high-output when he's using AI

-- More knowledge than principal about how to build when AI is in the loop.

- This is how Leon keeps his edge

- Also refuses to let his skills atrophy (Last bastion)

-- People get too comfortable with AI, and forget how to write functions

- Leon built a new Anki tool, and he uses this to review

-- Thinks the algorithm is better and the experience is more fun

--- Using it for a month now and likes it

- Leon has no problem being confident, because he works hard to justify it

====

- Maintaining attention span vs. looking for quick fixes?

- Leon's ADHD is his superpower. 10 projects at once

-- Learning at an accelerated speed when he's taking good care of himself. Very good at context switching

===

[3:03 PM PT]

- Don't worry about what employwer will decide

-- Just do the best you can, and then stay at your best so if you do get laid off, you're great

- 3-6 month contract? No problem. Once it finishes, if they don't hire you, you now have more experience to help you get the next job

===

[3:04 PM PT]

- Best to follow-up early in the week, as stuff sent over the weekend or just before is less likely to be interacted with, as it just piles up with a bunch of other stuff

===

[3:05 PM PT]

- Should only seriously start job-searching once you finish a program

- Should be networking the entire time. At the end of the program, you should have at least dozens of people who can help you connect with an opportunity. That's what 100Devs does

- "Already had my heartbroken once this weekend, so I would love to hear back from you." (Since it's Valentine's Day)

-- This came from the chat, and Leon loves it so he's going to use it when he tries to get people to connect with him

===

[3:06 PM PT]

- New Dev Routine?

- Same thing as always: Don't make each day an accident

- Every day: Learning, Review, Building, and Networking

-- Learning: 100Devs, Books, Videos, etc.

-- Review: Anki, something that helps actively recall

-- Building: Use the things you just learned

-- Networking:

- If you keep doing these things consistently, you get an opportunity. That's how it works. Focus on what works

===

[3:07 PM PT]

- Leon does not price with freelancing

-- Finds out what client's budget is, and then he finds out what he can do for that price point

- Pricing in advance will cause a client to ask for everything but expect the price you set

-- Price point just makes customer unhappy. Does not help you at all

- Understand customer first, then determine if their budget will allow you to help them based on how much it will cost to make it make sense to you

- Leah apparently said it chat she's having trouble with too much work using this method. (Leon says it's not a problem for him either)

-- Using this strategy works for both of them

====

[3:09 PM PT]

- Work does not come from your job, unless you're +VERY+ lucky (Laravel seems awesome)

- Growth therefore has to come from outside of work

-- Too tired at the end of day? Have to get up early and study before work.

- Never eat lunch alone

- Keep stability of the current role until you get a new one

- This is how you transition to SWE and succeed at doing so

- Leon's normal schedule:

-- Teaching a little later (was 7AM before, now it's 8AM)

-- 9AM-11AM is coding for Leon (7-8 PM is also coding, if he can)

- Before 9 AM is family time

- Working remotely? How to eat lunch alone?

- Virtual lunch works. Do a Zoom eating lunch together. Book that time

===

[3:11 PM PT]

How to find a job doing open source?

- Very rare. You do open source because it's fun to do and it helps you provide proof

- Does not help you connect with a job. You have to go out there and Hunt

- Some open source projects do hire, but it's very rare and unlikely to be as successful as Hunting according to the 100Devs advice

===

[3:12 PM PT]

Scrummaster Cert?

- If a company wants to pay for it? Great.

- Leon has never seen a Scrummaster cert move the needle meaningfully for anyone

-- Many people get it because jobs often pay for it

- Need more than certs to set yourself apart

===

[3:13 PM PT]

- Being senior was never about coding proficiency or the ability to bang it out

- "Senior" is all made up anyway

- Leon tempted to do a podcast on this.

- Possible you may have equal code output to a senior with AI

-- Does not teach you teamwork, understanding stakeholder needs, etc.

- Much more to being a senior than just code output

===

[3:14 PM PT]

- System design course or tutorial?

- "System Design Primer" on Github

- Expontents YouTube channel (System Design interview walkthroughs)

- Leon agreed with "Stair-in" in the chat. That's how it sounded anyway

===

Only front-end dev on backend team. What to do?

- Growth does not come from job. Advice is to find the growth, mentorship, and help in other places so you feel like you're growing

- Also check with team and management. "I love the work I do, I love my team. I don't feel like I'm growing."

-- Maybe they'll pay for memberships and conferences

-- Any leader worth their salt wants their people to feel like their growing

- This is a great conversation to have. Leon loves if people who report to him ask him for help to be happier, healthier, or feel more like they're growing. As their manager, this is Leon's job

-- Could be snitching on yourself if you don't frame it right. "I love everything about what I'm doing, but I feel like I am missing opportunities to grow. (Leadership growth, etc.)"

-- Probably not a good conversation if you don't have a good relationship with management, but you need to work on these relationships, too

- Don't want to let people think you're looking to quit. Need to emphasize how happy you are otherwise

===

Leon's Theory Reading

"Philosophy of Software Design"

- Like "Clean Code" but all the crap is taken out, and this is all good stuff

"Deep Learning for Coders with FastAI and PyTorch"

- Practical Deep Learning for Developers from FastAI is a great intro to AI for developers course

-- This book is the companion to it

"Designing Data Intensive Applications"

- Leon really likes this book, but a new version is coming in March so he's going to wait for that

"SCIP"

- Full version in JavaScript now [this is the "big book" that everyone references. It's like 4 words, everyone references it. DM Michael if you want him to find the full name]

[3:20 PM PT]

- Leon loves that there's a JavaScript version of SCIP now

"Eloquent Javascript"

- Leon loves this book, especially if you're very new to the field

- Great book to help you start thinking bigger (Leon says this is a controversial take, though)

===

"Brain feels flat after getting a job. Feels stuck in the middle and not progresssing"

- growth doesn't come from job!

- Progress, and shaping the system, comes from getting buy-in from people around you and building influence on the team

-- This is highly important as it helps you grow your career

- "How to Win Friends and Influence People" helps you undersatnd the game

- Need to establish rapport first

- "Never Eat Alone" is good, too

- Once you get those relationships, you'll likely feel better about shaping the system

"We need a course on what to do after we get a job" - From chat

- Leon really likes this idea

[3:22 PM PT]

====

Leon put an open text form in the Slido

- Would love to get content ideas from the community, as he has time to work on this at the moment

- This feedback would be very helpful. If the Huddle regulars gave him ideas, it would help him provide more value at the Huddles, because he really appreciates us making the time

[3:24 PM PT]

===

Last Round of Interview. They loved Me. Now they're saying there's a delay

- This happens. This is why you need to have multiple opportunities lined up

- They are likely lying to you. Telling you and ten other candidates that they want to move on with you

-- May end up hiring someone internally

- This is why you have to always keep looking for opportunities. If you slow down whenever stuff starts to feel good, you're now in big trouble with your lack of momentum if those pan out. (They often DON'T pan out.)

-- Don't ride the rollercoaster, just keep getting those interviews lined up through HitListing, Banki, Leetcode, etc.

===

[3:26 PM PT]

If growth isn't at job, why are years of experience important?

- Years of experience has nothng to do with working

-- Years of doing engineering

- It's also +ALL MADE UP+

-- No way to easily quantify how good of an engineer you are. "10 years of experience" makes this easy

- Kids coming out of Waterloo can run circles around "senior" engineer in Midwest

-- Proxy that filters out most people, but it's not anything worth paying attention to

- See tips above to make sure you're always growing

- Front-End developer at day job? You're atrophying elsewhere if you're not continuing to work on them, especially outside of the job

-- Most people aren't passionate about this stuff, so you have to actively work at it, otherwise you're going to end up in a place career-wise that you don't want to be

[3:28 PM PT]

Commission-based Software Engineering role at a Startup? Pay is based on profits?

- This is awful. Do not go through with it.

- This is a scam.

- Whenever someone asks this question, someone in chat usually says something

-- 90% of companies fail. How many meaningfully exit is way smaller

- "Hey Leon. I got into Y Combinator. Getting guaranteed C funding. Can I give you equity beforehand? I can show you proof of all of this"

-- This works. But mostly, it's just people with an idea trying to get developers to work for free. Don't fall for it

===

[3:30 PM PT]

- Leon really wants feedback from people so that he knows his content is helpful. That's EXTREMELY rewarding for him

===

Bullets for 100Devs?

- X,Y,Z format for stuff you've done

- Client, 100Hours project, get real users and real feedback

-- Not here to make up things that are impressive. We do impressive things, and then we help you sell them effectively

- No shortcut. Have to do the work

===

Coding is not the whole piece of being an SWE

- Lots of other stuff. Working together, how to do testing, how to do review, how to deploy

-- Layers of learning

- Once you've committed to learning, you now have a system you can use to apply to these other systems

- Companies you work for likely have guides to follow that you can get good at

[3:32 PM PT]

- Strain posted bullet point example in chat

- Need users? Have a great reputation at 100Devs, and many people here will likely be there to help you, including by using your thing or helping you find opportunities

-- There's a self-promo channel where you can share what you're working on and ask for people to help you (as users, and to provide feedback)

[3:34 PM PT]

- From Chat: Tonight, 7 PM PT, there's something going on that Leon thought looked interesting (hopefully, it will be recorded later :p)

- "The Social Network"

-- Not "The Facebook". Sam's calling his thing "The Daily Challenge". Leon says, just call it The Challenge.

===

Do not use AI to do your 100Hours project

- 100Devs GPT is just helping you with ideas for good projects based on your background

===

"Don't attribute to malice what may just be a simple error"

- Always try to assume the best in people. this will cause them to be inclined to do the same with you in return

===

Content creation?

- Don't spend any money. Just start where you are and see if you like it.

===

"Any resources to learn how to do test-driven development"

- No courses. Just try it. Use Jest and Playwrite for end-to-end. Read the docs and add it to your code base

- Play with it for a while, and then figure out the best resources.

- Get used to end-to-end testing and optimize later

- Some things, Leon doesn't like courses. Thinks we should play with stuff first, then seek out a course

- TTD on Codewars all the time (says chat)

[3:38 PM PT]

===

How to use AI in the 100Devs program?

- Don't use AI, especially in the beginning

title: michaelgovaerts/2026-02-17
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Tues 2/17/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:00 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:32 PM PT]

Happy Lunar New Year.

Just a 30 minute Huddle Today because Leon needs to go celebrate Lunar New Year with family. (He said something like having a “Double Dragon” in his family?)

It was Matthias’s birthday recently! (Matthias, with the top hat, is a staple of the 100Devs community! <3)

“Is it normal to take more than 100 hours to build the 100 hours project? It took me an hour to learn and debug a REST API's connection to the DB, for example”

Yes! 100Hours is probably the minimum

Minimum that you should be spending on this project that is the cornerstone to you getting a job

Some people never stop working on their 100 Hours project

Some people turn their 100Hours projects into businesses

However, keep in mind that this is just one caveat of getting you a job

Don’t sink more hours in the 100Hours project to avoid doing HitListing and Networking. Those other things are going to get you the job

100 Hours project is about struggle. We learn in the struggle, and that learning is what’s going to help us rock the interviews.

“Is it realistic to work full-time as a freelance developer instead of a 9–5 job? I feel burnt out in corporate and I'm trying to weigh my options.”

Leon knows people that do this

Couple key things if we want to replace 9-5 pm or escape burnout/stress

Need two evergreen clients and a war chest (need money in case clients don’t come in for an extended period of time)

Leon says get 3 months of funding. If you have family you are supporting, get at least 6 months

Evergreen clients are those who keep coming back routinely to use you and pay for your services

War chest is there to allow you space to work for new clients

Not having new clients is common. We have to prospect for them, so we need to ALWAYS have the war chest so that we have the flexibility and time to focus on finding good clients

Don’t be desperate. That causes us to make bad decisions and end up with bad clients and even more stress and burn out

Lots of 100Devers that freelance specifically (in chat)

C95 (Marcia Cripps) has lots of experience with this

Chat says pizza ovens are awesome, and Leon agrees and is jealous

“help negotiating an offer for a role 3h away. They want me to relocate. Do I ask for as much money as possible or just ask to work 1 day a week in the office?”

Worthwhile to come up on stage and talk about this stuff during the Huddle. This is negotiation stuff that 100Devs specializes in

“What to do when I get stuck with a topic I don't understand which leads me to not want to work on it and start procrastinating and wasting time?(100 hours proj)”

This is a mindset shift. Leon now gets excited when he gets stuck. He enjoys it. This has helped him be wildly successful. Many good friends of his hate this experience. Freak out when they don’t know something. Leon understands where this is coming from

Need to get to the mindset that we know that getting stuck or feeling stress does not mean imminent danger (this is a common anxiety response)

Once we get over this, now we’re doing puzzles and lego bricks

Leon likes a GIF that shows you can be the kid screaming on the rollercoaster, of the Beyonce that is having the time of their life

This might be a therapy thing, but for many people it’s just a mindset shift

(GIF is in the chat)

Reevaluate anxiety as excitement is common in CBT

Leon has a deep-seated belief that he can figure out anything. Any problem that Leon faces, he knows he could figure it out given enough time and access to the internet. This may not be true, but it’s how he operates

Best way to avoid procrastination? Be bored.

Your only options are doing nothing, or doing the work. If you can’t do anything but what you’re supposed to do, eventually you’re going to want to do it

Remove all distractions. Control your environment to make the thing you want to do the easiest thing to do

Good self-care is also important

Exercise, eating right, sleep

Leon’s only B in his entire school run was AP Calculus (B+)

Leon was crushed because he thought he was going to lose offers to college and stuff

Good at math. He’s really good at memorization. Blessed with good memory. To succeed in school? Memorized, regurgigated, and forgot

Leon enjoys math more now because he has changed his mental model in approaching it

“What is the new CV/Resume format? I understood the 'Bob the coder' one might be out of date. Can you or anyone in chat share a good one?”

Two templates. Newer one is more Harvard style

Transitioning from another industry? Two column seems slightly more successful

Worthwhile experience in engineering already? Lots more proof? Single column layout seems to slightly edge out two-column, but not for new developers

Vish just posted both in chat [2:44 PM PT]

Micah in chat: Unless you’re doing math-based code (engineering and scientific computing), you really don’t need much math

Especially if focused on web development

Leon: Machine learning is more math heavy, and math knowledge will never hurt more generally. Don’t need math to be a good developer, though

“during job interviews, if HR asks about salary at 100devs, how should I explain properly? Could you guide me on the best way to present this experience?”

100Devs is a full-service agency that offers a software engineering training program. We were going through training program. Program was unpaid, but we got paid clients, who were X, Y, and Z

This is how most people answer this

“What are your tips to have/get/work on having the mindset of not freaking out and procrastinate when getting stuck? I really want this for me.”

[2:46 PM PT]

Rejection therapy works for Leon

Freaking out, procrastinating, etc. is about how they’re being perceived by others

Worried that their family will be disappointed

Therapy for Leon: Once a year, for a month, he always asks for a discount on ANYTHING he buys. Helps him get over initial fear about rejection, since most people laugh. However, some people give him discounts, so he sees that taking risks is GOOD, not bad

Can keep a list of what you’re learning so you can look back and see your progress

A month ago you thought you wouldn’t learn the thing you now know. This new thing is probably similar

Mindset/Identity shift: You are a capable, studious student who does what you want to do. Get social network bought into this. This helps you not want to violate this identity by procrastinating or doing things you don’t want to do

Most people don’t do all these things, and still feel stuck

Just invest the minutes a day to experiment with the above solutions, which work for many people, so go try it and see if it works for you

[2:49 PM PT]

“I need a 100hrs roadmap. I started without a plan and am lost on what comes next/ what it looks like when its "finished". I cant finish something i cant define”

No roadmap. What do you want to build? See in this world? Break it down into set of features to accomplish this. Everyone is different

It’s done when it’s something that someone would be willing to put their credit card information into and start using

Chat: Leon took suggestion of GPT that helps coach people through the 100Hours project

Added it to his to-do list

Don’t wait for the perfect idea. Something you wished existed. Ideally something related to your past career. Something you hate and want to change. Not really a finish, it’s more like an art [see chat for ideas]

Lost for what comes next? Get idea, break idea into features, and get it to a point where users will be enthusiastic about this

“If someone hasn’t accepted my LinkedIn connection request (with a note), what’s the best next step? Should I follow up by email? I don’t want to seem pushy”

No one cares. You’re not being pushy

If the person doesn’t want to talk to you. Who cares? Make the attempt, and if it doesn’t work, move on, and maybe circle back later

No one will remember a month later, definitely not a year later and Leon has said people will thank him for following up for months and not giving up on them. Then, they love to help him

Just keep moving. Some people might be jerks, but who cares

Leon uses Email, LinkedIn, and Twitter

Usually checks in every 3-4 days

After a month, remove the connection request, and then do another request to the same person. This moves to the top of their connection requests, and is often successful 60% of the time

Don’t waste time on the 1% who don’t want to talk to you and miss the 99% who do

Don’t use personal e-mails and contact information. That’s pushy

Public/professional stuff? That’s fine. That’s what it’s there for

“What's the format for a great story? Where do we start high school?”

Use the 100Devs Narrative GPT

“Is it okay to reach out to the same contact for referral more than once? What's the appropriate time frame between the asks?”

Yes. Depends on the level of the relationship

Good friend? Ask. Who cares?

Stranger? Ask. Who cares?

Nothing to lose by asking frequently. Just shoot your shot

“help negotiating an offer for a role 3h away. They want me to relocate. Do I ask for as much money as possible or just ask to work 1 day a week in the office?”

Once you have the offer letter in hand, everything is negotiable. Start with the best case scenario for you. What would make you the happiest?

How about 6-12 months before relocation?

How about 1x/wk in office while I get my life in order?

Convince them the entire time about how excited you are and how much you want to join. Ask them to help you make it work so that you can do right by yourself for you and your family. Love the team, love everything. Please. Human to human. Help me make it work

This is what Leon does

Go from best case to worst case scenario, but always start from the best and only go to worst if they reject your suggestions.Negotiate everything, especially the job title

“Leon, you are using a lot of new tools. where do you go when you get stuck?”

Leon has been part of online communities since he started writing code. Always on IRC, always on Discord

Asks for lots of help in those channels. Some Slacks

Group of friends that he’s always working with

Leon has lots of alt accounts, so that he can ask a lot of questions without it going back to him

Always researches himself first. Spends time digging through docs, talking to GPT. Spends time by himself because he enjoys the figuring out process

When he’s stuck-stuck? Goes to Discord or IRC, and 90% of the time they just make some small suggestion in 10 seconds that saves him 4 additional hours

Also spends a LOT of time consuming content (podcasts, YouTube, blogs, academic papers)

Lots of exposure to the cutting edge stuff

Leon mostly about learning concepts these days

“If only through class 37 and have a recruiter wanting to give interview would you recommend doing the interview or waiting until further along”

2 Options:

See what interview is like to get a sense of what they’re like for the future

Good for companies you don’t care about. The practice is VERY helpful

Ask if you can get the interview in 2-3 months, when you’re more actively looking for opportunities

Good for companies you would LOVE to work for

[3:00 PM]

Chat: IRC is what nerds use. Yup. But those are Leon’s homies

Cold-messaging people is fine. Don’t worry about it. Just do it.

Leon has to go celebrate Lunar New Year with family. Will be back on Friday for normal 1 Hour Huddle

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-02-20
html:
Some topics that I found helpful: I asked Leon what he thought about freelancing opportunities in DataEngineering, as they don't seem to be as plentiful as FS WebDev, (Code the Dream is in the process of opening a Data Engineering Pathway alongside their FS WebDev track, and I really like data-driven decision-making and its utility in helping scientific research, so I'm tempted :p) -- Leon agreed. More clients/companies seem to need websites and apps that help make their professional lives easier than those who need more specialized DataEng work. However, he's not sure if there's any clear data on this, and he thinks the opportunities in the high-end may favor DataEng instead of WebDev, so that's something to consider Leon also brought up why he DOES NOT share a syllabus for 100Devs, -- Too many enterprising people go, "Oh. The course is 30 weeks? I can do that in three," and then sabotage their learning in the process, either by not giving themselves enough time to really sit with the material by learning it at a slower pace, or by getting overwhelmed and just giving up. --- All the classes are available on CommunityTaught, so this doesn't seem to be helping people avoid this pitfall -- Another pitfall is that if people see all 60+ classes stacked together, it may seem insurmountable, and they won't even make the attempt. Leon prefers to have people stay consistent by paying attention to each step at a time. Then, once they're on top of the mountain, they can then look back and see how far those steps have taken them. (edited)Saturday, February 21, 2026 at 01:44

[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/20/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:32 PM PT]

“hi leon! i had an awkward debrief/semi-negotiation call with a recruiter yesterday; could we possibly talk it through on stage please?”

Yes! Raise your hand, let’s pull you up.

StevenMosesCodes

How do you handle negotiation? How should I be reading into this interaction?

Onsite on Tuesday. Grueling, but got through it. E-mail from recruiter for phone chat less than 48 hrs after ended

Phone: “Hey!” Not an offer yet. Asked how the on-site was. “Great experienced, learned a lot about product, super psyched”

“Feedback looks positive” Steven thinks he was vibing with everyone even if it was tough

Recruiter has some questions

Confirmed title: “Full Stack Engineer”

Path to senior when joining team

Confirmed desired pay range, which was higher than first conversation with Recruiter

Confirmed years of experience. 4, based on freelancing

Recruiter confirmed this was not counting internships (Steven has never had an internship)

Steven said he can confirm start date 3-4 after signing. Recruiter countered 2-3 weeks after signing

Team just needs to debrief and that she’ll get back to him next week

Steven very bad at playing it cool. So just checking with Leon

Leon: Seems like interview went well, and recruiter is trying to advance Steven to offer

At that stage, they have to weigh a bunch of things. Team needs someone soon and at current price point

Very good sign. Would have probably rejected him already if not

May be looking at multiple candidates

Leon likes not going up and down on rollercoaster. Steven played cards very well so far. Now just wait for offer, and keep going on the hunt

Navigated lots of big traps: Title, Years of Experience

Timing seems important to them, so Leon would have said something like “flexible on start date” to be vague so as not to create friction for them

Once we have formal offer, we can negotiate harder

At this point, probably not worth Steven reaching back out to the recruiter and say, “I can start whenever you want!”

(Especially for enough money)

Leon says don’t worry about this. Just wanted to bring it up for the Huddle

Recommends reaching back out to them if they don’t get back to him by Monday end of day. Ask if they need anything else on his end. (Shows continued interest)

Thinks Steven did very well

Looks forward to having Steven come back up with an offer so we can help him get more money

Only way to learn this stuff is by going through the process yourself and failing a bunch of time, or learning from others

Steven: (in the chat)

Recruiter: “Salary Expectation?”

S: “What’s the range?”

R: “We have a range… What’s your expectation?”

S: “Depends on the range.”

Leon says this is how we do it.

[2:41 PM PT]

100Devs version of Slido will not have character limit

“Have a written offer I accepted. Got another verbal offer somewhere else. Want to rescind original written but haven’t got new offer signed. How long do I wait”

Until the verbal becomes a written offer. Make sure new offer is what you want to pursue

Hopefully, you’re not in a country with slavery

Whoever wants to give you more money for labor gets your labor

Company will lay you off without a single thought

Don’t worry about going for more money/opportunity

[2:44 PM PT]

Be kind, respectful, and appreciative of all the people involved, but you have to pick the better offer

The company will always go with their better offer, too. It’s just business. Don’t take it personally, or be personally unkind when you make the same decision

MayanWolfe in the chat said someone at new job got laid off after 20 years without warning. “Go for the money. That’s what the company does”

Just be very kind and open about why you’re going with one offer over another, and then keep moving

“This other offer is just going to make more sense for my family, and I have to take it. Thank you so much for your time.”

No apology necessary

Good line with range and expectation overlap

“Are there fewer freelance opportunities in Data Engineering (Python, AI/Cloud) vs. FS WebDev? Any tips, Leon+Chat? Just a much less flexible career path? Thx!<3”

FS Web Dev has more freelance. Not sure any data supports this, just Leon’s take

Just easier. Everyone needs a website. Maybe everyone doesn’t need DataEng services

Every store/company needs a web presence. Most are building applications

More clients in this area

High end may actually be in DataEng. FS Web dev just seems easier to break into

Lots of opportunities in the field. Freelance would likely be harder. Taking on more serious projects, which probably needs more client service or extreme expertise

[2:47 PM PT]

Leon tries not to apologize as much. Would rather not do so unnecessarily

Argument: “We don’t need a website, we use Facebook”

Has it helped you get consistent leads?

“Are you at capacity? You wouldn’t want more clients?”

If you’re able to get consistent leads, you may be priced ineffectively. I helped client with better website, and they were able to get clients at higher price point. Now they can make more money by doing less work. Is this something you would be interested in?

Always an angle

[2:50 PM PT]

“Can cohort 3 hit advanced topics? 1-2 cover the fundamentals and I’d like to see how you unit test, load balance, back of envelope calculations and etc.”

Lot of people who are coming back in #celebrations at Year 2 or 3 of job

Not a lot of content for these people. Programming Podcast can help

Leon would love to have more formalized training. Thinks this is something that needs to happen in 100Devs, and hopes he can rely on community to help surface some of this stuff

[2:52 PM PT]

“How would you network your way in for a position posted on linkedin by an engineering manager, with a job application link?”

Talk to them

“Saw this opportunity. Very interested. My experience seems to fit for this role. Can we make a time to chat?”

Contact 2-3 other people at same company, too

(From chat) Leon thinks about Roman Empire quite a bit. (Identified strongly with a meme about this)

Book by Mary Beard mentioned in chat that Leon really likes

[2:52 PM PT]

Normal advice for question above. Just shoot your shot. Fortune favors the bold. No reason to hesitate

“Are you still finding AI to be useful? I loved it 2 years ago but now the output has been disappointing. Seems like it got worse over time or something.”

Yes. Leon actually feels the complete opposite

2 years ago it was trash and did not solve any of his problems

Now, he can produce more work at a speed he would not be able to without AI

Really still needs to be involved

Burnt out pretty hard this week. Very ill. Dizzy. Attributed to coding with AI too much. He has been going +very+ hard. Rarely doesn’t have an agent running, especially with long-running agents. Felt his brain got dopamine’d out

Thinks he went too hard. Has a backlog of ideas he keeps in a document. Has access to AI for free. Can trial agents for zero cost

He went 12-15 hr days with these for 2 weeks. Not a second where he wasn’t ripping. Thinks this was too much

Can see how fast and more he can go. Helps him realize what he wants to spend time on. Find balance of focusing in on 2-3 meaningful projects instead of 12. Code does not really matter anymore. It’s the engineering and product dev that is slowing him down now. Unburdens him in code production, but re-burdens him in the other areas. New bottlenecks now that coding is no longer one of them

Models change very quickly and have different strengths

AI-enabled engineering is now a new skill set to learn
