Ex. Project structuring, using tools to help AI understand. Need to employ all this effectively to harness it well

Month or two to learn these skills, and then you can really unlock the true functionality of AI

Most people underestimate how long this ramp is

Always someone new to learn, and AI is just it right now

Leon thinks there’s always going to be areas of incremental improvement that we need to be open to pursuing if we want to stay at the top of our game

Don’t stagnate, and we couldn’t even before AI if we wanted to be successful

Leon doesn’t think we’ll all be product managers. Thinks we need engineering more now. AI is just predicting next steps. Need to combine AI’s ability to grok a codebase and laser in on fixes with our real engineering ability. That’s the real weapon. No harness is really great at full project understanding. Great to save your time in moving to specific files and moving stuff around

Leon wants to do a course on this

Finding this helps SWE feel fun again. Dark arts that take away a lot of the pain points for him

[3:00 PM PT]

“Why don’t you share a syllabus in the cohort? Why is it bad to work ahead?”

2 reasons

When people reach a chapter end, they often quit (based on research)

Creates barrier to get over for next chapter

Syllabus gives exact times to stop learning

Even if you’re not quitting, people often burn themselves out

30 week course minimum? I can see everything on CommunityTaught. I’ll do it all in 2 weeks

Not learning anything, because brain hasn’t had enough time to absorb anything

Might make you feel productive, but is counterproductive to you achieving your goals

This type of individual would otherwise be phenomenally successful, but the syllabus causes them to get in their own way. Leon doesn’t want this to happen to them

Syllabus can also look like a big ladder from the bottom. Becomes too burdensome, and causes us to doubt our ability to climb the mountain. Leon would rather take it step by step

[3:03 PM PT]

“may I put pomodoro website in my CV experience or projects section or its to easy for junior role and I need to create more complex projects?”

Good to remove simpler projects as we get better ones

Pomodoro project may be great

No shame in having simpler projects in the beginning, but as we get better projects, we just let the simpler ones fall of our resume and what we’re using to sell ourselves

“with rising expectation of AI use in job descriptions should we intentionally create projects with Claude files to show we have ecperienxce using them?”

Should definitely understand how to use AI and what the limitations are

Roxy (Chosen One) has been asked to showcase ability to use AI

This is likely going to become increasingly common in interviews

Leah from chat: Also important to be able to talk about HOW you used AI. Need to be able to speak more about things like PRDs and workflows, how plan mode works, how to-dos get broken into agents and subagents. Commands for certain agents. Reptile, bugbot, etc. Vercell agent to check that my hosting is fine. Need a good sense of how to map what your AI workflow is

Leon’s familiarity comes from being online too much and playing with the AI tools

Thinks we should try to do both, in a sustainable fashion ;)

Leon and chat realizes this is an entire class

People often glaze over when Leon starts talking about AI in depth like this

Leon is prioritizing this AI class

[3:06 PM PT]

“Plan Mode” in AI is this product requirement stuff

“Do current interviews include AI use to test if we know how to use it efficiently? What AI should we learn Claude, Gemini or something else? Or stick to no AI?”

Yes. There are more commonly in interviews an expectation from companies that we should be proficient with AI tools

Coming up enough where we should at least have some answers for it. Like opening a door

100Devs goal is to open as many doors for ourselves as possible

Understanding AI tooling is a part of this now

Depends on types of roles. Some are more AI heavy than not

Cursor is Leon’s current favorite AI tool

He has received lots of credits from them, so he has spent lots of time here

Thinks they have the best harness for most of the model

Uses Gemini with Cursor harness to get the most out of Gemini

Thinks he would still use Cursor for this if he had to pay for this

Cursor allows us to switch between different models very easily. Leon now knows which tool he would use for which task. This seems like the secret sauce

Deep understanding of models, which has been helped by Cursor allowing him to very easily swap

New projects? Claude

Dashboards, one-off standalone apps? Gemini

Deep code? CodeX

Start with the free accounts on everything. Play with it. Google subscription gives you pretty good Gemini

Do similar projects across tools. Don’t pay for anything. Shouldn’t be necessary.

Antigravity has good perspective on agent window, but now CodeX has it

CodeX inside of CodeX is faster

Lots of people already have openAI subscription, so that helps with this

Likes CL utilities, but uses these less. Cursor has been a dream

[3:11 PM PT]

“I’m good at coding, but I haven’t had a salaried role yet. Is a junior dev role worth it? I work as a cable technician now and would like a different role soon”

Yes. Personally don’t like “junior”. Entry level roles are great places to start

Engineering is not just the coding. Learn a lot in early professional roles, so helpful to get your foot in the door wherever you can

[3:12 PM PT]

“Do I tie my story to my 100Hours project? They seem a bit different than each other”

Good 100Hours project is typically something that ties to your story, but might also be based on a passion

Use 100Devs GPT to help craft story, and see if your project can tie into it

From chat: AI skills are very transferable

“Can you recommend something to see how to write tests, how test should look like and what to test please? I would like to learn it as some job offers list this”

Every company does testing +wildly+ different. Leon hasn’t traditionally taught it because of this

Just take any of the big testing libraries, read their documentation from beginning to end, and start adding coverage to your application. Best ways to learn

“Test Driven Development” book or course can then help

CodeWars incorporates this, so you may be doing more test-driven stuff already than you realize

[3:14 PM PT]

“I listen to your podcast and got curious. Can you pls do one huddle when you can show how you work with Ai agents, assign them different tasks as team etc pls?”

Sure. We can do this

Don’t want people to get the idea that Leon knows what he’s doing. Doesn’t think anyone does if they’re honest. Leon may be a couple of days ahead because he has been doing it too much. Changes every three minutes, though. Anyone pretending to know if likely lying and trying to sell you something

Can share current setup, but Leon is just playing around and finding things that seem to work for him at a given time

Feels like people are getting taken advantage of online

“I got four Mac Studios and now I’m making $1mil/month and you can, too!”

Leon wants to be a counterbalance to this. Don’t get taken advantage of by the snake oil salesmen that are now using AI to swindle us

[3:16 PM PT]

“I keep hearing AI is taking over Software Engineer jobs, even on CNN. It's making me a bit nervous, because they're making it seem as if anyone can use it.”

It’s their job to make you scared. That’s how news makes a living. They need to keep your attention

Look at hiring data and major company. Hiring is still increasing. IBM is going to be hiring 3x the entry level talent going forward

AI is a multiplier of force, as opposed to a replacement of force

One or two articles shouldn’t dictate your future

Companies are shipping more than ever before. Need way more people to help with this. Leon is very optimistic

[3:19 PM PT]

“I saw that Spotify developers haven’t written code since December and are just checking the AI written code. How do we learn/practice this?”

Try it. They have no idea what they’re doing. They’re just trying it more than you are. Try stuff, adjust, and try it again

Doing it at scale. Non-deterministic systems. It’s a black box

No idea what’s happening beneath deep learning. All made up and we’re just experimenting

“Power law of scaling”

If you keep investing more into LLM compute, you keep getting better outcomes

More electricity and compute gives you better outcomes. Just need to put resources into it to get better outcomes. Not actually sure why this is happening fundamentally. It’s all just experimentation at this point

[3:20 PM PT]

“Leon can you share a doc with these AI models and what they’re good for. This is a lot of information. I get to play with it, but help us start”

Programming Podcast covers this well whenever a new tool drops

Cursor puts out a good price chart. This should be the most important factor, says Leon

Lots of people talk to have either different opinions or very similar opinions as him

Lots of it likely comes down to personal preference

[3:22 PM PT]

“Is this weeks episode of The Programming Podcast going to be released?”

Lots of life stuff right now. Danny has a new opportunity. Might be a little delayed

[3:22 PM PT]

“what are the best and worst months for job hunt. I will be ready in 2-3months to apply and bit worry that it would be worst time as cs grads may join job market”

Best times are typically January, May, October. Those are the three best months to be in the market. Other softer ones. March, September a little less. Summer is typically kind of bad because people aren’t in office as much.

100Devs tries to schedule around these

Great time when CS graduates are coming in, because lots of jobs open up, and few CS grads have a lot of stuff that they’ve done. They’re usually just kids that studied, got decent grades, and typically don’t have any projects. 100Devs graduates have way more career-related experience, so CS grads should not be much competition

[3:24 PM PT]

“Are we going to make it?”

Definitely! Great hiring period right now. Easiest to learn at the moment, easiest way to get connected with people now. 10 years ago? Way harder to get in touch with people. Now, everything is online. Much easier to network and connect.

HuntOber is key, but that’s more preparing you for January. HuntOber helps you get all your stuff together so that you’re +on it+ in January. Lots of hiring at the beginning of October, too, though.

Feb can slow down a bit, and then March can come back hot depending on what area you’re in

[3:25 PM PT]

“Can you name resources to learn ai engineering please”

FastAI course. Practical Deep Learning for Coders (Jeremy Howard)

Best place to start.

Companion book from library “Deep Learning FastAI…”

Chip’s books on FastAI

Better suited for experienced engineers

Howard’s books are a little bit easier to approach

Danny also has an AI book coming out, but it’s not released yet

Check stage chat for more resources [3:27 PM PT]

This should be more than enough to get started

Thoughts on crafting interpreters?

No. He’s very practical. Doesn’t do as much learning to learn stuff. Wants very practical engineering stuff

He’s not as much into CS theory

“should we put udemy coursera and freecodecamp course cetrificates on linkedin or only certificates from masters like AWS or CS50 pls?”

None of these should be on LinkedIn

These just make you look like a beginner

AWS is fine if it’s the actual professional, industry standard certifications

“I just learned this thing” cert just makes you smell like a newbie, and we don’t want that. We want to reek that we’re a professional who is capable of solving our clients’ problems immediately

This is Leon’s personal opinion, but he thinks they hurt us more than help us

Leah in chat says we should use these skills to build a project, and then feature the project all over the place, not the cert. Leon agrees 1000%

[3:30 PM PT]

“We have Jobuaray and HunTober, are we going to have 'May-ployment' or 'May-nifest'?”

No. May/June is Leon’s vacation time. Lots of time in the woods

“When do you recommend starting to read ai related topics if you’re still early in 100devs? Like when to introduce it?”

Supplemental reading at any point is fine. Don’t use AI in learning process until you’re done with a course (like 100Devs)

Need to learn fundamentals without AI. Otherwise, our learning process is getting interrupted

Can read about and play with AI, but focus on coursework first. The coursework is what is going to get us the job, the AI tool is just extra. AI is a speed unlock, it should not be used at all when we’re learning how to do something

Talk about a 100Devs summer camp in the chat

[3:31 PM PT]

Leon makes a lot of tofu and rice dishes when camping. Tofu, rice, veggie dishes. Shelf-stable for a very long time. Lots of bean chilis. Has a really nice cooler that can help keep them out there for two weeks at a time

Bring musical instruments to play. Harmonica practice recently. (Laughed that ocarina was pretty close. [Zelda reference, Michael thinks <3] )

Back on Tuesday.

“One of the best times to be alive, to be a developer, and if you’re here with 100Devs, you’re so far ahead. You could have been picking your nose and watching Netflix, but you’re here putting in your vision of being a badass SWE into motion. Let’s keep up the momentum, and we’ll meet again to re-energize together next Tuesday <3”

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-02-24
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Tues 2/24/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:33 PM PT]

Talking about the Blizzard on the east coast. Shout out to the people digging out there.

stevenMosesCodes

“hi leon! i have a new development in my not-offer-yet saga 😅😅 may i talk it through with you? Thanks!”

Discussing live

Followed up with the people. Got a response. How does he respond to a specific e-mail, and what does it mean?

S: “Hey, just checking in about any updates. Still very excited and looking forward to next steps”

Recruiter: “I will get shortly back to you. There may be another link I would like you to apply to, and I will send that out by tomorrow”

[Michael’s audio from Leon is cutting out]

Leon thinks there’s a fit thing here

Recruiter might be finding a role that’s a better fit for Steven. Not a negative flag. Recruiter seems to be in Steven’s quarter, and now he just needs to work with them

Recruiter confirmed with Steven that title and pay band were okay. Steven somewhat frustrated. Thinks role might be too high for him, so maybe that’s the problem

Startupy team at a giant company

Leon said smaller subsidiaries can have wild head count requirements

Doesn’t seem odd that this is happening. Good that they didn’t ghost Steven

Don’t put all eggs in one basket. Keep hitlisting going, but don’t be too worried

Steven e-mailed, and they responded 6 minutes later, so Leon sees that they’re being responsive, which is a great sign

June in chat (2:39 PM PT)

Sometimes subsidiaries have more budget with parent, so they need you to apply to the parent. Internally, this helps with budgeting

Steven was afraid that with another link he’ll have to do the entire process again

Leon isn’t worried about this. They’re very responsive, they’re keeping Steven in the loop. This happens. Just moving stuff across different buckets. Leon doesn’t think we should be worried, but don’t get too invested in this opportunity. Keep exploring other opportunities, even if there don’t seem to be any red flags here

Leon agrees that Steven should follow up with confirmation. Give them a couple days, and then follow up again

[2:41 PM PT]

“In 100Devs, we learned about creating Classes when learning OOP but I've never seen anyone use Class in a project yet. How do we use/practice them in projects?”

Just have to practice

Can take OOP to code bases or any project we build

Can use principles of OOP in many ways. Especially in any of the node frameworks

Encapsulation, OOP best practices, etc.

May not be what we’re envisioning like if we were making a game

Different projects are organized in different ways. Open Source tools can have larger codebases that put some of these principles in play

Some teams you join may use fully functional programming and not use OOP every day, so don’t sweat it too much

Steven built a Discord bot using OOP principles

Leon likes building a project, especially using AI, and then have the AI give him advice on how he could have done it more optimally, and maybe how he could have done it using OOP

Build without AI, and then use the LLM to give you feedback. No longer need to find a human to do this

Just keep building

[2:44 PM PT]

Chat doesn’t think anyone is doing full-on academic OOP anymore in the real world

Leon not concerned about AI taking over and stealing his job. He has more work now than he has ever had before AI

Leon often gets stuck in ruts and will build random stuff to make sure his unused skills stay sharp

OOP is often used in many ways we’re not really paying attention to (see chat for examples)

[2:45 PM]

Chat thinks the tech that AI is creating will keep SWE’s employed for a long time. Leon kind of agrees

Slido clone Leon is building is using lots of AI tools

Leon has twelve projects right now just because he’s having lots of fun building. Impacting his sleep schedule 😛

Building a project to help address one of our pain points is one of the best ways to improve

Something we’re passionate about is great fuel

[2:46 PM PT]

Chat discussing that we shouldn’t feel like we’re late to the game. We’re here, and we’re ready for AI, so we’re exactly where we’re supposed to be

MayanWolfe in chat saying no one in her family is talking about AI at all. It’s just that we’re in a tech echo chamber that it’s a big topic for us

“Each dot is 3.2 billion people” AI usage image from Twitter that Leon screenshared

Very few people are actually using AI. Even fewer are paying for it

We’re worried, but only 2-5 Million people on the planet are seriously using AI at all

If we’re in this group, we’re so far ahead of the curve that it’s absurd

People are really overestimating how far ahead everyone else is in using AI, and at the same time underestimating how well we’re doing in terms of using AI in our growth

We’re just in a bubble. If we’re actively using it, or even if we’re not but at least understand something about AI, we’re doing wonderfully

[2:50 PM PT]

“What do I prep and expect for an online js and language agnostic technical? There will be 5 Qs to do in 60-90 mins within 3 days? Im out of practice 😕”

Always ASK first

“Super broad. Five questions about what? What should I be prepared for? I just want to make sure we’re both using our time effectively. What am I walking into?”

Most of the time, the people who want to interview will tell you

Then, turn over every stone. Ask LLM, go on Glassdoor, gitHub, anyone who may have been asked these questions by this company before, and then prepare from that

Try +really+ hard to go into an interview knowing exactly what’s going to happen

Language agnostic, so pick your favorite language

60-90 min is normal technical interview

Doesn’t seem like much to worry about

Just make sure our coding challenge game is good in terms of technical prep (LeetCode/Codewars)

Have a project recently built in the language you plan to use, just in case there’s a practical component

Best step is to ask to make sure that you know as much as possible about what to expect

Steven in chat [2:53 PM PT]

Typical pattern to these challenges. Make sure you know DS&A basics

“I have a final interview with the Managing Director, who seems to be the office's "boss." What should I expect?”

[2:54 PM PT]

Again, always ask what you’re about to walk into

Need to have at least a rough idea of what’s about to happen

Do your research

Managing Director (MD) can do one of two ways

Last technical check to make sure everyone else was right about you

Higher level interview is more common

Do you know company culture, strategy, etc?

Not only do you have raw engineering skills, but the ability to transfer those skills to provide value for the team

Did you do the due diligence to show that you’re a good person to work with who can help the team?

Leon has had interviews with CEOs who have asked him to reverse a string, so it can go either way

Companies often have published things about what they want you to bring up in interviews

Amazon has a guide for interviewees, for example, and if you don’t follow this guide, you’re not getting the opportunities, because they know you didn’t do your homework

“how to answer what are your weakness on interview to make it acceptable and show we learn the lesson. Also "why Google?" question as ppl say same boring stuff”

[2:56 PM PT]

Weaknesses is something you prepare

100Devs BANK is +very+ important

After an interview, you need to actively recall what was asked, so you have better answers the next go-around

Interview is more about preparing you for all the other job opportunities you have going forward than about the actual interview

This allows every interview to be a success, especially since most interviews will not lead to an offer

Focus on improving interview-to-interview, and that’s what’s going to get you the opportunities

If Leon doesn’t have a “greatest weakness” answer that rocks, he’ll be going home after the interview, getting with community, and making sure he has that great answer

If you have to think about answers to behavioral questions during an interview, you fucked up. Leon said this twice

Behavioral answers should be second nature +before+ the interview

All about doing your Banki. Are you using the CAR method for every question on Banki? Do you have a good CAR answer and a way to develop a solid answer afterwards to a novel question?

Specifically:

Tie weakness to something that’s real. Show you are reflective and can grow. Not something like “I’m too much of an overachiever”

“When working with customers in the past, I was too quick to acquiesce too quickly to their demands, which created too much work for team at the last minute. Learned to validate customer demands, put together a reasonable time frame for them to get what they want, and also make sure my team’s time was protected. Constantly working on this to improve so that I’m doing my best to help all stakeholders, internal and external”

Leon likes this because it show growth

“Why Google?”

This is an easy prep from doing research. Need to know about the company and give a reason for why you’re applying to that company

[3 PM PT]

Someone in the chat was new to the Huddle and feeling like it’s an entirely new language with all the terms we’re using

Leon says that’s how everyone starts. Just stick with us and it will become your language, too

Strain made a good comment in the chat about Weakness questions

MayanWolfe as well. Shout out to Banki

Weaknesses tied to actual event (lot of gold in the chat that Leon is highlighting)

HIGHLY IMPORTANT: Do not blame other people

Take accountability for what happened, demonstrate capacity for growth. Blaming others is not something someone is going to be impressed by

“Not a question just need some encouragement right about now”

Did you see that graph about most people not using AI to code?

You’re ahead of 8 billion people

You’re more informed, and with more skills, than 8 Billion other people on this planet

If this doesn’t convince you that you’re doing well, Leon can’t really do much more

Showing up to the Huddle? You’re fantastic. Just keep up the great work

No matter what the future of AI holds, you’re already ahead of 8 Billion people. You can seize the opportunities before they can, adapt before they can, and you’ll be alright

Keep showing up, keep working, and help blaze the path for others to follow in your successful footsteps

[3:03 PM PT]

“If we have time, can we quickly go over my story? I used the GPT '100 Devs Craft Coach, but feel I might be focusing on the wrong things in my life story” - UncleNoob

Yup. Leon has other questions in the Slido, but he seems to like prioritizing the live answers, so don’t be afraid to raise your hand and ask live

Don’t need camera for live answers. Audio is fine. Noob is putting story in the chat

Story:

“(Career of solving problems → code as another tool)

"I spent years organizing other people's chaos - coordinating authors, editors, and designers at Bint Publishing, helping small businesses at TELUS find systems that actually fit how they worked, maintaining operations at a medical clinic where things breaking had real consequences.

I got good at spotting what was broken and figuring out what would fix it. But I kept running into the same wall as an individual: I couldn't find a task management tool that worked for how I actually think. Every app I tried showed me everything at once - my entire backlog, every priority, every project - and instead of helping me start my day, it paralyzed me.

I tried to hire developers to build something better it was not practical at the time, or they didn't understand the problem well enough to solve it.

So I learned to code. Not to become a developer - to build the thing I actually needed. That became BloomSpace: a workload system built around individual execution, not project tracking. The idea is simple - separate what you need to capture from what you need to do today, so you can start your day without staring down everything you're not doing."

[Link from chat: https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475991903324606709 ]

Leon thinks this is a solid first step. Hopes chat will share their thoughts, too

Couple pieces. If he’s just talking to another Dev, this is a good story

May be some things you can do to be better

Try to keep it a singular paragraph. Shorter is better

Speak more grand. “Big publishing” becomes “One of the largest publishing firms on the east coast”

Want audience’s mind to wander about what your experience could have been, and have them overestimate

Be vague in ways that help make you look better

Ex. Helping medical clinics. Don’t be too specific. Talk on grander scale. Be specific about what you fixed. “Helped hundreds of medical clinics fix X”

“Running into the same problem”

Second half of this paragraph seems negative. Project management but got paralyzed? Maybe Noob can’t handle workload. Too risky as it can cause people to go negative

“As I was working for the largest publishers and on medical breakthroughs, task management became a challenge, and so I build this tool to help me and teams of folks I worked with, and now I want to bring these same skills to your organization and help solve these problems together”

Name-dropping BloomSpace seems like more of a pitch for BloomSpace than a pitch for UncleNoob

Frame everything as a hook that puts us in the best possible light

[3:10 PM PT]

Gotta be the main hero of your story:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475993162563457247

Leon strongly agrees

Leon tries to put himself in the shoes of the grumpiest hiring manager

Just checking boxes and keeping stuff moving. Doesn’t want to read more than what they have to. Don’t give them the easy “No’s” for your candidacy. Bangers only

100Devs wisdom from chat: “When you walk into the interview, you are the savior.”

Leon remembers a quote “All your gods are dead. I’m here now” Loves it.

“How to prepare for cultural fit interview. Its easy to find online possible questions but answering them is another story. Any website with good examples pls?”

[3:12 PM PT]

Can find answers to these. Still have to practice and prep

RECORD YOURSELF saying your answers. Leon can’t understand why people don’t do this more

Helps you see what your answers look like. Once you watch the recording, you can see much more about how you’re conveying yourself. Much easier to do this vs. trying to do it live when you’re actually saying it

Banki Brunch can help others help you surface these issues

Can also pass your responses to an LLM, ask it to be as critical as possible, and help you improve them

Other thing: They are looking for a specific answer. Your goal, and it’s a difficult skill for many of us, is to look at stated culture on their website and how your background upholds the values they are looking for

This is a lot of work, but we need to put the work in before an interview for it to be natural, and we need to be willing to do this for every interview if we really want to do our best

MayanWolfe from the chat: Can’t just watch guitar videos. Need to practice it yourself if you want to get good

[3:14 PM PT]

WhisperFlow and ChatGPT for feedback

Single party consent state? Record your interviews, and feed them to GPT to see what you can improve

Gemini seems really good for this. Leon will record his audio for everything. Sales calls, interviews, and then he asks Gemini what he could have done differently

Chat: Rufio is streaming tonight, and Leon wants your support for the 100Devs stream team, as always

[3:15 PM PT]

“how come i dont get any phone calls after applying for 100s of roles (java backend) on linkedin? is there anything better than linkedin? YEO: 4 years”

Yes. applying for jobs DOES NOT WORK

Watch the 100Devs videos on how to get a job

If you’re just clicking apply, this does not work. You might get lucky once in a blue moon, but it’s not nearly as good as 100Dev’s approach of networking your way in and having a standardized process

Something may be wrong with your online presence, narrative, resume, etc. 100Devs has tools for this, including a Narrative GPT, that can help

Check with the 100Devs community about this

LinkedIn is also very flooded. If you did something other than LinkedIn, and did nothing else, that might help (100Devs uses HiringCafe)

Use the 100Devs methods. It still works

If you’re using AI in any part of the application process, it’s an auto-reject now, so don’t use AI. Everything needs to be in our own words. People want authenticity

Leon offered to help this questioner live

Every major AI tool leaves a signature in our text. As soon as they paste your text into an ATS system, it will flag it as AI generated. Really need to type it out and edit it in your voice

Leon has talked to countless hiring managers that will automatically reject anything that has AI in it. They +HATE+ dealing with AI-generated applications that waste their time.

No copy-pasting AI-generated content, as it can keep the AI signature

[3:19 PM PT]

Even if they don’t use an ATS to check, all the AI submissions are going to look similar enough for them to tell

“Brain Freezing Up When Encountering New Tools and Unexpected Bugs. Resources to Recommend?”

Brain Freezing up is likely a mindset shift

Biggest mindset shift for Leon, especially for non-deterministic stuff

Used to ask when he hit a bug: “What went wrong?”

Now thinks this is a mistake

Now: “I wrote the code in a particular way. Why was this the outcome?”

Slight difference, but now the bug is the by-product of what he has done. It’s not something that randomly happened to him. It’s something he did that led to a result

Did he do something differently? Is the bug repeatable? Does he keep getting the same error?

Realizing that he generated the bug helps empower him to know that he can also find a way to fix it and, ideally, not generate this type of bug again

Getting Good at Debugging as a Skill

Good to have process you go through. Pre-flight checklist, like if you were flying a plane

Should have something similar when debugging. Checklist of steps you walk through each time

Get used to using debugging tools. Use a debugger, logger, print statements, etc. to your advantage to get more clarity around what is happening

Get better at reading through the flow of information and all the touchpoints of your code to see how the error is being produced

Mindset that it’s fixable, checklist that you work through, and use of the appropriate tools

You now have a system to work through that can make the process of debugging much easier.

Typically 90% of what is going to happen to you is one of ten or so different things

Async thing? State thing? Only so many things that can go wrong.

If you’re able to repeat it, you can learn how to narrow down which thing is likely happening

GPT can give you a road map about debugging methods and what to look for. It’s definitely a learnable skill, and so we can find debugging techniques that have worked for people that are likely to work for us, too

Much of debugging is about practice and experience. The more we do it and stick to an effective checklist, the more the issues surface in a familiar way, we get more practice at getting familiar with a process that works, so each time we debug it can be a little easier and we can be more confident in our capacity

Straine in the chat: “The mindset Leon is talking about is engineering condensed”

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475996294949638206

Leon agrees 1000%

[3:22 PM PT]

“Five years of experience. Talked to a recruiter who said I would be good for an associate position. How do I get rid of the smell that’s keeping me from getting a full stack job?”

With five years of experience, you need to be leaning into your expertise that allows you to demonstrate that you are obviously the best fit for a role.

This takes some effort to do

Have to look at all the things you put forth in your applications or online presence that may be contrary to you being a good senior fit for a full stack role

Old portfolio, lack of distilling experience into a meaningful narrative that highlights meaningful things that would enable you to enact positive change at the company from day one

Good narrative, good story, good examples of meaty problems you’ve worked on and the solutions that you’ve crafted

Be able to articulate this and share it with others

Maybe make content around this, so you can point to things and say clearly, “Here’s what I did”

If you haven’t yet, take stock of those five years and turn them into actionable insights that you can share during your interview and online publicly

Make sure that you did get rid of the smell. Get other people to review your online presence and materials. The 100Devs community is here for you, and hopefully you’re here for them, too.

Anything that doesn’t sit right? You’ve got to get rid of it to make sure you’re obviously the best candidate for the roles you’re pursuing

Chat: “Pro tip: Keep a "wins" diary as you do your work.”

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1475997050364756131

[3:25 PM PT]

Absolutely. Current cohort of RC (Resilient Coders) has to do a daily wins journal and share their wins every day

Sometimes it’s silly stuff: “I found the hot sauce in the back of my pocket. Wonderful.”

Important to get in the habit of writing stuff down. Lots of time you’ve done something cool, and having it marked down is very helpful when you’re looking for opportunities, in line for promotions, showing up to performance reviews, etc

Without this log of wins, you’re not going to remember something that happened months or years ago, and we have to be our biggest cheerleaders and keep track of this stuff to effectively sell ourselves and provide proof for why we clearly deserve the things we’re striving for

“Contract looking to negotiate says pay range is around 70K for a QA role. Should I keep the negotiation written in email?”

You do not begin negotiating until you have a signed offer letter (Leon’s typical advice)

Once you get a signed offer letter, you can play hardball

Until then?

“Sounds great! Your range seems like it might be within my range…”

Once you get the signed offer letter, then it’s time to negotiate

For negotiation:

Find out what the real range is for the job

Check GlassDoor about what people are getting paid

Salary bands for a specific area

QA compensation for those in NYC is going to be very different from compensation in a small Midwestern city.

Local demands on salary is very important

Use all of this information when you go to negotiation. Important to be as informed as possible, just like during the interview

Classic 100Devs line: “It seems like we’re close. I’m very excited to join the team. This seems like a great opportunity, is there any wiggle room in the starting salary?” … and then we play it from there

Leon vastly prefers negotiation on video, or at least via phone.

When he throws out a number, facial expressions tell him a +ton+

From these expressions, he can see if he’s reaching too far with his ask, or maybe he’s not reaching far enough

Writing it down is not optimal for him, because he loses all of this information

Some people may prefer written negotiation, but Leon finds this very suboptimal for him, and he identifies as someone with social anxiety, so if it’s worth the effort for him, it’s likely to be worth the effort for us to do this stuff on video

“Name of Networking App that Leon uses?”

Leon uses Apollo.io to find people’s professional contact

Free account gives you enough information to find people and their contact information

“Hunter” is a good one, too

“AI Tools that you recommend checking out?”

Anything that has a free plan, you should check it out

You’ve got to play with everything

Leon a big fan of Cursor

Raffle should happen on Friday

Most of his stuff is done is Cursor these days

Has Claude, Gemini, and Whisperflow. With Cursor, these four are his main stack

Does some local stuff with oLlama

Leon feels pretty happy with his tooling at the moment.

[3:30 PM PT]

Michael had to leave to donate blood, so he missed the end of the Huddle after this point. Sorry :(

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-02-27
html:
[Rushed notes, and I’m still behind on some projects, but hopefully I can spellcheck this at some point.]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Leon usually posts the Slido in the 100Devs Discord (#follow-along-materials channel) about 30 minutes before the Huddle begins. Alternatively, Huddle attendees can put their Discord hands up and ask live, as long as it’s about the job hunt.]

These notes were taken on Google Docs during the Huddle.

As always, I hope it helps someone and that everyone reading this is having a wonderful day. Thanks, as always, for all the good that you do, especially by taking excellent care of yourself, too <3

Discord Link to Join the community: ​

https://discord.gg/100devs

… which is necessary to access any Discord links in the community I might have below. Just please make sure to be on your best behavior, and review the server rules channel, as I think 100Devs is a very special place to be <3)

Here’s a link to the 100Devs Discord server thread with notes from previous Huddles, too: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/1410358067450740746/1464407740003778560

100Devs Huddle - Fri 2/27/26 - 2:30 PM PT - 3:30 PM PT

[Huddle started at 2:32 PM PT]

Happy Pokemon Day

Someone in the chat got headhunted from a Hackathon

Hopes people post in the #celebrations channel

Big Update planned for Tuesday, likely from Leon tracking the #celebrations channel

Audio cutting in and out on Microsoft Edge, so Michael has had to refresh

Jason shared some good news in the chat

Only one Slido question at the moment

“starting my new role next monday (yippee) but i am nervous. I was sent onboarding stuff yesterday and do i need to do this asap before wknd or on monday?”

Hopefully, they have shared Onboarding context

Often there’s a guide to follow

Often they give stuff ahead of time that you work on the first day

Check the materials. Check with your contacts for clarity

Leon likes putting the extra step forward to get stuff done in advance if you can. Good to start strong

Nervousness on first day is normal. Lean into being the new person

Grace period for the first month or two

100Devs Wisdom for the first 90 days

Get there early, leave late

Never eat alone. Form good relationships with people on team

Get permission to ask all questions and promise to document answers so you never ask them twice and can help the next wave of people with their onboarding

Write down everything that you work on (Win log, great for performance reviews)

Talk to manager early to make sure you have good targets to aim for

Confirm via e-mail to have in writing

Can then circle back for performance reviews and raises and show you did what was expected (and hopefully more)

“How do I frame a layoff due to restructuring or short work terms on resume in a way that feels confident not defensive”

Most working professionals in tech have experienced a layoff themselves or know those in network who have

No stigma anymore. No pull-back

Everyone has understood this game for 10+ years. Part of the tech hiring cycle to overhire and do layoffs, especially in the post-COVID era. Don’t need to worry about it at all

Always have something after the layoff. Freelancing or something to show you’ve been active, building, and working with others

Short periods of unemployment not usually a problem

2 Schools of Thought:

  1. Never make the recruiter think

If recruiter has to think about what something means, you miss an opportunity

Some people put “Layoff, 4000 people laid off”

Recruiter: Okay. Lots of layoffs. Not based on performance. No problem

  1. Omit the negatives

Now recruiter has to wonder, and you need to have a good explanation for gaps

Chat: Double-employed?

Need a good explanation. Most employers don’t like their employees double-dipping and not giving them their full attention

Leon is wearing a Bulbasaur shirt that is greenscreening

Image is in the Huddle announcement if you want to see the full shirt

Chat: Drew is up on the YouTube culture

[2:40 PM PT]

Leon met them at CollectaCon and got the shirt there

Some vendors recognized him there while he was buying Pokemon cards

Had to tell people not to give him discounts

Leon is soft-resetting on Leaf Green for a “Shiny Bulbasaur”. That’s going to be his Friday night

“did you rethink/update your AI agent permissions/auth after that last programming podcast? What did you change? What have you learned?”

Last programming podcast had VP of engineering from Octa

Powers lots of the sign-on flows for many companies.

Lot around now about how to in agentic systems, make sure agents have the right permissions and are authenticated in the right way, especially as we scale

Leon has been playing with OpenClaw

Lots of his life he could give access to, but is apprehensive

Not happy with anything right now. Hard to find balance with overengineering when we’re iterating, and have stuff we’re confident in

He’s still work this out, and will share when he figures it out more

Still in the figuring out mode for a lot of AI stuff

Shout out to Octa for sponsoring that podcast and sponsoring going forward

[2:42 PM PT]

Chat: Can you run it local?

Yup. Don’t need a Mac Mini, but lots of people like being in the Apple Ecosystem to iMessage it

Need to find balance of time between experimenting and doing too much work

Leon said he finds himself in this trap often (from chat response)

James shared something in the chat that Leon liked

“is it ok to list tech stack used at 100devs on github/Linkedin/CV without context and project separately or we need to back up every single stack with projects?”

No international committee for the standardization of resumes. All made up

Test what works in local market

Leon thinks anything on resume should be backed up with what technology you use for a project

Goal is not to break flow of resume

Every skill you list should have something to back it up and you can use an example of how you used it

Local market likes FlimFlam?

Need strong proof of FlimFlam proficiency throughout resume

“I fail to consistently plan out my days; I do it for a week or two and then stop altogether. Do you have any advice?”

First thing is to figure out why, and how you’re getting value

If you’re planning out your days and its beneficial, you should probably keep doing this

Some people don’t get value out of it and stop

Could be lots of issues. Executive Functioning, ADHD (like Leon)

When his day is planned, things go smoother

Uses his Calendar aggressively. Shares this with family so he has both buy-in and external motivation

Phone reminders to keep him on track

Time blocks that are dedicated for different things

Lots of reminders directing him to do the things he wants to do

Systems in place to help him with planning

Systems are designed to help him succeed even on his worst days

If you can build out a system that works even on the worst day, you’re more likely to succeed

Figure out the why. And was it impactful for you? Get buy-in from people in social life

Get reminders and systems

Remove friction

Don’t get a wild Obsidian setup that is tough to manage. Use the easiest note app and use that to plan

Leon’s to-do list is one file that he adds to the top of. Other stuff gets pushed down

Things either get done or deleted. Very simple

Chat: How many hours a day do you work?

Start teaching at 7AM. Work until 5 PM. Family time until like 8:30-9 PM. Tries to get another hour or two in at night.

12 hour days, typically. That helps Leon keep his brain happy. He likes to work and be productive, though, so this may not work for everyone.

Why question:

Why is it a struggle?

Good question to bring to see if we need professional help

If there’s something we want to do but can’t do it on our own, then it’s time to get help

Overarching why

Why are you choosing to do what you want to do?

“What would udo if LinkedIn forced u to upload ID? There are many articles this week with Discord and LinkedIn and all the Persona data leaks. Can I avoid LI?”

Not having a LinkedIn is closing a massive door

Big part of professional lives

Unless there’s something safety-wise stopping you from verifying, I think it’s probably the most important to do this on LinkedIn

Only want you to have one account, and want to make sure you’re a real person

Very common to ask for verification in a non-US area

Personal decision. His thoughts and feelings shouldn’t dictate your behavior, but he thinks a polished LinkedIn is huge

Discord stepping back from verification, but Leon thinks it’s going to be coming to every platform in the near future

[2:51 PM PT]

Leon has heard of people get flagged by LinkedIn and have to verify to continue to be featured on the platform.

California is also potentially going to require verification on operating system (may be a meme)

Chat says they miss the old internet, and Leon agrees

“I was told all I need to get job in my area is Python and I don't need front end. Do you agree? Can I build just backend? What projects can be build that way?”

Leon would need to know your area

[2:52 PM PT]

Leon is a big fan of learning Full Stack Web Development. Strong Advocate

Build full stack and specialize later based on preferences and local demand

Now, everyone is expected to be more of a generalist with an understanding of the full stack

Be very cautious about specializing, as this can close us off from opportunities

Lots of the frontend stuff is also not challenging to pick up, but can lead to more opportunities. Easy win

Leon loved using AIM (from the chat)

[2:54 PM PT]

Leon misses before social media, when people were able to connect without all the social comparison and posturing

“When looking at roles on company sites, is it generally worth doing the "Join Our Talent Community" thing? Do hirers actually look at that?”

Leon doesn’t think this hurts. Doesn’t think it should be our strategy, though. Don’t lean on this as only way into a company

“I had my first big tech interview recently. I think I did well on one leetcode question but bombed the 2nd one. I think being too nervous got to me. Any advice?”

Why do you think….

Couple big things:

Is it true that you bombed? Did you go back and review your answer and make sure you prepped well? Make sure you’re not missing fundamentals

Nervousness is all about time in saddle, butt in seat. Need more practice and exposure

Couple big things with 100Devs:

Can’t lose a job you don’t have

Don’t ride rollercoaster of interviews. Stay smooth throughout

Leon tries not to get too excited about interviews. Helps him not get too devastated in the normal situation where it doesn’t work out

Get lots of practice. Record yourself. Practicing with others, like on the 100Devs Discord

Nervousness will get easier to manage over time. May not ever go away. Just need to learn to live with it

Lots of people with public speaking issues can do things like Toastmasters and other things, Commit Your Talk thing

This can help with comfort, but it’s really just about getting the practice in, regardless of how we’re doing it

“PREP” is also key for technical interviews, says Chat. Leon agrees. 100Devs tech interview method

“I am working through Cohort 2 and verbally committed to never send a Linked-In connect without a message. Now they limit those unless we pay. Any advice?”

Be selective with message that you send

Find another way to send message when you make connection

LinkedIn through the Group method works

Join common group with the person, and then send a message to them through the group page

Find them on twitter, e-mail (Apollo.io to find e-mails), and say “Hey, added you on LinkedIn, trying to connect. etc.”

“in the past huddle I heard freelancing can be done as agency instead of self-employed. Can you please tell us more about it how to advertise ourselves as agency”

Just terminology. Still self-employed

Agency is just the assumption that you’re working with multiple clients/individuals. No actual terminology definition around it

Often better to speak in grander words if your goal is to get a job. Agency sounds more professional

[2:59 PM PT]

Even if you work for a full-blown agency, you’re often still a contractor, so it’s the same thing as being self-employed or 1099’d

“People keep saying that at tech interviews if I use methods to solve a challenge they will tell me to do it without methods. Is this true? Shouldn't I use them?”

Have you tried it?

Lots seems to depend on how you communicate/collaborate in an interview

If Leon has someone who is struggling and throws out a method, he’s going to ask them to explain the method to make sure they understand it

If someone has been rocking PREP and uses a method to progress through things, Leon will not second-guess this

Communication is key

Has been in interviews where he has been asked to sort something, he used a sort method, and they ask him to do something more advanced

Helps test the base level DS&A knowledge

Leon is fine with built-in methods. Often the most optimal way to solve things

Leon loves it when people know the underlying foundations of the methods

Chrome has a sort method different from other browsers, and you know this? Great

Don’t listen to the internet. Use the interviews to inform your strategy for the next one. Nothing beats the interviews, especially in the local market, to inform your strategy. This should trump even Leon’s advice

Chat: Framing is a problem. People do the right things but don’t articulate well

This is why Leon emphasizes Banki and GPT narrative. Lots of blocks that prevent people from featuring themselves well

Leon blames capitalism, but it’s a story for another day (He thinks, I believe, that this prevents us from feeling capable)

“Is Bob the mannequin Uncle Bob?”

No? Uncle Bob is the “Clean Code” author, we think

Bob is lit, though (Leon showed him on the stream. It’s who he uses when he goes on break during the classes)

Manchester United scarf

Pokemon City championship medals

“Bob’s kind of lit”

Lots of bling. Definitely dripping

[3:04 PM PT]

“How do you feel about posting your projects on LinkedIn or twitter?”

YOU SHOULD BE DOING IT! Every single day and week

Put it up there. Build in public

If you learn something specifically in programming or AI, there are a million other people who don’t know. Don’t write your content for the TurboNerd that knows this stuff. Write for the people who don’t know it yet

Don’t care about the four super trolls. Focus on the millions of other people who might be interested in what you have to say (and, from Michael, who you can potentially help <3 )

Chat says it’s a badge of honor if you post something and get a “Well, actually”

Big joke in Leon’s friend group. Just yell “NERD” to each other a lot

With a newer friend, and they were eating lunch somewhere (Cheesecake Factory)

Telling story about how they yell nerd at each other

Dropped him off at the middle of Harvard Square. People all around. Yelled “NERD” at the friend as he drove off

Wife told him as they drove off that the girl he screamed at was in the bathroom and missed the nerd story

Last time Leon talked to this person again, likely because they had no idea why Leon yelled and them like that

“Oops” [Leon giggling]

Chat: “How to Lose Friends and Influence People” Leon LOL’d

“Am no experience career changer. How many projects should I list in CV as I have nothing else and not sure if I can list doing freelancing - no paid project yet”

Have to think of everything in terms of steps

Start with what you have

One project? Put the project. Add two as you get them

More projects? List the better projects. More clients? List the better clients where you did/made more

Be quick to change your resume and project list as stuff improves

100Devs emphasizes paid client. You should really try to get one. It +really+ makes a difference to have someone pay you

At the end of 100Devs, you’ll likely have a much different resume that what you had in the beginning and throughout the process

Start with what you’ve got, and improve over time

Chat: Boston Drivers?

Leon thinks California drivers are way worse. Boston is competent, but wild drivers

If you’re a wild driver, once the snow hits in Boston, you crash your car. Teaches you how to handle car well

Wind up with wild drivers who are competent

Los Angeles doesn’t have a forcing method to shake out the silliness

Wild drivers who have no idea how to handle car

Speed up to get in front of you, then slow down to exit, as opposed to just getting behind you and exiting

Leon +HATES+ this in LA driving. Just merge behind me. It’s not hard

Shakes with rage every time this happens

[3:11 PM PT]

Video of the new crosswalk. Crosswalk brick where you hold a brick while crossing, and cars will stop because they’re threatened by the brick

“What is better do virtual hackathon with team of total 4 strangers or go solo to make sure project gets done and no one breaks code it just before deadline?”

Depends

If you’re going to a Hackathon, it’s to make friends. Leon is not a competitive Hackathoner

Go make friends, have fun, meet people. He’ll always pick strangers. Network, meet people, have fun building with strangers

Some people take Hackathons very seriously

RC (Resilient Coders) Cohort had a group that just rocked Hackathons. Good performance at previous hackathons? Can stick with this group and build up

Leon mostly about having fun and meeting people, though

Leon says his ASMR videos are on the OnlyFans (joking)

His educational stuff is free, he says. The other stuff is premium <3

Chat: “OnlyBob” Leon LOL’d

ASMR reading of the React doc? Asked chat?

Leon said he would do it if we all promise to put it on our YouTube Sleep Playlist (Leon laughing)

“Any advice before scoping and pricing a client portal for a real estate company? I'm handling the frontend and meeting their backend engineer on Monday”

Figure out what budget is first and foremost

“What is your budget to get this done? Wonderful. What do you want to get done at this price point?”

Only then, do you scope

Chat talked about sleep playlist

Leon says something about pawn videos really help him fall asleep, better than meds

“You’re a medieval peasant, here are 40 ways you’ll die”

“What will life be like as a medieval peasant”

He said this channel went really wild, but it was good in the beginning for him

Ballpark budget, then worry about pricing after

“I got gifted my first raspberry pie and don't know how to start with it, and what to do with it? Do you have any idea or sugestions or resources please?”

You eat it, Silly. (joking)

Don’t like putting his pies in the fridge. Doesn’t like the cold affecting the crust

Leon just joked this

Chat saying someone should cut his mic

Rasberry Pi is a tech thing. Making fun the typo

Lots of Leon’s Pi stuff is from YouTube

Pi is just a computer that you can plug into. Nothing special

Do sell hats or attachments for the Pi that can help it do stuff

Different from Arduino where you have to program it more

Pi can be treated like a normal Linux machine. Just play with it

Youtube and Reddit are good resources for Leon

Find projects you can do on the Pi

OpenClaw

Alternative Internet

Lots of projects using Pi’s

Leon has an offline internet that has a Wifi Signal

Has all of Wikipedia and Ted Talks

Leon has helped build computer labs around the world that feature this offline internet of content

PiHole in the chat

Lots of good resources, including the offline Wikipedia stuff, shared in the chat

[3:18 PM PT]

“in live coding if you can't remember syntax what do you do? ask to google or use ai or something else”

Like an interview? Or on Twitch?

Twitch: You google it, or ask chat

Interview: It depends on what they allow. Always ask +before+ the interview, and then you’ll know what to expect

“I talked to Y before interview, and they said this was allowed…”

PREP’ing can really come in handy

Get people to say yes and help you the entire time. Makes it very natural for them to help you when you get stuck

PREP’s big value is to help build rapport

Most companies instruct interviewers to give you a hint

Google’s lead recruiter told Leon specifically this. You have to ask for the hint, and you have to get to the point where it’s helpful for you

Always communicating during interview is key

“Just got a new job with a government agency in a construction field. Any tips to move sideways into technology side of things?”

[3:20 PM PT]

Tech is often separate from the groups

Not every group has own tech team. Often uses outside consultants

If there’s a tech arm of the group?

Normal strategy is apply. Never eat alone. Build good rapport and float idea that you’ll be interested in more technical aspects

More about enhancing current role

Take on more technical bits, and then can float idea about moving to tech side of org

Say you’re interested in opportunities on the tech team. Get manager buy-in and team buy-in, and make your switch

Chat talked about value of getting to know consultants, which Leon agreed with

“Will there be an announcement outside of the calls that a Cohort 3 launch date is announced?”

All announcements are done in the newsletter

Leon wants us to open up every newsletter, click on everything, because he wants engagement

He’s joking, though

[3:23 PM PT]

When Leon sends the newsletters, that will be the way

He only really sends these when he thinks there’s something important to share. Tries not to overwhelm us

Chat asking for monthly newsletter, which Leon is fine with

Reply to one of the e-mails to make sure it doesn’t go to Spam or a Promotions folder

This helps e-mail providers realize 100Devs newsletters is a valuable e-mail for people. It’s a very big help for the 100Devs community for us to let our e-mail provider know that the newsletter is important to us

Can also turn on ping notifications on Discord to make sure we don’t miss anything

Helps us stay informed so we don’t miss stuff. Leon tries not to overwhelm us.

“Hi Leon, should I create a portfolio site before I finish my 100-hour project?”

Yup. Takes a while for the SEO stuff to kick in, so put it out early so you can start to rank on search engines. The earlier the better

Chat: “Let it bake” Exactly

[3:25 PM PT]

“Is Twitter still as good for networking as it was a few years ago? I keep hearing not but it's from non-tech folk. There may be some political influence in that”

Leon thinks it might be better now

Yeah, for sure. Fell out of favor due to politics

Leon thinks it’s the only place where people aren’t fully overwhelmed with work stuff

LinkedIn mostly people looking for jobs

E-mail is often full of noise

Twitter is something people may often check, especially if they post often

One of Leon’s best methods to communicate with people

Don’t use the “For You” page on Twitter

Just look at people that you’re following

Helps reduce the rage-bait on Twitter

Leon thinks BlueSky is great, but Twitter is often much more active

[3:27 PM PT]

Leon gets that some people don’t like Twitter

Still thinks it has immense value in the job search, but respects those who don’t like the politics

Leon’s Twitter feed is full of Pokemon stuff right now, which he loves

Pokemon, AlphaSchool, and ClawBot for homeschooling

95% of Leon’s feed right now.

“what sites or sources can we use to find Start ups companies? (LinkedIn doesn't seem to be cutting it)”

Couple that Leon has shared in the past

The WatchListAI

Shows people who have raised fundraising

“BroJo”?

HitList magic inside of Google search will surface all sorts of companies

“WellFound” in the chat

Leon thinks this is okay, but not as good as it used to be

[3:29 PM PT]

Leon likes Pokemon Wave and Wind getting updated graphics.

Still going to play it (from the chat)

“How would you feel about moving from a modern startup dev role to working with enterprise tools like AEM in a corporate environment?”

[3:29 PM PT]

Thinks this depends on what you want out of career and pay

Bigger orgs often have more stability, pay, but less growth

Smaller teams can be a preference for lots of startups, and can provide a better environment

Startup to Corp gives you other unique experiences

How to manage larger orgs of people and larger codebases

Can help keep your resume better rounded

Leon thinks this is a personal preference thing

[3:31 PM PT]

Happy Pokemon Day!

“May all your catches go on the first try”

See you next Tuesday <3

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-11-07
html:
(Notes taken from the live Huddle by Michael Govaerts)

[Unedited, un-spellchecked notes. Posted as soon as possible after the huddle. Please see screenshots in the 100Devs Huddle Notes thread [under the catchup crew channel] for screenshots related to this session and Huddle Notes from previous Huddles.]

[More 100Devs Lectures from Leon about Technical Interviews, DS&A, and Networking Our Way into jobs:

https://communitytaught.org/class/all ]

100Devs Huddle: - Fri 11/7/25 at 2:30 PM PT

(100Devs approach for Solving Technical Problems, as demonstrated during HuntOber 2025 in the 100Devs Discord)

[2:36 PM PT. Michael is multitasking, so he may miss some things. Sorry :(

Michael also isn't planning to take exactly what Leon is doing, as he thinks the 100Devs lectures likely have Leon solving problems. He's focusing instead on the tips and tricks Leon emphasizes.

(Solving a coding problem together, with Leon sharing his screen)

- 100Devs uses the PREP method (Parameters, Returns, Examples, Pseudocode, emphasizing communication at all stages and developing rapport with the interviewer, who will often give us hints.)

- First question to ask: "Can I take notes?" (Great way to start building that rapport and get the interviewer to start saying, "Yes", and responding to your questions with real answers.)

- Try to avoid writing down your method for problem-solving in the IDE. We don't need to show our interviewer(s) that we're using a specific method for technical interviewing. (So don't write out "PREP" or "UMPIRE" in the IDE.) We want to make it look like we're seasoned veterans with our internalized system

- Please remember: If we get someone in an interviewer who does something +very+ different than what we're used to, don't sweat it. Just do your best and keep optimizing for the majority of interviews where the interviewer is hoping to help you, don't optimize for the rare meanies that are out there, who you probably don't want to work with anyway <3

- Ask questions. Clarify if Googling is okay

(Example problem we worked: "Count all the occurring characters in a string. If you have a string like aba, then the result should be {'a': 2, 'b': 1}.)

-- String problem? Confirm normal characters, that it's always going to be a string or empty. All lowercase

^ This is the "P" of PREP, where we identify the Parameters specifically

- Now we do "R" Returns

-- Confirm what you should be outputting

- "E" - Example(s)

-- Write a function that takes your parameters and returns what you confirmed you'll be returning

[Questions from yesterday that Michael remembers: If you're going for Full Stack Web Development, JavaScript is probably your best bet for a language to use for technical interviewing. If you're going for Big Tech or something else, Python or C++ may be better. However, JavaScript is the 100Devs default, so there doesn't seem to be any reason not to focus on that language, especially since we're going for Full Stack. Leon cross-trains with multiple languages when he does LeetCode; however, in order to stay nimble.]

- Leon took 15 minutes to go through the first three stages of PREP. No big deal. Gives him lots of time to think, allows him to focus on building rapport with the interviewer, and also makes it less likely for the interviewer to try to get Leon to solve something else at the end. Leon often recommends confirming at the beginning of the interview, "Will this be the only problem we're solving today?" Then, if the interviewer says something like "We would have solved more if you were faster," at the end, you can confirm you established in the beginning that we were just going to solve the one, and therefore even if you +could+ have gone faster, you didn't because you established the scope of the interview at the beginning.

- When you open things when programming, make sure you close them right away (so, in Javascript, just make an empty for loop that's closed, vs. having to worry about whether or not you still need to close it at the end.)

- Confirm if, "on our team," we should leave comments in the code or not.

-- Emphasizing "our team" as much as possible in the conversation to message that Leon's a good fit for team culture (and thus, a great person to extend an offer to.)

- Likes this problem (from Michael's screenshots in the Huddle Notes thread), because it helps introduce hash maps

-- Much more optimal solution than a quadratic solution that would have used nested loops, but may be tempting when trying to brute force the solution

- Hash maps are a +very+ elegant way to solve problems like these. Great tool to have in our technical interviewing toolbox

- JavaScript has "Map", which is a fun tool to use that's language-specific, but hash maps are something to know regardless of what language we're in.

--> Presented TwoSum

-- Brute force way to find all pairs that add up to the given sum?

--- Nested for loop

---- Outside loop grabs the first number, inside loop tests it against all other values, finds pair, and then moves on.

- Brute forcing first is often something interviewers like to see. Don't be afraid to do that. Getting a win on the board, even a suboptimal one, is okay

-- If all you have is the brute force solution, best to start with that, and just leave hints that you think there's a better solution out there.

--- Why leave hints? All about communication. Also helps demonstrate mastery in that you can figure out a solution. Also makes it seem like you haven't seen the problem before. Don't want to make it look like you've memorized a problem, because the interviewer is just going to change up the problem to try to trip you up.

- Need to realize that we're playing a game here. Don't want to "tip our hat" too early

- Get the Brute Force on the board for TwoSum. Can name-drop that we think we can do a hash map to go from quadratic to something closer to linear

- Leon says he almost always starts off with the brute force solution, and then optimizes it, unless it's painfully obvious

-- This helps demonstrate his quest for improvement, and gives him more stuff to talk about with the interviewer. (Goal of the interview is to demonstrate that you can code +AND+ you're going to be an excellent team member that strives to continuously improve.)

- Rarely, an interviewer will ask you to just solve the problem as optimally as possible. This is +VERY+ rare. Don't optimize for the outliers. Most like collaboration and working through the problem methodically

(Another of Michael's favorite lines from 100Devs Huddles: "We don't optimize for a$$holes.")

[Michael got interrupted for a couple of minutes]

- Goal to move to a linear solution whenever possible (using TwoSum as an example)

- Many interviewers like you to talk through your code. May have a different phrasing of TwoSum that uses sets.

- Current solution with HashMap (3:18 PM, see screenshot)

- Using Hash Maps is +very+ common in technical interview problems

- Each of these big tools that you can incorporate, you're now much more comfortable in 10% more interviews. As you build them on top of each other, you get increasingly confident

[3:19 PM PT --> Moving to the Slido to answer questions from there]

- Most companies, especially big companies, expect us to brute force first. +REALLY+ like seeing us work through our thought process and improve a solution to be more optimal

Q: "I have an in-person final round interview. What types of questions should I be asking at this point?"

- Leon: Please stay away from questions you would ask +after+ you have an offer. Don't get too comfortable. Generic questions from 100Devs Bank. Ideally, informed, researched questions from the company blog, and potentially related to the most recent company change. "What prompted this recent adoption at the company?"

-- Popular with DannyT: "If this final interview goes phenomenally well, what will the first three months of us working together look like?"

- Don't ask questions that make the interviewer more doubtful. You're not coworkers yet. Don't start asking about the unlimited PTO policy. Ask all that stuff +AFTER+ you get the offer, not before. Get the offer first

- The more researched and informed the questions, the better received they will be

- Whiteboarding process is exactly the same as PREP

-- Leon writes notes with his dominant hand, practices on sheets of paper to know how much space he takes up. Easier to be cheeky by introducing methods he plans to use later

- Also have to be mindful about communicating. Need to turn periodically and present the whiteboard to the interviewer. Don't just stay staring at the whiteboard and never turn to recognize the interviewer

- If you want a specific company, especially one that hires globally, the goal is to network with everyone at the company possible. Global remote roles are a lot more competitive, so we have to go the extra mile.

- After a gap? Try to make the most recent job title "Software Engineer" if that's what you're going for.

- Daily LeetCoding? How to motivate?

-- Money. Leon: "Do you mean to tell me that there's an interview process that if we study for we can know if we did well or not well at the end of, and even if we're with the most racist interviewer that hates my guts, I know I rocked the interview and that only reason I'm not getting selected is some other stuff outside my control?" DS&A stuff is awesome because you can know whether or not you did well, unlike any other types of interviews, where you never really know. Technical questions and coding challenges are much more equitable because you can study for them. The only thing between you and a six-figure offer is how much you prepare for it

-- Make it part of your daily practice

-- At least 30 minutes per day of Anki and 30 minutes a day of technical practice

--- Start with CodeWars early, then build up to Leetcode

- Most folks can replace some leisure time with Leetcode/CodeWars

-- You might even start to have fun with it. (Competitive programming does +not+ make you better at interviewing, but it is fun :p )

- VERY important to negotiate for a better title whenever possible. Much easier than other stuff (money, relocation, etc.)

-- Try to get "software engineer/developer" vs. "web developer"

--- Often much easier for a company to offer than money, but some companies are very rigid about this and don't have the wiggle room

-- Be sure to revisit this at any periodic reviews

- Where/How do you practice interviewing?

-- Mock interviews are the best way to practice, after actually interviewing. (So, don't be afraid to start interviewing! Even if you bomb them. Most companies let you reapply after 3 to 6 months anyway.)

--- Lots of practice options on the 100Devs Discord Server

- Try to know the efficiencies and details behind the technical interviewing methods that you use

- How to time manage?

-- Leon gets up very early. Works 6 AM - 5 PM, then 5 PM - 8 or 9 PM is family time. 9 to 10-11 PM is work again. It may not work for everyone, but it works well for Leon. Leon doesn't play video games as much as he used to or would otherwise want to. Plays Pokémon on his phone if his kid is napping, though. Allows him to free up more time for the more important stuff

--- Has periods in life for dedicated recharge time, because he knows that helps him go hard when it matters. Recharge hard or work hard, and know that one helps you more effectively do the other.

- Upcoming interview? Rebuild a current project with the technologies that this new company uses, or use a tutorial to brush up on this stuff so you can talk about it effectively with the interviewer(s)

[Ended at 3:35 PM PT]

Screenshots from Leon walking us through how to solve a problem using PREP and 100Devs techniques:

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-11-14
html:
100Devs - Friday 11/14/25 Huddle (Huntober 2025) - 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT

[Final Huddle of Huntober 2025. Huddles will resume the normal Tu/Fri 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM schedule after this Huddle.]

(Notes taken by Michael Govaerts, who was multi-tasking, so there may be interruptions. Timestamps are given periodically to make it easier to follow the Huddle chat on Discord.)

(Like all Huddles, the Huddle took place on the 100Devs Discord stage, and was led by Leon Noel.)

100Devs Platform to be released next week.

Subscribe to the Newsletter to get the most recent updates, although please pay attention to the Discord as well:

https://leonnoel.com/newsletter

Leon is answering questions via a Slido, released to the Discord just before the Huddle. Also, Leon lets people come up on stage and ask questions live, which he asks them to make sure are job-search related. (This is standard Huddle format.)

Leon is planning to send out a longer form post summarizing what we covered in Huntober 2025. (This is likely to come out next week, either via the Newsletter or on Discord, or both.)

The purpose of our 100Devs Hit List is to play for the roles that aren't posted. Networking our way in, therefore, allows us to target the 70-80% of roles that don't get posted. Current job listings (or at least job listings from the past year) just help us see which companies are hiring and have the funding to extend opportunities to people.

Can always apply for the current open positions, but realize we're not just aiming for those. Still try to network your way into these open positions, too. Don't just click apply.

Please post in the celebrations channel on Discord to let the 100Devs community know what's going on, too. Your successes will help motivate others in the community.

Be careful with scam job openings. Leon's seeing what may be data farming with "opportunities" that are asking for recorded interview videos. Leon personally wouldn't do this unless he had determined it was a legitimate organization with someone willing to talk with him first. If it's just some random listing asking for a recorded video, he's very disinterested. Search the internet about a given company to see if other people have feedback about them or this process, too.

Remember, pursuing opportunities is a marathon. Putting the effort to get through 100Devs, so we have to put a similar amount of effort into networked-applications. That's why Leon recommends doing the job search seriously only +after+ finishing 100Devs. Otherwise, it's hard to give the necessary effort to both.

Don't put all the effort into 100Devs just to fail to put the effort into networking your way into an opportunity. Yesterday, Leon said he has +never seen+ a case where someone used the 100Devs Networked-In Strategy and made 60 Networked-In applications without getting an offer. Often it takes far less, but it has never taken more than 60. Definitely worth the effort.
