Realize that the effort you're putting into the "network your way in" is what's going to potentially get you to that six-figure opportunity. How much work is that six-figure opportunity worth for you, especially since you're almost to the finish line at that point?

You can't control if someone ghosts you, invites you to an interview, how the interview goes, or if you get an offer. However, you can put the work into networking your way into those opportunities to make it much more likely to get you those opportunities.

[2:46 PM PT]

Avoid burnout by focusing on the things that are in your control. (Networking your way in. Daily (or at least very routine) HitListing, Banki, and technical interviewing practice). Don't focus on the interviews, their outcomes, or the offers. You're just doing what's in your control, and the offer(s) will come automatically. (If you're constantly bombing interviews because you're missing an important technology, especially for your local market, that's a good indicator to go get some experience in that technology so you avoid that skills gap in the future, however.) Still, realize that we don't optimize for outliers. Thus, if you have one bad interview, that may just be one bad interview. Trust the process, and focus on the long track record of success that Leon's recommended method has had (over at least ten years now.)

[2:49 PM]

If you have an issue with the Discord community, especially about someone not being kind, please let the mods know via a ModMail. However, please make sure you're putting the effort in to be kind and inclusive as well.

(Quoted questions are taken from the Slido, which Leon is answering live.)

"my projects don't ever seem "professional" enough to share with recruiters or showcasing them in general. feels like i get caught up in making something perfect"

- Leon says this question was answered in the podcast today. So, if you want a deep dive on this (20 minutes) check out the next podcast episode

- Professional enough and getting caught up on something perfect are two different pieces

- If someone sees your site and would be comfortable entering their credit card information into the site? That's a good enough site in terms of professionalism.

- If you're trying to figure out what you've done in 100Devs that you can showcase to recruiters, there are levels to it.

-- When you're first starting out, your projects are not going to be as good as when you're a year into it.

--- As you get better projects, your portfolio is going to get better

- Start with websites that help you get clients, then have full-stack applications that help you get jobs

- So many ways that you can have a good design that looks good out of the box. 100Devs are big fans of templates, UI libraries, that helps things look good enough, and you don't need to nerd out on the specifics unless it's something you want to grow in

-- Learn what you need to learn, and move on to the next thing (This should help with the perfectionism thing.)

[2:53 PM PT]

"On a contract that isn't likely to convert to FTE. I have a recruiter call next week. If asked why I'm looking, is "I'm looking for a FT opportunity" ok?"

- This is the best thing to ever tell a recruiter. "Hey, I'm currently employed as an engineer as a contractor, and I'm looking for a more stable, long-term, full-time role"

-- Not that you're unhappy with a role, or missing skills. This is recruiter catnip. Great reason to be able to give, as contracts end, so this is a very common thing

[2:54 PM PT]

"If we are getting traction for senior/ intermediate level roles, should we go for that or more entry level roles for the mentorship and less demanding role ?"

- Go for the role that pays you the most. Some entry-level roles are more demanding and less supportive than more senior roles. Regardless of experience level, you still have to do the same thing for the first 90 days anyway to establish yourself well. Therefore, if you're doing well at higher-level roles, don't level down. What experience means at different companies is also wildly different, so don't limit yourself with firm experience definitions. Go for the highest-paying opportunities you can. Let them limit you if they want to. Don't limit yourself.

"Whats the difference between Software Engineer & Web Developer positions? Do Software Engineers focus more on desktop apps than web apps? Which role to target?"

- One role pays more. That's it. One role doesn't understand your skill set and hopes you'll take less money, and the other pays more. A software engineer can work on anything web dev-related. People like to make up stuff about what a software engineer is. (Tools, theory, etc.) However, in the US, there's no protected term for software engineer, so it doesn't matter. Some countries, including Canada, are more limiting for what terms you can use without degrees or certifications, but as long as you're not misrepresenting yourself based on these requirements, the terminology doesn't matter. If you're doing 100Devs, you're a software engineer. (Like Realtor (trademarked term) vs. real estate agent. Just avoid the trademarked term if you don't qualify.)

[2:59 PM PT]

"Haven't updated my LinkedIn yet to reflect a layoff from only SWE job, but recruiters message me. (How/When) do I (ever) discuss it w/o giving up opportunities?"

- No reason to update it if it's working for you. Update it when/if you feel comfortable and have time. No rule says it has to be updated, though.

- Don't lie or fudge your resume if you get asked about stuff, but taking time to update stuff is totally fine. (No worries if it's 6 months later and you're now making the update)

[3 PM PT]

"Hey Leon, did you post the list of DSA problems that cover most interview questions? Thank you so much for everything you do."

- This should happen next week.

[3 PM - 3:14 PM Discord break. Michael stepped away briefly and switched to mobile, where he wasn't taking notes.]

(Topics covered)

- Do I need live-hosted site?

-- Can be helpful, especially when actively applying, but there were ideas in the chat for how to get around this, especially if you're considering paying for it.

- Should I do sales?

-- Don't dump 100Devs because it's hard mid-way through for sales. Software engineering is likely a better career path. Sales can chew you up and spit you out. That's fine, just make sure you come back to 100Devs and software engineering if that happens :p. Leon is a fan of taking pathways to the end and then, and only then, making an informed decision about how to proceed. Quitting before the finish can cause you to make a less-informed decision that may not be the best for you.

- How about product management?

-- That's fine. But realize that you should optimize your search and resume for that role. Don't be half-in product management and half-in software engineering. Go full on either and focus on that

[3:14 PM PT]

"Interview Sunday: HTML/CSS, JS, Python one task. Job mentions ERP and I've never worked with it Should I study ERP basics or just focus on main topics and prep"

- Ask them what you should prepare. Google the Earth to see if someone has posted a similar task. Just know enough to be dangerous in the interview, regardless of what it is you need to learn

- What is up with Sunday interviews? Third person Leon has seen recently with a Sunday interview. He would not be a fan of this.

-- Hallman (DevRel at Microsoft) just did a talk about how we should not be perpetually available for our employer

[3:20 PM PT]

- 100Devs Alum (Rai Bread) talks about success (randomly. Leon didn't plan this). Program works!

-- Offer with a nonprofit. Leon says still negotiate. "Nonprofits have MONEEEEEY!!!!"

- Alum just more than tripled their salary. Almost four. Everything else from this point is gravy, says Leon

- 2 things with nonprofits:

-- Some nonprofits have lots of money. Some do, some don't. Grassroots orgs are likely leaner. Established nonprofits can have 30mil/yr budget with great staff that support the mission, but also have lots of funding

-- Every nonprofit files a 990 each year with the IRS, or makes other public reports. Reviewing these can help determine what their budgets are and what the salary ranges are for people who work there

--- Leon recommends this to help find the right ballpark for pay. Leon typically recommends aiming for the top of the range

- Always be grateful for opportunities and good interviews, and demonstrate enthusiasm for the role. When written offer letter comes in later, "So excited to join, I have a few questions, can we talk by phone?"

- Phone: Very excited for opportunity. Is there any room for the base salary? [Just wait to see what they say.]

-- [This org already increased the offer from a previous offer.]

-- If they say no salary wiggle room, then we can negotiate for other things. PTO, relocation, signing bonus, title (this is +crucial+ for other roles. Leon says not to change your titles unless it's a legitimate change, because recruiters can notice and it may give them pause. (They will likely look up your title and your dates of work to verify employment, and will notice any title changes.) Negotiate for better titles whenever you can, either with the original offer or whenever you have a performance review.)

- Danny Thompson from chat: "I think if Rai said the pay is also at the top end of the previous salary, this is a good sign they are respecting Rai in this process not going low"

-- Leon says keep the energy up, and agrees. This seems like a great offer. Rai says they also did DSD with Danny, and it was a great experience.

- Leon says this is a +great+ way to end Huntober, but still wants to help people with the couple of minutes he has left.

[3:26 PM PT]

- 100Dever (ichtragebrille [Amanda]) comes up for salary range discussions, more senior role, with given range of 150-180K/yr

-- "What would a 155 engineer look like vs. a 180 engineer?"

--- "None of our seniors are in the 150 range, everyone is in the 180 range. If you got hired, you'll be at 180."

- 100Dever: Just by asking a question, she may have just gotten herself a 30K/yr boost. Don't be afraid to simply ask. ("More evidence than the 100Devs process +works+!" - Michael G.)

Two docs from Danny Thompson at the end:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1439034856435748894

"Leon can I share two Google docs? I think it could help folks. It's a list of questions I have been asked recently and a list of questions I have asked"

Questions you Ask: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wEx41EiaefS4r6Xf9BBBtu9yOJrGPgTi7I7uqqSrLxM/edit?usp=drivesdk

Questions:

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1XjkrUclMvGopb9jGVpQeP95jqqtcWnAAmUFroY3AnR0/edit?usp=drivesdk

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-11-21
html:
(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan: https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts )

[3:44 PM PT - Nov 21, 2025 -> Rushed notes, Pre-Spellcheck or editing.]

100Devs Huddle - Friday Nov 21, 2025 at 2:30 PM PT

100Devs: https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

- Sessions are focusing on helping people with "The Hunt" (for paid opportunities)

[Leon and Danny (Danny Thompson, co-host of the Programming Podcast with Leon and Founder and Director of the Commit Your Code Conference) just got off a panel for a pre-recorded conference. Apparently, there was a beach background at some point, per the chat.]

2:34 PM PT (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from the 100Devs Discord, from the server stage channel where the Huddle takes place)

Q: Should I make my 100-Hour Project Repo public?

Leon: The goal of the 100Hours project is to have proof that you can perform in a paid role. Public allows it to be more easily shareable. Really needs to be posted somewhere so recruiters can see it and so you can feature it. Many interviews end up asking questions that reference the 100Hours project. If you're using it as a get-a-job tool, it should probably be public. If you're trying to turn it into a business or make money directly off it, then you can consider making it private.

- Don't let perfect be the enemy of the good. Get proof of your hard work out there as much as possible. No need to go deep on all the edge cases, either. Get all the big features done. Recruiters aren't going to care about the minute details. Once you've got all the big useful impactful stuff done, then you can start going all-in on the tiny details, but +only if you want to+. The returns of focusing on the minor stuff will likely just yield very diminishing returns

- Almost all the AIs just repeat the same things over and over again, so be careful using AIs for content. People get bored with this, can recognize it easily, and it's therefore much less impressive. Try not to rely too much on AI for our featured projects for this reason, unless you want to feature your AI proficiency for some reason

- Leon uses AI for a first draft, and then will elevate the design in his own way to reduce the glaring AI smell.

2:39 PM PT

Q: How to transfer internally from one role to another?

Leon: 2 key things:

  1. Build relationships with the teams you want to be on. Go to lunch with them, schedule time to chat with them, offer to be helpful to them on their projects, even though you're in a different part of the company

  2. Get your boss into the mindset that part of your career plan is to transition to the other team. Probably shouldn't do it right away, because this can backfire a little bit. (Your manager may not be very happy that you're not planning on sticking around with the team, especially if you just got there, and we want our manager to love us and be willing to support us.) A couple of months in, or when your manager is talking about long-term career growth, you can then mention your interest in development work and how you're hoping to get opportunities on your current team to help you move in that direction and potentially transition to another team within the company. Then, later, after you've established your ability and have a much better rapport with your manager, you can ask for support for a transition. Good relationships really help with the transition, and you can then ask for an option to interview for the next available opportunity for the role(s) that you're interested in and have been working towards

- (Michael: You can also transition to roles at another company if your current company isn't supportive, and at that point you'll have a built track record to show your ability to make this transition based on the work you've been doing while in your current role.)

2:42 PM PT

Q: how do I reach out to someone to connect with who hasn't responded to my initial message, I don't want to come off as pushy or make someone uncomfortable.

Leon: Difference between sending a DM a day, or sending something every few months.

- Leon expects people to either tell him no or block him, and he makes sure not to be overwhelming.

-- Follow up after initial meeting. No response? No problem. Maybe they're busy

--- Reach out about once a month, until they tell him to stop. Sometimes, it takes Leon 6-7 months to connect with someone, and they frequently will tell him, "Thank you for not giving up on connecting with me." People get busy. They may really want to connect with you, so don't be afraid to put in the effort on your end to help with that.

- Leon isn't showing up at their school or house or otherwise invading their privacy. He's trying to establish a professional/business relationship with a person using either the contact they gave him or their public-facing contact information. As long as you keep it professional and respectful, it's okay to have more outreach.

[Leon took a moment to reflect on Danny sharing in the chat how he grills fish specifically because he knows Leon is pescatarian:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1441559755108913294

. This is an example of a great relationship that has been built over time]

2:45 PM PT

Q: Need motivation:) how much time does it take to get some interviews going if I did my Anki and hitlisted every day

Leon: Good things tend to happen all at once. If you're reaching out to people (hitlisting) and being consistent at doing the work, you should start to see responses pretty quickly. If you're not, someone may need to be fixed. Part of that journey is figuring out all the little things that may be turning people off that need to be improved. This is part of the process. You're going against a ton of candidates, and the hiring team is trying to dismiss people quickly to focus on a smaller number of people who are better fits. HuntOber and the 100Devs job searching content help cover this. Maybe you don't have a good Narrative that explains your career transition or target. After the Narrative passes, maybe your Resume or LinkedIn doesn't clearly establish that you're a software engineer who can solve their professional problems right now. If people are hitlisting every day without these key things, they're not going to find success. You have to put the effort in and then make sure you're putting in the effort, which includes making sure your effort is going in the right direction, and you're not just working really hard at something that isn't going to bring you success. Everything is feedback, including a lack of success, so don't ignore those data points, and make sure you stay connected with the 100Devs community for support and additional perspective, since they (including during the Huddles!) might be able to help you see some issue that you're missing. Come to Huddles and Banki Brunch to make sure you're not missing something major. It's a process of continual refinement

2:49 PM PT

Q: What happened with the google form we filled out to get our linkedins reviewed?

[All questions coming from the Slido, where people ask questions before/during every Huddle.]

Leon: Leon is working on this and will likely have an unlisted video posted after Thanksgiving break.

(No Huddles next week due to Thanksgiving)

~~

Q: Asked for a ref for a SE1 position, got a ref for Sr. SE (by mistake) and applied. If get an interview, should I ask to move me to SE1 position?

Leon: Hard to answer. If someone is reaching out to you, it could be two things:

  1. They're reaching out to you because they see something you don't see. If so, you should trust them and go with it

  2. They're just spamming people and not being thoughtful, and then this may not be worth it.

If it's 1), Leon loves riding that wave. If it doesn't work out, then you can ask the person supporting you to give you opportunities that are less "senior," even though that experience designation is largely just made up anyway. Someone who phoned it in for 5 years somewhere does not have the same experience as someone who really applied themselves for one year. Don't miss out on intentional outreach by people who see your potential and want to help you connect with opportunities. "This might have been a little too advanced, but I would love if you shared me some less senior roles."

If it's clearly 2), you can still ask them that you're looking for less senior roles.

Leon also says if it's a local opportunity, the "seniority" doesn't matter as much, especially with smaller/newer companies. For the Big Tech roles, seniority mentions in the long-established roles likely makes more of a difference.

2:53 PM PT

Q: I left my job (7years+) now I am lost in job market and imposter syndrome hitting hard. Can you recommend mentorship or advice how to assess skill level?

Leon: Start applying to roles you think you're a good fit for. Reassess after five networked-in applications. If it's working, keep going; if it's not working, check in with 100Devs (or a similar community) and determine if your technique may be off or if you may be a better fit for a slightly different role. The best place to know where you are is to start getting into the job search and interviewing game to see how you're doing and what you may need to improve on, either for the roles you're pursuing or if you should consider pursuing different types of roles based on your background and skill set.

2:55 PM PT

Q: Do I put 100Devs as prev work exp for my backg check even though I never received a check from you all. It's on my resume as work exp. This is a clearance job.

Leon: Don't put "Work Experience" on the resume. Put "Experience." That way, you don't have to worry as much about paid or unpaid.

- If it's a clearance job, they dive deep, so 100Devs should likely be training or education. If you list it in Experience, make sure you're clear that 100Devs didn't pay you. If you got a paid client, be clear the paid client was separate from 100Devs, even if the 100Devs training is what got you the client, which you can reference.

- Leon went through clearance, and they go deep. One clearance check involved the investigators calling every person Leon had ever lived with over the entirety of his life. Don't create any ambiguity for them to be concerned about.

2:58 PM

Q: What's up with the newsletter and all its good stuff, and it not being sent out?

Leon: No newsletter with this content yet, so you didn't miss anything. It's coming.

2:58 PM PT

Q: How are you feeling? I hope you and the family are recovered and set for the holiday!

Leon: Dealing with lots of stuff off 100Devs, and he's looking forward to the week off to recharge. Leon likes literally going into the woods when recharging (camping), so he's going to be doing that. Leon's looking forward to coming back strong and continuing to do the good work that he's doing. (Michael's definitely a big fan. If you're not familiar with Leon, he's the best.)

2:59 PM PT

Q: I know we shouldn't click apply BUT is it shady for apps to ask for home address ? (Not just city, state)

Leon: Early in the process, kind of weird. Later in the process, it's less weird. Lots of scams right now are out there and trying to data mine you in order to sell your data. Video interviews, lots of personal data, they reject you and make you think you didn't get to the next round, but really, it was all fake, and they're just selling your data to a data broker.

- This is why sites like Hiring Cafe are way better than other sites. Hiring Cafe listings are scraped from actual company websites. No way to post opportunities, so it's far less likely to have scams.

- If it's just a random place that you haven't yet validated is a real, established business, you should be very careful what personal information you give

(Michael: This is probably another good reason to network our way in and not just click apply to random opportunities we find without contacting an actual person who seems legitimate.)

3:01 PM PT

Q: How much AI is too much AI? I don't really know if I'm using too much, or if I'm just vibe coding at this point.

Leon: It depends on what your purpose is. If your goal is to ship product for a business or learn deeply to upgrade your skill set. Learning? Learn deeply. Don't let AI steal the learning from you. People who learn their craft deeply and understand how software is built will continue to be weapons regardless of AI. Those who offload thought process onto AI, especially early in the process, are failing to internalize the learning and getting the most value out of the time invested. If Leon were doing a learning process (100Devs, freecodecamp, whatever), he would not use AI at all until he were completely done with the process. Otherwise, he thinks 100Devs's educational content is diminished because it's very hard for people not to rely heavily on AI. As we offload our thinking and effort, we may be getting better at prompting AI, but that's all we're improving. We're not improving our abilities in the areas that are much more meaningful. Once you're established with all the fundamentals, then you can use AI as a thought partner and a tutor. You know enough not to just take AI's content as fact, you're now using it to enhance your process.

[3:05 PM PT - 3:13 PM - Michael got interrupted. Sorry!]

Q: Any tips for continuing to learn with a concussion?

Leon: Stop. Talk to a doctor who is experienced with this issue, or several for a consensus view, and follow their advice. Don't have just bashed your head in and decided to fit in our Huddle and are asking for advice. Go get medical attention from the experts.

- Leon used to play Rugby. Knows what concussions are like. Take good care of yourself and come back strong.

3:16 PM PT

Q: do you still use a kinesis keyboard? if so, which model?

Leon: Yup. Kinesis 360 Pro (Michael may have heard wrong, but it was definitely Kinesis and likely 360). Leon gets very severe wrist pain, and if he doesn't use this type of ergonomic keyboard, he feels it and he suffers. [Leon has also mentioned elsewhere that it is +very important+ that we really take breaks at least every 60 minutes, like the 100Devs videos emphasize, to make sure that we're emphasizing our short-term and long-term health as much as possible.]

3:16 PM PT

Q: For those of us without prior professional exp. as a SWE, what's the best strategy to present ourselves as strong candidates and get hired VS someone w/exp?

Leon: Need to know the answer to this question yourself, and make sure you have a built-up portfolio of evidence that clearly establishes the answer to a hiring manager.

3:17 PM

Q: I'm a special ed teacher taking this year off to do Cohort 2 and transition into tech. Are there any tips to make this gap year productive?

Leon: Start networking immediately from the very beginning.

- Three key tasks of 100Devs: Be consistent, manage frustration, and take care of yourself

-- Don't try to speed run. Take everything very seriously. When 100Devs tells you to Network, take it seriously. Make a schedule to be consistent. At the end of the journey of 100Devs, if you've followed the process and stayed consistent, you'll have the proof at the end of the process to establish yourself as a good hire

3:19 PM PT

100Devers On Stage (Class 21) [Jake_Haver_of_Catz]

- Another speaker at a conference Jake was part of just tried to recruit him for a position at Microsoft. Jake doesn't feel ready.

- Leon: Don't worry about it. Don't take yourself out of the game until you get a rejection, and remember, a rejection now isn't a rejection forever.

-- 100Devs routinely gets into Microsoft through the LEAP program

-- Every 6 months, you can reapply with most Big Tech companies. (Michael: and this restriction may not even exist at other companies, so you can just keep trying if there's a place you +really+ want to work.)

--- Based on this and since January is usually the big hiring push, it makes sense to ask the Microsoft contact for a January opportunity to interview

- Jake gave a shout-out to Danny for encouraging him to automate at his job and ask for a title change to help him in his future developer transition. Jake wants to emphasize that the 100Devs advice works. We just need to stick with it and trust the process.

3:23 PM PT

Q: I've been practicing PREP with code challenges but when you're on the job, do folx talk through coding decisions in a similar way? Thanks Leon! 😊

Leon: Core communication and collaboration? Absolutely yes. During the day, you're going to be defending positions and collaborating with others. Working together to find the optimal way to do something. Very useful skill to have post-interview, too. The ability to clearly articulate what you're thinking is an incredibly valuable communication skill to have.

3:25 PM PT

Q: perfectionism and comparison has led me to essentially no networking for over 2 years. i've built apps, and got clients, but just don't feel professional enough

Leon: Some people always feel imposter syndrome. Just accept that it's there, just like with most anxiety issues, and do your best anyway. (Michael: A common quote in mental health, especially when it relates to anxiety: "Feel the fear [since it's likely not going to be a fatal thing], and do it anyway.")

(Michael thought this was interesting: "Impostor syndrome prevalence rates range considerably from 9 to 82%, depending on the screening method and threshold used.[5]" <-- It's very common. If you feel it, just realize it's probably a part of being human. We're all trying to manage it alongside each other <3

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impostor\_syndrome#Management )

- Remember, very few people remember their 8 great-grandparents, let alone any detail about them. If we fail or look foolish, years from now it's not going to matter. No one is likely to remember. If you mess up while networking, that's fine, just keep moving and improving and realize that most people are just going to forget about it, and we're all in this together doing the best we can at any given time.

3:29 PM PT

Q: I made lots of typos in code. I do not do that in my native language. Is there a way to improve that before interview? Should I even look at keyboard or screen?

Leon: Just let people know from the start that you may have challenges. Anything you're worried about, just put it out in the beginning, establish rapport, and if they're good people representing a good opportunity, they'll be fine with it and support you. If not? Don't optimize for the unkind outliers, just keep moving and doing the best you can. You don't want to work in an unkind environment anyway, right?

author: Michael Govaerts
title: michaelgovaerts/2026-12-16
html:
[Rushed notes. To be SpellChecked/Edited if I can make the time. As always, I hope this helps!]

(Notes by Michael Govaerts, 100Devs Superfan:

https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelgovaerts

)

100Devs Huddle - Tuesday, Dec 16, 2025, at 2:30 PM PT 100Devs:

https://100devs.org/about/

Part of the Tu/Fri recurring 2:30 PM - 3:30 PM PT Huddles, hosted on the 100Devs Discord by 100Devs Founder, Leon Noel

Sessions are focusing on helping people with “The Hunt” (for paid opportunities) (Timestamps to help follow the chat messages from Discord) [Leon answers questions primarily from a Slido, where people can upvote the questions they want answered. Or, they can put their Discord hands up and ask live, and Leon just asks that live questions are job-related.]

Q: “What would you consider to be “prepared” for the January hiring surge?” Leon: Always kind of waves to hiring. Always do HuntOber at 100Devs, it’s a yearly tradition. Jobuary also sometimes happens because January is usually big. January, sometimes March, and definitely October are the big hiring waves. HuntOber is the vibe set for January. That’s when a lot of companies get budget and start thinking about their new hires, and folks realize they may be able to get that extra employee they need for the team. Interviews tend to happen around January as a result. Huntober is trying to get people motivated so that they have a phenomenal January. How to be prepared? If you do everything that was covered during HuntOber? You’re prepared. Cleaned up online presence and LinkedIn, and anything else you’ll be sharing with employers. Keeping up with Banki and Coding Challenges as if they were interviews. Try to solve them on different platforms, because you never know which platform is going to be used for the interview. Learn how to solve problems using a blank environment with no bells or whistles. Some can solve really good DS&A problems, but struggle with getting functions written from scratch because platforms like LeetCode give them the functions to complete already. Once you get all that stuff dialed in, then you still need to network. Keep contacts warm. January is a great time to reconnect, even with very old contacts: “Happy New Year. Hope you had a great one, and I would love to reconnect to catch up.” December really didn’t slow down for hiring, so Leon thinks January is going to be wild for connecting people with opportunities. [2:41 PM PT] What platform to use for networking? Best place is probably LinkedIn and Twitter. Easiest ways to get in touch with people. For Coding challenges? JSBin, Replit went heavy with AI and Leon doesn’t like Replit anymore as a result. CodeSandbox if we’re practicing backend stuff. JSBin for Javascript and CodeSandbox for Backend is what Leon recommends. Networking strategies that are best for outreach? Watch the 3-4 videos in the classes that cover Networking. Madeleine (community member) routinely shares a bank of networking questions on the Discord. The classes are a great starting point, though, and then come to the Discord and the Huddles for more help. [From Madeleine:

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1450619383243083918

] [2:43 PM PT] Holidays can also be a good time to recharge, and then you can come back far stronger after the recharge. Self-care should always be prioritized. How to prioritize for hitlisting? Use Hitlist Magic, and see what companies have hired in the past year. Those are companies that are worth considering. Only 30% of opportunities get posted publicly, so don’t wait for the public postings on Hiring Cafe. Reach out to the companies that you’re interested in, especially if they’re local to you, and you can form an ongoing relationship with them much more easily due to that local proximity [2:45 PM PT] SlidoQ: Is it worth learning to build desktop apps vs web apps, or should we stay focused on websites? If we wanted to learn to make desktop apps where can we start? Leon: Focus should be on learning to be full-stack, in terms of maximizing job opportunities. The number of jobs related to desktop development is far smaller than for web development. Best to learn full-stack web development and then complement it with whatever is in demand in the local area. Full-stack is in demand everywhere, and then look for local demand. Doubtful local demand will be in desktop development, but it’s possible. Can do Electron, ReactNative, etc. for desktop stuff, and many skills are transferable. “Don’t go chasing waterfalls,” or chase the shiny objects. Stick with what has been proven to be in demand, and then go get the job using those in-demand skills. Don’t fall into an endless hole of learning forever and never getting the job. Always new things to learn, but focus on the in-demand stuff, and then get the job with that in-demand stuff, and learn the things you need to learn based on what gaps you may have to get the job. (Are you not doing well in interviews because you’re missing key technologies in your toolbox? Then go learn those technologies, and come back and rock those interviews!)

TypeScript is dominant currently. Leon recommends FastAI if you’re learning fundamentals of AI engineering, but that’s as much Python as Leon would push people towards. Stick to the in-demand stuff. [2:48 PM PT]

“Practical Deep Learning For Coders” is the recommended resource in AI, but it’s +very+ tough to get a field that’s dominated by people with degrees (like AI), so he usually doesn’t lead people in these directions, as it’s very low yield for getting jobs for people who go through programs like 100Devs with zero preexisting technical or educational experience.

Check Hiring Cafe and go to meetups to see what technologies are coming up frequently in your local area. Not every job is online, so you’re just trying to find the right direction to go. Are most postings asking for RubyOnRails? Then go learn more about that so you can be more successful in getting interviews related to those opportunities

First look local, because remote opportunities are a lot harder to get. It’s possible to get remote, but local is always easier. SlidoQ: Working through 100Devs, love the community, but need a job ASAP. Any leads on where to find no-code tech roles in the meantime? Leon: Doesn’t have to be in tech. Just get stable in terms of your economic needs. The beautiful thing is you can find something less stressful, so you can put more time into your freelancing and learning. Finding something tech-adjacent can keep you stuck in that tech-adjacent role. QA and Support roles can get people trapped. They get comfy in a role they don’t really want, but they stay there and miss out on the better opportunities. Leon sees this A LOT. Most successful job for people Leon has placed out of programs like RC and 100Devs is Uber driving. They know how to hustle, they know how to work for the money and learn how to work well with strangers (clients that they drive around), and they know that Uber Driving is not fun and Uber is out to exploit them so they really want to get out of it. Teaching works too (Michael thinks substitute teaching is also a very easy option for people with degrees [Leon brought this up later and agreed]). Leon says the emphasis on education is helpful for learning and growth. Teachers and Uber drivers seem to be the popular jobs for 100Devs success. Anything that has really draining hours is hard. Teachers get school vacations off, often, so they can work on personal growth. Do whatever you can to bring sufficient financial stability to your life and then focus on education through 100Devs to enhance that financial stability and professional growth as quickly as possible. *** Quick spellchecked to here so far. Michael will try to do the rest later. *** [2:53 PM PT] [From the Discord Chat, and read aloud by Leon]: Numbers games and 1 out of 60? How to handle 59 Rejections? (Michael: Leon says that in his experience, he has never seen it take more than 60 networked-in applications for someone to get a job offer.) Leon: Know it’s going to be tough. Stop going on the rollercoaster (don’t focus on the highs and lows, just focus on the process). Successes are great, but the failures can burn you out, so try to treat it like a business. Call 60 restaurants and ask them to build a website? You’re going to get turned down by most. That’s the price of doing business. Don’t see each and every role as a potential opportunity that can change your life, as that can make the losses hurt too much. (Michael: Happiness = Reality - Expectations [So keep those Expectations low! Negative would probably be better :p]) Focus on the things that are in your control. Did you practice your Banki, Codewars, polish your resume, and hit your hitlisting numbers today, like you planned? Great. Celebrate that. Not the things out of your control like if someone responded to a message or said you rocked an interview. [2:58 PM PT] SlidoQ: If we can’t learn all there is to know about a language/framework/tool, how can we know when to confidently include it on our resume? After 1-2 small projects? Leon: I think differently here. No international committee for resumes. Just don’t lie, and then his bar is can I reliably build something with these tools? Then it goes on the resume if he thinks it will give him an edge in getting an interview. Could be a weekend of experience or a lifetime, but that’s Leon’s minimum threshold. Q: How many companies would you consider a good amount for the first round of the hitlist? Enough to work with but not be overwhelming? Leon: Minimum is 60, with at least three people at each company. Most people will do way more than that, but put the effort into building the list initially, build out the network, and supplement it with regular networking from your other activities, and that’s a good goal to hit. Remember, we are aiming for one opportunity out of 60 networked-in applications, as that’s typically how many networked-in attempts it takes.

Rare to join company as support and then transition to dev. People get comfortable, so that may contribute, but Leon doesn’t think this is a good strategy. Aim for the role you want originally. SlidoQ: What is your favorite Christmas cookie and yall better upvote this bc it’s a very important question in this joyous holiday season OK Leon: Loves making sugar cookies. Best buttercream icing recipe ever, but it’s an absolute problem for him. Everything made from scratch. (Leon is +NOT+ sharing this. He has to keep some stuff for himself. Go kick rocks. From the chat: “CookieChat is the only time Leon is toxic.” Leon will make this the Twitch channel point reward. Not the top tier one, but 100K and he gets a DM from him with the recipe, and that’s it. Hilarious) [3:02 PM PT] SlidoQ: Is there a recommended amount of time to wait before sending out a new coffee chat invite? Leon: A week minimum, and always give folks an out. “I know you’re probably busy, but I would love to chat.” The longer in between the better. Good to take a break during the holidays, and then use the New Year as an opportunity to reconnect. Follow Madeleine’s advice in the messages they sent (She’s active right now in the chat) Danny (from the chat): Most people aren’t checking LinkedIn at all. Leon agrees. Realize that you’re much more active than most on the hunt, so be willing to switch up your contact channels, too. E-mails to certain people is like putting them into the void. Twitter can pop for some people, though, so switch it up. Signal to Noise ratio is different for each purpose and on each platform. Some people are just good at sharing real advice on LinkedIn that wasn’t cooked up on AI to get presents (engagement/likes). People sharing battle stories on LinkedIn is what Leon truly admires and appreciates. [3:06 PM PT] SlidoQ: Is doing the Google IT cert and cohort for an IT support role and then grow into a dev role too much or should I focus on one thing? Have enough time for both Leon: Focus on what you want to focus on. Path might be more straightforward to generalist IT roles, but Leon is an advocate for Software Dev route as he sees it lifechanging for many and a path that remains very open. Leon has visited South Africa lot (responding to the chat) Programming Podcast has also been very popular (Leon and Danny hosts this). Leon feels special to be very popular on many podcast charts [3:07 PM PT] Special to see the impact they are having through the podcast. (Danny says it is listened to in 119 countries) Leon a big fan of FreeCodeCamp. Loves the curriculum, but he doesn’t care about certs. Certs don’t really make a difference. Good for burning for warmth, if they’re paper, but they don’t make much of an impact in terms of hiring. All about the skills you have and the proof of what you’ve built. Leon most excited about the English proficiency resources, as the language barrier is a big problem for people learning to code. Leon also likes FreeCodeCamp’s push to get a free bachelor’s for people. That’s the awesome stuff at freeCodeCamp that he’s excited about. Danny Concurs. No one every asks about certs, unless they’re getting the cert at the moment: ​

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/959571473188659280/1450625942945796247

SlidoQ: I keep getting asked for code reviews from person I met in a event. At what point do I ask to get paid? Leon: The first time. Any time someone is asking you to help them get paid, you need to be getting paid for freelancing. If you’re helping someone learn, that’s fine, or point them towards community that can help them (like 100Devs --> Coding Help channel) so that you’re not the only source of support. Delegate to 100Devs community to give yourself more space No reason to get Associates in CS, unless you want it. Not helpful in getting a job vs. time/money invested. If it’s free, or something you’re interested in, then go ahead. Not a good Time/$ in terms of getting a job. Some people like degrees, but Leon doesn’t find it necessary to get the job. Doesn’t see it helping, and sees associates degrees slow down people’s sucess. (Summary of LiveAnswer: If they have a Bachelor’s, people can look down on Associate’s degrees. Leon has seen this happen a lot. Seems like it could be a classist thing. Anecdotal evidence from Leon that informs this caution.) From the chat: Certs are not what people are looking for. People are looking for proven skills through things you have built.

Bachelor’s can hold people back, too, when they could be really putting the work in to get hired without it. WGU can be speedrun in 6 months, but it’s still $6K, and takes a lot of time and effort. Leon doesn’t recommend this. Just do 100Devs SlidoQ: how to learn to do code reviews & merge github conflicts. Can you recommend any resources to get exposure and learn this skills and understand what,how, why pls Leon: Open source, community projects (100Devs has lots of these), Danny does DSD cohorts that have curated open source stuff with the community. Leon hates UpWork. Race to the bottom unless you have a specialized skill set. Some people get business off it, but Leon thinks it’s just too competitive. MarciaC95 (100Devs alum) has earned five figures, but it’s all niche in AgTech. Leon recommends building own network and e-mail list, because UpWork could close your account for no reason and you lose all of your contacts SlidoQ: Re: freelancing How can we answer someone who wonders why they should pay $$$ have someone build a website rather than using something pre-made like Wix? Leon: I never sell things to people. People come to Leon because the no-code solutions aren’t cost-effective for them. They want to go to Leon to use his skills and professional experience. He doesn’t compete with the pre-made free stuff out there. Leon can refer people to these for those with no budget, and then if they have more funding and don’t want to waste time on websites and tech, they can come back to them. Don’t sell someone something they don’t need, especially because when you do this, they’ll appreciate you for not taking advantage of them, and they’ll be very likely to come back when they do need something they can pay you to provide (or refer someone else to you.) SlidoQ: Am doing 1 project with young ppl.Their ignoring +know it all attitude due to age gap makes me wonder what to do if that happen at my 1st job as career changer. Leon: ““Hanlon’s razor is an adage, or rule of thumb, that states: “Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity.”” ​

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanlon’s_razor

”

Leon loves youthful energy, and tries to give people the benefit of the doubt, especially if they seem inexperienced. Most jobs also aren’t filled with one age demographic, so you should be fine. SlidoQ: You’ve shared examples of outreach messages to send before. Is there any new/updated advice on what to send these days? These shouldn’t be too long, right? Leon: Classics don’t change. Just keep sticking with them. Danny: Be willing to pivot. Best Leon advice: Form strong opinions, but hold them weakly and be willing to incorporate new information and change. (Best mindset for software engineering) [3:23 PM PT] SlidoQ: Are you still interested in doing a class on Mandala Charts for goal setting? Looking forward to it as I find it hard to organize the chaos that’s in my life. Leon: New Year will be for helping people with goal setting and planning. SlidoQ: Did a part time (pay cut) to learn new tech. Praised, hinted at FT senior. Offer: 6-mo extension only. Feel disrespected, but job is cutting-edge. Do I take it? Leon: Yes. Take the extension, and find a different job. Keep getting paid, and use free time outside of the job to get a better offer. Leon doesn’t believe in taking pay cuts to learn new tech. Hard to dig out of a pay cut. Any time a company hints at a FT offer, get it in writing. “If I do this, I can expect this type of offer” should be in writing. If it’s not in writing, it doesn’t exist. That should be your guiding principle. Get contracts Q: Class Pacing for 100Devs? Leon: Keep the recommended pacing. 2 classes per week. Need mastery of topics before moving on, and speedrunning does not help with mastery. Focus on going deep, not going fast, if you have extra time/energy. That’s what’s going to help you find success after the program.

Live Questions from @Alec: [3:26 PM PT] First ever paid job in a network role. Got it through practicing behavioral questions, and slam dunked it. Q: Big internet company, and wants to get into development (software dev background). How do I make friends in the org to work on this transition? Leon: 2 big books: “The first 90 Days” (get from library) “Never eat alone”

Starting places for advice. You’ll get foundation for current role so that you are liked and respected in the workplace. Never eat alone helps take advice of title to heart. Make really good connections with team, management, and folks on other teams over the next few months so when opportunities for transition arise, you can make that transition. Take “more responsibilities” in current role that are more development related, and that will give you the dev foundation that you can use to provide proof for a transition. Can also build rapport with your current manager by focusing on your current team to help your transition go much easier. Get comfortable in your current role and succeed wildly, so that people like you and will support your transition Alec: Role is very independent, so he may not be around other people in the org much. How to continue to network with this limitation? Leon: Virtual networking. Team channels on Slack, that’s how Leon would do it. Be public, be visible, and start making reaches. Doesn’t have to be someone at water cooler. “I see you work on this team and am intersted in development, can we talk about your experience?” A lot more likely to succeed with these internal contacts on the company platform vs. an external networking attempt on a platform like LinkedIn or random e-mail. [Ended at 3:31 PM PT] Leon: “You’re either working hard, or recharging hard. There should be no in between!” Happy Holidays! Very Thankful for those who are giving back to 100Devs and staying active in the community. Leon appreciates us showing up. Leon wants us to know that he loves us deeply, and it has been an honor to get to know us, and see us continue to show up and be part of the community. Hopes to see us again on the 30th.

author: sheep (Kayla)
title: sheepkayla/2024-05-11
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

took some notes during the huddle, had to leave about half way through but some notes are better than none

a good amount of jobs/interviews/people paid recently!, A lot of people on social media seem to be doom posting when it comes to the job market,

I have my very very very first interview, they said it's a quick chat with the hiring manager and a senior dev I am freaking out HELP,

have to remove emotion from the hunt, even excitement, and act normal! this can be hard to do for many though,

the people interviewing you are your peers, they'll have an easier job if you get hired so don't freak out!,

one interview is just one interview, you'll have others, do your banki, talk about your 100 projects, know the stack that they want and know the company, ask about what kind of interview it is,

how many rounds of interviews is too much?, depends on the company level and compensation, entry-level should be one, FAANG will have more,

3.Hi Leon, I came across this defeated linkedin post from one of our own. How can we avoid becoming utterly crushed like this? [link removed for privacy] things will weigh on you (it's called the trough of sorrow for a reason) it's not easy! there's a reason Leon shows the journey first thing.,

this is not an easy path, but if you make it through, it's worth it. compare it to a traditional college degree, 4-5 years of work.

be realistic, things will fail and you'll have to keep going. take care of yourself, manage your frustration, and be consistent. it's the journey, not the end goal. you'll drain yourself otherwise., the goal is to be a better developer than you were yesterday., Atomic Habits is a really good book to read,

Is May a hot month for job searching for the hunt?, yes, but it's not optimized for non-traditional learners., internship/entry-level jobs pop up for college students and companies have a better grasp on the hiring budget for the year,

What is the best way to contribute or get involved with open-source projects? I am confused about how even to get started., get to a point where you understand code basics to be helpful,, look for first-timer-only projects that'll teach you how to contribute, get on projects you care about! look for low-level issues and get involved with the community. don't contribute just to contribute., 100devs will have community projects soon!,

Any news regarding Cohort3?, it'll happen when it happens,

How do you separate learning for gaining knowledge versus exploration? I often get overwhelmed when reading the coursework (Shay Howe, for example) trying to capture all the important things for my Anki, and this holds me back from progressing in the coursework., always ask yourself what the point of the material is., a lot of engineering is not knowing precisely but knowing where to get information. get the GIST and the fingers on the keyboard practice., What will this do for you? will it help you accomplish what you want? make a list of things you want to check out later.,

I got to the final interview of a job 6 months ago, rejected. they've been posting the same job every month for the past year but no new hires. Thoughts?, the clown show came to town, don't be surprised they have clowns., it's free to post on most job boards and nobody doing quality control., some companies want to seem like they're hiring. keep moving or hit them back up.,

How can we volunteer for cohort 3?, fill out the maintainer's form,

I'm getting to the 2nd/3rd interview, usually behavioral, & getting declined. I'm autistic & struggle with interviews. I'm doing banki. Anything else I can do?, practice interviews with people who will tell you the truth, be more aware/get BRUTAL feedback and be analytical about it, and come up with solutions., you will find a fit.,

Hey Leon, I am 6 interviews deep. Half a year into the hunt, got to a few final rounds but all rejections. Have any advice?, same thing. as the previous question., it's 1 out of 60 interviews that you get a win.,

What are your thoughts of accepting a role in tech support? I currently drive for Uber so it would be a pay increase and I’d get to work at a tech company., yeah. get paid., take any role that will make your life more comfortable for the hunt but don't forget the hunt.,

Hey Leon should I only fill out the talent base form if I have finished huntober. Also thank you for reminding me the other day to be kind to myself., nah, fill it out wherever you're at., Leon needs info to adjust things., make sure to give view permission for all the links you submit, he hasn't been able to try and match people because he can't see their stuff,

bombed the role, reached back out, and got a second chance. is the expectation higher now, no, it's usual that companies extend the chance if the role isn't filled., companies understand people have bad interviews sometimes. don't expect more unless they verbalize it., a lot of companies have a default reapply after 6 months and know that the hiring process is broken.,

author: sheep (Kayla)
title: sheepkayla/2024-05-14
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

is cohort 3 going to take place in May or are there plans to postpone it for some other date? don't delay, start today. don't put off becoming a software engineer if you want to be a software engineer. when a start date is made for cohort 3, it will be abundantly clear it's happening. there's no way you'd miss it.

i don't have a portfolio since i'm in class 8. how can i join the talent base? don't worry about the talent base right now, it doesn't have any practical value for you since you haven't finished the cohort. having people fill it out was to try and figure out what people need right now it's more for people who are finished/close to finished.

When’s the deadline for 100devs talent base? there's no deadline because it's not a fully fledged thing yet even though some people have gotten interviews from it.

My 1st interview & 2 qs that caught me off guard 3 words ur cowrkrs would use to describe u & 3 words ur friends &family wuld use to describe u how do I answer? those are silly interview questions but make sure to be doing your banki questions because keywords you could use come from Banki. don't snitch on yourself/say something negative about yourself when answering questions like this, interviews are a game and there are ways to play it well, not a time for blunt honesty. there's no humbleness in the HUNT! you want the company to know you're awesome.

As a guy, how can I make sure when I invite someone to a coffee chat, it's an extra safe space? I want to only make friends and absolutely respect boundaries. if this is a worry you have, you're probably going to do okay. keep all professional commuication; keep on Linked In/Twitter, don't ask for personal phone numbers to call or text. if you don't know how to be professional, don't do coffee chats with strangers. do a practice coffee chat with someone you know(coworker/friend) and get feedback. don't comment on physical attributes. you don't need to talk about someone's clothes, hairstyle, or body in any way. if you do mess up, apologize. it's pretty easy to tell when you've messed up and you should recognize it, apologize, and make sure to not do it again moving forward.

I work in a small startup and my boss is pressuring me about task deliveries even though I'm on time with them I even do design besides coding because no UI dev. startups have a habit of overworking people for little compensation a lot of start-up bosses are folx that have never been bosses. they might not know how to manage or treat employees. keep track of your own tasks and show them the body of work, show them you're doing more work and exceeding expectations share your wins with your team/your boss, they might not notice otherwise. know when to set and hold your boundaries. a lot of startup founders want to find people they can trick into doing a bunch of work for little pay. PLAN YOUR DAY AND HAVE WELL DEFINED GOALS. Ask in an email what they want to be accomplished and when, so if they try to move the goalpost later, you can point that out. be aware of how "fragile" your boss is when it comes to their management style.

How to answer the question what is some critical feedback you've gotten recently? This got me tripped up during an interview there's a reason Leon has us do a freelancing project. It lets you get feedback that you can use to answer. if you can't work for whatever reason, you can use volunteer work to get feedback. when answering a question like this, explain what the issue was that you got feedback on, what action(s) you took to resolve it, and the result.

My local area hires a lot of php and laravel should learn these? yes. you've learned how to learn through the course, start learning and make a project.

I was data analyst in Africa (1.5 YOE), since moving to Canada couldn't get any job for a year. Do you think I can have more chances in SWE ? Also, any advice? Leon doesn't know the specifics of getting a job w/ Canada and a visa issues if any. build a list of global remote hiring companies see what you can improve on before switching and think of what's best for your long-term

Haven't got an interview all month after getting many last month. Feeling down and letting doubts getting into my head. Thoughts? hiring is cyclical, it'll ebb and flow. don't get caught up in the pattern, be consistent with what you're doing to get hired REMOVE THE EMOTION, IT'S ONE OF 60 INTERVIEWS don't get excited about getting an interview so often, the first one is super exciting because it's the first but getting so hyped up on every single one will make the doubt feel worse don't stop doing the things that got you interviews. some people start off trying to get interviews strong but after a while stop going at it so consistantly what time of year do companies hire the most? January, March, May, November, and October are usually pretty booming but companies hire all year I interviewed and got an offer but I need to stay working with them for 2 years, normal? Recruiter said not to worry about that it's normal practice no. there's a rise in these companies that convince you that you're less than others and that you "need to be trained before becoming full-time". And if you leave before the two years, you'll have to pay them for the "training" they provided. might be illegal or legal depending on where you live.

The company I'm interviewing for told me there is no hierarchal structure and they aren't big on role progression is that a red flag? it depends on the company and depends on the size. some places are great but some can be lord of the flies-esque sometimes it is sort of a stinky smell so make sure to check the company out. check Glassdoor for reviews, check Reddit, and any other place you can think of. if it's a good offer take it, rather get paid for 3 months and quit than not get paid for 3 months remember what they say during the interview but evaluate it later

How long is too long to spend on 100Hours project? it depends, some 100Hours projects turn into businesses but be careful not to only build on the 100hrs project, work on getting employed

salary not keeping up with the cost of living. like what I do, who I do it with but it's becoming less affordable. been getting back into the hunt. staffing agency pipelined to a company that I like. But once I said didn't want a temp job, the recruiter's tone changed immediately. Positions are only 3 months long according to the recruiter. am i wrong for wanting more? how to proceed in the hunt in the age of AI? it's the recruiter's job to make the most money for them and the company, not you staffing agencies don't always have your best interests in mind and the recruiter has an economic interest in you getting a lower salary for them to get a larger cut. in general, don't take the word of ONE person as fact, people are incentivized to make you feel a certain way Use AI as a tool to: help you tweak cover letters, resumes, and messages, and find companies the practicality of AI is up to you always do the last pass yourself, AI can generate some WILD stuff. watch the classes on speedrunning you can connect GPT to Google Sheets and do prompts in the Google Sheets and Leon's been meaning to do a video on that now that GPTs are more accessible a kind of side conversation that was had was about how great libraires are. in a lot of places, you can get cards for free and it lets you get access to books, magazines, music, movies, etc. some services like Libby and Hoopla let you get access to things remotely even if you don't live an area where you have a library near you

author: sheep (Kayla)
title: sheepkayla/2024-05-18
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

I’ve made it to the final stage. It’s a cultural INT with the VP of tech and head of product. Already had a behavioural INT,what’s the difference between the 2? depends on the size of the company, especially if it's a small one the VP might want to sign off on the new people coming on always ask what you're walking into is there anything about the interview that you can find online? check sites like glassdoor know about the company, values of the company, missions, etc, so you can talk about how you uphold them the hardest interview of leon's career was a cultural interview with the CEO that went completely technical How do you handle employment gaps on your resume and discuss them in interviews (Returning to school, young children, etc.)? freelancing! you were freelancing the freelancing project you are asked to do will back that up and give you clients that they can talk to had a coffee chat, but didn’t quite hit it off with the other person. i feel awkward asking for anything else, let alone a referral. how to move forward? there is no HUMBLENESS IN THE HUNT can't lose a job you don't have, always make the ask what is pride? shoot your shot (for the job) sometimes the interaction is only awkward for you, assume the best you're your own worst critic sometimes, your brain is good at being mean to you for people in catchup crew - if let's say you start cohort 3 in couple months, and catchup ain't done, should they redo the full live cohort ? or is it okay? make that decision when it happens if you're deep in cohort 2, you might pick up a few more things but the content will largely be similar is it true that we are lying by saying that 100devs was an employer? ive seen this posted around discord it depends on what you say you did training program tied to an agency is the truth the threshold people have for resumes is made up show the freelancing you did because of it, that's undeniable work that you did you can't please everybody; some interview folx might not agree but one of 60, keep going What’s the best way to increase dms to a coffee chat? I customize each message but seem to have such a low response rate even just for a coffee chat. Thanks! what's a low response rate? think in the hundreds depends on the type of message and type of area(Twitter, LinkedIn, Instagram, email) keep reaching out, and think about what type of message would YOU open and respond to. always snowball, "Who should I talk to next?" Im the person who got the opportunity to teach esl in korea & I was wondering how to network in a country where my visa only allows me to teach? is it worth it? you have to be super careful, this is Korean labor law, talk to someone who knows reach out to fellow expats working in tech don't listen to people on the internet talking out of their butt about legal issues Friends co is hiring 4 DevOp/Cloud Engineer position. Asked me to apply & send resume so he can forward 2 HR. Do I ask to lmk if a SWE opens or apply to this? depends have honest convo if they're an actual friend if not, go the route they say(apply and send in your resume) and add on that you'd like to know about SWE positions too if they pop up How do you never eat alone in a remote position? the same way you do in person, eat awkwardly in front of your coworkers ask to hangout over lunch, or let people know you're available to talk during lunch be the person to set up events! gatheround was a site used early on in the cohort I have a company that wants proof that I worked at 100devs through pay stubs what do I do. I have volunteer experience, do I just tell them about it if you completed Huntober, ask via ModMail for the mods to verify your time how to i reach out to busy people who said they'd get back to me about coffee chats but haven't yet, what kind of message can I send them be persistent! you don't know what people's lives are like, they could of had something crazy going on, just follow up Do we need a job to play csgo? no Hey Leon, Is it okay to use the same cv bob template when applying to the next job or is it for first job only ? the CV Bob template is more geared toward first job as SWE while the template itself is fine, some people switch to the Harvard CV after How and what do we ask about an interview's content - for example, who is interviewing me, is it technical, and if so what kind of technical questions? that's exactly the way you ask just make it more formal and interesting sounding! How many months of work experience does it count as? If I started 100devs 2 years ago and only made money a few months ago? Is that a few months or 2 years? up to you, that's the beauty of self-paced learning some people only count the time they were most recently actively working on the cohort Whats going on with the CS:Go server? I saw mention of it on your twitter; can we make this a thing and get to work (at the office 😉)? Leon has it running but hasn't launched it there's a planned week of events when maintainers launch and it might be fun to hangout in the evening there 17.Im at 6kyu in codewars, and still my code is mostly brute forced. Am I lacking in my basics? no 6kyu is the time to switch to LeetCode if you're brute-forcing be analytical of your solutions and reinforce your patterns use the 20 min rule before analyzing the solution and adding the pattern to Anki feeling competent and confident helps a LOT and that's what code challenges should do know your strengths and weaknesses but don't take it to heart! it's a game! it should be fun side conversation: this is a pretty great video to watch if you have a Mac, it brought a lot of settings Leon didn't know about to his attention, especially a break timer called Time Out that has you take small 10 second breaks by greying out your screen. Perfect time to take a break from typing. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GK7zLYAXdDs take longer breaks are important too ! stretch! move a bit! take a quick walk! YouTube Syntax Set up a Mac in 2024 for Power Users and Developers Image

author: sheep (Kayla)
title: sheepkayla/2024-06-05
html:
by sheep (Kayla)

Missed some of the questions due to wifi issues but @HEMAD (Class 12) tapped in and took some notes that will be coming soon. That being said I want to start off the huddle notes properly.

If you haven't read the article Leon posted in the #follow channel, please take a moment and do so now here (

https://t.ly/zqDmx

) . For people having issues with the link, I'm attaching a pdf version as well.

Federal_appeals_judges_rule_against_Fearless_Fund_grants_for_Black_women.pdf

1.8 MB

Federal_appeals_judges_rule_against_Fearless_Fund_grants_for_Black_women.pdf

Things like this affect everyone. Everyone should have access to the tools to make a living for themselves, put food on the table, and generally just live life. And day by day, these tools are being taken away for no other reason than the system is set up and maintained in such a way that the people on top stay on top. Leon talked about the importance of showing up and supporting each other, about building systems where everyone thrives especially since more and more doors are being shut and we should strive for that not only in our online spaces like this server but in our day-to-day lives. I take notes on huddles and post them here just because I want everyone to be able to access the information shared, even if they can't make it to the huddles. But as a small, small favor in return for today's notes, sometime this week, help a fellow human being. It doesn't have to be a huge act, just do something nice for no other reason than you could.

And in the spirit of contributing specifically to the 100Devs community, check out the Maintainer's form here (

https://discord.com/channels/735923219315425401/738891289071714388/1154886156056526938

)?📝follow-along-materials? and if you can help out, do so!

did few courses & skimmed thru materials, I know things bt not enough to do anything, how do I restart without feeling like I'm repeating the learning process?,

go deep not wide, don't jump from course to course. stick to something and see it all the way through to the end,

you're not learning if you're not engaging your brain, you're busy and that's the worst thing if you're trying to learn something. do the work, take the examples, and make it your own by adding more things. being able to build on to something shows you can do things.,

repetition isn't bad, it's the KEY to learning. repeating or SRS is how we fix the forgetting curve,

you're ahead of the people who haven't done anything and have just said they want to code. you have your own pace, attune to it, and stick to that, not anyone else. BE KIND TO YOURSELF, if you can think up negative thoughts, you can think positive ones,

2.I get SO many inbound recruiter interest which usually is just a song and dance that ends up leading no where. Tips to increase success rate when working w/ rec

have a prewritten response to give to MOST recruiters; essentially something that says "Here's my dream list, if you can meet it, let's talk.",

move away from a generic message if you're new to the scene. identify what brought the recruiters to you and highlight it,

you might be doing something unconsciously that's causing you to get ghosted, be analytical of what you're messaging them,

3.I go to meetups but I usually only meet other people looking for jobs. Any advice? (many lost jobs due to layoffs, etc.)

that's pretty normal, people hustling go to more meetups,

try more technical or hack night meetups, those generally will have fewer new-to-the-scene people,

think about what events would attract people in a more senior dev position and consider that when planning,

see if you have a local hackerspace and if you do manage to find people you vibe with, ask where they go.,

4.Can we still apply for program verification? I’ve completed the steps but never bothered to apply.

yeah, you can submit it. if you need it quickly, send a modmail,

5.Where did you camp in Yosemite on short notice?? Or did you stay in a lodge. Fellow outdoor enthusiast here.

Leon used a bot called campflare,

set up days you want to hike or stay and the bot will send you a push notification when people drop their reservations,

6.if we are in catchup crew and are going through the checklist , can we still be accepted into the linkedin 100devs group and endorse people?

of course, all get accepted unless people don't have a photo or title,

follow leon on LinkedIn! he wants to share more content there to get more people to 100devs! https://www.linkedin.com/in/leonnoel/,

7.Hey Leon, How do I renegotiate an increase in salary if I do two jobs in one, I design UI for mobile and web apps alongside frontend work.

depends, are you up for renegotiation?,

think about your points of leverage aka your body of work or have another offer to put some pressure,

be vocal about your value, not just then but all through the year! make 3/6/9/12 month goals in writing with your superiors and crush them! and let them know about it,

corporate culture isn't friend culture, but make an effort to acknowledge people and give credit where it's due,

show that you're doing both jobs and deserve the money for it. don't wait for the meeting either, take the intiative and set it up,

8.news on cohort 3?

Leon's ready to go to work,

he wants to have a huge focus on community support and serving the larger initiative of building systems of equality,

9.What do you mean before it’s too late?

look at wealth distribution, the scales are against us,

every step of the way there are barriers for every single person, doesn't matter your race or creed,

when Leon says it's too late, he means more and more windows are closing. it's up to us to make communities, support each other, and help each other through, nobody else is going to do it.,

10.hey leon hope you are doing well. during networking events and coffee chats should we bring up the fact we are actively looking for work?

no,

you're there to make friends, don't increase your new developer smell, most people aren't smooth enough to not make it weird,

11.I have built 3 full stack web applications but not gotten a job. should I continue building web apps with react JS or focus on learning another language like C#

none of the above, you need to network,

those things are nice to have, but you should be networking to have your foot in the door, you can't show off if you don't get the interview.

author: sheep (Kayla)
title: sheepkayla/2024-06-12
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

Extra Info: Leon is speaking at Render ATL this Friday at 9am. If you're attending, check out the thread here ?unknown !

Next week will have Huddles like normal but also smaller community sessions where the focus will be getting things nailed down for 100devs. There will be a teaspill lead by Mayanwolfe on the 21st.

[02:03]sheep:

At 54 years old and switching careers, do I realistically have a good chance of getting a coding job due to my age and lack of experience?

• yes, of course. will be harder, and age bias will be a thing. but you'll find a bunch of companies don't care.

• use your age to your advantage! So many skills can be transferred to tech. so many people have led the way already and 400+ people want to see you win!

• craft your story/narrative! show your value. don't let your age be an excuse, you're awesome, tell the world. use your hitlist and network!

[02:05]sheep:

2.After what happened to Twitter/X since cohort 2 , would you still recommend it as strongly for networking? Or do you have an alternate preferred platform?

• hustle everywhere you can! some people don't have the privilege to change platforms and Twitter/X can still be a strong place for networking. you can't be too picky when it comes to what will help you put food on the table

• if you don't want to use it, don't. LinkedIn, Instagram, etc are all options, just find out where folx in your local area are connecting.

[02:07]sheep:

I am worried that 10 years down the line all of this will be for nothing because of AI. And I have existential worry's about my future as well, Thoughts?

• there's a nonzero chance but SWE is a great job right now. If you listen to the doomers, you'll never know what's possible if you don't try.

• tech has big cycles/phases like any industry and many people saying that AI will take over aren't even SWEs. AI is pretty bad right now, you still have to go through verification on the code it does. And the worst case scenario, why not get paid well now and then deal with the "in 10 years" scenario in 10 years?

• not learning because the potential AI takeover is a really good excuse and a lot of people use it

[02:08]sheep:

How do you keep a coffee chat connection alive after the first chat without making it transactional?

• by remembering something about them that you can bring up later. people love to talk about themselves and have people bring up the things they like. don't be afraid to take notes in a Google Doc or set up Google alerts.

• don't be afraid to talk to people, sometimes talking with someone is transactional. and if you go too far, you can always apologize. don't overthink it.

[02:08]sheep:

5.Hey Leon! Can we still join the #100Devs linkedIn group if we don't have a job/are a student? (If that's not okay and we don't have a job, what should we do?)

• yeah, the only requirement is a photo and a title! And Leon plans to do more stuff on LinkedIn in the future.

[02:08]sheep:

6.How do folks manage in the catchup crew? I get that taking care of myself is important, but it feels like every minute could be better used by studying.

• work hard or recharge hard. The death of learning is when people exist in the in-between. kinda taking care of yourself and kinda learning doesn't work. focus or relax. Have a schedule, don't go into your day like an accident.

• people can rarely properly study for 12 hrs, most people do 2 hours a day. you're probably going to burn out quickly if you try to do more at first. after a while, try using body doubling, a silent study channel, or things like focusmate to increase your time. remember the focus and diffused mode.

[02:09]sheep:

7.If watching the classes aren’t enough to grasp a concept, is it ok to watchYouTube videos about it or read more documentations? For example javascript.info

• absolutely! use all your resources. sometimes when we introduce something in class, it's just the first pass. Watch the next class and see if you still don't understand and then check out more resources.

• sometimes, things are cognitively difficult (ie having to put in the actual work) vs you not understanding.

• CHECK OUT MAYANWOLFE! She has great YouTube videos going over much of the material

[02:09]sheep:

8.Will your talk at Render be streamed online for those that aren't attending and want to watch?

• some talks have been recorded before leon doesn't know if his talk will. leon will put up the slides and maybe record himself. it's not a learning talk, but more about about being a part of your community and giving back to your community.

[02:09]sheep:

9.I got hired, how do I make the most out of my first year on the job to learn and grow the most?

• make sure you talk to your manager about clear expecations about 3/6/9/12 month goals, have them written down, and leave a paper trail. keep regular communication about them and make a paper trail about any changes. so many people don't do this and worry about where they stand when it comes to performance reviews. This way you should know where you stand pretty easily.

• never eat alone. get to know your coworkers, team, managers then other teams, everyone should know who you are. hype people up and celebrate their wins! Check out the books: Never Eat Alone and How To Win Friends And Influence People.

• set the tone in the first 3 months for the next two years. be present, lean into asking questions, and document answers.

• document everything. wins, anything you've done, document it all. onboarding, setting up your dev environment, etc, and sharing this with new people or anyone else who asks.

[02:09]sheep:

10.Anything different you recommend we do during hunt compared to what you suggested during the 2nd cohort? AKA any new tactics?

• a lot of the advice is slow to change because it's more fundemental. the only things that change are how you do it, you might use more AI to help develop messages or your hitlist now.

[02:09]sheep:

11.Where do I find the video on coffee chats and networking?

• search through the yt channel for the networking vids

• planning on having more teaspills soon. The following Friday(the 21st) will be a teaspill Mayanwolfe leading. keep push notifications on!

[02:10]sheep:

12.You said if you can’t show your code from prev roles, talk about the project in a technical way. I worked on a big project how deep show I go? (8 sec rule?)

• as much as they'll let you. just ask them how deep they want you to go!

author: sheep (Kayla)
title: sheepkayla/2024-06-29
html:
notes by sheep (Kayla)

1.I filled the 100 Maintainers form, but wasn't added to anything on the server. On June 18th you said mentioned about launching it. Is there space to join or not More people will be added slowly, making sure things are working and getting a starting direction for the maintainers, 2.Worried about upward mobility. Heard from several team members that manager doesn't fight for us. 2nd week on the job. Signed a two year contract. What do? Never eat alone. Fight for yourself, keep notes of what you’ve done, weekly journal of what you did and what it did for the company. Get your own network at work and people who want to fight for you. Always have a counter offer ready on promotions and raises.
